## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Walkthrough

### 3.1 Worked Example — Non-Pre-emptive Priority Scheduling

**Question.** Consider the following set of five processes. **Lower priority number = higher scheduling priority.** Compute CT, TAT, WT for each process and the average values using **non-pre-emptive** priority scheduling.

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) | Priority |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| P1 | 0 | 7 | 2 |
| P2 | 1 | 5 | 1 |
| P3 | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| P4 | 3 | 1 | 4 |
| P5 | 4 | 2 | 5 |

#### 3.1.1 Gantt Chart Construction — Step-by-Step Reasoning

> [!NOTE]
> We track the **ready queue** at every clock tick and always dispatch the **lowest priority number** in it (highest urgency).

**Step 1 — Time $t = 0$ ms.**
The only arrived process is **P1**. The dispatcher selects **P1**.
P1 begins execution at $t = 0$.

**Step 2 — Time $t = 0 \rightarrow 7$ ms.**
P1 runs uninterrupted to completion (non-pre-emptive mode).
P1 completes at $t = 7$ ms. By this time, all five processes have arrived.

$$\text{Ready queue at } t = 7 : \{P2(\text{prio}=1),\;P3(\text{prio}=3),\;P4(\text{prio}=4),\;P5(\text{prio}=5)\}$$

**Step 3 — Time $t = 7$ ms.**
Lowest priority number = 1, which belongs to **P2**.
P2 begins at $t = 7$ and runs for BT = 5 ms.

**Step 4 — Time $t = 7 \rightarrow 12$ ms.**
P2 completes at $t = 12$ ms.

$$\text{Ready queue at } t = 12 : \{P3(\text{prio}=3),\;P4(\text{prio}=4),\;P5(\text{prio}=5)\}$$

**Step 5 — Time $t = 12$ ms.**
Lowest priority number = 3, which belongs to **P3**.
P3 runs from $t = 12$ to $t = 15$ ms.

**Step 6 — Time $t = 15$ ms.**
Ready queue = $\{P4(\text{prio}=4),\;P5(\text{prio}=5)\}$.
**P4** is chosen. P4 runs from $t = 15$ to $t = 16$ ms (BT = 1).

**Step 7 — Time $t = 16$ ms.**
Only **P5** remains. P5 runs from $t = 16$ to $t = 18$ ms.

#### 3.1.2 Final Gantt Chart

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt:} \quad & \underset{0}{\mid}\;\;\;P1\;\;\;\underset{7}{\mid}\;\;\;P2\;\;\;\underset{12}{\mid}\;\;\;P3\;\;\;\underset{15}{\mid}\;\;\;P4\;\;\;\underset{16}{\mid}\;\;\;P5\;\;\;\underset{18}{\mid}
\end{aligned}$$

#### 3.1.3 Computing the Performance Metrics

Using the formula $\text{TAT} = \text{CT} - \text{AT}$ and $\text{WT} = \text{TAT} - \text{BT}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CT}(P1) &= 7,   & \text{TAT}(P1) &= 7 - 0 = 7,   & \text{WT}(P1) &= 7 - 7 = 0 \\[4pt]
\text{CT}(P2) &= 12,  & \text{TAT}(P2) &= 12 - 1 = 11,  & \text{WT}(P2) &= 11 - 5 = 6 \\[4pt]
\text{CT}(P3) &= 15,  & \text{TAT}(P3) &= 15 - 2 = 13,  & \text{WT}(P3) &= 13 - 3 = 10 \\[4pt]
\text{CT}(P4) &= 16,  & \text{TAT}(P4) &= 16 - 3 = 13,  & \text{WT}(P4) &= 13 - 1 = 12 \\[4pt]
\text{CT}(P5) &= 18,  & \text{TAT}(P5) &= 18 - 4 = 14,  & \text{WT}(P5) &= 14 - 2 = 12
\end{aligned}$$

#### 3.1.4 Averages

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Average TAT} &= \frac{7 + 11 + 13 + 13 + 14}{5} = \frac{58}{5} = 11.60 \text{ ms} \\[6pt]
\text{Average WT} &= \frac{0 + 6 + 10 + 12 + 12}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8.00 \text{ ms} \\[6pt]
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{5 \text{ processes}}{18 \text{ ms}} \approx 0.278 \text{ processes/ms} \\[6pt]
\text{CPU Utilisation} &= \frac{7+5+3+1+2}{18} \times 100 = 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example — Pre-emptive Priority Scheduling (Same Data Set)

Using the **same** process table, we now allow pre-emption whenever a higher-priority process arrives.

#### 3.2.1 Event-by-Event Gantt Construction

**At $t = 0$:** Only P1 is ready. **P1** runs. Remaining BT of P1 = 6.

**At $t = 1$:** P2 arrives with priority 1 (higher than P1's priority 2).
The scheduler **pre-empts** P1 and dispatches **P2**. P1 (rem 6) goes to the ready queue.

**At $t = 2$:** P3 arrives with priority 3. P2 (priority 1) is still higher. **P2** continues. Remaining of P2 = 3.

**At $t = 3$:** P4 arrives with priority 4. P2 still higher. **P2** continues. Remaining of P2 = 2.

**At $t = 4$:** P5 arrives with priority 5. P2 still higher. **P2** continues. Remaining of P2 = 1.

**At $t = 5$:** No new arrival. **P2** continues. Remaining of P2 = 0. **P2 completes at $t = 6$ ms.**

**At $t = 6$:** Ready queue = $\{P1(\text{prio}=2,\text{rem}=6),\;P3(\text{prio}=3,\text{rem}=3),\;P4(\text{prio}=4,\text{rem}=1),\;P5(\text{prio}=5,\text{rem}=2)\}$.
Highest priority → **P1**. P1 runs from $t = 6$ to $t = 12$ (completes).

**At $t = 12$:** Ready = $\{P3(3,3),\;P4(4,1),\;P5(5,2)\}$ → **P3** runs from $t = 12$ to $t = 15$.

**At $t = 15$:** Ready = $\{P4(4,1),\;P5(5,2)\}$ → **P4** runs from $t = 15$ to $t = 16$.

**At $t = 16$:** **P5** runs from $t = 16$ to $t = 18$.

#### 3.2.2 Final Gantt Chart (Pre-emptive)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt:} \quad & \underset{0}{\mid}\;\;\;P1\;\;\;\underset{1}{\mid}\;\;\;P2\;\;\;\underset{6}{\mid}\;\;\;P1\;\;\;\underset{12}{\mid}\;\;\;P3\;\;\;\underset{15}{\mid}\;\;\;P4\;\;\;\underset{16}{\mid}\;\;\;P5\;\;\;\underset{18}{\mid}
\end{aligned}$$

#### 3.2.3 Performance Metrics

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CT}(P1) &= 12,  & \text{TAT}(P1) &= 12 - 0 = 12,  & \text{WT}(P1) &= 12 - 7 = 5 \\[4pt]
\text{CT}(P2) &= 6,   & \text{TAT}(P2) &= 6 - 1 = 5,    & \text{WT}(P2) &= 5 - 5 = 0 \\[4pt]
\text{CT}(P3) &= 15,  & \text{TAT}(P3) &= 15 - 2 = 13,  & \text{WT}(P3) &= 13 - 3 = 10 \\[4pt]
\text{CT}(P4) &= 16,  & \text{TAT}(P4) &= 16 - 3 = 13,  & \text{WT}(P4) &= 13 - 1 = 12 \\[4pt]
\text{CT}(P5) &= 18,  & \text{TAT}(P5) &= 18 - 4 = 14,  & \text{WT}(P5) &= 14 - 2 = 12
\end{aligned}$$

#### 3.2.4 Averages

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Average TAT} &= \frac{12+5+13+13+14}{5} = \frac{57}{5} = 11.40 \text{ ms} \\[6pt]
\text{Average WT} &= \frac{5+0+10+12+12}{5} = \frac{39}{5} = 7.80 \text{ ms} \\[6pt]
\text{Response Time (P1)} &= 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ ms (allocated immediately)} \\[4pt]
\text{Response Time (P2)} &= 1 - 1 = 0 \text{ ms (allocated on arrival)} \\[4pt]
\text{Response Time (P3)} &= 12 - 2 = 10 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **insight** KTU examiners expect when they ask a 7-mark "compare and contrast" sub-question.

| Criterion | Non-Pre-emptive Priority | Pre-emptive Priority |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Response time of high-priority tasks | **Unbounded** | **Bounded** by sum of longest BT |
| Context switches (this example) | 4 | 6 |
| Average TAT (this example) | 11.60 ms | **11.40 ms** (better) |
| Average WT (this example) | 8.00 ms | **7.80 ms** (better) |
| Suitability for hard real-time | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Risk of priority inversion | Low | **High** (needs PIP/PCP) |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Higher (timer-interrupt based) |

### 3.4 Derivation of the Worst-Case Response Time (Pre-emptive)

A KTU-favourite derivation: the worst-case response time of a task $\tau_i$ in a pre-emptive priority system (no shared resources) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_i &= C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $C_i$ = worst-case execution time (WCET) of task $\tau_i$,
- $T_j$ = period of every higher-priority task $\tau_j$,
- $hp(i)$ = the set of tasks with **higher** priority than $\tau_i$.

This is solved iteratively: start with $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ and iterate $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil R_i^{(k)}/T_j \right\rceil C_j$ until **fixed-point convergence**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Priority Dispatcher

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below depicts the **decision logic** inside the RTOS priority scheduler — the canonical "kernel entry point" of an OS like VxWorks or FreeRTOS.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tick Interrupt or New Arrival Event] --> B{Is Running Process NULL}
    B -- YES --> C[Pick Highest Priority from Ready Queue]
    B -- NO --> D{New Arrival Priority > Running Priority}
    D -- YES --> E[Save Context of Running Process]
    E --> F[Push Running Process to Ready Queue]
    F --> C
    D -- NO --> G{Quantum Expired}
    G -- YES --> C
    G -- NO --> H[Continue Running Process]
    C --> I[Perform Context Switch]
    I --> J[Dispatch Selected Process to CPU]
    J --> K[CPU Executes Task]
    K --> A
    H --> A
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP1[Application Task 1\nPriority 1] --> RQ
    APP2[Application Task 2\nPriority 2] --> RQ
    APP3[Application Task 3\nPriority 3] --> RQ
    APP4[Application Task 4\nPriority 4] --> RQ
    APP5[Application Task 5\nPriority 5] --> RQ

    RQ[Ready Queue\nOrdered by Priority] --> SCHED[Priority Scheduler\nPre-emptive Kernel]
    SCHED --> CPU[CPU Core]
    CPU --> TICK[System Tick Timer]
    TICK -- Tick --> SCHED

    SCHED -. Pre-empts .-> CPU
    SCHED -. Context Save Restore .-> CTX[Context Table\nPCB Array]
    CTX --> SCHED
```

### 4.3 Visualisation of the Gantt Chart (Pre-emptive)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Priority Scheduling Gantt Chart (Lower Number = Higher Priority)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section CPU
    P1 (prio 2) :p1a, 0, 1
    P2 (prio 1) :p2,  1, 5
    P1 (prio 2) :p1b, 6, 6
    P3 (prio 3) :p3, 12, 3
    P4 (prio 4) :p4, 15, 1
    P5 (prio 5) :p5, 16, 2
```

### 4.4 Priority-Inversion Sequence (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant L as Task L (Low Priority)
    participant R as Shared Resource
    participant M as Task M (Medium Priority)
    participant H as Task H (High Priority)

    L->>R: Lock Resource
    Note over L,R: L holds the resource
    H->>L: H needs R, blocks on L
    M->>L: M pre-empts L (normal priority dispatch)
    Note over M,L: Priority Inversion Begins
    M->>M: M runs to completion
    Note over L: L resumes
    L->>R: Unlock Resource
    H->>H: H unblocks and runs
    Note over H: Inversion bounded by Max(M_execution, L_remaining)
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following question bank mirrors the **actual KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination (ESE)** structure: Part A short-answer items (3 marks each) and Part B long-answer items (14 marks each, with **internal choice**).

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define priority scheduling. Differentiate between pre-emptive and non-pre-emptive priority scheduling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** Priority Scheduling is a CPU scheduling algorithm in which each process is assigned a priority and the CPU is always allocated to the process with the **highest priority** in the ready queue.
- **(1 Mark)** In **non-pre-emptive** priority scheduling, once a process acquires the CPU, it runs to completion or until it voluntarily yields (e.g., on I/O), regardless of any higher-priority arrivals.
- **(1 Mark)** In **pre-emptive** priority scheduling, if a newly arriving process has a **higher priority** than the currently running process, the scheduler **pre-empts** the running process and dispatches the higher-priority one, ensuring bounded response time for critical tasks.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is priority inversion? Mention one technique used to overcome it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** Priority Inversion is a scheduling anomaly in which a **higher-priority task is indirectly pre-empted by a lower-priority task**, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities — typically because a low-priority task holds a shared resource that a high-priority task needs, while medium-priority tasks keep running.
- **(1 Mark)** Example: Tasks H (high), M (medium), L (low); L holds a lock needed by H; M pre-empts L; H is forced to wait for **both L and M** to complete, although $H > M > L$.
- **(1 Mark)** **Technique:** The **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** — when L acquires a resource that H is waiting for, L is **temporarily boosted** to H's priority, preventing medium-priority tasks from pre-empting it and bounding the inversion duration.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

Consider the following process table. **Lower priority number = higher scheduling priority.**

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) | Priority |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| P1 | 0 | 4 | 3 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 | 1 |
| P3 | 2 | 1 | 4 |
| P4 | 3 | 2 | 2 |
| P5 | 4 | 5 | 5 |

**(a)** **(7 Marks)** Draw the Gantt chart and compute CT, TAT, WT for each process using **pre-emptive** priority scheduling. Calculate the average TAT and average WT.

**(b)** **(7 Marks)** Repeat part (a) using **non-pre-emptive** priority scheduling. Compare the two results and state which scheme is more suitable for a **hard real-time** system, justifying your answer.

---

##### Model Solution for Question A — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify arrivals and pre-emption points.**

At $t = 0$: P1 arrives (prio 3).
At $t = 1$: P2 arrives (prio 1) — higher than P1 (prio 3) → **pre-empt**.
At $t = 2$: P3 arrives (prio 4) — no pre-emption (P2 has prio 1).
At $t = 3$: P4 arrives (prio 2) — no pre-emption (P2 has prio 1).
At $t = 4$: P5 arrives (prio 5) — no pre-emption (P2 has prio 1).
At $t = 4$: P2 finishes (started at $t = 1$, BT = 3). Ready queue now: $\{P1(3,\text{rem}=3), P3(4,\text{rem}=1), P4(2,\text{rem}=2), P5(5,\text{rem}=5)\}$.
At $t = 4$: **P1** (prio 3) is higher than P4 (prio 2)? No — P4 has lower number = higher priority. So **P4** runs from $t = 4$ to $t = 6$ (P4 BT = 2).
At $t = 6$: Ready = $\{P1(3,\text{rem}=3), P3(4,\text{rem}=1), P5(5,\text{rem}=5)\}$. **P1** (prio 3) runs from $t = 6$ to $t = 9$ (P1 completes).
At $t = 9$: Ready = $\{P3(4,\text{rem}=1), P5(5,\text{rem}=5)\}$. **P3** runs from $t = 9$ to $t = 10$.
At $t = 10$: **P5** runs from $t = 10$ to $t = 15$.

**Step 2 — Final Gantt Chart.**

$$\begin{aligned}
& \underset{0}{\mid}\;\;P1\;\;\underset{1}{\mid}\;\;P2\;\;\underset{4}{\mid}\;\;P4\;\;\underset{6}{\mid}\;\;P1\;\;\underset{9}{\mid}\;\;P3\;\;\underset{10}{\mid}\;\;P5\;\;\underset{15}{\mid}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Metrics.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CT}: &\quad P1 = 9,\; P2 = 4,\; P3 = 10,\; P4 = 6,\; P5 = 15 \\[4pt]
\text{TAT} = \text{CT} - \text{AT}: &\quad
\begin{cases}
P1: 9-0 = 9 \\
P2: 4-1 = 3 \\
P3: 10-2 = 8 \\
P4: 6-3 = 3 \\
P5: 15-4 = 11
\end{cases} \\[6pt]
\text{WT} = \text{TAT} - \text{BT}: &\quad
\begin{cases}
P1: 9-4 = 5 \\
P2: 3-3 = 0 \\
P3: 8-1 = 7 \\
P4: 3-2 = 1 \\
P5: 11-5 = 6
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Averages.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Average TAT} &= \frac{9+3+8+3+11}{5} = \frac{34}{5} = 6.80 \text{ ms} \\[6pt]
\text{Average WT} &= \frac{5+0+7+1+6}{5} = \frac{19}{5} = 3.80 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

---

##### Model Solution for Question A — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Non-pre-emptive Gantt construction.**

At $t = 0$: only P1 is ready → P1 runs from $t = 0$ to $t = 4$ (no pre-emption allowed).
At $t = 4$: Ready queue = $\{P2(1), P3(4), P4(2), P5(5)\}$ → **P2** (prio 1) runs from $t = 4$ to $t = 7$.
At $t = 7$: Ready = $\{P3(4), P4(2), P5(5)\}$ → **P4** (prio 2) runs from $t = 7$ to $t = 9$.
At $t = 9$: Ready = $\{P3(4), P5(5)\}$ → **P3** runs from $t = 9$ to $t = 10$.
At $t = 10$: **P5** runs from $t = 10$ to $t = 15$.

**Step 2 — Final Gantt Chart.**

$$\begin{aligned}
& \underset{0}{\mid}\;\;P1\;\;\underset{4}{\mid}\;\;P2\;\;\underset{7}{\mid}\;\;P4\;\;\underset{9}{\mid}\;\;P3\;\;\underset{10}{\mid}\;\;P5\;\;\underset{15}{\mid}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Metrics.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CT}: &\quad P1 = 4,\; P2 = 7,\; P3 = 10,\; P4 = 9,\; P5 = 15 \\[4pt]
\text{TAT}: &\quad P1=4,\; P2=6,\; P3=8,\; P4=6,\; P5=11 \\[4pt]
\text{WT}: &\quad P1=0,\; P2=3,\; P3=7,\; P4=4,\; P5=6
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Averages.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Average TAT} &= \frac{4+6+8+6+11}{5} = \frac{35}{5} = 7.00 \text{ ms} \\[6pt]
\text{Average WT} &= \frac{0+3+7+4+6}{5} = \frac{20}{5} = 4.00 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison and Verdict.**

| Metric | Pre-emptive | Non-Pre-emptive |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Average TAT | **6.80 ms** | 7.00 ms |
| Average WT | **3.80 ms** | 4.00 ms |
| High-priority response (P2) | **0 ms** (immediate) | 3 ms (waited for P1) |

> **Conclusion (for 1 mark):** **Pre-emptive priority scheduling** is more suitable for **hard real-time** systems because it bounds the worst-case response time of high-priority tasks and meets strict deadlines, whereas non-pre-emptive priority scheduling can cause unbounded blocking of high-priority tasks that arrive during a long low-priority execution.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** **(7 Marks)** Explain the concept of **priority inversion** with a real-time example involving three tasks H, M, and L. Show how the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solves the problem. Mention one alternative protocol.

**(b)** **(7 Marks)** A real-time system has three periodic tasks with the following parameters. Using the **worst-case response time equation**, determine whether task $\tau_3$ is schedulable under pre-emptive priority scheduling.

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) | Priority |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\tau_1$ | 5 | 1 | Highest |
| $\tau_2$ | 10 | 3 | Medium |
| $\tau_3$ | 20 | 5 | Lowest |

---

##### Model Solution for Question B — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Priority Inversion Explanation (3 Marks).**

Priority Inversion is a scheduling anomaly in which a higher-priority task is **indirectly delayed** by a lower-priority task — typically because the low-priority task holds a shared resource (e.g., a mutex) that the high-priority task needs, while medium-priority tasks continue to execute.

**Real-time example (3 Marks):**
- $\tau_H$ (high) needs shared resource $R$.
- $\tau_L$ (low) acquires $R$ at $t = 0$.
- $\tau_H$ arrives at $t = 1$ and **blocks** on $R$.
- $\tau_M$ (medium) arrives at $t = 2$ and **pre-empts** $\tau_L$ (normal priority dispatch).
- $\tau_M$ runs to completion (say 4 ms, finishing at $t = 6$).
- $\tau_L$ resumes, finishes its critical section, releases $R$ at $t = 7$.
- $\tau_H$ finally unblocks at $t = 7$, runs for 2 ms, finishes at $t = 9$.

> **Inversion window = 6 ms** (from $t = 1$ to $t = 7$) — even though $\tau_H$ has the highest priority.

**Step 2 — Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) (1 Mark).**

When $\tau_L$ acquires $R$ and $\tau_H$ subsequently blocks on it, $\tau_L$'s priority is **temporarily raised** to $\tau_H$'s priority. This prevents $\tau_M$ from pre-empting $\tau_L$, and $\tau_L$ executes its critical section at high priority, releasing $R$ quickly. After release, $\tau_L$'s priority reverts to its original value.

**With PIP:** $\tau_L$ runs from $t = 1$ to $t = 3$ (no pre-emption by $\tau_M$), releases $R$, $\tau_H$ runs at $t = 3$ and finishes at $t = 5$. **Inversion window = 2 ms** (bounded by the critical section length).

**Step 3 — Alternative Protocol (0.5 Mark).**

**Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP):** Each mutex is assigned a priority **equal to the highest priority of any task that may lock it**. A task can lock the mutex only if its priority is **strictly higher** than the priority ceilings of all mutexs currently locked by other tasks. This **prevents deadlocks** and bounds inversion to **one critical section**.

---

##### Model Solution for Question B — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the Response-Time Equation (2 Marks).**

For pre-emptive priority scheduling of periodic tasks:

$$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

where $hp(i)$ = set of tasks with higher priority than $\tau_i$.

**Step 2 — Compute $R_3$ Iteratively (4 Marks).**

The set of higher-priority tasks for $\tau_3$ is $\{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$.

**Iteration 0:**

$$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 5 \text{ ms}$$

**Iteration 1:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(1)} &= C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2 \\[6pt]
&= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{5} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{5}{10} \right\rceil (3) \\[6pt]
&= 5 + 1 + 1 \cdot 3 = 5 + 1 + 3 = 9 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(2)} &= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{5} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{9}{10} \right\rceil (3) \\[6pt]
&= 5 + 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 3 = 5 + 2 + 3 = 10 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(3)} &= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{5} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{10}{10} \right\rceil (3) \\[6pt]
&= 5 + 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 3 = 5 + 2 + 3 = 10 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Convergence achieved at $R_3 = 10$ ms.**

**Step 3 — Schedulability Test (1 Mark).**

A task is schedulable iff $R_i \le D_i$ (where $D_i$ is the deadline). Assuming implicit deadlines $D_i = T_i = 20$ ms for $\tau_3$:

$$R_3 = 10 \text{ ms} \;\le\; D_3 = 20 \text{ ms} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Schedulable}$$

**Conclusion:** Task $\tau_3$ meets its deadline with a **slack of 10 ms**. The task set is schedulable under pre-emptive priority scheduling.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Priority-Scheduling Questions**
> 1. **Wrong convention (–2 marks):** Treating "priority 1" as lowest when the question uses "lower number = higher priority" — or vice-versa. **Always verify the convention from the question stem.**
> 2. **Forgetting to update remaining burst (–2 marks):** In pre-emptive problems, students often forget that a pre-empted process returns to the ready queue with its **remaining** burst, not the original burst. This causes cascading errors in CT, TAT, and WT.
> 3. **Skipping arrival-time checks (–2 marks):** Dispatching a process that has **not yet arrived** is a logical error. Always intersect "ready" with "arrived".
> 4. **Wrong formula for WT (–1 mark):** $\text{WT} = \text{TAT} - \text{BT}$ (not CT − BT). Many students write the latter and lose a mark.
> 5. **No units in averages (–0.5 mark):** "Average TAT = 6.8" gets penalised; "Average TAT = 6.80 ms" gets full credit.
> 6. **Gaps in Gantt chart (–1 mark):** Every millisecond of the time axis must be covered. If your chart has a hole, you have either (a) skipped an arrival, (b) used the wrong convention, or (c) forgotten a tie-breaking rule.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for Priority Scheduling**

- ✅ Priority Scheduling dispatches the process with the **highest priority** in the ready queue.
- ✅ Two flavours: **pre-emptive** (bounded response, hard real-time) and **non-pre-emptive** (simpler, soft real-time).
- ✅ Always check the **convention**: "lower number = higher priority" is most common in KTU papers.
- ✅ At every event (arrival, completion, tick), recompute the ready queue and **select the highest-priority process**.
- ✅ Formulas: $\text{TAT} = \text{CT} - \text{AT}$, $\text{WT} = \text{TAT} - \text{BT}$, $\text{RT} = \text{First CPU allocation} - \text{AT}$.
- ✅ **Priority Inversion** = high-priority task blocked by a low-priority task holding a resource.
- ✅ Solutions: **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** and **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**.
- ✅ **Starvation** is cured by **Aging** — gradually increase priority of long-waiting processes.
- ✅ Worst-case response time for periodic task $\tau_i$:

$$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

- ✅ A task is **schedulable** iff $R_i \le D_i$. Iterate the response-time equation until fixed-point convergence.
- ✅ Always draw the **Gantt chart first**, then read off the CT, then derive TAT, WT, RT.
- ✅ Quote averages to **two decimal places** and always include the unit (e.g., "ms").
- ✅ CPU Utilisation = $\frac{\sum \text{BT}_i}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100\,\%$; Throughput = $\frac{n}{\text{Total Time}}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
