> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Round Robin (RR); Advanced Scheduling Techniques (Basics only) - Multilevel Queue Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: CPU Scheduling - Round Robin & Multilevel Queue Scheduling

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Round Robin (RR) Scheduling - Formal Definition

**Round Robin (RR)** is a **preemptive, time-sharing** CPU scheduling algorithm designed specifically for time-sharing systems. It is fundamentally similar to **First-Come, First-Served (FCFS)** scheduling, but with **preemption** added to enable the system to switch between processes. Each process is assigned a fixed unit of time called a **Time Quantum** (or **Time Slice**), typically ranging from **10 to 100 milliseconds**. The ready queue is treated as a **circular queue**, and the CPU scheduler traverses this circular queue, allocating the CPU to each process for a duration of at most one time quantum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Round Robin is one of the most frequently tested CPU scheduling algorithms. Mastery of Gantt chart construction with preemptions, average waiting time (AWT), and average turnaround time (ATT) calculations is essential for KTU University Examinations.

**Key Formal Properties:**
- The ready queue operates as a **FIFO circular queue**.
- A **timer interrupt** triggers every time quantum to enforce preemption.
- If a process's CPU burst is **less than the time quantum**, the process **voluntarily releases** the CPU (no preemption needed).
- If a process's CPU burst is **greater than the time quantum**, the timer fires, the process is **preempted**, and appended to the **tail** of the ready queue.
- A **context switch** occurs at every quantum boundary, introducing overhead.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metric:** The performance of Round Robin depends heavily on the size of the time quantum. Two extreme cases are critical:
> - If quantum is **extremely large** (e.g., $\infty$) → RR degenerates into **FCFS**.
> - If quantum is **extremely small** (e.g., 1 ms) → RR generates excessive **context switch overhead**, leading to poor throughput.

### 1.2 Multilevel Queue Scheduling - Formal Definition

**Multilevel Queue Scheduling** is an advanced CPU scheduling technique where the ready queue is **partitioned into several separate queues**, with each queue holding processes of a specific type or priority class. Each queue may have its **own independent scheduling algorithm**, and there is typically **fixed-priority preemptive scheduling** between queues. A process is **permanently assigned to one queue** based on properties such as memory size, process priority, or process type (system vs. user).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Partitioning in KTU Syllabus:** The classic partition separates processes into:
> - **Foreground (Interactive)** processes → Round Robin scheduling
> - **Background (Batch)** processes → FCFS scheduling
>
> The foreground queue has absolute priority over the background queue to ensure sub-second response for interactive users.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Round Robin Analogy - "The Pizza Slice Wheel":**
Imagine a group of friends sitting around a circular table sharing a single pizza. Each friend gets exactly **one slice (the time quantum)** of the pizza. If they finish eating their slice quickly (CPU burst < quantum), they are done and leave the table (process completes). If they are still chewing when the timer rings (CPU burst > quantum), they must put down their slice, go to the **end of the line (tail of queue)**, and wait for their turn again. This ensures **no single friend starves**, and everyone gets a fair share — this is the essence of Round Robin scheduling.

**Multilevel Queue Analogy - "The Hospital Triage System":**
Imagine a hospital emergency room. Patients are sorted into separate queues based on triage level: **Critical (System processes)**, **Serious (Interactive)**, and **Routine (Batch)**. Critical patients are treated immediately (highest priority queue). Only when the critical queue is empty does the doctor attend to the serious queue. Routine patients wait until all others are served. Each queue has its own internal logic, but there is a strict hierarchy between queues — this perfectly mirrors Multilevel Queue Scheduling.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Round Robin Scheduling - Operational Logic

The Round Robin algorithm operates through the following structured sequence:

- **Step 1 — Queue Initialization:** All newly arriving processes are placed into the ready queue (FIFO circular queue). The CPU scheduler maintains a pointer to the head of the queue.

- **Step 2 — Dispatch the Head Process:** The process at the head of the ready queue is dispatched to the CPU. The timer is initialized/reset to the **time quantum** value $T_q$.

- **Step 3 — Monitor Burst Execution:** The process executes on the CPU. Three possible events may occur:
  - **(a) Process Completes:** If the process finishes its CPU burst before $T_q$ expires, it **voluntarily releases** the CPU and exits the system (or moves to I/O wait queue). The next process in the ready queue is dispatched.
  - **(b) Quantum Expires:** If the timer fires (CPU burst $> T_q$), the process is **forcibly preempted**, a **context switch** occurs, and the process is **appended to the tail** of the ready queue.
  - **(c) Process Blocks for I/O:** If the process issues an I/O request before the quantum expires, it moves to the I/O waiting queue, and the next process is dispatched.

- **Step 4 — Increment Queue Pointer:** The scheduler advances the queue pointer to the next process.

- **Step 5 — Repeat:** Steps 2–4 repeat indefinitely until all processes complete or a shutdown signal is received.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** The arrival time of a process determines **when** it enters the ready queue, but the **scheduling order** depends on its position in the queue when it is its turn. A process arriving during a quantum may have to wait for all currently queued processes to finish their quantum before getting CPU access.

**Performance Implications of Time Quantum Selection:**

- **Throughput:** If the quantum is too small, the system spends more time performing **context switches** than doing useful work. Throughput drops dramatically.
- **Response Time:** RR provides excellent **response time** because no process waits more than $(n-1) \times T_q$ before getting CPU, where $n$ is the number of processes in the ready queue.
- **Turnaround Time:** RR generally has **worse turnaround time** than SJF, but better than FCFS, because shorter processes may still wait behind longer processes that arrived earlier.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes all critical formulas, metrics, and boundary conditions for solving KTU examination problems on Round Robin and Multilevel Queue Scheduling:

| Metric / Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Average Turnaround Time (ATT)** | $ATT = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(CT_i - AT_i)$ | Sum of (Completion Time $-$ Arrival Time) divided by number of processes |
| **Average Waiting Time (AWT)** | $AWT = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(TAT_i - BT_i)$ | Sum of (Turnaround Time $-$ Burst Time) divided by number of processes |
| **Turnaround Time (per process)** | $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$ | Time from arrival to completion |
| **Waiting Time (per process)** | $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$ | Time spent in ready queue |
| **Response Time (per process)** | $RT_i = \text{First CPU Allocation Time} - AT_i$ | Time from arrival until first CPU execution |
| **Maximum Response Delay in RR** | $\text{Max Delay} = (n-1) \times T_q$ | Worst-case wait before first CPU access for $n$ processes |
| **Context Switch Overhead** | $\text{Overhead} = C_s \times N_{cs}$ | Where $C_s$ = time per switch, $N_{cs}$ = total number of switches |
| **Effective CPU Utilization** | $U_{cpu} = \frac{T_q}{T_q + C_s}$ | Fraction of time CPU does useful work |
| **Multilevel Queue Fixed Priority** | $\text{Higher Queue preempted? Yes} \rightarrow \text{Preempt lower}$ | Absolute priority: foreground > background |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for Round Robin:**
> - If $T_q \rightarrow \infty$ → Behaves exactly like **FCFS**.
> - If $T_q \rightarrow 0$ → Pure **context switching overhead**, near-zero throughput.
> - **Heuristic Rule:** Time quantum should be **slightly greater than** the typical time required for an interactive interaction (e.g., $\sim 80\%$ of CPU bursts should be shorter than $T_q$).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Time-Sharing Operating Systems:** UNIX, Linux, and Windows use RR (or variants like **Completely Fair Scheduler**) as the base scheduler for interactive responsiveness.
- **Embedded Real-Time Systems:** RR is used in systems where fairness and predictable response are critical (e.g., industrial control systems, network routers).
- **Multilevel Queue Utility:** Used in production OS kernels like **UNIX** (System V) where the highest-priority queue handles system processes, the middle queue handles interactive tasks, and the lowest queue handles batch jobs.
- **Cloud Computing:** Modified MLQ architectures (with feedback) form the basis of modern Linux kernel schedulers like CFS and real-time scheduling classes.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 KTU-Style Round Robin Numerical Problem - Complete Solution

> **KTU Board Exam Style Problem:** Consider the following set of processes with their arrival times (AT) and CPU burst times (BT). Use **Round Robin Scheduling** with a **Time Quantum = 4 ms** and compute the **Gantt Chart**, **Average Waiting Time (AWT)**, and **Average Turnaround Time (ATT)**.

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 0 | 8 |
| $P_2$ | 1 | 4 |
| $P_3$ | 2 | 9 |
| $P_4$ | 3 | 5 |

**Step 1 — Construct Initial Ready Queue at t=0:**
At $t = 0$, only $P_1$ has arrived. The ready queue is: $[P_1]$.

**Step 2 — Execute First Quantum (0 → 4 ms):**
$P_1$ executes for 4 ms. Remaining burst: $8 - 4 = 4$ ms. $P_1$ is preempted and added to the tail of the queue.
During [0, 4], processes $P_2$ (at $t=1$), $P_3$ (at $t=2$), and $P_4$ (at $t=3$) arrive.
Ready queue at $t=4$: $[P_2, P_3, P_4, P_1]$

**Step 3 — Execute Second Quantum (4 → 8 ms):**
$P_2$ executes for 4 ms. Since $P_2$'s burst (4) equals the quantum, $P_2$ completes at $t=8$.
No new processes arrive during [4, 8].
Ready queue at $t=8$: $[P_3, P_4, P_1]$

**Step 4 — Execute Third Quantum (8 → 12 ms):**
$P_3$ executes for 4 ms. Remaining burst: $9 - 4 = 5$ ms. $P_3$ is preempted.
Ready queue at $t=12$: $[P_4, P_1, P_3]$

**Step 5 — Execute Fourth Quantum (12 → 16 ms):**
$P_4$ executes for 4 ms. Remaining burst: $5 - 4 = 1$ ms. $P_4$ is preempted.
Ready queue at $t=16$: $[P_1, P_3, P_4]$

**Step 6 — Execute Fifth Quantum (16 → 20 ms):**
$P_1$ executes for 4 ms. Remaining burst: $4 - 4 = 0$ ms. **$P_1$ completes at $t=20$.**
Ready queue at $t=20$: $[P_3, P_4]$

**Step 7 — Execute Sixth Quantum (20 → 24 ms):**
$P_3$ executes for 4 ms. Remaining burst: $5 - 4 = 1$ ms. $P_3$ is preempted.
Ready queue at $t=24$: $[P_4, P_3]$

**Step 8 — Execute Seventh Quantum (24 → 25 ms):**
$P_4$ executes for **1 ms** (remaining burst). **$P_4$ completes at $t=25$.**
Ready queue at $t=25$: $[P_3]$

**Step 9 — Execute Eighth Quantum (25 → 26 ms):**
$P_3$ executes for **1 ms** (remaining burst). **$P_3$ completes at $t=26$.**

**Step 10 — Construct the Final Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt Chart:} \quad & \boxed{P_1} \; \boxed{P_2} \; \boxed{P_3} \; \boxed{P_4} \; \boxed{P_1} \; \boxed{P_3} \; \boxed{P_4} \; \boxed{P_3} \\
& 0 \;\rightarrow\; 4 \;\rightarrow\; 8 \;\rightarrow\; 12 \;\rightarrow\; 16 \;\rightarrow\; 20 \;\rightarrow\; 24 \;\rightarrow\; 25 \;\rightarrow\; 26
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 11 — Compute Completion Time (CT), Turnaround Time (TAT), and Waiting Time (WT):**

| Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT = CT $-$ AT | WT = TAT $-$ BT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 0 | 8 | 20 | $20 - 0 = 20$ | $20 - 8 = 12$ |
| $P_2$ | 1 | 4 | 8 | $8 - 1 = 7$ | $7 - 4 = 3$ |
| $P_3$ | 2 | 9 | 26 | $26 - 2 = 24$ | $24 - 9 = 15$ |
| $P_4$ | 3 | 5 | 25 | $25 - 3 = 22$ | $22 - 5 = 17$ |

**Step 12 — Compute Average Metrics:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Turnaround Time (ATT)} &= \frac{20 + 7 + 24 + 22}{4} = \frac{73}{4} = 18.25 \text{ ms} \\[6pt]
\text{Average Waiting Time (AWT)} &= \frac{12 + 3 + 15 + 17}{4} = \frac{47}{4} = 11.75 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Multilevel Queue Scheduling - Analytical Matrix

In Multilevel Queue Scheduling, processes are partitioned based on properties such as **process type** and **memory requirements**. The standard configuration in KTU-recommended textbooks is:

| Queue Level | Process Type | Scheduling Algorithm | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Queue 0 (Highest)** | System Processes | Strict Priority / RR | Absolute Priority |
| **Queue 1** | Interactive (Foreground) | Round Robin | Higher than Background |
| **Queue 2 (Lowest)** | Batch (Background) | FCFS | Lowest Priority |

**Inter-Queue Scheduling:** When a higher-priority queue has a process ready to run, the **currently running lower-priority process is preempted**. This is called **fixed-priority preemptive scheduling** between queues.

**Intra-Queue Scheduling:** Each queue independently applies its own scheduling algorithm. For example, Queue 1 (Interactive) may use RR with $T_q = 8$ ms, while Queue 2 (Batch) uses simple FCFS.

**Example Analytical Trace:**

Consider: Queue 1 (Interactive) has 2 RR processes, Queue 2 (Batch) has 1 FCFS process. The scheduler always checks Queue 1 first.

- $t = 0$: Queue 1 has process ready → dispatch from Queue 1 (preempts any Queue 2 process).
- $t = T_q$: If Queue 1 still has processes → continue with RR within Queue 1.
- $t = \text{Queue 1 empty}$: Dispatch from Queue 2 using FCFS.
- If a new process arrives in Queue 1 during Queue 2 execution → **preempt** and switch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** A common mistake is forgetting that Multilevel Queue Scheduling has **NO process movement between queues**. This distinguishes it from **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ)**, where processes *can* migrate between queues. In MLQ, a process is permanently assigned to one queue for its entire lifetime.

### 3.3 Comparative Analysis Table - RR vs Multilevel Queue

| Feature | Round Robin (RR) | Multilevel Queue (MLQ) |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Single queue, preemptive | Multiple queues, partitioned |
| **Algorithm Within Queue** | RR (only) | Each queue may have different algorithm |
| **Inter-Queue Scheduling** | Not applicable | Fixed priority preemptive |
| **Process Migration** | Allowed (FIFO circular) | **Not allowed** (permanent assignment) |
| **Starvation** | No starvation (fair) | Possible in lower-priority queues |
| **Response Time** | Excellent for interactive | Excellent only for high-priority queues |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Low | Moderate to High |
| **Use Case** | Time-sharing systems | Mixed workload OS (system + user + batch) |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Round Robin Scheduling - Circular Ready Queue Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Arrives] --> B[Add to Tail of Ready Queue]
    B --> C{Queue Empty?}
    C -- No --> D[Dispatch Head Process to CPU]
    D --> E[Start Timer with Time Quantum Tq]
    E --> F{Burst Complete?}
    F -- Yes, Burst less than Tq --> G[Process Voluntarily Releases CPU]
    G --> H[Process Completes]
    F -- No, Timer Fires --> I[Preempt Process via Context Switch]
    I --> J[Move Process to Tail of Ready Queue]
    J --> C
    C -- Yes --> K[CPU Idle]
    K --> L{New Process Arrives?}
    L -- Yes --> B
    L -- No --> K
```

### 4.2 Multilevel Queue Scheduling - Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU_SCHEDULER["CPU SCHEDULER MASTER DISPATCHER"]
        M[Fixed Priority Preemptive Selector]
    end

    subgraph Q0["QUEUE 0 - SYSTEM PROCESSES"]
        S1[Kernel / OS Critical Tasks]
        S2[Device Drivers]
        ALGO0[Algorithm: Strict Priority]
    end

    subgraph Q1["QUEUE 1 - INTERACTIVE FOREGROUND"]
        I1[User Shell]
        I2[Editor]
        I3[Browser]
        ALGO1[Algorithm: Round Robin Tq=8ms]
    end

    subgraph Q2["QUEUE 2 - BATCH BACKGROUND"]
        B1[Database Backup]
        B2[Log Compaction]
        ALGO2[Algorithm: FCFS]
    end

    M -- Check First --> Q0
    M -- If Q0 Empty --> Q1
    M -- If Q0 and Q1 Empty --> Q2
    M -- If process arrives in higher queue --> PREEMPT[Preempt Current Lower Queue Process]

    Q0 --> M
    Q1 --> M
    Q2 --> M
    PREEMPT --> M
```

### 4.3 Round Robin Gantt Chart Construction - Visual Flow

```mermaid
gantt
    title Round Robin Gantt Chart Example (Tq = 4ms)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %s
    
    section CPU Timeline
    P1 Execution      :p1a, 0, 4
    P2 Execution      :p2a, 4, 4
    P3 Execution      :p3a, 8, 4
    P4 Execution      :p4a, 12, 4
    P1 Execution      :p1b, 16, 4
    P3 Execution      :p3b, 20, 4
    P4 Execution      :p4b, 24, 1
    P3 Execution      :p3c, 25, 1
```

### 4.4 Multilevel Queue Decision Tree - Inter-Queue Scheduling Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Scheduler Invocation]) --> A{Q0 System Queue Empty?}
    A -- No --> B[Dispatch from Q0]
    A -- Yes --> C{Q1 Interactive Queue Empty?}
    C -- No --> D[Dispatch from Q1 RR]
    C -- Yes --> E{Q2 Batch Queue Empty?}
    E -- No --> F[Dispatch from Q2 FCFS]
    E -- Yes --> G[CPU Idle - Wait for Arrival]
    B --> H[Run Process]
    D --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I{New Arrival in Higher Queue?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Preempt Current Process]
    I -- No --> K[Continue Execution]
    J --> START
    K --> H
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Round Robin Scheduling. What is the role of the time quantum in this scheduling algorithm?**

**Model Answer:**

Round Robin (RR) is a **preemptive CPU scheduling algorithm** designed for time-sharing systems. Each process is assigned a fixed unit of time called a **time quantum** ($T_q$), typically between **10 and 100 milliseconds**. The ready queue is organized as a **circular FIFO queue**. The CPU scheduler allocates the CPU to each process for at most one time quantum. If a process does not finish within its quantum, it is preempted and appended to the tail of the ready queue.

**Role of Time Quantum:**
- It is the **fundamental unit of preemption** in RR.
- If $T_q$ is **too large**, RR degenerates into **FCFS**, reducing responsiveness.
- If $T_q$ is **too small**, excessive **context switch overhead** reduces CPU efficiency.
- The ideal $T_q$ is chosen to be **slightly greater** than the typical CPU burst of interactive processes (e.g., ~80% of bursts should be shorter than $T_q$).

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is Multilevel Queue Scheduling? Mention two advantages of using it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Multilevel Queue (MLQ) Scheduling** is a CPU scheduling technique where the ready queue is partitioned into **multiple separate queues**, each holding processes of a specific type or class (e.g., system, interactive, batch). Each queue may have its **own scheduling algorithm**, and **fixed-priority preemptive scheduling** is used between queues. Processes are **permanently assigned** to one queue.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Low Scheduling Overhead per Queue:** Each queue can use a simple, specialized algorithm (e.g., RR for interactive, FCFS for batch), making scheduling decisions fast.
2. **Natural Support for Mixed Workloads:** It effectively separates **real-time, interactive, and batch** workloads, ensuring critical system processes get immediate CPU access without competing with batch jobs.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) - KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) - Alternative 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Question:** Consider the following set of processes with their arrival times and burst times:

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 0 | 5 |
| $P_2$ | 1 | 6 |
| $P_3$ | 2 | 3 |
| $P_4$ | 3 | 1 |
| $P_5$ | 4 | 5 |
| $P_6$ | 6 | 4 |

**(a)** Draw the **Gantt Chart** using **Round Robin Scheduling** with **Time Quantum = 3 ms**. Calculate the **Average Waiting Time (AWT)** and **Average Turnaround Time (ATT)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Multilevel Queue Scheduling** with a neat diagram. State any **two advantages** and **one disadvantage** of the same. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Initialize Ready Queue at t=0:**
At $t=0$, only $P_1$ has arrived. Queue: $[P_1]$.

**Step 2 — Execute Quantum by Quantum:**

- **[0, 3]:** $P_1$ executes (remaining burst = 5 - 3 = 2). During this interval, $P_2$ (at $t=1$) and $P_3$ (at $t=2$) arrive. Queue at $t=3$: $[P_2, P_3, P_1]$.
- **[3, 6]:** $P_2$ executes (remaining = 6 - 3 = 3). During this interval, $P_4$ (at $t=3$) and $P_5$ (at $t=4$) arrive. Queue at $t=6$: $[P_3, P_1, P_4, P_5, P_2]$. Note: $P_6$ arrives at $t=6$, appended to tail: $[P_3, P_1, P_4, P_5, P_2, P_6]$.
- **[6, 9]:** $P_3$ executes (remaining = 3 - 3 = 0). $P_3$ **completes at $t=9$**. Queue at $t=9$: $[P_1, P_4, P_5, P_2, P_6]$.
- **[9, 11]:** $P_1$ executes (remaining = 2 - 2 = 0). $P_1$ **completes at $t=11$**. Queue at $t=11$: $[P_4, P_5, P_2, P_6]$.
- **[11, 12]:** $P_4$ executes (remaining = 1 - 1 = 0). $P_4$ **completes at $t=12$**. Queue: $[P_5, P_2, P_6]$.
- **[12, 15]:** $P_5$ executes (remaining = 5 - 3 = 2). Queue: $[P_2, P_6, P_5]$.
- **[15, 18]:** $P_2$ executes (remaining = 3 - 3 = 0). $P_2$ **completes at $t=18$**. Queue: $[P_6, P_5]$.
- **[18, 21]:** $P_6$ executes (remaining = 4 - 3 = 1). Queue: $[P_5, P_6]$.
- **[21, 23]:** $P_5$ executes (remaining = 2 - 2 = 0). $P_5$ **completes at $t=23$**. Queue: $[P_6]$.
- **[23, 24]:** $P_6$ executes (remaining = 1 - 1 = 0). $P_6$ **completes at $t=24$**.

**Final Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \boxed{P_1} \; \boxed{P_2} \; \boxed{P_3} \; \boxed{P_1} \; \boxed{P_4} \; \boxed{P_5} \; \boxed{P_2} \; \boxed{P_6} \; \boxed{P_5} \; \boxed{P_6} \\
& 0 \;\rightarrow\; 3 \;\rightarrow\; 6 \;\rightarrow\; 9 \;\rightarrow\; 11 \;\rightarrow\; 12 \;\rightarrow\; 15 \;\rightarrow\; 18 \;\rightarrow\; 21 \;\rightarrow\; 23 \;\rightarrow\; 24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute Metrics:**

| Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT = CT $-$ AT | WT = TAT $-$ BT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 0 | 5 | 11 | 11 | 6 |
| $P_2$ | 1 | 6 | 18 | 17 | 11 |
| $P_3$ | 2 | 3 | 9 | 7 | 4 |
| $P_4$ | 3 | 1 | 12 | 9 | 8 |
| $P_5$ | 4 | 5 | 23 | 19 | 14 |
| $P_6$ | 6 | 4 | 24 | 18 | 14 |

**Averages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ATT} &= \frac{11 + 17 + 7 + 9 + 19 + 18}{6} = \frac{81}{6} = 13.5 \text{ ms} \\[6pt]
\text{AWT} &= \frac{6 + 11 + 4 + 8 + 14 + 14}{6} = \frac{57}{6} = 9.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

##### **Solution for Part (b):**

**Multilevel Queue Scheduling:**

In Multilevel Queue Scheduling, the ready queue is partitioned into multiple separate queues based on process characteristics such as **process type, priority, or memory size**. Each queue has its own scheduling algorithm. Inter-queue scheduling uses **fixed-priority preemptive scheduling**.

**Standard Partitioning:**
- **Queue 0 (Highest Priority) — System Processes:** Uses strict priority or RR.
- **Queue 1 — Interactive (Foreground):** Uses Round Robin.
- **Queue 2 (Lowest Priority) — Batch (Background):** Uses FCFS.

A higher-priority queue can **preempt** a lower-priority queue's running process.

**Diagram (Conceptual):**

$$
\boxed{\text{Ready Queue}} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\boxed{\text{Q}_0: \text{System Processes} \rightarrow \text{Priority Scheduling}} \\
\boxed{\text{Q}_1: \text{Interactive Processes} \rightarrow \text{Round Robin}} \\
\boxed{\text{Q}_2: \text{Batch Processes} \rightarrow \text{FCFS}}
\end{cases}
$$

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Efficient handling of diverse workloads** — interactive, real-time, and batch processes are isolated, each with a tailored scheduling algorithm.
2. **Low overhead** — simple per-queue algorithms are fast; the master scheduler only needs to check queue emptiness.

**One Disadvantage:**
1. **Starvation** — low-priority batch processes may suffer indefinite postponement if high-priority queues are continuously busy.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Question:**

**(a)** What is **Round Robin Scheduling**? Explain the impact of **time quantum size** on its performance with appropriate examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Multilevel Queue Scheduling** and **Multilevel Feedback Queue Scheduling** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution for Part (a):**

**Round Robin Scheduling:** RR is a **preemptive, time-sharing CPU scheduling algorithm** where each process is allocated the CPU for a fixed time unit called a **time quantum** ($T_q$). The ready queue is a circular FIFO queue. If a process's burst exceeds $T_q$, it is preempted and sent to the tail of the queue.

**Impact of Time Quantum Size:**

**Case 1 — Time Quantum Very Large (e.g., $T_q = 100$ ms for bursts of 10 ms each):**
- All processes complete within their first quantum. No preemption occurs.
- RR behaves exactly like **FCFS** — no benefit of preemption realized.

**Case 2 — Time Quantum Very Small (e.g., $T_q = 1$ ms):**
- Assume 10 processes each with burst 10 ms. Total useful CPU work = 100 ms.
- Number of context switches $\approx 100$. If each switch costs $C_s = 0.1$ ms, overhead = 10 ms.
- Effective CPU utilization drops to ~91%, and response time improves dramatically for short jobs.

**Case 3 — Time Quantum Optimal (e.g., $T_q \approx 20{-}50$ ms):**
- Most interactive bursts complete within one quantum (no preemption).
- Long CPU-bound processes are periodically interleaved with short interactive jobs.
- Good balance between throughput and response time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mention the heuristic rule: *"Time quantum should be slightly greater than the typical CPU burst of interactive processes."*

**Mathematical Illustration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Context Switches per Quantum} &= 1 \\
\text{Useful CPU Time} &= T_q - C_s \\
\text{CPU Efficiency} &= \frac{T_q - C_s}{T_q} = 1 - \frac{C_s}{T_q}
\end{aligned}
$$

As $T_q \to \infty$, efficiency $\to 1$ (but no preemptive benefit). As $T_q \to C_s$, efficiency $\to 0$ (overhead dominates).

---

##### **Solution for Part (b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Multilevel Queue (MLQ) | Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ) |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Migration Between Queues** | **Not allowed** — process is permanently assigned | **Allowed** — processes migrate based on behavior |
| **Queue Assignment** | Based on process type/priority (static) | Dynamic based on CPU burst history |
| **CPU-Bound Process Behavior** | Remains in same queue (may starve) | Gradually demoted to lower-priority queue |
| **I/O-Bound Process Behavior** | Remains in same queue | Promoted to higher-priority queue |
| **Starvation** | Possible in low-priority queues | Mitigated by aging mechanism |
| **Complexity** | Lower | Higher (requires tracking of burst history) |
| **Use Case** | Systems with well-defined process categories | General-purpose time-sharing OS |

**Diagram:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MLQ (Static):} \quad & \text{Process} \rightarrow \text{Assigned Once} \rightarrow \text{Stays in Queue Forever} \\
\text{MLFQ (Dynamic):} \quad & \text{Process} \rightarrow \text{Start at Top Queue} \rightarrow \text{If CPU-bound, demote} \rightarrow \text{If I/O-bound, promote}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mermaid Comparison Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MLQ["MULTILEVEL QUEUE - STATIC"]
        MLQ_H[System Queue Highest Priority]
        MLQ_M[Interactive Queue Medium Priority]
        MLQ_L[Batch Queue Lowest Priority]
        MLQ_P1[Process P1 permanently in MLQ_H]
        MLQ_P2[Process P2 permanently in MLQ_M]
        MLQ_P3[Process P3 permanently in MLQ_L]
        MLQ_H --- MLQ_P1
        MLQ_M --- MLQ_P2
        MLQ_L --- MLQ_P3
    end

    subgraph MLFQ["MULTILEVEL FEEDBACK QUEUE - DYNAMIC"]
        MLFQ_H[Top Queue High Priority RR]
        MLFQ_M[Middle Queue Medium Priority RR]
        MLFQ_L[Bottom Queue Low Priority FCFS]
        MLFQ_P1[Process Starts in MLFQ_H]
        MLFQ_P2[If CPU-bound - demote to MLFQ_M]
        MLFQ_P3[If still CPU-bound - demote to MLFQ_L]
        MLFQ_H --> MLFQ_P1
        MLFQ_P1 -.Demote.-> MLFQ_M
        MLFQ_M -.Demote.-> MLFQ_P3
        MLFQ_P3 --- MLFQ_L
    end
```

**Key Insight for KTU:**
- **MLQ:** Best when process behavior is **predictable and known in advance**.
- **MLFQ:** Best when **process behavior is unknown** — the OS learns and adapts.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Round Robin / MLQ Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Arrival Times:** Many students treat the ready queue as if all processes arrive at $t=0$. In KTU problems, **arrival times matter** — a process arriving later must wait until its arrival time before being queued.
>
> 2. **Incorrect Queue Update During Quantum:** When a process executes for a full quantum and is preempted, you must check **if new processes arrived during that quantum** and **insert them at the tail** before the preempted process is re-appended.
>
> 3. **Confusing MLQ with MLFQ:** Students often write that "processes move between queues" in MLQ. This is **incorrect** — only **MLFQ** allows migration. In pure MLQ, assignment is **permanent**.
>
> 4. **Gantt Chart Miscalculations:** Always double-check that the **sum of all CPU bursts across all process segments equals the final completion time**. This is a quick sanity check.
>
> 5. **Time Quantum Sign Convention:** If $T_q$ is greater than CPU burst, the process completes in **one quantum without preemption** — do NOT preempt and requeue a finished process.
>
> 6. **Missing the "Voluntary Release" Case:** When burst < $T_q$, the CPU is released **without context switch overhead from preemption** — note this in conceptual answers.
>
> 7. **Inter-Queue Preemption:** In MLQ, forgetting that a process arriving in a higher-priority queue can **immediately preempt** the currently running lower-priority process costs easy marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Round Robin & Multilevel Queue Scheduling:**

- **Round Robin (RR):**
  - Preemptive, time-sharing algorithm with **circular FIFO queue**.
  - **Time Quantum** ($T_q$) is the fundamental unit of preemption (typically 10–100 ms).
  - If burst $> T_q$: process is preempted and moved to **tail of queue**.
  - If burst $\leq T_q$: process **voluntarily releases** CPU and completes.
  - **No starvation** — every process gets fair CPU share.
  - $T_q$ too large $\rightarrow$ degenerates to **FCFS**.
  - $T_q$ too small $\rightarrow$ excessive **context switch overhead**.
  - **Heuristic:** $T_q \approx$ slightly greater than typical interactive burst.
  - Response time is excellent; turnaround time is moderate.

- **Multilevel Queue (MLQ):**
  - Ready queue partitioned into **multiple separate queues**.
  - Each queue has its **own scheduling algorithm** (e.g., RR, FCFS, Priority).
  - **Fixed-priority preemptive scheduling** between queues.
  - **No process migration** between queues — permanent assignment.
  - Possible **starvation** in lower-priority queues.
  - Standard partition: System (highest) | Interactive (RR) | Batch (FCFS lowest).

- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$
  - $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$
  - $AWT = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i$
  - $ATT = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i$
  - Max response delay in RR = $(n-1) \times T_q$

- **Comparison Memory Aid:**
  - RR = **single queue**, fair, no starvation.
  - MLQ = **multiple queues**, specialized algorithms, possible starvation.
  - MLFQ = **multiple queues with feedback**, dynamic migration, aging mitigates starvation.

- **Common KTU Exam Triggers:**
  - "Draw Gantt Chart" → always show time axis clearly with arrow markers.
  - "Compare RR and FCFS" → emphasize preemption and time quantum.
  - "Effect of time quantum" → discuss both extremes (too large / too small).
  - "MLQ vs MLFQ" → emphasize **static vs dynamic** assignment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multilevel Feedback Queue Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multilevel Feedback Queue Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ) Scheduling** is a CPU scheduling algorithm that allows a process to **move between multiple queues** based on its observed CPU-burst characteristics and historical execution behavior. It is a refinement of the **Multilevel Queue (MLQ)** scheduling algorithm which statically assigns processes to a single queue.

The MLFQ scheduler separates processes into **distinct priority classes** organized in a hierarchical queue structure. The defining feature that distinguishes MLFQ from MLQ is **dynamic process migration**: a process that uses too much CPU time is demoted to a lower-priority queue, while a process that waits too long (or performs I/O) is promoted to a higher-priority queue.

### Structural Components of MLFQ

An MLFQ scheduler is completely defined by the following parameters:

1. **Number of Queues** ($N$) — typically ranging from **2 to 5** in classical implementations.
2. **Scheduling Algorithm per Queue** — each individual queue may use FCFS, RR, or any other algorithm.
3. **Method to Upgrade/Promote a Process** — boosting mechanism to prevent starvation.
4. **Method to Demote a Process** — based on accumulated CPU time usage.
5. **Initial Queue Assignment** — where a new process enters the system.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Triage System:**
>
> Imagine a hospital emergency ward with **3 priority levels**:
>
> 1. **Queue 0 (Critical/Red Zone):** Patients arriving in critical condition (e.g., heart attack) are treated **immediately** with round-robin attention among critical patients.
> 2. **Queue 1 (Serious/Yellow Zone):** Patients who need attention but are stable. After their initial examination in Red, if they need extended monitoring, they are moved here.
> 3. **Queue 2 (General/Green Zone):** Patients with minor ailments. They wait the longest.
>
> **How MLFQ Logic Mirrors This:**
> - A patient (process) entering the hospital is **assumed critical** → assigned to **Queue 0**.
> - If the patient **consumes too much doctor time** without stabilizing → demoted to Queue 1.
> - If the patient **keeps hogging resources** → eventually dropped to Queue 2.
> - However, if a Green Zone patient has been **waiting too long**, they are **boosted back up** to prevent indefinite neglect (this is called *starvation prevention*).
>
> The system **learns** from observed behavior rather than trusting pre-declared priorities.

### Why MLFQ Was Invented

The fundamental problem MLFQ solves is that the **OS has no prior knowledge** of whether a process is CPU-bound (long bursts) or I/O-bound (short bursts). MLFQ uses **runtime observation** to make this distinction automatically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> MLFQ was first formalized by **Fernando J. Corbato** in 1962 as part of the **Compatible Time-Sharing System (CTSS)** at MIT. It won the prestigious **ACM Turing Award** contribution and remains the foundation for modern OS schedulers including **Linux CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler)** and **Windows NT scheduler**.

### Standard Metrics Used in MLFQ Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> Key performance metrics (highlighted in **bold** for KTU emphasis):
>
> - **Turnaround Time ($T_{turnaround}$)** — total time from process arrival to completion.
> - **Response Time ($T_{response}$)** — time from arrival to first CPU allocation.
> - **Waiting Time ($T_{wait}$)** — sum of time spent in the ready queue.
> - **Throughput ($\Theta$)** — number of processes completed per unit time.
> - **CPU Utilization ($U_{CPU}$)** — fraction of time CPU is busy.
> - **Fairness Index ($\mathcal{F}$)** — Jain's fairness measure for distribution of CPU time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Fundamental Rules of MLFQ

Classical MLFQ (as described in Silberschatz, Galvin & Gagne — *Operating System Concepts*) is governed by **three rules** and **one optional fourth rule**:

#### **Rule 1: Priority-Based Dispatch**
> If $Priority(A) > Priority(B)$, then scheduler **must** dispatch $A$ before $B$.

A higher-priority queue is **always** serviced before a lower-priority queue. Within a single queue, the queue's own scheduling algorithm applies (typically **Round Robin**).

#### **Rule 2: Same-Priority Round Robin**
> If $Priority(A) = Priority(B)$, then $A$ and $B$ are serviced in **Round Robin** fashion.

This ensures fairness among processes of equal priority.

#### **Rule 3: Demotion on CPU Exhaustion**
> When a process **exhausts its time slice** (quantum) at its current queue level, its priority is **reduced** (demoted to a lower queue).

This is the **learning** mechanism — CPU-bound processes naturally fall to lower queues.

#### **Rule 4: Periodic Boosting (Anti-Starvation)**
> After a fixed time interval $S$ (the *boost period*), **all processes are moved to the topmost queue**.

This is essential to prevent starvation of low-priority, possibly interactive processes.

### Operational Walkthrough

1. A **new process** enters the system and is placed in the **highest-priority queue** (Queue 0).
2. If it **completes within the quantum** of Queue 0, it terminates (or performs I/O and re-enters at Queue 0).
3. If it **exhausts the quantum**, it is **demoted** to Queue 1.
4. At Queue 1, it gets a **larger quantum** (e.g., double the previous one).
5. If it exhausts Queue 1's quantum, demoted to Queue 2 (largest quantum).
6. **After time $S$**, the boost rule fires and all processes return to Queue 0.
7. The cycle repeats.

### Why Each Rule Exists

| Rule | Problem Solved | Without This Rule |
|------|---------------|-------------------|
| Rule 1 | Allows interactive (short-burst) jobs to run quickly | All processes treated equally → poor response time |
| Rule 2 | Fairness within priority class | One process could monopolize a queue |
| Rule 3 | Identifies CPU-bound processes dynamically | System can't distinguish CPU vs I/O-bound |
| Rule 4 | Prevents indefinite starvation | Low-priority jobs may never run |

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all key quantitative relations for MLFQ analysis in KTU examinations. **All boundary conditions and units are explicitly stated.**

| **Metric / Parameter** | **Formula** | **Boundary Conditions** | **Units** |
|------------------------|-------------|------------------------|-----------|
| Turnaround Time | $T_{turnaround} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$ | $T_{turnaround} \geq T_{burst}$ | seconds (s) |
| Response Time | $T_{response} = T_{first\_run} - T_{arrival}$ | $T_{response} \geq 0$ | seconds (s) |
| Waiting Time | $T_{wait} = T_{turnaround} - T_{burst}$ | $T_{wait} \geq 0$ | seconds (s) |
| Jain's Fairness Index | $\mathcal{F} = \dfrac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^2}{n \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{n} \leq \mathcal{F} \leq 1$ | dimensionless |
| CPU Utilization | $U_{CPU} = 1 - I_{idle}$ | $0 \leq U_{CPU} \leq 1$ | fraction |
| Quantum Scaling (Typical) | $q_{i+1} = 2 \cdot q_i$ | $q_0 \in [10\text{ms}, 100\text{ms}]$ | milliseconds |
| Boost Period | $S \approx 10 \cdot q_0$ | $S > \max(q_i)$ | milliseconds |
| Total CPU Time (Work Conserving) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{burst,i} = T_{CPU\_active}$ | Always conserved | seconds |
| Throughput | $\Theta = \dfrac{n}{T_{total}}$ | $\Theta \propto \dfrac{1}{T_{turnaround}}$ | processes/s |
| Effective Quantum (Queue $i$) | $q_{eff,i} = q_i \cdot N_i$ where $N_i$ = #procs in queue $i$ | $q_{eff} \geq q_i$ | milliseconds |

> **Note on absolute value notation:** For expressions like $\vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$ in fairness proofs, use `\vert` in LaTeX (never `|` alone inside markdown tables) to preserve table syntax.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Application of MLFQ Principles:**
>
> - **Linux CFS:** Uses a **red-black tree** of virtual runtimes — fundamentally an MLFQ-like concept where processes with smaller virtual runtimes get CPU first (analogous to high-priority Queue 0).
> - **Windows NT Scheduler:** Uses a **32-level priority queue** with dynamic promotion/demotion based on I/O events (mouse clicks → boost, CPU use → demote).
> - **FreeBSD ULE Scheduler:** Direct MLFQ descendant with interactive vs batch detection.
> - **Embedded RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks):** Fixed-priority MLQ is preferred over MLFQ because **determinism matters more than fairness** in hard real-time systems.

### Comparison: MLQ vs MLFQ

| **Property** | **Multilevel Queue (MLQ)** | **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ)** |
|--------------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Process-to-queue assignment | **Static** (decided at admission) | **Dynamic** (based on runtime behavior) |
| Movement between queues | **Not allowed** | **Allowed** (promotion + demotion) |
| Starvation risk | **High** (low-priority may never run) | **Mitigated by boosting** |
| A priori knowledge required | Yes (must classify processes) | **No** (learns from behavior) |
| Implementation complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Best suited for | Real-time, embedded systems | General-purpose time-sharing OS |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Worked Example 1: MLFQ Gantt Chart Construction

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> Consider the following processes arriving at time $t = 0$:

| Process | Arrival Time ($T_{arr}$) | CPU Burst ($T_{burst}$) |
|---------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| $P_1$ | 0 | 8 |
| $P_2$ | 0 | 4 |
| $P_3$ | 0 | 2 |

**MLFQ Configuration:**
- **Queue 0:** RR with quantum $q_0 = 2$ ms
- **Queue 1:** RR with quantum $q_1 = 4$ ms
- **Queue 2:** FCFS
- **Boost period** $S = 10$ ms

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Initial Queue Assignment**
All three processes arrive at $t = 0$ and are placed in **Queue 0**.

$$\text{Queue}_0 = \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}, \quad \text{Queue}_1 = \emptyset, \quad \text{Queue}_2 = \emptyset$$

**Step 2: Execute Queue 0 (Round Robin, $q_0 = 2$ ms)**

Each process gets 2 ms of CPU time:

| Time Interval | Process | Remaining Burst |
|---------------|---------|------------------|
| $[0, 2)$ | $P_1$ | 6 ms |
| $[2, 4)$ | $P_2$ | 2 ms |
| $[4, 6)$ | $P_3$ | 0 ms ✓ (Completes) |
| $[6, 8)$ | $P_1$ | 4 ms → **Demoted to Queue 1** |
| $[8, 10)$ | $P_2$ | 0 ms ✓ (Completes) |

**After Step 2:**
- $P_3$ completed at $t = 6$
- $P_2$ completed at $t = 10$
- $P_1$ has 4 ms remaining, now in **Queue 1**

**Step 3: Move to Queue 1 (Round Robin, $q_1 = 4$ ms)**

Only $P_1$ remains. Its remaining burst (4 ms) fits within $q_1$, so it completes:

$$[10, 14) \rightarrow P_1 \text{ executes and completes}$$

### Final Gantt Chart

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt:} \quad & \vert \; P_1 \; \vert \; P_2 \; \vert \; P_3 \; \vert \; P_1 \; \vert \; P_2 \; \vert \; P_1 \; \vert \\
t: & 0 \quad\quad 2 \quad\quad 4 \quad\quad 6 \quad\quad 8 \quad\quad 10 \quad 14
\end{aligned}$$

### Metric Computation

**Turnaround Time for Each Process:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{turnaround}(P_1) &= T_{completion} - T_{arrival} = 14 - 0 = 14 \text{ ms} \\
T_{turnaround}(P_2) &= 10 - 0 = 10 \text{ ms} \\
T_{turnaround}(P_3) &= 6 - 0 = 6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Average Turnaround Time:**

$$\overline{T}_{turnaround} = \frac{14 + 10 + 6}{3} = \frac{30}{3} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Response Time for Each Process** (time to first CPU access):

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{response}(P_1) &= 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
T_{response}(P_2) &= 2 - 0 = 2 \text{ ms} \\
T_{response}(P_3) &= 4 - 0 = 4 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Average Response Time:**

$$\overline{T}_{response} = \frac{0 + 2 + 4}{3} = 2 \text{ ms}$$

**Total CPU Utilization:**

$$U_{CPU} = \frac{\sum T_{burst}}{\sum T_{turnaround}} = \frac{8 + 4 + 2}{14 + 10 + 6} = \frac{14}{30} \approx 0.467 \; (46.7\%)$$

**Throughput:**

$$\Theta = \frac{n}{T_{total}} = \frac{3 \text{ processes}}{14 \text{ ms}} \approx 0.214 \; \text{processes/ms}$$

---

### Worked Example 2: Jain's Fairness Index Calculation

> **Problem Statement:**
> Compute Jain's Fairness Index for MLFQ if CPU times allocated to 4 processes are $x_1 = 5$, $x_2 = 3$, $x_3 = 4$, $x_4 = 2$ (all in ms).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute $\sum x_i$:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} x_i = 5 + 3 + 4 + 2 = 14 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\left(\sum x_i\right)^2$:**

$$\left(\sum_{i=1}^{4} x_i\right)^2 = 14^2 = 196 \text{ ms}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute $\sum x_i^2$:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} x_i^2 = 5^2 + 3^2 + 4^2 + 2^2 = 25 + 9 + 16 + 4 = 54 \text{ ms}^2$$

**Step 4: Apply Jain's Formula:**

$$\mathcal{F} = \frac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^2}{n \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2} = \frac{196}{4 \cdot 54} = \frac{196}{216} \approx 0.9074$$

### Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\mathcal{F} \approx 0.9074$, the system is **reasonably fair** (max value = 1 means perfectly fair). The closer $\mathcal{F}$ is to 1, the more equitable the CPU distribution. A value of $\mathcal{F} = \dfrac{1}{n} = 0.25$ would indicate one process got everything and others got nothing.

### Derivation of the Lower Bound for $\mathcal{F}$

We can prove $\mathcal{F} \geq \dfrac{1}{n}$ using the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:

$$\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^2 \leq n \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2$$

Rearranging:

$$\mathcal{F} = \frac{\left(\sum x_i\right)^2}{n \cdot \sum x_i^2} \leq \frac{n \cdot \sum x_i^2}{n \cdot \sum x_i^2} = 1 \quad \text{(upper bound)}$$

The lower bound occurs when **one process gets all the CPU** (extreme unfairness):

$$x_k = X, \quad x_i = 0 \; (i \neq k) \Rightarrow \mathcal{F} = \frac{X^2}{n \cdot X^2} = \frac{1}{n}$$

Thus:

$$\frac{1}{n} \leq \mathcal{F} \leq 1$$

---

### Worked Example 3: Comparative Performance — RR vs MLFQ

> **Problem Statement:**
> Compare Round Robin (quantum = 4 ms) vs MLFQ (3 queues, $q_0 = 2$, $q_1 = 4$, $q_2 = 8$ ms) for the workload in Example 1. Which gives better **average response time**?

### Pure Round Robin (Quantum = 4 ms)

**Execution Order:**
$$P_1[0-4], P_2[4-8], P_3[8-10], P_1[10-14]$$

**Response Times:**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{response}^{RR}(P_1) &= 0 \text{ ms} \\
T_{response}^{RR}(P_2) &= 4 \text{ ms} \\
T_{response}^{RR}(P_3) &= 8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\overline{T}_{response}^{RR} = \frac{0 + 4 + 8}{3} = 4 \text{ ms}$$

### MLFQ (from Example 1)

$$\overline{T}_{response}^{MLFQ} = 2 \text{ ms}$$

### Verdict

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MLFQ provides 50% better average response time** in this scenario because it identifies $P_3$ (shortest job) as interactive via the priority mechanism and completes it first. Pure RR has no such intelligence.

---

### Worked Example 4: MLFQ with Starvation & Boosting

> **Problem Statement:**
> Consider $P_1$ (CPU-bound, burst = 100 ms) and $P_2$ (interactive, burst = 2 ms, arrival = 50 ms). With $q_0 = 10$ ms and boost period $S = 30$ ms, trace execution and verify $P_2$'s response time.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: $t = 0$ to $t = 10$:** $P_1$ executes in Queue 0.

$$P_1: \text{remaining} = 90 \text{ ms, demoted to Queue 1}$$

**Step 2: $t = 10$ to $t = 20$:** $P_1$ executes in Queue 1 ($q_1 = 20$ ms assumed).

$$P_1: \text{remaining} = 70 \text{ ms, demoted to Queue 2}$$

**Step 3: $t = 20$ to $t = 30$:** $P_2$ arrives at $t = 50$ — but here $P_1$ still holds Queue 2.

**Step 4: At $t = 30$, BOOST fires:** $P_1$ returns to Queue 0.

**Step 5: $t = 30$ to $t = 40$:** $P_1$ executes in Queue 0.

$$P_1: \text{remaining} = 60 \text{ ms, demoted again}$$

**Step 6: $t = 40$ to $t = 50$:** $P_1$ continues in Queue 1.

**Step 7: $t = 50$:** $P_2$ arrives. Since $P_2$ is in Queue 0 and $P_1$ is in Queue 1, $P_2$ runs immediately.

$$T_{response}(P_2) = 50 - 50 = 0 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(without boost would have been } \approx 60\text{ ms)}$$

### Conclusion

> [!NOTE]
> The **boost rule saved $P_2$ from starvation**, keeping response time at 0 ms despite the dominant CPU-bound $P_1$. This demonstrates the **critical role of Rule 4** in MLFQ design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: High-Level MLFQ Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q0["Queue 0 - Highest Priority Quantum 2 ms RR"]
        procA["Process A"]
        procB["Process B"]
    end
    subgraph Q1["Queue 1 - Medium Priority Quantum 4 ms RR"]
        procC["Process C"]
        procD["Process D"]
    end
    subgraph Q2["Queue 2 - Lowest Priority FCFS"]
        procE["Process E"]
        procF["Process F"]
    end
    ENTRY["New Process Arrival"] --> Q0
    Q0 -->|Exhausts Quantum| DEMOTE1["Demotion Event"]
    DEMOTE1 --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Exhausts Quantum| DEMOTE2["Demotion Event"]
    DEMOTE2 --> Q2
    BOOST["Periodic Boost Timer S 10x q0"] -.->|Moves All| Q0
    Q0 --> IORET["I/O Completion Returns to Queue 0"]
    CPU["CPU Dispatcher"] --> Q0
    CPU --> Q1
    CPU --> Q2
```

### Diagram 2: MLFQ Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Process Enters MLFQ System"] --> qCheck{"Is Queue 0 Empty?"}
    qCheck -->|No| runQ0["Dispatch from Queue 0 RR q0"]
    qCheck -->|Yes| q1Check{"Is Queue 1 Empty?"}
    q1Check -->|No| runQ1["Dispatch from Queue 1 RR q1"]
    q1Check -->|Yes| q2Check{"Is Queue 2 Empty?"}
    q2Check -->|No| runQ2["Dispatch from Queue 2 FCFS"]
    q2Check -->|Yes| idleA["CPU Idle"]
    runQ0 --> checkA{"Process Used Full Quantum?"}
    checkA -->|Yes| demoteA["Move to Queue 1"]
    checkA -->|No| ioA["Process Blocked for I/O"]
    ioA --> returnA["Re-enter at Queue 0 upon Wakeup"]
    demoteA --> q1Check
```

### Diagram 3: Promotion-Demotion Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Q0_STATE: New Process
    Q0_STATE --> Q1_STATE: Quantum Exhausted
    Q0_STATE --> Q0_STATE: I/O or Block
    Q1_STATE --> Q2_STATE: Quantum Exhausted
    Q1_STATE --> Q1_STATE: I/O or Block
    Q2_STATE --> Q2_STATE: Running
    Q0_STATE --> Q0_STATE: Periodic Boost
    Q1_STATE --> Q0_STATE: Periodic Boost
    Q2_STATE --> Q0_STATE: Periodic Boost
    Q0_STATE --> [*]: Process Completes
    Q1_STATE --> [*]: Process Completes
    Q2_STATE --> [*]: Process Completes
```

### Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture (OS Kernel View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP["Application Layer Processes"] --> ADMIT["Admission Control"]
    ADMIT --> CLOCK["Clock Interrupt Handler"]
    CLOCK --> DISP["Dispatcher Component"]
    DISP --> Q0M["Queue 0 Manager Highest Priority"]
    DISP --> Q1M["Queue 1 Manager Medium Priority"]
    DISP --> Q2M["Queue 2 Manager Low Priority"]
    Q0M --> RR0["RR Scheduler q0"]
    Q1M --> RR1["RR Scheduler q1"]
    Q2M --> FCFS2["FCFS Scheduler"]
    Q0M --> DEMO["Demotion Logic"]
    Q1M --> DEMO
    Q2M --> DEMO
    Q0M --> BOOST["Boost Mechanism Period S"]
    Q1M --> BOOST
    Q2M --> BOOST
    RR0 --> CPU["Physical CPU Core"]
    RR1 --> CPU
    FCFS2 --> CPU
    CPU --> METRICS["Performance Metrics Collector"]
    METRICS --> TRACE["Scheduler Trace Logs"]
```

### Diagram 5: Comparison Timeline MLFQ vs RR

```mermaid
gantt
    title MLFQ vs RR Gantt Comparison
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section MLFQ
    P1 in Q0 :a1, 0, 2
    P2 in Q0 :a2, 2, 4
    P3 in Q0 :a3, 4, 6
    P1 in Q1 :a4, 6, 10
    P2 in Q0 again :a5, 10, 12
    P1 completes :a6, 12, 16
    section Pure RR
    P1 RR q4 :b1, 0, 4
    P2 RR q4 :b2, 4, 8
    P3 RR :b3, 8, 10
    P1 RR completes :b4, 10, 14
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define Multilevel Feedback Queue scheduling. List the parameters required to completely define an MLFQ scheduler.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **MLFQ Definition:**
> Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ) scheduling is a CPU scheduling algorithm in which processes are assigned to **multiple queues of different priorities**, and a process can **move between queues** based on its observed CPU-burst behavior. It overcomes the limitation of Multilevel Queue scheduling by allowing dynamic reassignment of processes.

**Parameters defining MLFQ:**
1. **Number of queues** in the system.
2. **Scheduling algorithm** for each queue (e.g., RR or FCFS).
3. **Method to promote** a process to a higher queue.
4. **Method to demote** a process to a lower queue.
5. **Initial queue assignment** rule for new processes.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2. What is the role of the "boosting" mechanism in MLFQ? What problem does it solve?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Role of Boosting:**
> The boosting mechanism **periodically moves all processes to the highest-priority queue** after a fixed time interval $S$.
>
> **Problem Solved — Starvation:**
> Without boosting, CPU-bound processes gradually settle into the lowest queue and may indefinitely delay I/O-bound or interactive processes that arrive later. Boosting **guarantees that every process gets CPU time periodically**, ensuring bounded waiting time and fair treatment.
>
> **Mathematical Justification:**
> If $S$ is the boost period, then the **worst-case response time** for any process is bounded by $S$, i.e., $T_{response} \leq S$.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **working of Multilevel Feedback Queue scheduling** with a neat block diagram. Describe the four fundamental rules governing MLFQ. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider three processes $P_1$, $P_2$, $P_3$ arriving at time 0 with CPU bursts 7 ms, 4 ms, and 1 ms respectively. Using **MLFQ with two queues** (Queue 0: RR with quantum 2 ms; Queue 1: FCFS), draw the **Gantt chart** and compute **average turnaround time** and **average waiting time**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — MLFQ Working

MLFQ uses **multiple queues** with decreasing priority. A new process enters Queue 0 (highest priority). The four rules are:

1. **Rule 1 (Priority Dispatch):** Higher-priority queue processes run first.
2. **Rule 2 (Same Priority RR):** Within a queue, Round Robin scheduling is applied.
3. **Rule 3 (Demotion):** Processes exhausting their quantum are moved to a lower-priority queue.
4. **Rule 4 (Boosting):** All processes are periodically returned to the topmost queue to prevent starvation.

**Block Diagram:**

$$\text{New Process} \rightarrow \text{Queue 0 (RR, q0)} \rightarrow \text{Queue 1 (RR, q1)} \rightarrow \text{Queue 2 (FCFS)}$$

Demotion arrows flow downward; Boost arrows flow upward periodically.

#### Part (b) — Gantt Chart Computation

**Initial State:** $Q_0 = \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}$, $Q_1 = \emptyset$

| Time Interval | Process | Queue | Action |
|---------------|---------|-------|--------|
| $[0, 2)$ | $P_1$ | Q0 | 5 ms remaining → demoted to Q1 |
| $[2, 4)$ | $P_2$ | Q0 | 2 ms remaining → demoted to Q1 |
| $[4, 5)$ | $P_3$ | Q0 | Completes ✓ |
| $[5, 7)$ | $P_1$ | Q1 | 3 ms remaining (FCFS continues) |
| $[7, 9)$ | $P_2$ | Q1 | Completes ✓ |
| $[9, 12)$ | $P_1$ | Q1 | Completes ✓ |

**Gantt Chart:**

$$P_1[0-2] \mid P_2[2-4] \mid P_3[4-5] \mid P_1[5-9] \mid P_2[9-11] \mid P_1[11-14]$$

**Metrics:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{turnaround}(P_1) &= 14 - 0 = 14 \text{ ms} \\
T_{turnaround}(P_2) &= 11 - 0 = 11 \text{ ms} \\
T_{turnaround}(P_3) &= 5 - 0 = 5 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{T}_{turnaround} &= \frac{14 + 11 + 5}{3} = 10 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{wait}(P_1) &= 14 - 7 = 7 \text{ ms} \\
T_{wait}(P_2) &= 11 - 4 = 7 \text{ ms} \\
T_{wait}(P_3) &= 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{T}_{wait} &= \frac{7 + 7 + 4}{3} = 6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Compare **Multilevel Queue scheduling** and **Multilevel Feedback Queue scheduling** in detail. State **two advantages** of MLFQ over MLQ. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Jain's Fairness Index** with a suitable example. For a system that allocated CPU time of $\{10, 8, 6, 4\}$ ms to four processes, compute the fairness index and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — MLQ vs MLFQ Comparison

| Aspect | Multilevel Queue | Multilevel Feedback Queue |
|--------|------------------|---------------------------|
| Assignment | Static (fixed at admission) | Dynamic (based on observed behavior) |
| Movement | No inter-queue movement | Promotion + Demotion allowed |
| Starvation | High risk | Mitigated by boosting |
| A priori info | Required | Not required |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher |

**Two Advantages of MLFQ:**
1. **No need for prior classification** — MLFQ learns process behavior automatically.
2. **Better response time** for interactive jobs through automatic priority promotion.

#### Part (b) — Jain's Fairness Index

**Definition:**
$$\mathcal{F} = \frac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^2}{n \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2}$$

**Given:** $x_1 = 10, x_2 = 8, x_3 = 6, x_4 = 4$ ms, $n = 4$.

$$\sum x_i = 10 + 8 + 6 + 4 = 28$$

$$\left(\sum x_i\right)^2 = 784$$

$$\sum x_i^2 = 100 + 64 + 36 + 16 = 216$$

$$\mathcal{F} = \frac{784}{4 \cdot 216} = \frac{784}{864} \approx 0.9074$$

**Comment:** Since $\mathcal{F} \approx 0.9074$ is close to 1, the scheduler provides a **fairly equitable** CPU distribution.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Demotion vs Boosting Confusion:** Many students incorrectly assume demoted processes **never** return to higher queues. Always mention the **boosting rule** explicitly.
> 2. **Time Unit Consistency:** When computing averages, **convert all times to the same unit** (ms or seconds) before averaging. Marks deducted for mixing units.
> 3. **Forgetting to Re-queue After I/O:** When a process blocks for I/O and wakes up, it should be returned to **Queue 0**, not its previous queue. This is a frequently missed point.
> 4. **Jain's Formula Units:** The numerator and denominator must have **consistent units**. If $x_i$ is in ms, then $\sum x_i^2$ is in $\text{ms}^2$ — do not forget the squared units in the formula.
> 5. **Boost Period Selection:** Choosing $S$ too small leads to **excessive overhead** (too many promotions); too large leads to **starvation**. A typical rule of thumb is $S = 10 \cdot q_0$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — MLFQ Scheduling:**
>
> - **Core Idea:** Processes move between queues based on observed CPU-burst behavior, not pre-declared priorities.
> - **Rule 1:** Higher priority queue always dispatched first.
> - **Rule 2:** Within same priority, Round Robin (or queue-specific algorithm).
> - **Rule 3:** Quantum exhaustion → demote to lower queue.
> - **Rule 4:** Periodic boost → all processes return to top queue (anti-starvation).
> - **Key Formulas:**
>   - $T_{turnaround} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$
>   - $T_{response} = T_{first\_run} - T_{arrival}$
>   - $T_{wait} = T_{turnaround} - T_{burst}$
>   - $\mathcal{F} = \dfrac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n \cdot \sum x_i^2}$, with $\dfrac{1}{n} \leq \mathcal{F} \leq 1$
> - **Quantum Pattern:** Typically $q_{i+1} = 2 \cdot q_i$ (doubling scheme).
> - **Boost Period:** $S \approx 10 \cdot q_0$.
> - **Advantage:** No a priori knowledge of process type needed.
> - **Disadvantage:** Possible starvation (solved by boosting) and **unpredictable behavior for hard real-time systems** (hence MLQ is preferred in RTOS).
> - **Historical Note:** Invented by **Corbato (1962)** for CTSS at MIT — later won Turing Award.
> - **Modern Use:** Foundation of Linux CFS, Windows NT scheduler, BSD ULE.
> - **Difference from MLQ:** MLQ = static assignment; MLFQ = dynamic assignment with feedback.
> - **Interactive vs CPU-bound:** MLFQ identifies interactives (short bursts) and keeps them at top queues; CPU-bound processes gradually sink.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real Time Operating Systems (RTOS) - Structure and Characteristics of RTOS; Task Management - Task States and Task Synchronization; Semaphores - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Real Time Operating Systems (RTOS) - Structure and Characteristics of RTOS; Task Management - Task States and Task Synchronization; Semaphores - Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)?

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
A **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** is a specialized operating system designed to manage hardware resources, run concurrent processes (tasks), and serve real-time application process data with **deterministic and bounded response times**, where the correctness of the system depends not only on the logical result of computation but also on the **time at which the result is produced**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** An RTOS is fundamentally different from a GPOS (General Purpose OS like Windows/Linux). In RTOS, **timing guarantees** (deadlines) are part of the system contract. Missing a deadline is considered a **system failure**, not merely degraded performance.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Air Traffic Controller

Imagine an **Air Traffic Controller (ATC)** managing multiple aircraft landing at an airport:

- **GPOS Analogy (e.g., Windows):** A ticket booking counter — if it takes 2 minutes extra to issue a ticket, no one dies.
- **RTOS Analogy:** The ATC tower — if a landing clearance is delayed by even 3 seconds, two aircraft may collide.

The ATC must:
1. **Prioritize** incoming flights (some emergency, some routine).
2. **Bound the response time** — every instruction must be delivered within a strict time window.
3. **Handle multiple events concurrently** — radar, radio, weather, runway lights.
4. **Never miss a deadline** — a missed instruction = catastrophe.

This is exactly the role an RTOS plays in embedded systems, automotive ECUs, pacemakers, and avionics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In a GPOS, the goal is to maximize **throughput** (jobs per second). In an RTOS, the goal is to meet **deadlines** for time-critical jobs. A slower but predictable system is preferred over a faster but unpredictable one.

### 1.3 Structure of an RTOS

The structural architecture of an RTOS is composed of several tightly integrated layers:

1. **Hardware Layer** — Microcontroller/Microprocessor, timers, interrupts, memory.
2. **RTOS Kernel (Core)** — The heart of the RTOS; provides task scheduling, interrupt handling, and inter-task communication.
3. **System Services Layer** — Semaphores, message queues, event flags, memory management.
4. **Application Layer** — User-written real-time tasks and ISR (Interrupt Service Routines).
5. **TCP/IP / Networking Stack** (optional, for connected RTOS like RTOS + IoT).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Most commercial RTOS kernels are **microkernel-based** (e.g., QNX, VxWorks) to keep the kernel small, fast, and verifiable for safety-critical certifications like **DO-178C** (avionics) and **ISO 26262** (automotive).

### 1.4 Core Characteristics of an RTOS (KTU 2024 Exam-Favorite)

| # | Characteristic | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Determinism** | The system responds within a known, fixed maximum time (bounded latency). |
| 2 | **Responsiveness** | How fast the OS services an interrupt or request after it occurs. |
| 3 | **Reliability** | Must operate without failure for long durations; often fault-tolerant. |
| 4 | **Predictability** | Worst-case execution time (WCET) must be calculable in advance. |
| 5 | **Concurrency** | Multiple tasks appear to execute simultaneously (multitasking). |
| 6 | **Pre-emptiveness** | A higher-priority task can immediately preempt a lower-priority one. |
| 7 | **Small Footprint** | Minimal memory and CPU usage; suitable for embedded systems. |
| 8 | **Priority-Based Scheduling** | Tasks are scheduled based on assigned priorities (static/dynamic). |
| 9 | **Interrupt Handling** | Fast, prioritized ISR dispatch with bounded interrupt latency. |
| 10 | **Hard vs Soft Real-Time** | Hard = missing deadline = system failure; Soft = deadline miss degrades quality. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Students often confuse *Responsiveness* and *Determinism*. **Responsiveness = how fast on average**, **Determinism = the worst-case bound is guaranteed known**. Always use both terms distinctly in answers.

### 1.5 Classification of Real-Time Systems

1. **Hard Real-Time System (HRTS):** Strict deadlines — missing one causes catastrophic failure.
   - *Examples:* Aircraft flight control, anti-lock braking systems (ABS), pacemakers, nuclear reactor control.
2. **Soft Real-Time System (SRTS):** Deadlines are desirable but missing them only degrades quality.
   - *Examples:* Video streaming, online gaming, audio playback, web servers.
3. **Firm Real-Time System:** Late results are useless and discarded, but not catastrophic.
   - *Examples:* Stock trading platforms, satellite image processing pipelines.

### 1.6 Differences Between GPOS and RTOS (High-Yield Comparison)

| Parameter | GPOS (General Purpose OS) | RTOS (Real-Time OS) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Maximize throughput | Meet deadlines (timeliness) |
| Scheduling | Fair share, time-slicing | Priority-based, pre-emptive |
| Response Time | Variable, non-deterministic | Bounded, deterministic |
| Kernel Size | Large (MBs) | Small (KBs to few MBs) |
| Interrupt Latency | Higher (µs–ms) | Very low (ns–µs) |
| Examples | Windows, Linux, macOS | FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS |
| Cost | Low (consumer) | High (industrial/automotive) |
| Priority Inversion Handling | Limited | Explicit (Priority Inheritance Protocol) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Task Management in RTOS

**Task (Thread):** An independent sequential thread of execution with its own stack, register context, and priority. Tasks are the basic unit of work scheduled by the RTOS kernel.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 syllabus, *task* and *thread* are often used interchangeably. A **process** contains one or more **tasks**.

#### 2.1.1 Task Control Block (TCB)

Every task in an RTOS is represented by a **Task Control Block (TCB)** — a data structure maintained by the kernel containing:

- **Task ID** (unique identifier)
- **Task State** (current state — see Section 2.1.2)
- **Task Priority** (static or dynamic)
- **Stack Pointer** (pointing to task's private stack)
- **Program Counter** (saved context)
- **CPU Registers** (saved context)
- **Task Pointer** (pointer to task code/function)
- **Task Delays / Timeouts**
- **Inter-task Communication Resources** (semaphores, queues owned)
- **List Pointers** (for ready queue, wait queue linkage)

#### 2.1.2 Task States (KTU 2024 — Frequently Asked)

A task in an RTOS can be in one of the following states (varies slightly by kernel, but the canonical model is):

1. **Running State** — The task is currently being executed by the CPU. Only ONE task per CPU core can be in this state at a time.
2. **Ready State** — The task is ready to execute but is waiting for the CPU because a higher-priority task is running.
3. **Blocked / Waiting State** — The task is waiting for some event (semaphore release, message arrival, time delay, I/O completion) and is NOT in the ready queue.
4. **Suspended / Dormant State** — The task has been explicitly suspended or terminated; it does not exist in the kernel's active task list.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Some KTU textbooks (e.g., referring to µC/OS-II by Jean Labrosse) include a 5th state — **Created** (task created but not yet started) and an **ISR-running** pseudo-state. Be prepared to draw **either 4-state or 5-state diagrams** in exams.

#### 2.1.3 Task State Transition Triggers

| Transition | Trigger |
|---|---|
| Ready → Running | Scheduler dispatches the task (highest priority ready) |
| Running → Ready | Preempted by higher-priority task, or time slice expired |
| Running → Blocked | Task calls `wait()`, `pend()`, or delays itself |
| Blocked → Ready | Event occurs (semaphore given, message received, timeout) |
| Running → Suspended | Task deleted or explicitly suspended |
| Suspended → Ready | Task resumed/restarted |

### 2.2 Task Synchronization

Multiple tasks in an RTOS often need to **coordinate access to shared resources** (e.g., a UART peripheral, a global variable, a buffer) to prevent **race conditions**.

**Synchronization Mechanisms in RTOS:**

1. **Semaphores** (Binary, Counting, Mutex) — covered in detail in Section 2.3.
2. **Mutexes (Mutual Exclusion Locks)** — Special binary semaphore with **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**.
3. **Event Flags / Event Groups** — Synchronize a task to multiple events.
4. **Message Queues / Mailboxes** — Inter-task message passing.
5. **Spinlocks** — Busy-wait lock for very short critical sections (multi-core).
6. **Condition Variables** — Used with mutexes in POSIX-style RTOS.
7. **Barriers** — Synchronize multiple tasks at a rendezvous point.

### 2.3 Semaphores — The Core Synchronization Primitive

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Note (KTU Exam Favorite):** The concept of semaphores was introduced by **Edsger W. Dijkstra** in 1965. The names `P()` (Proberen / "to test/wait") and `V()` (Verhogen / "to increment/signal") come from Dutch.

A **semaphore** is a non-negative integer variable accessed only through two atomic (indivisible) operations:

- **P(S) or wait(S):** Decrement S. If S becomes negative, the calling task is **blocked** and added to S's wait queue.
- **V(S) or signal(S):** Increment S. If any task is waiting on S, wake one of them.

**Atomicity Guarantee:** P() and V() must be executed atomically — typically by disabling interrupts or using hardware-supported atomic instructions (e.g., `LDREX/STREX` on ARM, `CMPXCHG` on x86).

#### 2.3.1 Types of Semaphores (KTU 2024 — Core Module 2 Topic)

**A) Binary Semaphore (B = 1 or 0)**

- Value is restricted to **0** or **1**.
- Used for **mutual exclusion** (when implemented as a lock) or **synchronization** (signaling between two tasks).
- **Disadvantage:** Does NOT have built-in Priority Inheritance → can suffer from **Priority Inversion** (see Section 2.3.4).

**B) Counting Semaphore (C ≥ 0)**

- Value can range from **0 to N**, where N is the number of identical resources.
- Used to protect a **pool of resources** (e.g., 5 UART ports, 3 buffers).
- Initialized to the count of available resources.

**C) Mutex (Mutual Exclusion Semaphore)**

- A **specialized binary semaphore** with additional features:
  - **Ownership:** Only the task that locked the mutex can unlock it.
  - **Recursive Locking:** Same task can lock multiple times (with counter).
  - **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** Temporarily raises the lock-holder's priority to that of the highest-priority waiter, preventing priority inversion.
  - **Deletion Safety:** Task deletion is blocked while holding a mutex.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** In µC/OS-II, a binary semaphore is used for synchronization, and a separate "mutex" structure is used for mutual exclusion with PIP. In POSIX/PTHREADS, `sem_t` is used, and `pthread_mutex_t` is the PIP-enabled lock.

#### 2.3.2 Pseudocode for Semaphore Operations

$$ \text{wait}(S): \quad \text{while } (S \leq 0) \text{ block task}; \quad S = S - 1; $$

$$ \text{signal}(S): \quad S = S + 1; \quad \text{if (any task waiting on } S) \text{ wake one}; $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `while (S <= 0)` check is illustrative. In real kernels, the check is non-blocking — if S ≤ 0, the task is added to the semaphore's wait queue *atomically* with the decrement to avoid the **race condition** that would occur between check-and-decrement.

#### 2.3.3 RTOS Scheduling & Timing Metrics (KTU High-Yield Formulas)

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\vert R_{i} \vert$ | $R_i = F_i - A_i$ | Response Time of task $i$ (Finish − Arrival) | Time |
| $\vert T_{i} \vert$ | $T_i = F_i - A_i$ | Turnaround Time (same as response when no I/O wait) | Time |
| $\vert W_{i} \vert$ | $W_i = T_i - B_i$ | Waiting Time (Turnaround − CPU Burst) | Time |
| $\vert U_{CPU} \vert$ | $U = \dfrac{\sum B_i}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$ | CPU Utilization | Percent |
| $\vert \Theta \vert$ | $\Theta = \dfrac{N_{processes}}{T_{total}}$ | Throughput (jobs completed per unit time) | Jobs/Time |
| $\vert U_{RM} \vert$ | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Rate Monotonic Schedulability Bound (Liu & Layland, 1973) | Ratio |
| $\vert D_{miss} \vert$ | $D_{miss} = \dfrac{\#\text{missed}}{\#\text{total}} \times 100\%$ | Deadline Miss Rate | Percent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Liu & Layland Bound:** For $n$ tasks with periods $P_i$ and computation times $C_i$ under Rate Monotonic Scheduling, the system is **guaranteed schedulable** if total utilization $U = \sum (C_i / P_i) \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. As $n \to \infty$, the bound approaches $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$ or **69.3%**.

#### 2.3.4 The Priority Inversion Problem (KTU High-Yield Concept)

**Scenario:** Three tasks — High (H), Medium (M), Low (L).
1. **L** starts, locks mutex `M1`, enters critical section.
2. **H** becomes ready, preempts L, tries to lock M1 → **blocked** (waiting for L).
3. **M** becomes ready, preempts L (since L is unblocked, it runs).
4. M runs to completion (preempting L's release of M1).
5. L finally resumes, releases M1, H finally gets M1.

**Result:** A medium-priority task has delayed a high-priority task — **priority inversion** lasting as long as M runs.

**Solutions:**
- **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** When H blocks on M1 held by L, L's priority is **temporarily boosted** to H's priority. M cannot preempt L. L releases M1, then reverts to original priority.
- **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP):** Each mutex is assigned a "ceiling priority" equal to the highest priority task that may lock it. Any task that locks it runs at ceiling priority.
- **Disabling Interrupts** (coarse, for very short critical sections).

### 2.4 Real-World Applications of RTOS Task Management & Semaphores

| Domain | RTOS Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Automotive (ECU)** | Engine control, ABS, airbag deployment — FreeRTOS/OSEK-OS |
| **Avionics** | Flight control, autopilot — VxWorks (DO-178C certified) |
| **Medical Devices** | Pacemakers, MRI control — QNX, SafeRTOS |
| **Industrial Robotics** | Multi-axis motion control — RTX (IntervalZero) |
| **IoT Edge Devices** | Sensor fusion, telemetry — Zephyr, Contiki-NG |
| **Telecommunications** | Base station signal processing — eCos, RTEMS |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Smart TVs, drones, wearables — FreeRTOS, ThreadX |
