## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Problems

### 3.1 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) Utilization Bound

**Liu & Layland's Theorem (1973)** — The most cited result in real-time scheduling theory.

**Statement:** For a set of $n$ independent, pre-emptable periodic tasks with deadlines equal to periods, scheduled under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** (shorter period = higher priority), the tasks are **guaranteed to meet all deadlines** if:

$$ U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{P_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) $$

**Derivation Outline (Critical Instant Analysis):**

The worst-case response time of a task occurs at its **critical instant** — the instant when the task is released simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } W_i(t) &= \text{total computation demanded on processor by time } t \text{ for task } i. \\
W_i(t) &= C_i + \sum_{j: P_j < P_i} \left\lceil \frac{t}{P_j} \right\rceil C_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** The processor is demanded by the execution of task $i$ itself, which requires $C_i$ time units.

**Step 2:** It is also demanded by all higher-priority tasks $j$ that have been released within the interval $[0, t]$. Each such task is released $\lceil t / P_j \rceil$ times, contributing $\lceil t / P_j \rceil \cdot C_j$ units of work.

**Step 3:** The task $i$ meets its deadline if there exists some $t$ in $(0, P_i]$ such that $W_i(t) \leq t$ — i.e., the demanded work is at most the available time.

**Step 4:** For the **least upper bound** of the schedulable utilization, Liu & Layland proved that for $n$ tasks with periods in harmonic ratio (worst case), the maximum schedulable utilization is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{max}(n) &= n \cdot \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) \\
\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{max}(n) &= \ln(2) \approx 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Values Table (KTU Exam Favorite):**

| $n$ (Number of Tasks) | $U_{max} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Approximate % |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1 \cdot (2 - 1)$ | 100.0% |
| 2 | $2 \cdot (1.414 - 1)$ | 82.8% |
| 3 | $3 \cdot (1.260 - 1)$ | 78.0% |
| 4 | $4 \cdot (1.189 - 1)$ | 75.7% |
| 5 | $5 \cdot (1.149 - 1)$ | 74.3% |
| $\infty$ | $\ln(2)$ | 69.3% |

> [!NOTE]
> The bound is **sufficient but not necessary**. Task sets with utilization > 82.8% (for n=2) can still be schedulable — but the bound provides a **safe, simple, polynomial-time test**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Synchronization Primitive

| Scenario | Recommended Primitive | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 2 tasks: one signals completion to another | **Binary Semaphore** | Simple signal-and-wait |
| 5 identical UART ports shared among 10 tasks | **Counting Semaphore (init=5)** | Tracks resource count |
| Protecting a critical global variable in HRTS | **Mutex with PIP** | Avoids priority inversion |
| Task waiting for "ALL 3 of these events" | **Event Flags (AND)** | Multi-event synchronization |
| Multi-core ISR exclusion for very short region | **Spinlock** | Avoid context switch overhead |
| Two tasks exchanging 16-byte data packets | **Message Queue** | Data + synchronization |
| 8 tasks must synchronize at phase boundary | **Barrier / Semaphore (init=8)** | Rendezvous |

### 3.3 Worked Problem — Scheduling Feasibility Test

**Problem:** Given 3 periodic real-time tasks:

| Task | Period $P_i$ (ms) | Computation Time $C_i$ (ms) | Deadline $D_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 20 | 4 | 20 |
| T2 | 50 | 10 | 50 |
| T3 | 100 | 15 | 100 |

**Check feasibility using Rate Monotonic Bound.**

**Step 1:** Compute utilization of each task.

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= \frac{C_1}{P_1} = \frac{4}{20} = 0.20 \\
U_2 &= \frac{C_2}{P_2} = \frac{10}{50} = 0.20 \\
U_3 &= \frac{C_3}{P_3} = \frac{15}{100} = 0.15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute total utilization.

$$
U_{total} = U_1 + U_2 + U_3 = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 0.55
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the RMS bound for $n = 3$.

$$
U_{max}(3) = 3 \times (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7797
$$

**Step 4:** Compare.

$$
U_{total} = 0.55 \leq 0.7797 = U_{max}(3) \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **guaranteed to be schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 exams, students often confuse the *necessary* and *sufficient* nature of this test. The test is **sufficient** (if satisfied, schedulable is guaranteed), but **not necessary** (failing it does NOT prove the task set is unschedulable).

### 3.4 Worked Problem — Priority Inversion & PIP

**Problem:** Three tasks:
- T_H (High priority) — needs 5 ms CPU, locks shared resource for 2 ms in the middle.
- T_M (Medium priority) — needs 10 ms CPU, no shared resource.
- T_L (Low priority) — needs 12 ms CPU, locks shared resource for 4 ms starting at t=0.

**Without PIP — calculate wasted high-priority time:**

**Step 1:** $t = 0$ → T_L starts, locks resource at $t=0$, runs.
**Step 2:** $t = 2$ → T_H preempts T_L, runs 2 ms, tries to lock resource → **BLOCKED** (waits for T_L).
**Step 3:** $t = 2$ → T_L resumes (since T_H is blocked, control returns to highest unblocked ready task, which is T_L).
**Step 4:** $t = 4$ → T_M becomes ready, **preempts T_L** (T_M has higher priority than T_L).
**Step 5:** $t = 4$ to $t = 12$ → T_M runs 8 ms remaining.
**Step 6:** $t = 12$ → T_M done, T_L resumes. T_L needs 4 more ms (had done 4 of 12).
**Step 7:** $t = 16$ → T_L releases resource. T_H unblocks.
**Step 8:** $t = 16$ → T_H runs remaining 3 ms, finishes at $t = 19$.

**Total time for T_H = 19 ms**, but it actually needed only 5 ms. **Priority inversion duration = 14 ms** (from t=2 to t=16).

**With Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):**
- At $t = 2$ when T_H blocks on resource, T_L's priority is **boosted to T_H's priority**.
- T_M cannot preempt T_L (because T_L now has equal-to-higher priority than T_M).
- T_L runs to $t = 6$ (finishes 2 more ms of its 4 ms critical section), releases resource.
- T_H unblocks at $t = 6$, runs 3 ms remaining, finishes at $t = 9$.

**Inversion reduced to 4 ms** — bounded by the critical section length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PIP does **not eliminate** priority inversion — it only **bounds** it to the duration of the critical section. This is the **bounded blocking** property critical for hard real-time schedulability analysis (e.g., Response Time Analysis, Stack Resource Policy).

    createdA --> readyA: Make Ready
    readyA --> runningA: Scheduler Dispatch

    blockedA --> readyA: Event Occurs Timeout

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define RTOS. List any four characteristics of an RTOS.** *(Module 2 — KTU University Exam, May 2023)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** is an operating system that is designed to process data and events within strictly defined time constraints (deadlines). It is used in time-critical embedded applications where the correctness of the system depends not only on the logical output but also on the time at which the output is produced.

**Four Characteristics of RTOS:**

1. **Determinism:** The maximum time to respond to an event is bounded and known in advance.
2. **Responsiveness:** The RTOS services an interrupt or task request in the shortest possible time.
3. **Reliability:** The system must operate continuously without failure, often for years.
4. **Pre-emptiveness:** A higher-priority task can immediately preempt a lower-priority task.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often list "throughput" or "multitasking" as RTOS characteristics. While multitasking is supported, **throughput is NOT a primary RTOS metric** — *timeliness* is. Avoid such generic OS terms.

---

#### **Question 2: Differentiate between Binary Semaphore and Mutex.** *(Module 2 — KTU University Exam, Dec 2023)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Binary Semaphore | Mutex |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Value | 0 or 1 | 1 |
| Ownership | No owner concept — any task can `signal()` | Only the locking task can unlock |
| Priority Inheritance | Not supported | Supported (PIP) |
| Recursive Locking | Not possible | Possible (with counter) |
| Use Case | Signaling/synchronization between tasks | Mutual exclusion for shared resources |
| Suitable for ISR | Yes (`signal` from ISR is safe) | No (should not be used inside ISR) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Conflating a binary semaphore with a mutex. The presence of **Priority Inheritance** and **ownership semantics** is the *defining* feature of a mutex — without these, it is just a binary semaphore.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (Choice 1): RTOS Structure & Characteristics**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the structure of an RTOS with a neat block diagram. Discuss any five key characteristics of an RTOS in detail.* *(Module 2 — KTU University Exam, May 2024)*

**Model Answer:**

**Structure of RTOS (Block Diagram):**

```
+---------------------------------------------------+
|              APPLICATION LAYER                      |
|   (User Tasks, ISRs, Device Drivers)               |
+---------------------------------------------------+
|              RTOS API LAYER                        |
|   (Task Create, Pend, Post, SemWait, QueueSend)   |
+---------------------------------------------------+
|           SYSTEM SERVICES LAYER                    |
|   (Semaphores, Mailboxes, Queues, Event Flags,     |
|    Memory Manager, Time Manager)                   |
+---------------------------------------------------+
|              RTOS KERNEL (CORE)                    |
|   (Scheduler, Dispatcher, Context Switcher,        |
|    Interrupt Handler, TCB Manager)                 |
+---------------------------------------------------+
|              HARDWARE LAYER                        |
|   (CPU, Timers, Interrupts, Memory, Peripherals)   |
+---------------------------------------------------+
```

**Five Key Characteristics — Detailed Discussion:**

1. **Determinism:** The RTOS kernel guarantees that the time between an event (e.g., interrupt) and the response is bounded by a *worst-case* value. This bound must be analytically proven for hard real-time systems. Example: A vehicle airbag system must deploy within 10 ms of crash detection — *determinism* ensures this.

2. **Pre-emptiveness:** When a higher-priority task becomes ready, the kernel immediately saves the current task's context, updates the TCB, loads the higher-priority task's context, and resumes it. This is called a **context switch**. The preemption latency must be minimal and bounded.

3. **Priority-Based Scheduling:** Tasks are assigned priorities. The kernel uses a **ready queue** sorted by priority. The scheduler always picks the highest-priority ready task. Priorities can be **static** (RMS) or **dynamic** (EDF).

4. **Fast Interrupt Response:** Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs) have a **latency** (time from interrupt assertion to first ISR instruction) that is bounded and small. Many RTOSes allow nested interrupts and prioritize interrupts.

5. **Small Memory Footprint:** The RTOS kernel fits in a few kilobytes of ROM/RAM — suitable for microcontrollers (e.g., FreeRTOS runs in <10 KB ROM). This enables use in deeply embedded systems with limited resources.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare GPOS and RTOS in detail. State two examples of RTOS used in industrial applications.* *(Module 2 — KTU University Exam, Dec 2022)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | GPOS | RTOS |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Maximize throughput & fairness | Meet deadlines (timeliness) |
| Scheduling | Time-sharing, fair share | Priority-based, pre-emptive |
| Response Time | Variable, often poor under load | Bounded & deterministic |
| Interrupt Handling | Optimized for general I/O, not real-time | Bounded interrupt latency |
| Kernel Size | Large (MBs) | Small (KBs) |
| Priority Inversion Handling | None or limited | Explicit (PIP, PCP) |
| Cost & Complexity | Low (consumer market) | High (industrial/medical/automotive) |
| Examples | Windows, Linux, macOS, Unix | VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS, RTEMS |
| Typical Use | PCs, servers, desktops | Embedded controllers, ECUs, IoT |
| File System | Full featured (NTFS, ext4) | Minimal (often absent) |

**Two Industrial RTOS Examples:**

1. **VxWorks (Wind River Systems):** Used in **NASA's Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance)**, **Boeing 787 Dreamliner** avionics, and **Siemens industrial controllers**. Certified to **DO-178C Level A** (highest safety level for avionics).

2. **QNX Neutrino (BlackBerry QNX):** Used in **automotive infotainment and ADAS** (Audi, BMW, Ford), **medical devices** (Siemens MRI scanners), and **railway signaling**. Certified to **ISO 26262 ASIL D** for safety-critical automotive.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "Linux" or "Windows" as RTOS examples. These are **GPOS** — they have real-time *patches* (RT-Linux, Xenomai), but vanilla Linux is NOT a hard real-time OS. Also, do not write "MS-DOS" — it is single-tasking and lacks any scheduling.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2): Task Management & Semaphores**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the various task states in an RTOS with a state transition diagram. Discuss the role of the Task Control Block (TCB).* *(Module 2 — KTU University Exam, May 2024)*

**Model Answer:**

**Task States (5-State Model):**

A task in an RTOS can exist in one of five states:

1. **Created State:** Task has been allocated memory and TCB initialized, but has not yet been activated by the kernel. Transition: `Make_Ready()` → Ready.

2. **Ready State:** Task is fully prepared to execute and is in the kernel's ready queue, waiting for CPU allocation. Multiple tasks can be Ready simultaneously.

3. **Running State:** Task is currently executing on the CPU. Only **one task per CPU core** can be in this state at any time.

4. **Blocked (Waiting) State:** Task has voluntarily relinquished the CPU because it is waiting for some event (semaphore, message, delay, I/O). It is removed from the ready queue.

5. **Suspended (Dormant) State:** Task has been explicitly suspended by another task or by itself, and is not eligible for scheduling. Useful for power-saving or temporary deactivation.

**State Transition Diagram:**

```
          Create
            |
            v
      +-----------+
      |  Created  |
      +-----------+
            | Make_Ready()
            v
      +-----------+    Preempt    +-----------+
      |   Ready   | <----------  |  Running  |
      +-----------+  Time Slice   +-----------+
            ^                      |   |   |
            |                      |   |   |
   Event    |  Scheduler           |   |   | Suspend
   Occurs   |  Dispatch            |   |   | or Delete
            |                      |   |   v
            |               Wait   |   |  +-----------+
            +----------------------+   +--| Suspended |
                                           +-----------+
                                                |
                                                | Resume
                                                v
                                              Ready
```

**Role of Task Control Block (TCB):**

The TCB is a kernel-managed data structure that stores all information required to manage a task. Its components include:

- **Task ID and Name:** Unique identifier for the task.
- **Task State:** Current state (Ready, Running, Blocked, etc.).
- **Task Priority:** Static or dynamic priority value.
- **Stack Pointer:** Points to the task's private stack region.
- **Stack Base / Limit:** Bounds checking for stack overflow detection.
- **Program Counter (PC):** Saved return address for context restoration.
- **CPU Registers:** Saved general-purpose and special registers.
- **Task Function Pointer:** Pointer to the task's entry function.
- **Time Slice Counter:** Tracks remaining ticks for round-robin tasks.
- **Semaphore/Mutex Ownership List:** Resources held by the task.
- **List Pointers:** For linking TCB into ready queue, wait queues.

The TCB enables **context switching** — saving the entire state of the currently running task into its TCB and loading the new task's state from its TCB, all in bounded time.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Explain the three types of semaphores used in RTOS. Describe the Priority Inversion problem and how the Priority Inheritance Protocol solves it.* *(Module 2 — KTU University Exam, May 2024)*

**Model Answer:**

**Three Types of Semaphores:**

1. **Binary Semaphore:**
   - Integer value restricted to **0 or 1**.
   - **P(S):** If S = 1, decrement to 0 and continue. If S = 0, block the task.
   - **V(S):** Set S = 1 and wake one waiting task (if any).
   - **Use Case:** Synchronization between two tasks (e.g., ISR signals a task that data is ready).
   - **Limitation:** No ownership, no priority inheritance — vulnerable to priority inversion.

2. **Counting Semaphore:**
   - Integer value can range from **0 to N**, where N = number of identical resources.
   - **P(S):** If S > 0, decrement and continue. If S = 0, block.
   - **V(S):** Increment S and wake one waiting task.
   - **Use Case:** Resource pool management (e.g., 4 memory buffers, 2 serial ports).
   - **Example:** Database connection pool with 10 connections — counting semaphore init = 10.

3. **Mutex (Mutual Exclusion Semaphore):**
   - A **binary semaphore with extended semantics** specifically for mutual exclusion.
   - Features: **Ownership** (only owner can unlock), **Recursive locking** (same task can lock multiple times), **Priority Inheritance Protocol**, and **task deletion safety**.
   - **Use Case:** Protecting shared resources (global variables, peripheral registers) in hard real-time systems.

**Priority Inversion Problem:**

*Scenario:* Three tasks — High (H), Medium (M), Low (L) — with priorities in the order H > M > L.

1. L starts execution at $t=0$ and acquires a mutex protecting a shared resource.
2. H becomes ready at $t=2$, preempts L, attempts to acquire the same mutex → **blocked** because L holds it.
3. M becomes ready at $t=4$, preempts L (since H is blocked, M has the highest unblocked priority).
4. M runs to completion (say 8 ms), precluding L from running.
5. L resumes, finishes critical section, releases mutex at $t=12$.
6. H unblocks, runs remaining time, finishes at $t=19$.

**Result:** High-priority task H waited for **17 ms** when it actually needed only 5 ms. The blocking time of H is **unbounded** — it depends on the duration of M's execution, which has nothing to do with H or the resource. This violates real-time guarantees.

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) Solution:**

When H blocks on a mutex held by L:
- L's **effective priority is immediately raised** to H's priority (the highest priority among all waiters).
- Since L now has priority equal to H, M (medium priority) **cannot preempt L**.
- L continues executing its critical section at the elevated priority, finishes quickly, and releases the mutex.
- L's priority reverts to its original low priority.
- H unblocks immediately and executes.

**Bounded Blocking:** With PIP, H is blocked for **at most the duration of one critical section** of L — not for the duration of unrelated medium-priority tasks. This **bound is provable**, enabling hard real-time schedulability analysis (e.g., Response Time Analysis, Stack Resource Policy).

**Mathematical Form of Bounded Blocking with PIP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_i &= \text{Total blocking time of task } i \\
B_i &= \sum_{k} \text{(length of longest critical section of any lower-priority task that uses a resource also used by } i)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in KTU Exams:**
> 1. Writing "Priority Inversion is solved by Priority Ceiling Protocol" without distinguishing PIP from PCP. **PIP = dynamic, on blocking. PCP = static, on lock acquisition.**
> 2. Claiming PIP "eliminates" priority inversion. It only **bounds** it to the critical section duration.
> 3. Forgetting that PIP applies to **mutexes**, not to binary semaphores.
> 4. Not mentioning the **chained priority inheritance** case (transitive inheritance through multiple blocked tasks) — this is a high-value advanced point.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- **RTOS Definition:** Correctness = Logical Result + **Time of Result**. Missing deadline = failure.
- **Hard vs Soft vs Firm Real-Time:** Hard = catastrophic, Soft = degraded quality, Firm = discarded.
- **5 Key RTOS Characteristics:** Determinism, Responsiveness, Pre-emptiveness, Reliability, Small Footprint.
- **RTOS vs GPOS:** RTOS optimizes for **timeliness**, not throughput.
- **Task States (5-State):** Created → Ready → Running → Blocked / Suspended.
- **TCB contains:** Task ID, State, Priority, Stack Pointer, PC, Saved Registers.
- **Context Switch:** Save current task's context to its TCB; load next task's context from its TCB.
- **Semaphore inventor:** **Dijkstra (1965)**. Operations: **P()** = wait/decrement, **V()** = signal/increment.
- **Atomicity:** P() and V() must be **non-interruptible** (atomic).
- **Binary Semaphore:** Value = 0 or 1. **No ownership, no PIP.** Used for signaling.
- **Counting Semaphore:** Value = 0 to N. Used for **resource pools**.
- **Mutex:** Binary + Ownership + **Priority Inheritance Protocol** + Recursive + Deletion Safety.
- **Priority Inversion:** A medium-priority task delays a high-priority task indirectly.
- **PIP solution:** Boost holder's priority to highest waiter's priority. **Bounds** blocking to 1 critical section.
- **PCP (Priority Ceiling Protocol):** Static; mutex ceiling = highest task that may lock it; acquired task runs at ceiling.
- **Liu & Layland RMS Bound:** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. For $n \to \infty$, limit = $\ln(2) \approx 69.3\%$.
- **RMS:** Shorter period = higher priority (static priority assignment).
- **EDF (Earliest Deadline First):** Dynamic priority — task with earliest absolute deadline runs next. Optimal for uniprocessor.
- **Response Time:** $R_i = F_i - A_i$. **Turnaround Time:** $T_i = F_i - A_i$. **Waiting Time:** $W_i = T_i - B_i$.
- **CPU Utilization:** $U = (\sum C_i / T_{total}) \times 100\%$. **Throughput:** $\Theta = N_{processes} / T_{total}$.
- **Deadline Miss Rate:** $D_{miss} = (\text{missed} / \text{total}) \times 100\%$.
- **Critical Section:** Code segment accessing shared resource — must be protected by mutex.
- **Race Condition:** Outcome depends on interleaving of concurrent task executions.
- **ISR-safe primitives:** `semSignal()` from ISR is OK; `mutexLock()` from ISR is **NOT** allowed.
- **Deadlock:** Circular wait on resources. **4 Coffman conditions:** Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait.
- **Popular RTOS:** FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS, Zephyr, ThreadX, µC/OS-II/III.
- **Certifications:** DO-178C (avionics), ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 61508 (industrial), IEC 62304 (medical).
- **Exam Mantra:** Always distinguish **sufficient vs necessary** conditions. Always quantify with formulas. Always draw state diagrams with **all valid transitions** and label **trigger conditions**.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Real Time Operating Systems (RTOS): Structure, Characteristics, Task Management & Semaphores**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Usage of Semaphores for Synchronization; Inter-Task Communication Mechanisms (Basics only) - Message Queues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Usage of Semaphores for Synchronization; Inter-Task Communication Mechanisms (Basics only) - Message Queues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Semaphores

A **semaphore** is a non-negative integer variable (or abstract data type) maintained by the kernel of a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS), used to control concurrent access to shared resources and to synchronize the execution of multiple tasks. It was originally proposed by **Edsger Dijkstra** in 1965 as a fundamental synchronization primitive for operating systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: A semaphore is a kernel-managed integer counter that supports two atomic operations — `Wait()` (also called **P** or `pend`) and `Signal()` (also called **V** or `post`). Atomicity is guaranteed because the RTOS disables interrupts (or uses lock-based atomic instructions) during the operation, making it uninterruptible.

**Two fundamental atomic operations:**

| Operation | Aliases | Effect on Counter |
|---|---|---|
| `Wait(S)` / `P(S)` / `pend(S)` | Proberen (Dutch: "to test") | If $S > 0$, decrement; else block the calling task |
| `Signal(S)` / `V(S)` / `post(S)` | Verhogen (Dutch: "to increment") | Increment $S$; possibly unblock a waiting task |

---

### 1.2 Types of Semaphores Used in RTOS

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight** — Students frequently confuse binary and counting semaphores. The distinction is critical for solving KTU exam problems.

**1. Binary Semaphore (Mutex-Flag)**
- Counter value is restricted to **0 or 1**.
- Used for **mutual exclusion** (one task in critical section) or simple **task synchronization** (event signalling).
- A binary semaphore used purely for mutual exclusion is often called a **Mutex**.

**2. Counting Semaphore**
- Counter can take any non-negative integer value $\geq 0$.
- Used to manage a pool of $N$ identical resources (e.g., 5 UART ports, 3 memory buffers).
- Initial value $S_0$ equals the number of available resources.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Toll Booth

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy for `Wait` and `Signal`**: Imagine a **single-lane toll booth** on a highway.
> - The semaphore counter = number of available toll lanes.
> - A car (task) arriving at the booth calls `Wait()`: it occupies a lane (counter decrements by 1). If all lanes are full (counter = 0), it must wait in a queue (task is blocked).
> - When a car leaves, it calls `Signal()`: a lane is freed (counter increments by 1), and a queued car is allowed to proceed.
> - The booth attendant (RTOS kernel) ensures the counter is updated atomically — no two cars can grab the same lane simultaneously.

---

### 1.4 Inter-Task Communication — Formal Definition

**Inter-Task Communication (ITC)** refers to the mechanisms provided by the RTOS kernel that allow independent tasks to exchange information and data in a structured, deterministic manner. In the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2), the focus is restricted to the **basics** of these mechanisms:

| ITC Mechanism | Brief Description |
|---|---|
| **Shared Memory** | Global variables accessible by multiple tasks (fastest, but requires synchronization) |
| **Message Queues** | Kernel-managed FIFO buffer of fixed-size messages (structured & safe) |
| **Mailboxes** | Single-slot message holder (size = 1 message) |
| **Pipes / FIFOs** | Byte-stream channels between tasks |
| **Signals** | Asynchronous software interrupts (used sparingly in hard RT) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 Specific Focus**: The KTU syllabus explicitly limits Module 2 to **Message Queues** as the representative ITC mechanism. Students are expected to know the conceptual model, blocking/non-blocking semantics, and use-cases — not implementation internals.

---

### 1.5 Formal Definition of Message Queues

A **message queue** is a kernel-managed, fixed-size FIFO (First-In-First-Out) buffer that allows tasks to exchange data by sending and receiving discrete *messages* of a pre-defined length. Unlike shared memory, no explicit synchronization primitives are required because the queue access primitives (`q_send`, `q_receive`) are themselves atomic.

**Key properties standardized in RTOS textbooks:**

- **Message size** is fixed at queue creation time (e.g., 4 bytes, 16 bytes).
- **Queue length** (number of slots) is also fixed at creation.
- **Blocking semantics**: Sender/receiver may *block* if the queue is full/empty (with optional timeout).
- **Message priority** (in some RTOS like FreeRTOS): Out-of-band priority bit allowing urgent messages to jump the queue.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the `Wait` and `Signal` Operations (Pseudocode Logic)

The textbook pseudocode for the two semaphore operations is fundamental for KTU exam derivations:

$$
\text{Wait}(S): \quad
\begin{cases}
\text{while } S \leq 0 \text{ do} \\
\quad \text{block this task} \\
S \leftarrow S - 1
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{Signal}(S): \quad
\begin{cases}
S \leftarrow S + 1 \\
\text{if any task is blocked on } S \text{ then} \\
\quad \text{wake one blocked task}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the `while` loop?** The check-then-decrement sequence must be **atomic**; otherwise two tasks could both observe $S = 1$, both decrement to 0, and both enter the critical section — defeating mutual exclusion. The kernel disables interrupts during this window.

---

### 2.2 Usage Pattern #1: Mutual Exclusion (Protecting Critical Sections)

The most common use of a **binary semaphore** (mutex) is to protect a shared resource such that only one task accesses it at any instant.

**Initialization:**
$$
S_{\text{mutex}} \leftarrow 1 \quad \text{(representing "resource free")}
$$

**Access pattern in every accessing task:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Wait}(S_{\text{mutex}}) \quad &&\text{(acquire lock)} \\
&\text{Critical Section — read/write shared data} \\
&\text{Signal}(S_{\text{mutex}}) \quad &&\text{(release lock)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility**: Used for protecting peripheral registers (e.g., SPI bus, ADC), shared global buffers, and Linked-List data structures shared between an ISR and a worker task.

---

### 2.3 Usage Pattern #2: Task Synchronization (Event Signalling)

A binary semaphore initialized to **0** becomes a pure **synchronization token**. Task A performs an action and signals; Task B waits for that signal before proceeding.

$$
S_{\text{sync}} \leftarrow 0
$$

- Task B (consumer): $\text{Wait}(S_{\text{sync}})$ → blocks until Task A signals.
- Task A (producer): $\text{Signal}(S_{\text{sync}})$ → unblocks Task B.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Real-world RTOS use case**: A motor-control task waits for an ADC-Done interrupt. The ISR (or a deferred ISR task) signals the semaphore; the motor task wakes up, reads the sample, and computes the next PWM duty cycle. This pattern is the textbook KTU favourite.

---

### 2.4 Usage Pattern #3: Counting Semaphore for Resource Pool

When a system has $N$ identical resources, a counting semaphore initialized to $N$ arbitrates access.

$$
S_{\text{pool}} \leftarrow N
$$

- $\text{Wait}(S_{\text{pool}})$ decrements — task acquires one resource.
- $\text{Signal}(S_{\text{pool}})$ increments — task returns one resource.
- If all $N$ resources are in use, $S_{\text{pool}} = 0$ and the next task blocks.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Save this table for revision.** The KTU valuation key marks directly from this.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|
| Mutex Semaphore init value | $S_0 = 1$ | Binary |
| Sync Semaphore init value | $S_0 = 0$ | Binary |
| Resource Pool init value | $S_0 = N$ (resources) | Counting |
| Wait operation effect | $S \leftarrow S - 1$ if $S > 0$ | Atomic |
| Signal operation effect | $S \leftarrow S + 1$ | Atomic |
| Invariant of $S$ | $S \geq 0$ always | Hard constraint |
| Active waiting tasks | $\leq S_0$ (no deadlock condition) | Counting |
| Message queue size | $Q_{\text{bytes}} = L_{\text{msg}} \times N_{\text{slots}}$ | Bytes |
| Round-trip blocking | $T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{wait}} + T_{\text{copy}}$ | ms / $\mu$s |
| Priority inversion max delay | $T_{\text{inv}} = \sum T_{\text{lower-prio tasks}}$ | Bounded by design |

**Critical safety invariants (must hold always):**

$$
\boxed{S \;\geq\; 0}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Number of blocked tasks} \;\leq\; S_0}
$$

---

### 2.6 Why Semaphores Alone Are Not Enough — Priority Inversion

> [!WARNING]
> **High-weightage KTU concept.** A common 7-mark question.

**Priority Inversion** occurs when a higher-priority task is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority task — effectively "inverting" the relative priorities for an unbounded duration.

**Classic scenario (three tasks):**
- Task **H** (high priority), Task **M** (medium), Task **L** (low).
- L acquires mutex $M$. H becomes ready and tries `Wait(M)` → blocks.
- M (medium) preempts L (because L is no longer running).
- H is still blocked behind L — even though H has the highest priority.

**Solution — Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):**
- When H blocks on a mutex held by L, the kernel temporarily **boostes L's priority to H's priority**.
- L thus runs to completion of critical section, releases mutex, then H immediately preempts and runs.

This bounds the inversion time to the duration of one critical section execution.

---

### 2.7 Message Queue — Operational Model

A message queue operates on the **producer-consumer** paradigm but with a kernel-managed buffer. The canonical operations are:

$$
\text{q\_send}(Q, m, \tau) \quad ; \quad \text{q\_receive}(Q, m, \tau)
$$

where:
- $Q$ = queue handle (ID returned at creation).
- $m$ = pointer to message buffer (must match declared message size exactly).
- $\tau$ = timeout in ticks (0 = non-blocking; $\infty$ = wait forever; finite = bounded wait).

**States of the queue:**

| Queue State | Effect on `q_send` | Effect on `q_receive` |
|---|---|---|
| Not full, not empty | Message enqueued, returns OK | Oldest message dequeued, returns OK |
| Full | Blocks (or returns error if $\tau=0$) | Dequeues normally |
| Empty | Enqueues normally | Blocks (or returns error if $\tau=0$) |
| Multiple tasks waiting | First task in FIFO order wakes | First task in FIFO order wakes |

> [!NOTE]
> **FreeRTOS note** (frequently asked): FreeRTOS message queues use **copy-semantics** — the data is copied *into* the queue buffer on send and *copied out* on receive. This means the original buffer can be reused immediately after `q_send` returns. Contrast this with **pointer-semantics** (used in some RTOS like POSIX mqueues) where only the pointer is queued.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Correctness Proof for Mutual Exclusion

We prove that the `Wait`/`Signal` pair guarantees mutual exclusion — i.e., two tasks can never be simultaneously inside the critical section (CS).

**Setup:** Two tasks $T_1$, $T_2$. Mutex semaphore $M$ initialized to 1.

**Statement to prove:** $\forall$ execution traces, $|\{T_i : T_i \in \text{CS}\}| \leq 1$.

**Proof by contradiction:**

Assume at time $t$, both $T_1$ and $T_2$ are inside CS simultaneously.

For $T_1$ to enter CS, it must have completed `Wait(M)` which sets $M \leftarrow 0$.

For $T_2$ to also enter CS, it must have *also* completed `Wait(M)`. But `Wait` checks $M > 0$ first — if $M = 0$, $T_2$ blocks.

The only way $T_2$ could pass is if $M$ became positive between $T_1$'s check and $T_2$'s check. But the operations are **atomic** (interrupts disabled). Therefore $T_2$'s observation of $M$ is either before or after $T_1$'s decrement — never interleaved.

- If before: $T_2$ sees $M = 1$, decrements to 0, enters CS. Then $T_1$ sees $M = 0$, blocks. Mutual exclusion holds.
- If after: $T_2$ sees $M = 0$, blocks. Mutual exclusion holds.

**Therefore, the assumption leads to contradiction. Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often write the proof assuming "two tasks decrement simultaneously." The atomicity argument must be **explicitly** invoked — the KTU examiner allocates 2 marks specifically for mentioning atomicity/interrupt disablement.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Bounded Waiting (No Starvation) Property

**Statement:** A task that successfully executes `Wait(M)` will exit the critical section within a bounded number of steps of other tasks entering CS.

Let $\Phi(t)$ = number of tasks waiting on $M$ at time $t$.

**Step 1.** When a task $T$ successfully executes `Wait(M)`:
$$
\Phi(t^+) \;=\; \Phi(t^-) \;-\; (\text{tasks that just entered CS}) \;+\; 0
$$

**Step 2.** When a task $T_k$ exits CS and calls `Signal(M)`:
$$
\Phi(t^+) \;=\; \Phi(t^-) - 1
$$
(one waiter is dequeued and woken)

**Step 3.** Total wait time for a task $T$ that enters the waiting set:
$$
T_{\text{wait}} \;\leq\; (N - 1) \cdot T_{\text{CS, max}}
$$
where $N$ is the number of tasks contending for $M$ and $T_{\text{CS, max}}$ is the maximum critical-section execution time.

**Step 4.** In RTOS context with strict priorities, the wait bound becomes:
$$
T_{\text{wait, RT}} \;\leq\; (N - 1) \cdot T_{\text{CS, max}} \quad \text{(FIFO scheduling)}
$$

**Engineering insight:** This bound is the theoretical foundation for **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** analysis in hard real-time systems.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Trace: Producer–Consumer Using Counting Semaphores

**Problem:** Single producer task $P$, single consumer task $C$, buffer of $B$ slots. Synchronize correctly.

**Semaphores declared:**
- `empty` initialized to $B$ (counts free slots).
- `full` initialized to $0$ (counts filled slots).
- `mutex` initialized to $1$ (protects buffer index).

**Producer task $P$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{repeat forever:} \\
&\quad \text{produce item into } \textit{next\_p} \\
&\quad \text{Wait}(\text{empty}) \\
&\quad \text{Wait}(\text{mutex}) \\
&\quad \text{buffer}[\textit{next\_p} \bmod B] \leftarrow \textit{item} \\
&\quad \textit{next\_p} \leftarrow \textit{next\_p} + 1 \\
&\quad \text{Signal}(\text{mutex}) \\
&\quad \text{Signal}(\text{full})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Consumer task $C$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{repeat forever:} \\
&\quad \text{Wait}(\text{full}) \\
&\quad \text{Wait}(\text{mutex}) \\
&\quad \textit{item} \leftarrow \text{buffer}[\textit{next\_c} \bmod B] \\
&\quad \textit{next\_c} \leftarrow \textit{next\_c} + 1 \\
&\quad \text{Signal}(\text{mutex}) \\
&\quad \text{Signal}(\text{empty}) \\
&\quad \text{consume } \textit{item}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step trace for $B = 2$ (showing the semaphore evolution):**

| Step | Action | `empty` | `full` | `mutex` | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial state | 2 | 0 | 1 | Buffer empty |
| 1 | $P$: `Wait(empty)` → $P$ decrements | 1 | 0 | 1 | Slot 1 reserved |
| 2 | $P$: `Wait(mutex)` | 1 | 0 | 0 | Enters CS |
| 3 | $P$: writes buffer[0] | 1 | 0 | 0 | |
| 4 | $P$: `Signal(mutex)` | 1 | 0 | 1 | Exits CS |
| 5 | $P$: `Signal(full)` | 1 | 1 | 1 | Item available |
| 6 | $C$: `Wait(full)` | 1 | 0 | 1 | |
| 7 | $C$: `Wait(mutex)` | 1 | 0 | 0 | |
| 8 | $C$: reads buffer[0] | 1 | 0 | 0 | |
| 9 | $C$: `Signal(mutex)` | 1 | 0 | 1 | |
| 10 | $C$: `Signal(empty)` | 2 | 0 | 1 | Back to initial |

> [!TIP]
> **Why two separate semaphores `empty` and `full`?** They replace polling (`while(buffer_is_full())`) with efficient blocking. The kernel puts $P$ to sleep when `empty = 0` and $C$ to sleep when `full = 0` — saving CPU cycles.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Semaphore vs Message Queue for ITC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequently asked comparison** in KTU Module 2.

| Comparison Axis | Semaphore (sync only) | Message Queue (ITC) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary purpose | Synchronize *execution* | Transfer *data* |
| Carries payload? | No (just a flag) | Yes (fixed-size message) |
| FIFO order guaranteed? | N/A (only one waiter wakes) | Yes (FIFO order of slots) |
| Blocking support | Yes | Yes (with timeout) |
| Overhead | Very low (counter only) | Higher (memory copy) |
| Latency | $\sim 1 \mu$s | $\sim 5$–$50 \mu$s (depending on size) |
| Used for | Event notification, mutex | Structured data passing |
| KTU exam example | ISR-to-task signalling | Command packet to motor task |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-style)

**Question:** A system has 4 identical printers managed by a counting semaphore. At time $t = 0$, the semaphore value is **4**. Three tasks $T_1, T_2, T_3$ simultaneously call `Wait` on the semaphore in that order. Then $T_2$ finishes and calls `Signal`. Find the semaphore value and the state of each task.

**Step 1.** Initial: $S = 4$.

**Step 2.** $T_1$ calls `Wait`: $S > 0$, so $S \leftarrow 3$, $T_1$ proceeds.

**Step 3.** $T_2$ calls `Wait`: $S > 0$, so $S \leftarrow 2$, $T_2$ proceeds.

**Step 4.** $T_3$ calls `Wait`: $S > 0$, so $S \leftarrow 1$, $T_3$ proceeds.

**Step 5.** $T_2$ calls `Signal`: $S \leftarrow 2$.

**Step 6.** Final state:
- $T_1$: holds 1 printer.
- $T_2$: released printer (idle).
- $T_3$: holds 1 printer.
- $S = 2$ (2 printers free, 2 tasks holding).

**Verification:** Total printers accounted for: $1 + 0 + 1 = 2$ in use, $S = 2$ free, sum = $4 = S_0$. $\checkmark$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU expected answer format**: Students must show the step-by-step increment/decrement in a tabular form similar to the matrix above to secure full 7 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Semaphore Interaction with Kernel State

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TaskA["Task A Producer"]
    TaskB["Task B Consumer"]
    Mutex["Binary Semaphore S value"]
    Critical["Critical Section Shared Resource"]
    WaitQ["Blocked Task Wait Queue"]

    TaskA -->|Wait S| Mutex
    Mutex -->|S greater 0 decrement| Critical
    Mutex -->|S equals 0 enqueue task| WaitQ
    Critical -->|Signal S| Mutex
    WaitQ -->|Wake task on signal| TaskB
    Mutex -->|Atomic Update| Mutex
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide**: Solid arrows show flow of control. The loop arrow on `Mutex` denotes the atomic read-modify-write performed by the kernel with interrupts disabled.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Counting Semaphore for Resource Pool ($N = 3$)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["Task 1"] -->|Wait| PoolSema["Counting Semaphore S equals 3"]
    T2["Task 2"] -->|Wait| PoolSema
    T3["Task 3"] -->|Wait| PoolSema
    T4["Task 4 blocked"] -.->|enqueued| WaitQueue["Wait Queue"]
    PoolSema --> R1["Resource 1 in use"]
    PoolSema --> R2["Resource 2 in use"]
    PoolSema --> R3["Resource 3 in use"]
    R1 -->|Signal| PoolSema
    WaitQueue -->|dequeue on signal| T4
```

**Interpretation:** With $S = 3$ and 4 competing tasks, Task 4 sits in the kernel-managed wait queue until a `Signal` frees a slot.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Message Queue Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sender["Sender Task Producer"]
    Recv["Receiver Task Consumer"]
    KernQ["Kernel Managed Message Queue"]
    Slot1["Slot 0 msg id 0x01"]
    Slot2["Slot 1 msg id 0x02"]
    Slot3["Slot 2 EMPTY"]
    Slot4["Slot 3 EMPTY"]

    Sender -->|q_send msg ptr| KernQ
    Recv -->|q_receive ptr timeout| KernQ
    KernQ --> Slot1
    KernQ --> Slot2
    KernQ --> Slot3
    KernQ --> Slot4
    Slot1 -->|FIFO dequeue| Recv
```

**Components explained:**
- **Sender**: Passes a pointer (or in copy-mode, the buffer) to the kernel.
- **Kernel Queue**: Circular buffer maintained by the RTOS.
- **Slots**: Each holds one message of fixed declared length.
- **Receiver**: Pulls the oldest message; blocks if queue is empty.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: ISR-to-Task Synchronization with Binary Semaphore

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ISR as Hardware ISR
    participant Kern as RTOS Kernel
    participant Sem as Binary Semaphore S equals 0
    participant WT as Worker Task

    WT->>Kern: Wait S (blocks because S equals 0)
    Note over WT: WT enters blocked state<br/>Saves context to TCB
    ISR->>Kern: Signal S from ISR handler
    Kern->>Sem: Increment S to 1
    Kern->>WT: Move WT from blocked to ready queue
    WT->>Kern: Resume execution
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why binary and not counting?** An ISR fires once per event; we just need to wake one waiting task. Counting semaphore would over-engineer and waste RAM.

---

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Producer–Consumer with Message Queue vs Shared Memory + Semaphores

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SM["Approach A: Shared Memory plus Semaphores"]
        direction TB
        ProdA["Producer Task"] -->|Wait empty mutex| BufA["Shared Global Buffer"]
        BufA -->|Signal full mutex| ConsA["Consumer Task"]
        ProdA -.->|extra Wait empty| SA["Counting Sem empty"]
        ProdA -.->|extra Signal full| SA2["Counting Sem full"]
        ConsA -.->|Wait full| SA2
        ConsA -.->|Signal empty| SA
    end

    subgraph MQ["Approach B: Message Queue"]
        direction TB
        ProdB["Producer Task"] -->|q_send msg| Q["RTOS Message Queue"]
        Q -->|q_receive| ConsB["Consumer Task"]
    end
```

**Engineering takeaway:** Message queue reduces the design complexity — no separate `empty`/`full`/`mutex` semaphores are needed because queue operations are inherently synchronized by the kernel.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> Define a **binary semaphore** and a **counting semaphore**. State one application of each in a real-time embedded system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **binary semaphore** is a kernel-managed integer variable restricted to values $\in \{0, 1\}$. It supports atomic `Wait` and `Signal` operations.

A **counting semaphore** is a kernel-managed integer variable that can take any non-negative integer value $\geq 0$.

**Applications:**

| Type | Application |
|---|---|
| Binary | Mutual exclusion (e.g., protecting SPI bus access between two tasks) |
| Counting | Managing a pool of 3 identical UART channels in a multi-sensor data logger |

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition each + 1 mark for correct application mapping)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> Differentiate between a **semaphore** and a **message queue** as RTOS inter-task communication mechanisms.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Semaphore | Message Queue |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Synchronization (event signalling) | Data transfer (message passing) |
| Data carried | No payload, just a counter | Fixed-size payload |
| Order | N/A | FIFO guarantee |
| Blocking | Yes (on `Wait`) | Yes (on `send` if full, on `receive` if empty) |
| Memory cost | ~4 bytes (counter) | $N_{\text{slots}} \times L_{\text{msg}}$ bytes |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **priority inversion** in RTOS. Describe the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** with a neat diagram showing how it solves the problem.

**Model Answer:**

**Priority Inversion — Definition [2 marks]:**
Priority inversion is a scheduling anomaly in which a higher-priority task is forced to wait for the execution of a lower-priority task, while medium-priority tasks preempt the lower-priority task, thereby indirectly delaying the high-priority task for an unbounded duration.

**Scenario with three tasks [2 marks]:**
Let $P_H$ (high), $P_M$ (medium), $P_L$ (low) be three tasks competing for a shared mutex $M$.

1. $P_L$ acquires $M$ and enters critical section.
2. $P_H$ arrives, preempts $P_L$, and tries to acquire $M$ → **blocks**.
3. $P_M$ arrives, preempts $P_L$ (since $P_H$ is blocked and $P_L$ is ready).
4. $P_M$ runs to completion — possibly very long.
5. $P_L$ resumes, exits critical section, releases $M$.
6. $P_H$ finally gets $M$.

The inversion duration is bounded only by the runtime of medium-priority tasks, not by the low-priority critical section length — violating real-time guarantees.

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) solution [3 marks]:**
- Whenever a high-priority task $P_H$ blocks on a mutex held by a lower-priority task $P_L$, the kernel **temporarily raises $P_L$'s priority to $\max(\text{priority}(P_H), \text{priority}(P_L))$**.
- $P_L$ thus inherits the priority of $P_H$ and cannot be preempted by medium-priority tasks.
- $P_L$ finishes its critical section, releases $M$, and its priority is restored to original.
- $P_H$ immediately acquires $M$ and runs.

**Diagram (drawn in exam):**

```
Initial:    P_H (prio 3)   P_M (prio 2)   P_L (prio 1)
T0:         P_L acquires M
T1:         P_H arrives, blocks on M → P_L inherits prio 3
T2:         P_M arrives but CANNOT preempt P_L (same prio 3)
T3:         P_L exits CS, releases M → priority restored to 1
T4:         P_H acquires M, runs to completion
```

**Result**: The inversion duration is now bounded by the duration of one critical section execution, not by medium-priority task runtimes.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **producer–consumer problem** solved using **three semaphores**. Show the semaphore evolution trace for a buffer size of $B = 2$.

**Model Answer:**

**Problem statement [1 mark]:**
A producer task generates items and places them into a shared bounded buffer of size $B$. A consumer task removes and processes them. We must guarantee:
- No buffer overflow (producer waits when full).
- No underflow (consumer waits when empty).
- No race condition on buffer index.

**Semaphores used [2 marks]:**

| Semaphore | Init Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `empty` | $B$ | Counts free slots in the buffer |
| `full` | $0$ | Counts filled slots |
| `mutex` | $1$ | Protects critical section (buffer index manipulation) |

**Producer pseudocode [2 marks]:**
```
repeat:
    produce_item(item)
    Wait(empty)
    Wait(mutex)
    buffer[in] = item
    in = (in + 1) mod B
    Signal(mutex)
    Signal(full)
```

**Consumer pseudocode [2 marks]:**
```
repeat:
    Wait(full)
    Wait(mutex)
    item = buffer[out]
    out = (out + 1) mod B
    Signal(mutex)
    Signal(empty)
    consume_item(item)
```

**Trace table for $B = 2$** — Refer to the analytical trace table in Section 3.3 (this is the expected answer; paste the full trace here in the actual exam paper).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall**: Students frequently forget to `Wait` and `Signal` the `mutex` semaphore, leading to race conditions on the shared index. **Always lock the critical section** with `mutex` *after* checking `empty`/`full` to avoid deadlock.

---

#### **QUESTION B — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024, modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **message queue** mechanism in RTOS. Explain the **blocking** and **non-blocking** modes of `q_send` and `q_receive` operations. Illustrate with an example of command dispatching from a user-interface task to a motor-control task.

**Model Answer:**

**Message queue definition [2 marks]:**
A message queue is a kernel-managed FIFO buffer of fixed-size messages used for inter-task data communication. It is created with two parameters: message length $L$ and queue depth $D$. Tasks communicate via `q_send` and `q_receive` primitives, both of which are atomic at the kernel level.

**Blocking vs Non-blocking modes [3 marks]:**

| Mode | Sender (`q_send`) | Receiver (`q_receive`) |
|---|---|---|
| **Blocking (timeout = WAIT_FOREVER)** | Task suspended if queue full; wakes when slot freed | Task suspended if queue empty; wakes when message arrives |
| **Non-blocking (timeout = 0)** | Returns immediately with error code if full | Returns immediately with error code if empty |
| **Bounded blocking (timeout = $T$ ticks)** | Blocks up to $T$ ticks; returns error on timeout | Blocks up to $T$ ticks; returns error on timeout |

**Command-dispatch example [2 marks]:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UI["UI Task button press"] -->|q_send CMD id payload| Q["Message Queue depth 5"]
    Q -->|q_receive blocks| MC["Motor Control Task"]
    MC -->|executes cmd| M["Motor Actuator"]
```

- UI task sends: `q_send(motor_q, &cmd_packet, 0)` — non-blocking.
- Motor task: `q_receive(motor_q, &rx_packet, WAIT_FOREVER)` — blocks until a command arrives.

This decouples UI responsiveness from motor-task latency.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **shared memory** and **message queue** approaches for inter-task communication. Mention **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of each. State one real-time scenario where message queues are preferred over shared memory.

**Model Answer:**

**Shared Memory Approach [3.5 marks]:**

*Advantages:*
1. **Lowest latency** — direct memory access, no kernel copy overhead.
2. **Highest throughput** — suitable for high-bandwidth data (e.g., video frame buffers).

*Disadvantages:*
1. **Requires explicit synchronization** — programmer must use semaphores/mutexes; bug-prone.
2. **No copy semantics** — race conditions if both tasks modify simultaneously.

**Message Queue Approach [3.5 marks]:**

*Advantages:*
1. **Built-in synchronization** — `q_send`/`q_receive` are atomic; no extra semaphore needed.
2. **Decouples tasks** — sender does not need to know receiver's state; supports buffering of bursts.
3. **Copy semantics** (in FreeRTOS-style queues) — original buffer can be reused immediately.

*Disadvantages:*
1. **Memory overhead** — queue buffer of size $L \times D$ must be allocated.
2. **Copy overhead** — extra latency proportional to message length.

**Scenario preferring message queues [bonus point]:**
In a **safety-critical automotive ECU** where a CAN-receive ISR task sends parsed command packets to a vehicle dynamics control task, message queues are preferred because:
- Strict data ownership (no shared memory race conditions).
- Traceable message ordering for diagnostic logging.
- Built-in blocking semantics simplify the design under load.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist for the night before the exam.**

**Semaphore Fundamentals:**
- ✅ Semaphore = atomic integer with `Wait` (decrement/block) and `Signal` (increment/wake).
- ✅ Binary semaphore $\in \{0, 1\}$; counting semaphore $\in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$.
- ✅ Atomicity is enforced by **interrupt disablement** during the read-modify-write window.
- ✅ Invariant: $S \geq 0$ always holds.
- ✅ `Wait` and `Signal` are the **only** allowed operations on a semaphore.

**Three Core Usage Patterns:**
- ✅ **Mutual exclusion**: Init to 1, pair `Wait`/`Signal` around critical section.
- ✅ **Event signalling**: Init to 0; ISR or producer signals, consumer waits.
- ✅ **Resource pool**: Init to $N$; `Wait` to acquire, `Signal` to release.

**Priority Inversion & PIP:**
- ✅ Priority inversion = high-prio task blocked behind low-prio task, preempted by medium.
- ✅ PIP boosts the low-prio task's priority to the waiting high-prio task's priority.
- ✅ PIP bounds inversion time to one critical section duration.
- ✅ Alternative: **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** — assign each mutex a ceiling equal to the highest priority of any task that may lock it.

**Message Queues:**
- ✅ Created with `(message_length, queue_depth)` parameters.
- ✅ `q_send(Q, msg, timeout)` and `q_receive(Q, buf, timeout)` are the only operations.
- ✅ Blocking timeouts: $0$ (non-blocking), $\infty$ (forever), finite $T$ (bounded wait).
- ✅ FreeRTOS-style queues use **copy semantics**; POSIX mqueues use **pointer semantics**.
- ✅ Priority queues (e.g., FreeRTOS `xQueueSendToFront`) allow out-of-band urgent messages.

**Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid:**
- ❌ Forgetting to protect critical sections with `Wait`/`Signal` pairs.
- ❌ Using a binary semaphore instead of a counting semaphore for an $N$-resource pool.
- ❌ Calling `Wait(mutex)` *before* `Wait(empty)` in producer — leads to **deadlock**.
- ❌ Confusing "binary semaphore used as event flag" with "mutex" — they are semantically similar but PIP applies to **mutex**, not to event-flag binary semaphores.
- ❌ Writing `Signal` without a matching `Wait` in producer-consumer — leaks semaphore count.

**Memory Aid (Mnemonic):**
> **"P**roducer **W**aits on **E**mpty, **S**ignals **F**ull; **C**onsumer **W**aits on **F**ull, **S**ignals **E**mpty."
> (PWEFS / CWFSF — pronounce "perfect" and "cwfsf" for recall.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Final Pitfall Callout**: In long 14-mark answers, KTU valuators **strictly deduct** 1 mark for:
> 1. Missing atomicity statement when describing `Wait`/`Signal`.
> 2. Wrong order of `Wait(empty)` vs `Wait(mutex)` in producer.
> 3. Omitting the trace table in producer-consumer questions.
> 4. Drawing the priority inversion diagram without showing the medium-priority preemption arrow.
> Memorize and include all four elements to secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Pipes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)** and process management, a **Pipe** is a kernel-managed, unidirectional communication channel that enables **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** between two or more processes (or threads) by streaming byte-oriented data through a circular buffer maintained in kernel memory. It is a fundamental **message-passing primitive** that abstracts the underlying transport mechanism behind a simple file-descriptor interface (read/write semantics). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (High-Yield):** > A pipe is a one-way data conduit operating on the **First-In-First-Out (FIFO)** principle, possessing a finite bounded buffer (typically **4096 bytes / 4 KB** on POSIX systems, configurable up to **64 KB** on Linux). One end is designated the **write-end** and the other the **read-end**; data written to the write-end emerges in the same order at the read-end. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Water Pipe** > Imagine a physical water pipe connecting two rooms. Water poured into one end (write-end) by Process A flows strictly in **one direction** and emerges at the other end (read-end) where Process B collects it. Process B cannot push water backward, and if the pipe gets full, the sender must wait. This is exactly how an OS pipe behaves: data is the "water," processes are the "rooms," and the kernel is the "plumbing system" managing flow and pressure (backpressure/blocking). ### 1.3 Types of Pipes (KTU Module 2 Classification) | Type | Visibility | Scope | Persistence | RTOS Relevance | |------|------------|-------|-------------|----------------| | **Anonymous Pipe** (Unnamed) | Identified only by file descriptors | Parent–Child related processes | Destroyed when last FD closes | Low (mainly POSIX/Linux desktop) | | **Named Pipe** (FIFO) | Has a name in the filesystem (`/tmp/myfifo`) | Any unrelated processes | Persists in filesystem | **High** (used in VxWorks, QNX IPC) | | **Message Queue** (Pipe-like) | Identified by a key/ID | System-wide | Survives until explicitly removed | **Very High** in RTOS (VxWorks `msgQCreate`) | ### 1.4 Core Properties (Mandatory Recall) - **Unidirectional**: Half-duplex. Bidirectional communication needs **two pipes**. - **Byte-stream oriented**: No message boundaries (unlike message queues). - **Synchronous blocking**: Reads block on empty pipes; writes block on full pipes. - **Kernel-allocated buffer**: Default size is **PIPE_BUF = 4096 bytes** (POSIX minimum) and **PIPE_MAX = 1048576 bytes** on modern Linux. - **Atomic writes**: Writes of size $\leq$ **PIPE_BUF** are atomic; larger writes may interleave with other writers. - **Bounded capacity**: Fixed maximum bytes (`pipe size`). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Pipes in RTOS are part of **Task Communication** mechanisms. While desktop OS pipes are byte-stream, **RTOS message queues** (a pipe-like primitive) provide **fixed-size message boundaries** with **priority-based delivery** — a critical real-time requirement. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown **Step 1 — Creation** The kernel allocates a **circular buffer** (pipe inode), initializes a wait queue, and returns **two file descriptors** in a user-supplied array: `fd[0]` (read-end) and `fd[1]` (write-end). **Step 2 — Writing** - Process performs `write(fd[1], buf, n)`. - Kernel copies `n` bytes from user space to the pipe buffer. - If buffer has $\geq n$ free bytes $\rightarrow$ data is appended, and any **blocked reader** is woken up. - If buffer has $< n$ free bytes $\rightarrow$ writer is **blocked** (in non-blocking mode, `write()` returns `-1` with `errno = EAGAIN`). **Step 3 — Reading** - Process performs `read(fd[0], buf, n)`. - Kernel copies up to `n` bytes (or until pipe is empty) from the buffer to user space. - If pipe is empty $\rightarrow$ reader is **blocked** until a writer provides data or all write-ends close. - If all write-ends are closed and buffer is empty $\rightarrow$ `read()` returns **0** (EOF semantics). **Step 4 — Closing** - `close(fd[0])` and `close(fd[1])` release references. When **all** read-ends **and** write-ends are closed, the kernel deallocates the buffer. ### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Design - **Why circular buffer?** Allows continuous producer/consumer operation without reallocation; O(1) write/read pointer movement. - **Why blocking semantics?** Provides natural **synchronization** — no need for explicit semaphores in single-producer/single-consumer cases. - **Why byte-stream (not datagram)?** Simpler kernel implementation; the consumer can read partial data. For RTOS, message queues are preferred when **message boundaries** matter. - **How is real-time determinism affected?** Blocking pipes introduce **unbounded wait times** if a consumer never reads. For **hard real-time** systems, RTOS message queues with **priority inheritance** (e.g., VxWorks `msgQSend`) are preferred. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter / Symbol | Formula / Value | Description | |--------------------|-----------------|-------------| | Effective Throughput | $T_{eff} = \dfrac{N_{bytes}}{t_{write} + t_{read} + t_{copy}}$ | Bytes transferred per unit time | | Pipe Capacity | $C_{pipe} = \text{pipe\_size} = 2^{n}$ bytes (typically $n = 12 \rightarrow 4096$) | Default **4096 B**, max **1 MiB** | | Atomic Write Size | $S_{atomic} = \text{PIPE\_BUF} = 4096$ B | POSIX minimum guaranteed atomic write | | Blocking Wait Time | $t_{wait} = t_{signal} - t_{arrival}$ | Time task blocks before wakeup | | Deadlock Probability (n producers) | $P_{deadlock} \propto \dfrac{1}{C_{pipe} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i}$ | Higher contention when $C_{pipe} \to$ small | | Buffer Occupancy | $B_{occ}(t) = \sum_{i} w_i(t) - \sum_{j} r_j(t)$ | Bytes in pipe at time $t$ | | FIFO Ordering | $\text{Order}(out) = \text{Order}(in)$ | Strict byte-order preservation | | Reader Wakeup Count | $N_{wake} = 1$ per `write` of $\geq$ 1 byte | Avoids **thundering herd** problem | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Pipes are foundational in **shell pipelines** (`ls \vert grep txt`), **OS kernel IPC**, **embedded RTOS task messaging**, and **streaming signal-processing pipelines** (DSP-to-Actuator chains). In VxWorks and FreeRTOS, pipe-like message queues drive **deterministic inter-task communication** with bounded latency. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Pipe Buffer Size for Bounded Latency **Problem:** Given a producer writing at rate $R_p$ bytes/second and a consumer reading at rate $R_c$ bytes/second, derive the **minimum pipe buffer size** $C_{min}$ that prevents the producer from ever blocking (assuming consumer is faster on average). **Step 1 — Define the burst size** The producer may emit a burst of $B$ bytes between consumer wakeups. Worst-case inter-read interval is $T_{r,max}$. $$B = R_p \cdot T_{r,max}$$ **Step 2 — Define consumer drain rate** The consumer drains bytes at rate $R_c$, so in time $T_{r,max}$ it removes: $$D = R_c \cdot T_{r,max}$$ **Step 3 — Net buffer requirement** The buffer must hold what arrives minus what is drained: $$C_{min} = B - D = (R_p - R_c) \cdot T_{r,max}$$ **Step 4 — Substitution with concrete units** Let $R_p = 1\,\text{MB/s}$, $R_c = 1.5\,\text{MB/s}$, $T_{r,max} = 10\,\text{ms}$: $$\begin{aligned} C_{min} &= (1{,}048{,}576 - 1{,}572{,}864) \cdot 0.01 \\ &= (-524{,}288) \cdot 0.01 \\ &= -5242.88 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation** Since the result is negative, the consumer is **strictly faster** than the producer — no buffering is required (theoretical minimum is **0 bytes**). However, OS minimum allocation is **PIPE_BUF = 4096 B**. Therefore: $$C_{alloc} = \max(C_{min},\, \text{PIPE\_BUF}) = 4096 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 6 — General case (consumer slower)** If $R_p = 2\,\text{MB/s}$, $R_c = 1\,\text{MB/s}$, $T_{r,max} = 10\,\text{ms}$: $$\begin{aligned} C_{min} &= (2{,}097{,}152 - 1{,}048{,}576) \cdot 0.01 \\ &= 1{,}048{,}576 \cdot 0.01 \\ &= 10{,}485.76 \text{ bytes} \\ &\approx 10.24 \text{ KiB} \end{aligned}$$ Thus the pipe must hold at least **~10.5 KB** to absorb the worst-case burst. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Blocking vs Non-Blocking Behavior | State | Reader Action | Writer Action | Reader Returns | Writer Returns | |-------|---------------|---------------|----------------|----------------| | Buffer Empty | `read()` | — | **Blocks** | — | | Buffer Empty, no writers | `read()` | — | **0 (EOF)** | — | | Buffer Full | — | `write()` | — | **Blocks** | | Buffer Full, non-blocking | — | `write()` | — | **−1, EAGAIN** | | Data Available | `read()` | — | **n bytes** | — | | Space Available | — | `write()` | — | **n bytes written** | | All write-ends closed | `read()` | — | **0 (EOF)** | — | | All read-ends closed | — | `write()` | — | **SIGPIPE → termination** | ### 3.3 RTOS-Specific Mapping (VxWorks / FreeRTOS / QNX) | Feature | POSIX Pipe (Linux) | VxWorks `pipeDev` | FreeRTOS Stream Buffer | QNX `MsgSend` | |---------|--------------------|-------------------|------------------------|---------------| | Direction | Unidirectional | Bidirectional (`pipeDevCreate`) | Unidirectional | Bidirectional | | Buffer Size | Configurable, default 4 KiB | Configurable per instance | User-defined (`xBufferSizeBytes`) | System-wide | | Blocking | Yes (configurable) | Yes (with `select`) | Yes (`xStreamBufferReceive` with timeout) | Yes (with `MsgSend`) | | Message Boundaries | **No** (byte stream) | **No** | **No** | **Yes** (typed messages) | | RTOS Determinism | Low | **High** | **High** | **Highest** | | Priority Inheritance | No | Yes (with INHERIT flag) | **Yes** (mutex-based) | **Yes** | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pipe Architecture in Kernel Space ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Process A
Producer] -->|write fd1| B[Kernel Space
Pipe Buffer
Circular FIFO] B -->|read fd0| C[Process B
Consumer] B -.->|wakes| C B -.->|blocks if full| A D[Parent Process] -->|fork+pipe| A D -->|inherits fd| C ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Producer-Consumer Data Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant P as Producer Task participant K as Kernel Pipe Buffer participant C as Consumer Task P->>K: write(fd1, data, n) alt Buffer has space K-->>K: Append data to circular buffer K-->>C: Wake blocked reader C->>K: read(fd0, buf, n) K-->>C: Return n bytes else Buffer full K-->>P: Block producer Note over P: Suspended on wait queue C->>K: read(fd0, buf, n) K-->>C: Return data K-->>P: Wake producer end P->>K: close(fd1) C->>K: read(fd0, buf, n) K-->>C: Returns 0 (EOF) C->>K: close(fd0) Note over K: Pipe deallocated by kernel ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Pipe Lifecycle States ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Created Created --> Active: first open fd0 and fd1 Active --> WriterBlocked: write to full pipe WriterBlocked --> Active: consumer reads Active --> ReaderBlocked: read from empty pipe ReaderBlocked --> Active: writer writes Active --> HalfClosed: all write-ends closed HalfClosed --> EOFReached: buffer drains EOFReached --> Destroyed: all fds closed Destroyed --> [*] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Block Architecture: RTOS Message Queue vs Pipe ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TraditionalPOSIX[POSIX Pipe Model] P1[Task 1] -->|stream bytes| BUF1[Circular Byte Buffer] BUF1 --> P2[Task 2] end subgraph RTOSQueue[RTOS Message Queue Model] M1[Task 1
Priority 5] -->|fixed msg| QBUF[Message Queue
Priority Aware] M2[Task 2
Priority 3] -->|fixed msg| QBUF QBUF -->|priority dispatch| M3[Task 3
Priority 7] end ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define a pipe in an operating system. Mention any two characteristics of pipes.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A pipe is a kernel-managed, unidirectional inter-process communication channel that allows byte-stream data transfer between processes using the FIFO (First-In-First-Out) discipline. It is accessed via two file descriptors — one for reading and one for writing. **Two characteristics (1.5 marks each):** 1. **Unidirectional**: Data flows in one direction only; two-way communication requires two pipes. 2. **Blocking by default**: A `read()` on an empty pipe blocks the calling process until data is written, and a `write()` on a full pipe blocks the writer until space is available. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Differentiate between a named pipe and an unnamed pipe.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Unnamed Pipe | Named Pipe (FIFO) | |---------|--------------|-------------------| | Identification | File descriptors only | Has a filesystem path name | | Process Relationship | Parent–child related | Any unrelated processes | | Persistence | Destroyed when fds close | Persists in filesystem | | Creation | `pipe()` syscall | `mkfifo()` syscall | (1.5 marks for clear definition of each + 1.5 marks for valid differentiation points.) --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > Students commonly lose marks by: (1) confusing **byte-stream pipes** with **message queues** (forgetting message boundaries), (2) omitting **blocking semantics** and **SIGPIPE** handling, (3) failing to mention **PIPE_BUF atomicity**, and (4) not drawing the **kernel buffer architecture**. Always include a labeled diagram in IPC questions — it scores 2–3 free marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Explain the concept of pipes in inter-process communication. With a neat diagram, describe the working of an **anonymous pipe** including its creation, data transfer, and termination. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):** A pipe is a one-way FIFO communication channel provided by the OS kernel, identified by two file descriptors — `fd[0]` (read) and `fd[1]` (write). It enables byte-stream communication between related processes, typically created by a parent before `fork()`. **Step 2 — Creation (1.5 marks):** The parent calls `int pipe(int fd[2])` which returns two fds in the array. The kernel allocates a circular buffer (default 4 KiB) and a wait queue. **Step 3 — Data Transfer (2 marks):** After `fork()`, both parent and child inherit both fds. Conventionally, the parent closes `fd[0]` and writes to `fd[1]`; the child closes `fd[1]` and reads from `fd[0]`. Data written by the producer emerges at the consumer in FIFO order. **Step 4 — Termination (1 mark):** When all fds are closed (typically after `close()` calls), the kernel deallocates the buffer. If all write-ends close and the buffer is empty, `read()` returns 0 (EOF). **Step 5 — Diagram (0.5 marks):** ``` Parent [fd1] --write--> [Kernel Pipe Buffer] --read--> Child [fd0] ``` **(b)** Compare **pipes**, **message queues**, and **shared memory** as IPC mechanisms. Discuss the suitability of each for a **hard real-time system**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Mechanism | Communication | Boundaries | Synchronization | RT Suitability | |-----------|---------------|------------|-----------------|----------------| | **Pipe** | Byte stream, unidirectional | **No** | Implicit (blocking) | Low (non-deterministic latency) | | **Message Queue** | Fixed-size messages, bidirectional | **Yes** | Explicit (priority-based) | **High** (VxWorks, QNX) | | **Shared Memory** | Direct memory access, bidirectional | N/A | Manual (semaphores required) | **Medium-High** (fastest, but needs sync) | **RT Suitability Discussion (3 marks):** - **Hard real-time** requires **bounded blocking time**. Pipes provide only blocking semantics with **unbounded wait**, hence unsuitable. - **Message queues** with **priority inheritance** (e.g., VxWorks `msgQSend`) deliver messages in **O(1)** and respect task priorities — best for RT. - **Shared memory** is the **fastest** (no kernel copy) but requires **explicit synchronization** via mutex/semaphore. In RTOS with priority inheritance mutexes (e.g., `pthread_mutexattr_setprotocol`), it becomes deterministic and is widely used (e.g., QNX, RTEMS). **Conclusion:** Message queues are the standard RTOS pipe-equivalent; shared memory with priority-inheritance mutexes is preferred for high-throughput deterministic IPC. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain **named pipes (FIFOs)** in Unix/Linux. How are they created, opened, and used for communication between **unrelated processes**? Give an illustrative example. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition (1.5 marks):** A named pipe (FIFO — First-In-First-Out) is a special file in the filesystem that behaves like an anonymous pipe but has a name (e.g., `/tmp/myfifo`). It allows communication between **unrelated processes** since any process can open it by path. **Step 2 — Creation (1.5 marks):** Created using the shell command `mkfifo /tmp/myfifo` or the system call `mkfifo(path, mode)`. It appears in the filesystem as a file with type `p` (verified by `ls -l`: the mode starts with `p`). **Step 3 — Opening (1.5 marks):** A FIFO is opened with `open()`. The `open()` call **blocks** until **both** a reader and a writer have opened the FIFO (unless `O_NONBLOCK` is set). **Step 4 — Illustrative Example (2.5 marks):** - **Writer process:** `int fd = open("/tmp/myfifo", O_WRONLY); write(fd, "Hello", 5); close(fd);` - **Reader process:** `int fd = open("/tmp/myfifo", O_RDONLY); read(fd, buf, sizeof(buf)); printf("%s", buf); close(fd);` - Both processes must be running; the `open()` calls synchronize them naturally. **(b)** Discuss the **limitations of pipes** in real-time systems. Explain how **VxWorks message queues** overcome these limitations. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Limitations of POSIX Pipes in RT (3.5 marks):** 1. **No message boundaries** — data is a byte stream, so the receiver cannot distinguish message boundaries; extra framing logic required. 2. **No priority handling** — pipes deliver in FIFO order regardless of task priority, violating RT scheduling semantics. 3. **Unbounded blocking** — a slow consumer can indefinitely block a high-priority producer. 4. **No timeouts** in basic pipe I/O (must use `select()` or `poll()`). 5. **Limited to related processes** (anonymous) or filesystem overhead (named). **How VxWorks Message Queues Overcome These (3.5 marks):** 1. **Fixed-size messages** preserve message boundaries. 2. **Priority-based delivery** (`MSG_Q_FIFO` or `MSG_Q_PRIORITY` options) allows high-priority tasks to jump the queue. 3. **Bounded blocking with timeouts** via `msgQSend()` and `msgQReceive()` accepting `timeout` ticks. 4. **Counting semaphore semantics** allow multiple senders/receivers with deterministic blocking. 5. **Direct task wakeup** without `select()` overhead — `O(1)` operation suitable for hard real-time. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Final High-Density Revision Block** - ✅ Pipe = **unidirectional**, **byte-stream**, **FIFO** IPC channel. - ✅ Created via `pipe(int fd[2])` — returns `fd[0]` (read), `fd[1]` (write). - ✅ Default pipe size on Linux = **4096 bytes** (max **1 MiB**); atomic write threshold = **PIPE_BUF = 4096 B**. - ✅ Anonymous pipes: parent–child only; Named pipes (FIFOs): unrelated processes. - ✅ Blocking semantics: `read()` blocks on empty pipe; `write()` blocks on full pipe. - ✅ `read()` returns **0** (EOF) when all write-ends closed **and** buffer empty. - ✅ Writing to a pipe with **no readers** raises `SIGPIPE` (default action: process termination). - ✅ **RTOS equivalent**: Message Queues (VxWorks `msgQCreate`, QNX `MsgSend`) — provide **message boundaries, priority delivery, timeouts, and determinism**. - ✅ Pipes are **NOT** suitable for hard real-time due to **unbounded blocking** and **no priority semantics**. - ✅ Kernel implements pipe as a **circular buffer** with two wait queues (one for readers, one for writers). - ✅ For bidirectional communication, use **two pipes** (SVR4 style) or **socketpair()`. - ✅ Shell usage: `cmd1 \vert cmd2 \vert cmd3` — each `|` is a pipe. - ✅ **Formula recap**: $C_{min} = (R_p - R_c) \cdot T_{r,max}$; $S_{atomic} = \text{PIPE\_BUF} = 4096$ B. ---

topics.title: Event Registers and Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Event Registers and Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Event Registers — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (VxWorks RTOS Context)**
> *Event Registers* are a lightweight, **task-to-task synchronization mechanism** provided by the VxWorks real-time kernel. Each task owns a dedicated set of event bits (organised into 32-bit registers, typically **8 registers = 256 event bits** per task in modern VxWorks 6.x/7). Other tasks or Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs) can *set* individual bits in a target task's event registers. The receiving task can *wait* (block) on specific combinations of events, and resume execution only when one or more of those bits become asserted.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic falls under **inter-task communication and synchronization primitives**, parallel to semaphores, message queues, and pipes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Event Registers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Office Mailbox with Slotted Compartments**
> Imagine you are a manager working in a sealed office (the *waiting task*). You tell your secretary: *"I will only open my door when the *red flag* is raised OR the *blue flag* is raised, but not for any other colour."* Meanwhile, multiple assistants (other *tasks*) walk past your office and selectively raise specific coloured flags inside your office. The moment any of the flags you care about is raised, your door unlocks and you resume work.

In this analogy:
- **The sealed office** = the blocked (waiting) task
- **The flag colours** = individual event bits
- **The secretary's rules** = `eventReceive()` options (`EVENTS_WAIT_ANY`, `EVENTS_WAIT_ALL`)
- **The assistants raising flags** = `eventSend()` calls
- **The flag-post inside the office** = the task's event register

### 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|------|---------|------------------|
| `events` | 32-bit unsigned integer of bits | Defines *which* event(s) the task waits on |
| `EVENTS_WAIT_ANY` | Resume if *any* of the requested bits is set | Logical **OR** wait condition |
| `EVENTS_WAIT_ALL` | Resume only when *all* requested bits are set | Logical **AND** wait condition |
| `eventsReceived` | Output variable storing the actual bits received | Critical for handling *spurious wakeups* |
