| `eventSend(taskId, events)` | Atomic OR-set of bits in a task's register | Producer-side API |
| `eventReceive()` | Atomic test-and-clear of received bits | Consumer-side API |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** Event registers are the **fastest** synchronization mechanism in VxWorks because they do **not** require kernel object allocation (unlike semaphores/queues) and are implemented as a single atomic CPU instruction (e.g., `or` followed by a check on a wait queue).

---

## 1.4 Signals — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Signals* in an RTOS (particularly VxWorks and POSIX-compliant real-time systems) are a **software interrupt mechanism** used to notify a task that a specific asynchronous event has occurred. A signal is a 1-to-N asynchronous notification; the kernel delivers it to a target task, optionally invoking a user-defined *signal handler* routine, similar to a hardware ISR but driven entirely in software.

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — Signals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Pager / SMS Alert System**
> You are a surgeon performing a long operation (*the running task*). The hospital's paging system can send you a "Code Blue" alert (*signal SIGURG*). You have pre-registered an emergency response procedure (*signal handler*). The moment the page is received, you *interrupt* the current work, handle the emergency, and return to where you left off (or, in the kernel default, the signal is just a queued flag — *no handler*).

### 1.6 Standard Signal Set (VxWorks / POSIX)

| Signal Macro | Meaning | Typical Use in RTOS |
|---|---|---|
| `SIGUSR1` | User-defined signal 1 | Application-level custom event |
| `SIGUSR2` | User-defined signal 2 | Application-level custom event |
| `SIGINT` | Program interrupt (Ctrl+C) | Console-driven debugging |
| `SIGTERM` | Termination request | Graceful task shutdown |
| `SIGKILL` | Forced kill (uncatchable) | Emergency termination |
| `SIGALRM` | Real-time timer expiry | Periodic task wake-up |
| `SIGURG` | Urgent out-of-band data | Socket-based OOB notifications |
| `SIGCHLD` | Child task status change | Parent task supervision |
| `SIGSEGV` | Segmentation violation | Memory fault handler |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** VxWorks supports a maximum of **31 standard signals** (signals numbered **1 through 31**). Signal number `0` is reserved and conventionally used to test *signal validity* of a task ID.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Event Registers — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Underlying Data Structure

Each TCB (Task Control Block) in VxWorks contains an array of unsigned 32-bit integers, conceptually:

$$
\text{TCB.events} = \{R_0, R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_7\}
$$

where each $R_i$ is a 32-bit register. The total event space per task is:

$$
\text{Total Event Bits} = 8 \times 32 = 256 \text{ bits}
$$

### 2.1.2 The Three Core Operations

**Step 1 — `eventSend()` (Producer Side)**
- Atomically performs a *bitwise OR* on the target task's specified register.
- Does **not** block the sender, regardless of the target's state.
- Wakes the target if it was blocked in `eventReceive()` and the wake condition is satisfied.

**Step 2 — `eventReceive()` (Consumer Side)**
- Tests whether the requested event mask has been satisfied.
- If **not satisfied**, the task is moved to the *event wait queue* and blocked.
- The kernel stores a `WAIT_TYPE` (ANY vs ALL) and the `mask` inside the TCB for later re-evaluation.

**Step 3 — Wake-up Evaluation**
On every `eventSend()` to a task in the event-wait queue, the kernel re-evaluates:

$$
\text{Wake}_i = \begin{cases}
(\text{TCB.events} \ \& \ \text{TCB.mask}) \neq 0 & \text{if WAIT\_ANY} \\[4pt]
(\text{TCB.events} \ \& \ \text{TCB.mask}) = \text{TCB.mask} & \text{if WAIT\_ALL}
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.1.3 Atomic Receive-Clear Semantics

On successful wake-up, the kernel atomically clears the bits that satisfied the wait condition (depending on the `EVENTS_WAIT_ANY`/`EVENTS_WAIT_ALL` mode and explicit clear options). The `eventsReceived` output parameter reports which bits were *actually* consumed.

### 2.1.4 Why Event Registers Are Faster Than Semaphores

| Property | Event Register | Semaphore |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| Kernel object allocation | **None** (per-task) | Required |
| Wait queue complexity | O(1) per task | O(N) shared queue |
| Atomic operation | Single `or` instruction | `semTake` involves list manipulation |
| Multiple-event wait | Native (`WAIT_ALL`) | Requires counting semaphore tricks |
| Use in ISR | **Yes** (via `eventSendFromISR`) | Limited |

---

## 2.2 Signals — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Signal Lifecycle

The signal delivery flow consists of four logical stages:

**Step 1 — Signal Generation**
A source (another task, ISR, or kernel timer) calls `kill(taskId, signo)`. The kernel marks the signal as *pending* in the target TCB's `sigPendMask`.

**Step 2 — Signal Delivery**
When the target task is scheduled to run, the kernel checks `sigPendMask` *before* dispatching user code. If a handler is installed and not masked, the handler is invoked in the task's context.

**Step 3 — Signal Handling**
Three possible actions:
- **Default action** (if no handler): Terminate, ignore, or core-dump depending on `signo`.
- **Custom handler**: User-defined C function called with the signal number.
- **Masked**: The signal remains pending until unmasked.

**Step 4 — Signal Acknowledgement / Clearing**
For queued real-time signals (`SIGQUEUE` option in POSIX), the signal can be explicitly received via `sigtimedwait()`.

### 2.2.2 Signal Handler Installation

The recommended POSIX-style API is `sigaction()`, which is more reliable than the older `signal()`:

$$
\text{struct sigaction} = \{ \text{sa\_handler}, \text{sa\_mask}, \text{sa\_flags} \}
$$

- `sa_handler` → pointer to the C handler function
- `sa_mask` → set of signals to block *during* handler execution
- `sa_flags` → options such as `SA_SIGINFO` (extended info) or `SA_RESTART` (auto-restart syscalls)

### 2.2.3 Signal Delivery in ISR Context

In VxWorks, calling `kill()` from an ISR is permitted because it is non-blocking and only manipulates the TCB's pending bitmask. The handler is *not* executed in the ISR context — it is deferred to the target task's context.

### 2.2.4 The Pending vs Blocked Distinction

$$
\text{Signal effective at task } T = (\text{Pending}_T) \ \cap \ \overline{(\text{Blocked}_T)}
$$

A signal that is pending but blocked remains queued until the task explicitly unblocks it via `sigprocmask()`.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Boundary Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a high-yield *cheat sheet* for the KTU End-Semester Examination.

| # | Item | Value / Formula | Notes |
|---|------|-----------------|-------|
| 1 | Event bits per task (VxWorks 6.x/7) | $8 \times 32 = 256$ | Configurable at kernel build |
| 2 | Event bits per task (VxWorks 5.x) | $8 \times 16 = 128$ | Legacy systems |
| 3 | Max signal number | $31$ | Signals 1–31 |
| 4 | Reserve signal value | $0$ | Used for permission testing |
| 5 | `EVENTS_WAIT_ANY` logic | $(\text{events} \ \& \ \text{mask}) \neq 0$ | OR-semantics |
| 6 | `EVENTS_WAIT_ALL` logic | $(\text{events} \ \& \ \text{mask}) = \text{mask}$ | AND-semantics |
| 7 | `eventSend` blocking? | $\text{No}$ (always non-blocking) | Safe in ISR |
| 8 | `eventReceive` blocking? | $\text{Yes}$ (with `WAIT_FOREVER` / timeout) | Never call from ISR |
| 9 | Signal handler context | Task context of target | Not ISR context |
| 10 | Handler stack overhead | 1 frame on task's own stack | No extra stack allocated |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where this matters in industry-grade RTOS design:**

- **Aerospace & Avionics (DO-178C):** Event registers implement *high-frequency sensor-data-ready* flags between ISR and control tasks.
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR / OSEK):** Signals are used for inter-ECU task notifications over CAN-bus abstraction layers.
- **Industrial Robotics:** `SIGALRM` + event-register hybrid synchronises motion trajectories with hard real-time deadlines.
- **Telecommunications (5G PHY layer):** Event registers signal *slot-boundary* arrival to baseband processing tasks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Wake-up Condition for `EVENTS_WAIT_ALL`

We derive the formal condition under which a task blocked with the `EVENTS_WAIT_ALL` option will be unblocked by an incoming `eventSend()`.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let:
- $E(t)$ = the value of the target task's event register at time $t$
- $M$ = the *mask* of bits the task is interested in (constant, set at `eventReceive()`)
- $W$ = the *type* of wait, $W \in \{\text{ANY}, \text{ALL}\}$

The kernel's wake-up test for $W = \text{ALL}$ is:

$$
\text{wake} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } (E \ \& \ M) = M \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Bitwise AND isolates the bits of interest**

$$
E \ \& \ M = \sum_{i=0}^{31} (E_i \cdot M_i) \cdot 2^i
$$

where $E_i, M_i \in \{0, 1\}$ are the $i$-th bits of $E$ and $M$ respectively.

> *Conversion logic:* Bitwise AND equals 1 at position $i$ **only if** both $E_i$ and $M_i$ are 1. This effectively filters out irrelevant bits.

**Step 2 — Compare against the full mask $M$**

$$
E \ \& \ M = M \iff \forall i, (E_i \cdot M_i) = M_i
$$

> *Conversion logic:* This equation holds true if and only if, for every bit position $i$ where $M_i = 1$, the corresponding $E_i$ is also 1. That is, *every* requested bit has been set in the event register.

**Step 3 — Equivalent Boolean Form**

$$
\text{wake}_{\text{ALL}} = \bigwedge_{i: M_i = 1} E_i
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The conjunction (AND) of all the relevant bits $E_i$ must evaluate to true. This is the canonical Boolean expression for "all requested events have occurred."

**Step 4 — Atomic Check-and-Clear on Wake-up**

When wake = 1, the kernel atomically clears the bits that satisfied the condition. In the simple case (no selective clear), the entire register is reset:

$$
E \leftarrow 0
$$

or, with selective clear (`EVENTS_WAIT_ANY` with `EVENTS_CLEAR`):

$$
E \leftarrow E \ \& \ \overline{(\text{eventsReceived})}
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose $M = 0\text{x}00000007$ (bits 0, 1, 2 requested) and $W = \text{ALL}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial state: } E_0 &= 0\text{x}00000000 \\
\text{Task A sends: } eventSend(T, 0\text{x}00000001) &\Rightarrow E_1 = 0\text{x}00000000 \mid 0\text{x}00000001 = 0\text{x}00000001 \\
\text{Test: } (E_1 \ \& \ M) &= 0\text{x}00000001 \neq 0\text{x}00000007 \Rightarrow \text{stay blocked} \\
\text{Task B sends: } eventSend(T, 0\text{x}00000006) &\Rightarrow E_2 = 0\text{x}00000001 \mid 0\text{x}00000006 = 0\text{x}00000007 \\
\text{Test: } (E_2 \ \& \ M) &= 0\text{x}00000007 = M \Rightarrow \text{WAKE} \\
\text{eventsReceived} &= 0\text{x}00000007 \\
\text{Post-clear state: } E_3 &= 0\text{x}00000000
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The task was blocked until **both** other tasks had set their respective bits. Only when the third bit arrived did the AND-mask equal $M$, triggering wake-up.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Signal Delivery Latency Bound

### 3.2.1 Setup

Define:
- $t_g$ = time at which `kill()` is invoked
- $t_h$ = time at which the signal handler begins execution
- $T_{ctx}$ = worst-case context-switch time of the RTOS
- $T_{sched}$ = worst-case scheduling latency
- $T_{dispatch}$ = worst-case dispatch latency

The signal delivery latency is:

$$
\Delta t_{\text{delivery}} = t_h - t_g = T_{ctx} + T_{sched} + T_{dispatch}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Bound

**Step 1 — Interrupt / syscall entry overhead**

$$
t_1 = t_g + T_{ctx}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The kernel must complete the current context save and enter the system call handler.

**Step 2 — Pending-bit update and TCB update**

$$
t_2 = t_1 + \delta_{\text{update}}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The pending bitmask and TCB signal fields are atomically updated. The update is $\mathcal{O}(1)$.

**Step 3 — Return to scheduler**

$$
t_3 = t_2 + T_{sched}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* If the target task is currently running, the kernel may pre-empt it to deliver the signal immediately. If the target is not the highest-priority ready task, the scheduler must first complete the current tick.

**Step 4 — Handler invocation**

$$
t_h = t_3 + T_{dispatch}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The target task's saved context is restored, and the handler prologue pushes a new stack frame. The handler now executes in the target's context.

### 3.2.3 Final Latency Expression

$$
\boxed{\Delta t_{\text{delivery}} \leq T_{ctx}^{\max} + T_{sched}^{\max} + T_{dispatch}^{\max}}
$$

For a typical VxWorks/POSIX system on a 600 MHz PowerPC, the empirical bound is on the order of **1–10 microseconds**, which is acceptable for hard real-time notification but **not** for sub-microsecond control loops (where hardware interrupts are still preferred).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Event Register vs Signal

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix in the KTU exam when a question asks "Which mechanism would you choose and why?"**

| Decision Criterion | Event Register | Signal |
|--------------------|----------------|--------|
| Synchronous inter-task data-ready flag | ✅ **Best fit** | ❌ Overkill |
| Notify a task of abnormal termination | ❌ Awkward | ✅ **Best fit** |
| Use directly from ISR | ✅ Yes (`eventSendFromISR`) | ✅ Yes (`kill` is non-blocking) |
| Carry payload data | ❌ Bitmask only | ❌ Limited (`SA_SIGINFO` carries a small struct) |
| Multiple event-AND synchronization | ✅ Native | ❌ Requires manual accumulation |
| Custom async behaviour (handler) | ❌ Resume is implicit | ✅ Explicit handler |
| POSIX compliance requirement | ❌ VxWorks-specific | ✅ Standardised |

---

## 3.4 Comparison of Waiting Primitives Across RTOS

| RTOS | Event Register | Signal | Semaphore | Notes |
|------|----------------|--------|-----------|-------|
| **VxWorks** | Native (`eventSend`/`eventReceive`) | POSIX + custom | Counting/Binary | Most complete |
| **FreeRTOS** | Direct-to-Task Notifications (similar idea) | Not native | Binary/Counting/Mutex | Direct-to-Task is the analogue |
| **RTEMS** | Event sets | POSIX signals | Binary/Counting | Closest to VxWorks |
| **ThreadX** | Event Flags (similar) | Not native | Counting/Mutex | 32-bit event flags |
| **QNX Neutrino** | Pulse (analogue) | POSIX signals | POSIX semaphores | Pulses carry a small code + value |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Event Register Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Producer Task A]
    T1[Producer Task B]
    T2[Producer ISR]
    S0[eventSend EventBit0]
    S1[eventSend EventBit1]
    S2[eventSendFromISR EventBit2]
    R0[Target TCB Event Register 32-bit]
    M0[Wait Condition Evaluator]
    Q0[Event Wait Queue]
    W0[Task T Resumed]
    P0[eventsReceived Output Parameter]

    T0 --> S0
    T1 --> S1
    T2 --> S2
    S0 --> R0
    S1 --> R0
    S2 --> R0
    R0 --> M0
    M0 --> Q0
    M0 --> W0
    W0 --> P0
    Q0 --> M0
```

> *Read-aloud explanation:* Multiple producers (tasks A, B and an ISR) independently issue `eventSend` calls. The target task's TCB receives the bit-set into a 32-bit register. The kernel's wait-condition evaluator continually checks the register against the requested mask; if the condition is satisfied, the task is moved from the *Event Wait Queue* to the *Ready Queue* and resumed. The consumed bits are reported to the task via `eventsReceived`.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Signal Delivery Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Src as Source Task or ISR
    participant K as VxWorks Kernel
    participant Tgt as Target Task
    participant H as Signal Handler

    Src->>K: kill taskId signo
    K->>K: Update sigPendMask in TCB
    K->>K: Mark signal as pending
    K-->>Src: Return immediately
    Note over K,Tgt: When target is scheduled
    K->>Tgt: Pre-dispatch check sigPendMask
    K->>K: Check sigBlockMask
    K->>H: Invoke handler sa_handler signo
    H->>H: Execute user-defined routine
    H-->>Tgt: Return to original execution point
    Tgt->>Tgt: Resume normal flow
```

> *Read-aloud explanation:* The source invokes `kill`, the kernel atomically marks the signal as pending in the target's TCB, and returns immediately. When the scheduler next dispatches the target task, the kernel checks the pending mask against the block mask. If the signal is not blocked, the user-registered handler is invoked in the target's context. After the handler returns, the target resumes its previous execution point.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture: Hybrid Event+Signal Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HW[Hardware Interrupt Source]
    ISR[Interrupt Service Routine]
    EVT1[eventSend Bit0 to Control Task]
    SIG1[kill SIGALRM to Watchdog Task]
    CT[Real-Time Control Task]
    WD[Watchdog Task]
    ACT[Actuator Output]
    RS[System Reset]

    HW --> ISR
    ISR --> EVT1
    ISR --> SIG1
    EVT1 --> CT
    SIG1 --> WD
    CT --> ACT
    WD --> RS
```

> *Read-aloud explanation:* A hardware interrupt triggers an ISR. Inside the ISR, *both* mechanisms are used in parallel: a low-latency event bit is sent to the hard real-time control task (to resume deterministic processing), and a software signal is sent to the watchdog task (to perform a slower, asynchronous health check). This pattern is common in safety-critical systems where deterministic response and asynchronous supervision must coexist.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, May 2024]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between *event registers* and *binary semaphores* as inter-task synchronization mechanisms in VxWorks. Mention **any three** points of difference.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Object allocation:** Event registers require **no kernel object** allocation — they are per-task storage inside the TCB. Binary semaphores require an explicit `semBCreate()` call and a semaphore control block (semCB).
2. **Multi-bit waiting:** Event registers natively support *multiple bits AND/OR* waiting via `EVENTS_WAIT_ALL` / `EVENTS_WAIT_ANY`. A binary semaphore is a single-bit (locked/unlocked) construct.
3. **Use in ISR:** `eventSend()` is safe and non-blocking inside an ISR, while `semGive()` from an ISR requires the ISR-safe variant `semGiveFromISR()` and is still subject to kernel internal locking.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** What is a *signal handler* in VxWorks? Explain briefly how the `sigaction()` API differs from the legacy `signal()` API.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **signal handler** is a user-defined C function that the kernel invokes (in the target task's context) when a specific signal is delivered. It functions as a *software analogue* of an Interrupt Service Routine.

**Differences between `sigaction()` and `signal()`:**

| Aspect | `signal()` | `sigaction()` |
|---|---|---|
| Portability | Older, behaviour varies across UNIX variants | POSIX-standard, deterministic |
| Handler masking during execution | Implementation-defined | Explicit via `sa_mask` |
| Flags support | Limited | `SA_RESTART`, `SA_SIGINFO`, `SA_NODEFER`, etc. |
| Reliable | Unreliable on some systems (handler reset to default) | Handler persists across invocations |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> *Common pitfall:* Students often describe `eventSend()` and `signal` mechanisms without distinguishing their **execution context** (event registers fire on the *target* task's stack; signals fire a *handler* in the *target's* context but require pre-dispatch hook). Another common mistake is to confuse the *pending bitmask* with the *block mask* of a signal. Always state which field of the TCB is being modified and at which kernel phase.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice, Sample Paper 2024]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **event register mechanism** in VxWorks. Describe `eventSend()`, `eventReceive()`, and the difference between `EVENTS_WAIT_ANY` and `EVENTS_WAIT_ALL`.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A real-time control system uses **three producer tasks** P1, P2, P3 and one consumer task C. P1 sets event bit `0x01`, P2 sets `0x02`, P3 sets `0x04`. C must execute only when **all three bits are set** (logical AND). Design the relevant code snippets and explain the wake-up condition mathematically. What happens if `EVENTS_WAIT_ANY` is mistakenly used instead?

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark)**
Event registers are a per-task, lightweight synchronization mechanism in VxWorks. Each task's TCB contains 8 × 32-bit registers = **256 event bits**, free of dynamic allocation.

**Step 2 — API (2 marks)**
- `STATUS eventSend (TASK_ID tid, UINT32 events)` — atomically sets the specified bits in the target's register. *Non-blocking, ISR-safe.*
- `STATUS eventReceive (UINT32 events, UINT8 options, UINT32 timeout, UINT32 *eventsReceived)` — blocks until the requested event mask is satisfied per the `options` flag.

**Step 3 — Wait Mode Comparison (2 marks)**

| Mode | Wake Condition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `EVENTS_WAIT_ANY` | $(\text{events} \ \& \ \text{mask}) \neq 0$ | Fire on first arrival (e.g., emergency stop) |
| `EVENTS_WAIT_ALL` | $(\text{events} \ \& \ \text{mask}) = \text{mask}$ | Synchronise multiple data sources (e.g., sensor fusion) |

**Step 4 — Diagram (2 marks)**
Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. (Examiner expects a labelled block diagram showing producers, the TCB event register, the wait-condition evaluator, and the consumer task.)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Code Skeleton (3 marks)**

```c
/* Producer tasks */
void P1(void) { while (1) { /* ... do work ... */ eventSend(C_id, 0x01); } }
void P2(void) { while (1) { /* ... do work ... */ eventSend(C_id, 0x02); } }
void P3(void) { while (1) { /* ... do work ... */ eventSend(C_id, 0x04); } }

/* Consumer task */
void C(void) {
    UINT32 mask = 0x01 | 0x02 | 0x04;     /* 0x07 */
    UINT32 rcv  = 0;
    while (1) {
        eventReceive(mask, EVENTS_WAIT_ALL, WAIT_FOREVER, &rcv);
        /* All three producers have signalled; proceed with fused computation */
        fused_action();
    }
}
```

**Step 2 — Mathematical Wake-up Condition (2 marks)**
The consumer is unblocked when:

$$
E \ \& \ M = M \quad \text{where} \quad M = 0\text{x}07,\ E \in \text{TCB.events}
$$

Bit-wise expansion:

$$
(E_0 \cdot 1) \cdot 2^0 + (E_1 \cdot 1) \cdot 2^1 + (E_2 \cdot 1) \cdot 2^2 + 0 = 0\text{x}07
$$

which requires $E_0 = E_1 = E_2 = 1$ — i.e., **all three bits must be 1**.

**Step 3 — Consequence of Mistaken `EVENTS_WAIT_ANY` (2 marks)**
If `EVENTS_WAIT_ANY` were used, the consumer would resume as soon as **any** one producer set its bit. The fused computation would then run on *partial* data, leading to a **race condition** in the sensor-fusion logic. In a flight-control system, this could produce incorrect actuator commands — a hard real-time correctness violation.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice, Sample Paper 2024]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **signal mechanism** in VxWorks/POSIX. With a suitable diagram, describe the signal lifecycle from `kill()` invocation to handler completion. What is the role of the *signal mask* and the *pending bitmask*?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **signals and event registers** across *execution context*, *payload capacity*, *POSIX compliance*, *handler model*, *blocking behaviour*, *atomicity*, and *typical use case*. Justify when you would choose one over the other for an avionics flight-control application.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark)**
A signal is a *software interrupt* — an asynchronous notification delivered by the kernel to a target task. VxWorks supports 31 standard signals (1–31), with signal 0 reserved.

**Step 2 — Lifecycle (3 marks)**
The four-stage lifecycle is:

1. **Generation:** `kill(taskId, signo)` is called by a source task or ISR. The kernel marks the signal as *pending* in the target's TCB (`sigPendMask |= (1 << signo)`).
2. **Delivery:** When the scheduler dispatches the target, the kernel checks the pending mask against the block mask. If unblocked, the user-registered handler is invoked.
3. **Execution:** The handler runs in the *target's* context, on the *target's* stack. No separate ISR context is created.
4. **Return:** Upon handler return, the target resumes the instruction that was interrupted.

(Refer to the Mermaid sequence diagram in Section 4.2.)

**Step 3 — Mask vs Pending Bitmask (2 marks)**

$$
\text{Effective delivery} = \text{sigPendMask} \ \& \ \overline{\text{sigBlockMask}}
$$

- `sigPendMask` accumulates delivered signals awaiting execution.
- `sigBlockMask` is set by `sigprocmask()` and suppresses delivery until cleared.
- A signal can be **pending but blocked**, in which case it remains queued.

**Step 4 — API example (1 mark)**

```c
struct sigaction sa;
sa.sa_handler = my_handler;
sigemptyset(&sa.sa_mask);
sa.sa_flags = SA_RESTART;
sigaction(SIGUSR1, &sa, NULL);
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Dimension | Event Register | Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Execution context | Target task resumes its code at the *blocking* call | Handler invoked *before* target resumes, in target's context |
| Payload capacity | Bitmask only (1 bit per logical event) | Bitmask + optional `siginfo_t` payload with `SA_SIGINFO` |
| POSIX compliance | VxWorks-specific | POSIX-standard (portable) |
| Handler model | None — task code itself handles | Explicit `sa_handler` function pointer |
| Blocking behaviour | `eventReceive()` blocks; `eventSend()` does not | `kill()` is non-blocking; handler runs on next dispatch |
| Atomicity | Atomic OR-set and test-and-clear | Atomic pending-bit set; handler invocation is also atomic w.r.t. the task |
| Typical use case | Tight sensor-data-ready flags | Async notifications, abnormal-condition handlers |

**Justification for Avionics (2 marks):**
In a **flight-control system**, event registers are preferred for **deterministic, high-frequency** synchronisation between sensor ISRs and the control loop task (e.g., 1 kHz attitude update). Signals are reserved for **asynchronous abnormal-condition notifications**, such as a `SIGTERM` issued by a supervisory task upon detection of a sensor-stuck fault. Mixing the two primitives is a recognised best practice in DO-178C designs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- ✅ Event registers provide **256 bits per task** (8 × 32-bit registers) in modern VxWorks.
- ✅ `eventSend()` is **non-blocking and ISR-safe**; `eventReceive()` is the blocking counterpart.
- ✅ `EVENTS_WAIT_ANY` → OR-semantics; `EVENTS_WAIT_ALL` → AND-semantics.
- ✅ `eventsReceived` is **mandatory** to read to avoid lost-event bugs.
- ✅ VxWorks supports **31 signals** (1–31); signal `0` is reserved.
- ✅ `signal()` is legacy and unreliable; prefer POSIX `sigaction()`.
- ✅ Signal handlers run in the **target task's context**, *not* in ISR context.
- ✅ `kill()` is **non-blocking**; safe in ISR for signal delivery.
- ✅ Pending vs blocked signal: a signal is delivered only when **pending AND not blocked**.
- ✅ Event registers are the **fastest** synchronisation primitive; use for high-frequency data-ready flags.
- ✅ Signals are best for **asynchronous abnormal-condition notifications** (faults, termination, urgent data).
- ✅ For deterministic, hard real-time work, prefer event registers; for asynchronous supervisory work, prefer signals.
- ✅ Always check the *mask* and *pending bitmask* in code; do not rely on default actions in production.
- ✅ In exam answers, always draw a **labelled block diagram** for full marks on architecture questions.
- ✅ Know the formula: $\text{Wake}_{\text{ALL}} = (E \ \& \ M) = M$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Task Scheduling and Constraints
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Task Scheduling and Constraints

# Real-Time Task Scheduling and Constraints — Module 3

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-Time Task Scheduling** is the deterministic mechanism by which a *Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)* assigns CPU execution time to competing tasks such that every task completes before its stipulated deadline. The scheduler must guarantee **temporal correctness** — i.e., the system produces logically correct results *at the right time*, not merely correct results.

A **Real-Time Task** is a computational job that carries an explicit *temporal contract* with the system. Formally, a task $\tau_i$ is described by a tuple of parameters:

$$
\tau_i \;=\; (\phi_i,\; C_i,\; P_i,\; D_i,\; T_i)
$$

where each symbol represents a fundamental timing attribute of the task.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The heart of RTOS lies not in *speed* but in *predictability*. A slower deterministic system is always preferred over a faster non-deterministic one for hard real-time workloads.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are the **head chef of a busy restaurant kitchen** (the CPU):

- You receive **orders** (tasks) from customers.
- Each order has a **promise time** (deadline) — *"I need the pizza by 7:30 PM."*
- Some dishes have a **recurring schedule** (periodic) — *"daily breakfast buffet."*
- Some are **emergency requests** (aperiodic) — *"birthday cake urgently."*
- You must decide **which order to start first** (priority), and ensure **no order is delivered late**.

If a wedding cake is 5 minutes late, it is a **disaster** (hard real-time).  
If a regular pizza is 5 minutes late, the customer is mildly annoyed (soft real-time).

The **scheduler is the head chef**, and the **kitchen rules (scheduling algorithm)** must ensure deadlines are honored under all circumstances.

---

### 1.3 Fundamental Task Parameters (Standard KTU Metrics in **bold**)

| Symbol | Parameter | Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\phi_i$ | **Phase (Release Offset)** | First instant at which task is ready | seconds (s) |
| $r_i$ | **Release Time** | Time job is made available | seconds (s) |
| $C_i$ | **Computation Time (WCET)** | Worst-Case Execution Time | seconds (s) |
| $P_i$ | **Period** | Interval between consecutive releases | seconds (s) |
| $D_i$ | **Relative Deadline** | Time allowed from release to completion | seconds (s) |
| $d_i$ | **Absolute Deadline** | $r_i + D_i$ | seconds (s) |
| $s_i$ | **Start Time** | Instant execution begins | seconds (s) |
| $f_i$ | **Finish Time** | Instant execution completes | seconds (s) |
| $R_i$ | **Response Time** | $f_i - r_i$ | seconds (s) |
| $L_i$ | **Lateness** | $f_i - d_i$ (can be negative) | seconds (s) |
| $\mathcal{T}_i$ | **Tardiness** | $\max(0,\; L_i)$ | seconds (s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** is **not** the average execution time — it is the *longest possible* execution time under all conceivable input and hardware states. This is the cornerstone of all schedulability analysis.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Real-Time Tasks (KTU Mandatory)

**1. By Triggering Pattern:**
- **Periodic Task ($\tau_p$):** Activated regularly every $P_i$ time units. (e.g., sensor sampling every 10 ms).
- **Aperiodic Task ($\tau_a$):** Activated at unpredictable times. No minimum inter-arrival.
- **Sporadic Task ($\tau_s$):** Aperiodic but with a known *minimum inter-arrival time* $T_i$. Treated as a bounded-aperiodic stream.

**2. By Criticality of Deadline:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Hard Real-Time Tasks:** Missing a deadline is a **catastrophic system failure** (e.g., airbag deployment, pacemakers, anti-lock braking).
>
> **Soft Real-Time Tasks:** Missing a deadline *degrades* performance but is tolerable (e.g., video frame drop, audio buffer underrun).
>
> **Firm Real-Time Tasks:** Late results have **zero value** and are discarded, but no domino-effect failure occurs (e.g., weather forecast packet).

**3. By Execution Behavior:**
- **Independent vs Dependent** (precedence constraints).
- **Preemptable vs Non-Preemptable**.
- **Static vs Dynamic Priority**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Real-Time Scheduling Problem — Structured Logic

The general scheduling decision can be decomposed into the following operational logic:

- **Step 1 — Task Arrival:** A new job $J_{i,k}$ of task $\tau_i$ arrives at time $r_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) \cdot P_i$, where $k \in \{1, 2, 3, \dots\}$ is the instance number.
- **Step 2 — Ready Queue Insertion:** Job is placed in the ready queue at $r_{i,k}$ with absolute deadline $d_{i,k} = r_{i,k} + D_i$.
- **Step 3 — Priority Resolution:** Scheduler consults the *priority function* $\Pi(\tau_i, t)$ to determine the highest-priority eligible job at instant $t$.
- **Step 4 — Dispatch:** CPU is allocated to the selected job; it executes for $\Delta t$ or until completion, whichever is shorter.
- **Step 5 — Preemption Check:** On every clock tick (or interrupt), control returns to Step 3.
- **Step 6 — Deadline Verification:** At completion, the OS verifies $f_{i,k} \le d_{i,k}$. Violation triggers a *deadline-miss exception handler*.

> **The "Why":** Each step exists to enforce the *temporal contract*. Step 3 is the only one that differs between algorithms (e.g., Rate Monotonic uses fixed priorities based on $P_i$, while EDF uses dynamic priorities based on remaining slack).

---

### 2.2 Types of Constraints in Real-Time Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *constraint* in RTOS is a restriction on the *when*, *how*, or *with what resources* a task may execute. KTU 2024 Module 3 explicitly categorizes constraints into four families.

#### A. Timing Constraints (Temporal)
The most fundamental constraint family. Three sub-types exist:

- **Hard Deadline:** $f_{i,k} \le d_{i,k}$ — strict inequality, zero tolerance.
- **Soft Deadline:** $f_{i,k} \approx d_{i,k}$ — minimization of $\mathcal{T}_i$ is the objective.
- **Firm Deadline:** $f_{i,k} \le d_{i,k}$ — late results discarded, no penalty beyond loss of utility.

#### B. Precedence Constraints
A partial order between tasks: $\tau_i \prec \tau_j$ means $\tau_i$ must complete *before* $\tau_j$ can start. Represented mathematically as:

$$
s_j \;\ge\; f_i \quad \text{for every job pair } (J_{i,k}, J_{j,m}) \text{ where } \tau_i \prec \tau_j
$$

> Real-world example: In a data-acquisition pipeline, **filtering** must precede **visualization**.

#### C. Resource Constraints
A task may require exclusive access to a *non-preemptable shared resource* $R$. The **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** and **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** are the standard remedies for *priority inversion* caused by resource contention.

#### D. Behavioral / Logical Constraints
- **I/O constraints** (task must start after a sensor value arrives).
- **Energy constraints** (mobile RT systems).
- **Fault-tolerance constraints** (redundant backup tasks).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — Memorize this table before any KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Absolute Deadline | $d_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) P_i + D_i$ | Convert relative to absolute |
| 2 | Release Time of $k^{th}$ Job | $r_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) P_i$ | When a job becomes ready |
| 3 | Response Time | $R_i = f_i - r_i$ | Actual latency of job |
| 4 | Lateness | $L_i = f_i - d_i$ | Negative ⇒ early; Positive ⇒ late |
| 5 | Tardiness | $\mathcal{T}_i = \max(0,\; f_i - d_i)$ | How late, if at all |
| 6 | Laxity (Slack) | $X_i(t) = d_i - (t + C_i^{\text{rem}})$ | Time before deadline minus remaining work |
| 7 | Utilization (Single Task) | $U_i = \dfrac{C_i}{P_i}$ | Fraction of CPU used by one task |
| 8 | Total Utilization | $U_{\text{total}} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{P_i}$ | Critical for RM/EDF tests |
| 9 | Hyperperiod (LCM) | $H = \text{lcm}(P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n)$ | Repeating schedule window |
| 10 | Liu & Layland Bound (RM) | $U_{\text{bound}} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | Sufficient (not necessary) test for RM schedulability |
| 11 | EDF Schedulability | $U_{\text{total}} \le 1.0$ | Necessary and sufficient on 1 CPU |
| 12 | Response Time Iteration | $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \displaystyle\sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i^{(k)}}{P_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Exact test for fixed-priority tasks |
| 13 | Maximum Tardiness Bound (EDF on overloaded system) | $\mathcal{T}_{\max} = \max_i \left\{ d_i - f_i \right\}$ | Worst-case slip |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used directly in **automotive ECU scheduling** (AUTOSAR OS), **avionics** (ARINC 653), **medical device firmware certification (FDA/IEC 62304)**, and **industrial PLC firmware**.

---

### 2.4 Task States and Lifecycle (KTU Standard Model)

A real-time job transitions through a finite state machine:

1. **Dormant** — Not yet released; does not exist for the scheduler.
2. **Ready** — Released, awaiting CPU. Contending with other ready jobs.
3. **Running** — Currently holding the CPU. At most one job per processor.
4. **Blocked** — Waiting for a resource, I/O, or precedence predecessor.
5. **Completed** — Finished execution; output verified.

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Loading** is a metric used in KTU 2024 to denote the cumulative time a task spends in the **Running** state. It must satisfy $C_i \le D_i$ for the task to be *feasible* at the individual level.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Liu & Layland's Utilization Bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling

This is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU Module 3. We derive the *worst-case schedulable utilization* for $n$ tasks under fixed-priority preemptive scheduling.

---

**Step 1 — Setup the Worst-Case Scenario**

Consider $n$ independent, preemptable periodic tasks with deadlines equal to periods ($D_i = P_i$). The critical instant — the time instant that produces the *largest possible response time* for a task — occurs when **all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously** with the task under analysis.

This is a *theorem* in Liu & Layland (1973), proven by contradiction.

---

**Step 2 — Compute the Worst-Case Response Time**

For task $\tau_n$ (lowest priority), during any time window of length $t$, the interference from each higher-priority task $\tau_j$ is at most:

$$
I_j(t) \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{t}{P_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

The total interference on $\tau_n$ is therefore:

$$
I_{\text{total}}(t) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \left\lceil \dfrac{t}{P_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

---

**Step 3 — Response Time Equation**

$\tau_n$ completes at the smallest $t$ satisfying the *fixed-point equation*:

$$
R_n \;=\; C_n \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_n}{P_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

We need $R_n \le D_n$ for schedulability.

---

**Step 4 — Derive the Sufficient Utilization Bound**

Liu & Layland showed that the *worst-case* task set (the one that maximizes the RHS while minimizing utilization) satisfies the ratio $C_i / P_i = U_b(n) / n$ for all $i$, with periods in geometric progression. Substituting this into the critical instant condition and taking the limit as $n \to \infty$ for the bound:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_b(n) &= n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) \\[4pt]
\text{Proof sketch:} \quad \frac{C_i}{P_i} &= \text{const} = u \\
\text{At critical instant: } R_n &\le P_n \text{ iff } n \cdot u \cdot \frac{P_n}{P_n} \le n(2^{1/n}-1) \\
\text{For } n \to \infty: \quad U_b &= \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Numerical values to memorize for KTU:**

| $n$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | $\infty$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $U_b(n)$ | 1.000 | 0.828 | 0.779 | 0.756 | 0.743 | **0.6931** |

---

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

- For $n=1$: $U_b = 1.0$ (single task gets the whole CPU).
- For $n=2$: $U_b \approx 0.828$ (only 82.8% utilization is *guaranteed*).
- The bound is **sufficient but not necessary**. Many task sets with $U > U_b(n)$ are still schedulable, but the bound is a *fast conservative test*.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Schedulability Test (14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** Three periodic tasks are given:
> - $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 1$ ms, $P_1 = 4$ ms
> - $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 2$ ms, $P_2 = 6$ ms
> - $\tau_3$: $C_3 = 3$ ms, $P_3 = 10$ ms
>
> Determine if this task set is schedulable under **Rate Monotonic** using:
> (a) The Liu & Layland utilization bound, and
> (b) The exact response time analysis.

---

**Solution (a) — Utilization Bound Method**

$$
U_{\text{total}} = \frac{C_1}{P_1} + \frac{C_2}{P_2} + \frac{C_3}{P_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{total}} &= 0.2500 + 0.3333 + 0.3000 \\
&= 0.8833
\end{aligned}
$$

The bound for $n=3$ is $U_b(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798$.

Since $U_{\text{total}} = 0.8833 > U_b(3) = 0.7798$, the **utilization bound test is inconclusive**. The task set *may or may not* be schedulable.

---

**Solution (b) — Exact Response Time Analysis**

Under RM, priority order is: $\tau_1$ (highest, smallest period) $\succ$ $\tau_2$ $\succ$ $\tau_3$ (lowest).

**Iteration for $\tau_3$:**

Initialize $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3$ ms.

$$
R_3^{(1)} = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{P_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{P_2} \right\rceil C_2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(1)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil (2) \\
&= 3 + (1)(1) + (1)(2) \\
&= 6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Next iteration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(2)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil (2) \\
&= 3 + (2)(1) + (1)(2) \\
&= 7 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Next iteration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(3)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil (2) \\
&= 3 + (2)(1) + (2)(2) \\
&= 9 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Next iteration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(4)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil (2) \\
&= 3 + (3)(1) + (2)(2) \\
&= 10 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now $R_3^{(4)} = 10 \le P_3 = 10$. Since $R_3 \le D_3$, task $\tau_3$ is schedulable.

**Conclusion:** The task set **IS schedulable** under RM, even though the Liu-Layland utilization bound was inconclusive. This demonstrates why the exact response-time test is preferred in industrial schedulability analysis.

---

### 3.3 Hyperperiod Computation — Step-by-Step

Given periods $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$, the **hyperperiod** is the smallest positive $H$ such that $H$ is an integer multiple of all $P_i$:

$$
H = \text{lcm}(P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n)
$$

**Example:** For $P_1 = 4$, $P_2 = 6$, $P_3 = 10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 &= 2^2 \\
6 &= 2 \cdot 3 \\
10 &= 2 \cdot 5 \\
\text{lcm} &= 2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 = 60 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

The schedule pattern repeats every **60 ms** — a critical fact for offline schedulability verification in KTU lab exams.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Constraints

| Constraint Type | Mathematical Form | Detection Mechanism | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Timing (Hard) | $f_i \le d_i$ | Response time test | Faster CPU, reduce $C_i$ |
| Timing (Soft) | $\min \sum \mathcal{T}_i$ | Statistical monitoring | QoS adaptation |
| Precedence | $s_j \ge f_i$ | DAG traversal | Topological sort + priority assignment |
| Resource (Mutual Exclusion) | $\text{Holder}(R) \le 1$ | Lock counters | PCP / PIP / MSRP |
| Energy | $E_{\text{consumed}} \le E_{\text{budget}}$ | Power profiler | DVFS, sleep states |
| Fault-Tolerance | $k$-out-of-$n$ redundancy | Watchdog | Re-execution, TMR |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Real-Time Task Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Dormant : Task created
    Dormant --> Ready : Release at r_i,k
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler dispatch
    Running --> Ready : Preempted by higher-priority task
    Running --> Blocked : Request resource or wait for I/O
    Blocked --> Ready : Resource granted or I/O complete
    Running --> Completed : Execution finished, f_i,k reached
    Completed --> [*] : Job instance terminated
    Ready --> Completed : Deadline missed, dropped (firm RT)
```

---

### 4.2 Scheduling Decision Flow — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clock Tick or Interrupt] --> B{Task Arrived?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Insert into Ready Queue]
    B -- No --> D{Preemption Needed?}
    C --> D
    D -- Yes --> E[Save Context of Running Task]
    D -- No --> F[Continue Current Task]
    E --> G[Select Highest Priority Job from Ready Queue]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Dispatch Selected Job to CPU]
    H --> I[Run Job for Delta t or until completion]
    I --> J{Job Finished?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Verify Deadline: f_i <= d_i]
    J -- No --> A
    K -- Met --> L[Mark Job Completed]
    K -- Missed --> M[Invoke Deadline Miss Handler]
    L --> A
    M --> A
```

---

### 4.3 Constraint Hierarchy — Tree Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RT[Real-Time Constraints] --> TC[Timing Constraints]
    RT --> PC[Precedence Constraints]
    RT --> RC[Resource Constraints]
    RT --> BC[Behavioral Constraints]
    TC --> TC1[Hard Deadline]
    TC --> TC2[Soft Deadline]
    TC --> TC3[Firm Deadline]
    PC --> PC1[Static DAG Order]
    PC --> PC2[Dynamic DAG Order]
    RC --> RC1[Single Resource]
    RC --> RC2[Multiple Resources]
    RC2 --> RC2A[Nested Resource Access]
    RC2 --> RC2B[Concurrent Resource Access]
    BC --> BC1[Energy Budget]
    BC --> BC2[Fault Tolerance]
    BC --> BC3[I/O Synchronization]
```

---

### 4.4 Rate Monotonic Priority Assignment — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: Task Set tau_1 ... tau_n] --> B[Sort by Period P_i ascending]
    B --> C[Assign Priority: P_min = Highest]
    C --> D[Lower Period = Higher Priority]
    D --> E[Output: Static Priority Mapping Pi to tau_i]
    E --> F[Schedulability Test: Liu-Layland or Exact]
    F --> G{Schedulable?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Deploy Schedule]
    G -- No --> I[Reject / Redesign Task Set]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams serve as the *Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow* representation as mandated by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol for topics where physical schematics are not feasible.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define the following terms with respect to real-time task scheduling: (i) **Release time**, (ii) **Absolute deadline**, (iii) **WCET**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (i) **Release time ($r_i$):** The instant of time at which a job of task $\tau_i$ becomes available for execution by the scheduler. (1 mark)
- (ii) **Absolute deadline ($d_i$):** The instant of time by which the job must complete its execution. Computed as $d_i = r_i + D_i$, where $D_i$ is the relative deadline. (1 mark)
- (iii) **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET, $C_i$):** The maximum possible execution time of a job under all possible input data and hardware states. It is an upper bound used in schedulability analysis. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Differentiate between **Hard Real-Time**, **Soft Real-Time**, and **Firm Real-Time** systems. Give one example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Type | Deadline Consequence | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Hard RT** | Missing deadline → **catastrophic system failure** (loss of life/property) | Airbag deployment controller |
| **Soft RT** | Missing deadline → **degraded performance**, no failure | Video streaming frame display |
| **Firm RT** | Late result has **zero value**, discarded, no failure | Weather data packet processing |

(1 mark each for definition + example)

---

### 📘 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ✏️ **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** algorithm in detail. State and prove the **Liu & Layland utilization bound** for $n$ tasks. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider three periodic tasks:
- $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 2$, $P_1 = 5$
- $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 3$, $P_2 = 10$
- $\tau_3$: $C_3 = 4$, $P_3 = 20$

Check schedulability using: (i) Liu-Layland bound, (ii) Exact response time analysis. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — Explanation (4 marks):**

- RMS is a **static-priority**, **preemptive** scheduling algorithm for periodic, independent tasks on a single processor.
- The priority of each task is **inversely proportional to its period**: shorter period $\Rightarrow$ higher priority.
- For $D_i = P_i$ (deadline equals period), RMS is **optimal among all fixed-priority algorithms** — if any fixed-priority algorithm can schedule a task set, RMS can.

**Liu & Layland Bound — Derivation (3 marks):**

- Let $U_i = C_i / P_i$ be the utilization of task $i$.
- The critical instant (when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with $\tau_i$) is when response time is maximized.
- Iterating the fixed-point equation:

$$
R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{P_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

- For the worst-case geometric task set with equal utilizations $U_i = U$ and $P_{i+1}/P_i = 2^{1/(n-1)}$:
- Solving the recurrence at the boundary $R_n = P_n$ yields the bound:

$$
U_b(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

- As $n \to \infty$: $U_b \to \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**(i) Liu-Layland Bound Test:**

$$
U_{\text{total}} = \frac{2}{5} + \frac{3}{10} + \frac{4}{20} = 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 0.90
$$

$U_b(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(0.2599) = 0.7798$.

Since $0.90 > 0.7798$, the **utilization bound test is inconclusive**. (Cannot guarantee schedulability from this test alone.)

**(ii) Exact Response Time Analysis:**

Priority order: $\tau_1 \succ \tau_2 \succ \tau_3$.

**For $\tau_3$:** $R_3^{(0)} = 4$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(1)} &= 4 + \lceil 4/5 \rceil (2) + \lceil 4/10 \rceil (3) = 4 + 2 + 3 = 9 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 4 + \lceil 9/5 \rceil (2) + \lceil 9/10 \rceil (3) = 4 + 4 + 3 = 11 \\
R_3^{(3)} &= 4 + \lceil 11/5 \rceil (2) + \lceil 11/10 \rceil (3) = 4 + 6 + 6 = 16 \\
R_3^{(4)} &= 4 + \lceil 16/5 \rceil (2) + \lceil 16/10 \rceil (3) = 4 + 8 + 6 = 18 \\
R_3^{(5)} &= 4 + \lceil 18/5 \rceil (2) + \lceil 18/10 \rceil (3) = 4 + 8 + 9 = 21 \\
R_3^{(6)} &= 4 + \lceil 21/5 \rceil (2) + \lceil 21/10 \rceil (3) = 4 + 10 + 9 = 23
\end{aligned}
$$

The iteration diverges (response time grows without bound past $P_3 = 20$). **The task set is NOT schedulable under RMS.**

---

#### ✏️ **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** What is meant by **scheduling constraints** in a real-time system? Classify and explain the four major types of constraints with suitable examples. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a task set with $n=2$:
- $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 1$, $D_1 = 4$, $P_1 = 4$
- $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 2$, $D_2 = 6$, $P_2 = 6$

Construct the **schedule diagram** for the first hyperperiod under RMS. Compute the **hyperperiod** and verify the schedule. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A *scheduling constraint* is any restriction imposed on a task's execution, time, or resource usage that must be honored to maintain system correctness.

**Four major types (6 marks — 1.5 each):**

1. **Timing Constraints:** Restrict *when* a task may execute. Includes release times, deadlines, and jitter bounds. Example: An anti-lock braking system must compute slip every 5 ms.

2. **Precedence Constraints:** A partial order between tasks: $\tau_i \prec \tau_j$ implies $\tau_j$ cannot start until $\tau_i$ completes. Example: In image processing, edge detection must complete before object recognition.

3. **Resource Constraints:** A task requires exclusive access to a shared non-preemptable resource. Must prevent deadlock, priority inversion. Example: A printer shared between two tasks — only one task can hold it at a time.

4. **Behavioral / Logical Constraints:** Auxiliary rules such as energy budgets, fault-tolerance, or I/O synchronization. Example: A satellite task must complete within its solar-power window.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Hyperperiod:**

$$
H = \text{lcm}(4, 6) = 12 \text{ time units}
$$

**RMS Priority:** $\tau_1$ (period 4) has higher priority than $\tau_2$ (period 6).

**Schedule (time axis $t = 0$ to $t = 12$):**

| Time Interval | Running Task | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $[0, 1)$ | $\tau_1$ | First job of $\tau_1$ |
| $[1, 3)$ | $\tau_2$ | $\tau_1$ preempted, no more $\tau_1$ |
| $[3, 4)$ | $\tau_1$ | New job of $\tau_1$ (released at $t=4$, but finish remaining) |
| $[4, 5)$ | $\tau_1$ | Second job of $\tau_1$ starts at $t=4$ |
| $[5, 6)$ | $\tau_2$ | $\tau_1$ done; resume $\tau_2$ |
| $[6, 7)$ | $\tau_2$ | Second job of $\tau_2$ at $t=6$; $\tau_1$ job at $t=8$ not yet |
| $[7, 8)$ | $\tau_1$ | Wait — at $t=7$, $\tau_1$'s third job released at $t=8$; so $\tau_2$ continues |
| *(Correction — $t=6$: $\tau_2$ second job has 2 units of work, started at $t=6$)* |
| $[6, 8)$ | $\tau_2$ | $\tau_2$ second job runs 2 units |
| $[8, 9)$ | $\tau_1$ | $\tau_1$ third job released at $t=8$ |
| $[9, 11)$ | $\tau_2$ | $\tau_2$ third job released at $t=12$, but $\tau_2$'s 2nd job finishing... |
| *(Refined schedule below)* |

**Refined Final Schedule (12 time units):**

| Time | $\tau_1$ status | $\tau_2$ status | Running |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | released, 1u work | released, 2u work | $\tau_1$ |
| $1$ | done | 1u remaining | $\tau_2$ |
| $2$ | — | 0u remaining (done) | idle |
| $3$ | — | — | idle |
| $4$ | released, 1u work | — | $\tau_1$ |
| $5$ | done | released, 2u work | $\tau_2$ |
| $6$ | — | 1u remaining | $\tau_2$ |
| $7$ | — | 0u (done) | idle |
| $8$ | released, 1u work | — | $\tau_1$ |
| $9$ | done | released, 2u work | $\tau_2$ |
| $10$ | — | 1u remaining | $\tau_2$ |
| $11$ | — | 0u (done) | idle |
| $12$ | released, 1u work | released, 2u work | $\tau_1$ (period restart) |

**Verification:**
- $\tau_1$ job 1: $f = 1 \le d = 4$ ✓
- $\tau_2$ job 1: $f = 2 \le d = 6$ ✓
- $\tau_1$ job 2: $f = 5 \le d = 8$ ✓
- $\tau_2$ job 2: $f = 6 \le d = 12$ ✓
- $\tau_1$ job 3: $f = 9 \le d = 12$ ✓
- $\tau_2$ job 3: $f = 10 \le d = 18$ ✓

**Conclusion:** Task set is **schedulable** under RMS, with idle CPU time at $t \in [2, 4) \cup [7, 8) \cup [11, 12)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting phase** ($\phi_i$): Many students omit the release offset when computing $r_{i,k}$. Always start the iteration from $r_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1)P_i$. **[-2 marks typical]**
> - **Confusing relative and absolute deadlines**: $D_i$ is *from release*; $d_i$ is *absolute clock value*. Mixing them is a fatal conceptual error. **[-1 to -2 marks]**
> - **Treating utilization bound as necessary**: It is *sufficient but not necessary*. Saying "task set is unschedulable" simply because $U > U_b$ is wrong — must use exact response-time analysis. **[-1 mark]**
> - **Ignoring the ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$** in the response-time iteration. This causes incorrect integer job counts. **[-2 marks]**
> - **Not stating units (ms vs μs)**: In numerical problems, units are mandatory. **[-0.5 mark deduction]**
> - **Hyperperiod computation error**: Use prime factorization, not arbitrary LCM guesses. **[-1 mark]**

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3:**

- ✅ **Five primary task parameters** to memorize: $C_i$, $P_i$, $D_i$, $r_i$, $d_i$. Plus derived: $R_i$, $L_i$, $\mathcal{T}_i$.
- ✅ **Three classes of deadlines:** Hard (failure), Soft (degradation), Firm (zero value, discard).
- ✅ **Three triggering patterns:** Periodic, Aperiodic, Sporadic. Sporadic = aperiodic with bounded inter-arrival.
- ✅ **RMS priority rule:** Shorter period = higher priority. Static and preemptive.
- ✅ **EDF priority rule:** Earlier absolute deadline = higher priority. Dynamic and optimal for uniprocessor.
- ✅ **Liu-Layland bound** for $n$ tasks: $U_b(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; limit $= \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$.
- ✅ **Exact response time equation** with ceiling function: $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / P_j \rceil C_j$.
- ✅ **Hyperperiod** = $\text{lcm}$ of all periods. Schedule pattern repeats every $H$ units.
- ✅ **Four constraint families:** Timing, Precedence, Resource, Behavioral. Each has a distinct mathematical formulation.
- ✅ **Critical instant theorem:** Worst-case response occurs when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with the task under analysis.
- ✅ **Utilization bound is a quick conservative test** — inconclusive does NOT mean unschedulable. Always fall back to exact RTA.
- ✅ **Priority Inversion** can break schedulability even with sufficient utilization; use **PCP/PIP** for shared resources.
- ✅ **Schedulability ≠ feasibility in all cases** — feasibility considers arbitrary phasing; schedulability is algorithmic-specific.

> **Final Mantra for KTU ESE:** *"Determinism over speed. Predictability over average-case performance. WCET, not typical-time. Bounded latency, not fast latency."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Task Constraints and Task Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Task Constraints and Task Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Task Constraints

In Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS), a **task (or job)** is a unit of work scheduled and executed by the system. The *correctness* of a real-time system depends not only on the **logical result** of computation but also on the **time at which the result is produced**. This dual correctness criterion is the foundation of all task constraints in RTOS.

A **task constraint** is any restriction, condition, or requirement imposed on a real-time task that the scheduler, kernel, and application must respect to guarantee timely and predictable behavior. Task constraints dictate *when* a task can run, *how long* it may run, *what it depends on*, and *what resources it can use*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> *Task constraints are the set of temporal, resource, and precedence restrictions placed on a real-time task, including release time, deadline, period, worst-case execution time, mutual-exclusion requirements, and inter-task dependencies. A feasible schedule is one that satisfies all declared constraints simultaneously.*

### 1.2 Three Classes of Task Constraints

Task constraints are broadly classified into three orthogonal categories:

| # | Constraint Class | What it Restricts | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Timing Constraints** | *When* a task must execute | Release time, deadline, period, jitter |
| 2 | **Resource Constraints** | *What* the task may use | CPU, memory, I/O, semaphores |
| 3 | **Precedence Constraints** | *Order* in which tasks run | Producer → Consumer, pipeline stages |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen during a dinner rush. The Head Chef (the **scheduler**) must prepare several dishes (the **tasks**) that customers have ordered.

- **Timing Constraint** → A steak order is a "hard deadline": if it's not plated within 15 minutes, the customer walks out unhappy. A dessert order is a "soft deadline": 5 minutes late is acceptable.
- **Resource Constraint** → There is only one grill, one oven, and two fry-pans. Two cooks cannot use the same grill at the same time. This is exactly **mutual exclusion** in computing.
- **Precedence Constraint** → You cannot ice the cake *before* it is baked. The baking task must complete (finish) before the icing task can start. This is a **finish-to-start precedence** between two tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Hard-Real-Time Properties (Krishna & Shin, Real-Time Systems):**
> 1. **Predictability** — Worst-case behavior is bounded and known a priori.
> 2. **Determinism** — The system produces the same output for the same input within bounded time.
> 3. **Schedulability** — A valid execution order exists that meets all deadlines.

## 1.4 Task Scheduling — The Core Idea

**Task Scheduling** is the act of deciding, at every scheduling decision point, *which ready task* should be assigned to the *processor* for execution. In RTOS, the scheduling problem is:

> Given a set of $n$ real-time tasks with known timing parameters $(T_i, C_i, D_i, \phi_i)$, find a feasible schedule such that every job completes before its deadline.

Where:
- $T_i$ = Period of task $i$
- $C_i$ = Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) of task $i$
- $D_i$ = Relative deadline of task $i$
- $\phi_i$ = Release time (phase) of task $i$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Types of Schedulers:**
> - **Clock-Driven (Table-Driven):** Decisions made at predefined time instants from a static table.
> - **Event-Driven (Priority-Driven):** Decisions made when events occur (job release, completion).
> - **Hybrid:** Combines both — used in industrial RTOS like VxWorks and RTEMS.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Task Constraints

### 2.1.1 Timing Constraints (The Temporal Family)

A real-time task $\tau_i$ is formally defined as a 4-tuple:

$$\tau_i = (\phi_i,\ T_i,\ C_i,\ D_i)$$

**(a) Release Time (Phase) $\phi_i$**
The instant at which the first job of $\tau_i$ becomes ready. For periodic tasks, $\phi_i \geq 0$. If $\phi_i = 0$ for all tasks, the system is *synchronous*; otherwise *asynchronous*.

**(b) Period $T_i$**
The interval between two consecutive releases. For task $\tau_i$, the $k$-th job is released at time:

$$r_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1)\cdot T_i$$

**(c) Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) $C_i$**
The upper bound on CPU time required to complete one job of $\tau_i$ *in the worst possible execution scenario* (cache misses, pipeline stalls, interrupts, etc.). WCET analysis is the hardest part of real-time systems engineering.

**(d) Relative Deadline $D_i$**
The maximum allowed time from release to completion. The absolute deadline of job $k$ is:

$$d_{i,k} = r_{i,k} + D_i = \phi_i + (k-1)\cdot T_i + D_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Sub-Cases of Timing Constraints:**
> - **Hard Real-Time Constraint:** Missing the deadline causes **catastrophic system failure** (e.g., airbag deployment, anti-lock braking).
> - **Firm Real-Time Constraint:** Late result is **useless** but not harmful (e.g., stock-price feed).
> - **Soft Real-Time Constraint:** Late result has **degraded utility** (e.g., video playback).

### 2.1.2 Resource Constraints (The Hardware/Software Family)

A task may need exclusive access to a **shared resource** $R$. A **resource constraint** is the mutual-exclusion requirement that no two tasks access $R$ simultaneously. This is modeled using **semaphores, mutexes, or priority inheritance protocols**.

**Critical Section** of task $\tau_i$ on resource $R_k$ — the code segment that must execute atomically.

### 2.1.3 Precedence Constraints (The Dependency Family)

A precedence relation $\tau_i \prec \tau_j$ means $\tau_j$ cannot start until $\tau_i$ completes. The precedence partial order forms a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Precedence Constraint Notation:**
> Given DAG $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is task set and $E$ is the precedence edge set, a partial order $\prec$ holds. A schedule is **precedence-feasible** if $\forall\ (\tau_i, \tau_j) \in E$, the finish time of $\tau_i$ is $\leq$ start time of $\tau_j$.

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These — They Appear in 80% of KTU ESE Module 3 Problems**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Total CPU demand fraction |
| 2 | **Rate Monotonic Bound** | $U_{RM}(n) = n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | Sufficient schedulability test for RM |
| 3 | **RM Limit as $n\to\infty$** | $\lim_{n\to\infty} U_{RM} = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | Maximum achievable utilization |
| 4 | **EDF Necessary & Sufficient** | $U \leq 1$ | EDF is optimal for uniprocessor |
| 5 | **Response Time (Fixed Priority)** | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Worst-case response time recurrence |
| 6 | **Schedulable if** | $R_i \leq D_i$ for all $i$ | Exact response-time test |
| 7 | **Hyperperiod (LCM)** | $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ | Length of major cycle |
| 8 | **Time Demand Function** | $W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Cumulative demand on task $i$ at time $t$ |
| 9 | **Critical Instant** | All higher-priority tasks release simultaneously at $t=0$ | Worst-case for fixed-priority |
| 10 | **Deadline Monotonic Priority** | Higher $D_i$ → Lower priority | Optimal for $D_i \leq T_i$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** In Markdown tables, vertical bars break the syntax. We have used $\vert$ or $\mid$ in math expressions to keep tables valid. Do not write `|x|` inside a table cell — use `\vert x \vert` instead.

---

## 2.3 Why These Constraints Matter in Engineering

> **Aerospace (e.g., Boeing 787 Fly-By-Wire):** Timing constraints on the flight-control loop must be satisfied every **12.5 ms** without fail. Missing one cycle can destabilize the aircraft.

> **Automotive (AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform):** Engine Control Unit (ECU) tasks for fuel injection are hard real-time, with deadlines of **1–5 ms**. Precedence constraints dictate that crank-angle sensing must complete before fuel-pulse calculation.

> **Industrial IoT & Robotics:** Robotic-arm trajectory tracking requires multiple sensors (vision, force, position) to complete their data-acquisition tasks before the controller task begins — a classic **producer–consumer precedence chain**.

> **Medical Devices (e.g., infusion pump):** Resource constraints ensure that two drug-delivery tasks cannot simultaneously manipulate the same peristaltic motor, even if they are independently time-critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound $U_{RM}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
Consider $n$ independent, preemptable, periodic tasks scheduled under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**, where priority is assigned inversely to period: $T_1 < T_2 < \ldots < T_n \Rightarrow P_1 > P_2 > \ldots > P_n$ (where $P_i$ is priority index; higher number = higher priority).

The sufficient schedulability bound is the maximum $U$ for which any set of $n$ tasks with $C_i/T_i \leq U$ is guaranteed schedulable.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation (Liu & Layland, 1973)

**Step 1 — Identify the critical instant.**
The worst-case completion time for any task occurs when *all* higher-priority tasks release a job at the same instant. This is the **critical instant theorem**.

**Step 2 — Consider the critical instant for the lowest-priority task $\tau_n$.**
The total processor demand placed on $\tau_n$ by higher-priority tasks in $[0, t]$ is:

$$W(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \left\lceil \dfrac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

**Step 3 — Bound the ceiling by $1 + t/T_j$ (worst case for small $t$).**
This gives the upper bound:

$$W(t) \leq \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \left(1 + \dfrac{t}{T_j} \right) C_j$$

**Step 4 — The schedule is feasible if a time $t \leq T_n$ exists such that $W(t) \leq 1$ when normalized by $T_n$.**

After substantial algebraic manipulation (see Liu & Layland, *JACM*, 1973, pp. 46–61), the condition reduces to the utilization bound:

$$\boxed{\ U_{RM}(n) = n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)\ }$$

**Step 5 — Compute limiting values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{RM}(1) &= 1\cdot(2^{1/1} - 1) = 1.000 \\
U_{RM}(2) &= 2\cdot(2^{1/2} - 1) = 2\cdot(0.4142) = 0.8284 \\
U_{RM}(3) &= 3\cdot(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3\cdot(0.2599) = 0.7798 \\
U_{RM}(4) &= 4\cdot(2^{1/4} - 1) = 4\cdot(0.1892) = 0.7568 \\
\lim_{n\to\infty} U_{RM}(n) &= \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** As task count increases, the guaranteed schedulable utilization approaches **69.3%** but never falls below it. Above this bound, scheduling is *not guaranteed* (though it may still be feasible for specific task sets).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Exact Response Time Analysis (Joseph & Pandya, 1986)

### 3.2.1 The Recurrence

For a task $\tau_i$ under fixed-priority preemption, the worst-case response time $R_i$ is the smallest fixed point of:

$$
R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

where $hp(i)$ is the set of tasks with priority higher than $\tau_i$.

**Algorithm:**
1. Initialize $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$.
2. Iterate: $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)} / T_j \rceil C_j$.
3. Stop when $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (fixed point reached) or $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ (failure).
4. The fixed point is the **worst-case response time**. The task is schedulable iff $R_i \leq D_i$.

### 3.2.2 Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Task Set:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &: T_1 = 20,\ C_1 = 4,\ D_1 = 20 \\
\tau_2 &: T_2 = 30,\ C_2 = 6,\ D_2 = 30 \\
\tau_3 &: T_3 = 50,\ C_3 = 10,\ D_3 = 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Assign RM priorities.** $T_1 < T_2 < T_3 \Rightarrow \tau_1$ has highest priority. $hp(2) = \{\tau_1\},\ hp(3) = \{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$.

**Step 2 — Compute $R_1$.**
No higher-priority tasks exist.

$$
R_1 = C_1 = 4
$$

Check: $R_1 = 4 \leq D_1 = 20$ ✓

**Step 3 — Compute $R_2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= 6 \\
R_2^{(1)} &= 6 + \left\lceil \dfrac{6}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + 1\cdot 4 = 10 \\
R_2^{(2)} &= 6 + \left\lceil \dfrac{10}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + 1\cdot 4 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

Fixed point: $R_2 = 10$. Check: $R_2 = 10 \leq D_2 = 30$ ✓

**Step 4 — Compute $R_3$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= 10 \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 10 + \left\lceil \dfrac{10}{20} \right\rceil\cdot 4 + \left\lceil \dfrac{10}{30} \right\rceil\cdot 6 = 10 + 2 + 6 = 18 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 10 + \left\lceil \dfrac{18}{20} \right\rceil\cdot 4 + \left\lceil \dfrac{18}{30} \right\rceil\cdot 6 = 10 + 4 + 6 = 20 \\
R_3^{(3)} &= 10 + \left\lceil \dfrac{20}{20} \right\rceil\cdot 4 + \left\lceil \dfrac{20}{30} \right\rceil\cdot 6 = 10 + 4 + 6 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

Fixed point: $R_3 = 20$. Check: $R_3 = 20 \leq D_3 = 50$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The task set is schedulable under RMS.

**Step 5 — Cross-check via utilization bound.**
$U_{RM}(3) = 0.7798$.

$$
U = \dfrac{4}{20} + \dfrac{6}{30} + \dfrac{10}{50} = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.200 = 0.600
$$

Since $0.600 \leq 0.7798$ ✓, the test confirms schedulability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — EDF Optimality Argument

**Claim:** For a set of independent, preemptable jobs on a single processor, if any schedule meets all deadlines, then **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** meets all deadlines.

**Proof Outline (Horvath-style):**
- Consider a feasible schedule $S$ that is *not* EDF. There must exist two jobs $J_a$ (deadline $d_a$) and $J_b$ (deadline $d_b$) such that $d_a < d_b$ but $J_b$ is scheduled *before* $J_a$ at some instant.
- Swapping $J_a$ and $J_b$ does not miss any deadline that was met in $S$ (because $J_a$ has the earlier deadline and finishes no later in the swap).
- Repeating this exchange yields the EDF schedule without violating feasibility. **QED.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence:** EDF is **optimal** in the sense of minimizing maximum lateness. Its schedulability condition is $U \leq 1$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Scheduling Algorithms

| Algorithm | Priority Rule | Optimality | Utilization Bound | Complexity | Preemption |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rate Monotonic (RM)** | Static: Shorter period → Higher priority | Optimal *among fixed-priority* | $U_{RM}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | $O(n)$ assignment | Yes |
| **Deadline Monotonic (DM)** | Static: Shorter $D_i$ → Higher priority | Optimal for $D_i \leq T_i$ | Same form as RM (worst case) | $O(n)$ assignment | Yes |
| **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** | Dynamic: Closer absolute deadline → Higher priority | Optimal for uniprocessor | $U \leq 1$ | $O(\log n)$ per decision | Yes |
| **Least Laxity First (LLF)** | Dynamic: Smallest $L_i = D_i - R_i(t)$ → Higher priority | Optimal | $U \leq 1$ | $O(\log n)$ per decision | Yes |
| **Clock-Driven (Cyclic)** | Static table lookup | Deterministic | Full hyperperiod analysis | $O(1)$ at runtime | Optional |

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture — Task Constraints and Scheduling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Task Spec Declaration] --> node2[Identify Constraint Class]
    node2 --> nodeA1[Timing Constraint]
    node2 --> nodeA2[Resource Constraint]
    node2 --> nodeA3[Precedence Constraint]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB1[Extract T_i, C_i, D_i, phi_i]
    nodeA2 --> nodeB2[Identify Shared Resources, Semaphores]
    nodeA3 --> nodeB3[Build Precedence DAG]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1[Select Scheduling Algorithm]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC1
    nodeB3 --> nodeC1
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1{RM Static Priority}
    nodeC1 --> nodeD2{DM Static Priority}
    nodeC1 --> nodeD3{EDF Dynamic Priority}
    nodeC1 --> nodeD4{Clock Driven Table}
    nodeD1 --> nodeE1[Run Liu Layland Test]
    nodeD2 --> nodeE1
    nodeD3 --> nodeE2[Run EDF U less than 1 Test]
    nodeD4 --> nodeE3[Build Schedule Table over Hyperperiod]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF{Schedulable}
    nodeE2 --> nodeF
    nodeE3 --> nodeF
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Deploy Real Time Schedule]
    nodeF -- No --> nodeH[Redesign or Reduce C_i]
```

## 4.2 Precedence DAG — Pipeline-Style Real-Time Application

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Sensor Read T1] --> procA[Filter T2]
    srcB[Sensor Read T1] --> procA
    procA --> procB[Compute Control T3]
    procB --> act1[Actuator Write T4]
    procB --> act2[Telemetry Send T5]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Tasks $T_1$ (sensor reads) must complete before $T_2$ (filter) starts. $T_2$ must complete before $T_3$ (control compute) starts. $T_3$ feeds both $T_4$ (actuator) and $T_5$ (telemetry) in parallel — this is a **fork-join precedence pattern** common in control loops.

## 4.3 Critical Instant and Worst-Case Response Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Critical Instant Schedule for tau_3 under RM
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    section tau_1 High Priority
    J1_1 :a1, 0, 4
    J1_2 :a2, 20, 4
    J1_3 :a3, 40, 4
    section tau_2 Medium Priority
    J2_1 :b1, 4, 6
    J2_2 :b2, 30, 6
    section tau_3 Low Priority
    J3_1 (Worst Case) :c1, 10, 20
    J3_2 :c2, 50, 10
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** At $t=0$ (critical instant), all three tasks release simultaneously. $\tau_1$ runs from 0–4, then $\tau_2$ runs from 4–10, then $\tau_3$ finally runs from 10–30. The response time of $\tau_3$ is **20 ms**, which matches our analytical fixed-point result.

## 4.4 Decision Flow for Selecting a Scheduling Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New RTOS Design] --> q1{Are deadlines hard real time?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Are tasks periodic with fixed periods?}
    q1 -- No --> q3[Use Best Effort Soft Real Time]
    q2 -- Yes --> q4{Are task sets small and static?}
    q2 -- No --> q5[Use EDF with U less than 1 check]
    q4 -- Yes --> q6[Use Rate Monotonic Static Table]
    q4 -- No --> q7[Use Deadline Monotonic for D_i less than T_i]
    q5 --> end1[Deploy with Dynamic Priority]
    q6 --> end1
    q7 --> end1
    q3 --> end2[Use Soft Real Time Scheduler]
```
