## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*State and explain Jackson's rule for scheduling aperiodic tasks. Under what conditions is it optimal?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Jackson's rule states that on a single processor, a set of $n$ independent, preemptable aperiodic tasks with release times $r_i = 0$ should be scheduled in **non-decreasing order of their absolute deadlines** $d_i$. The rule is optimal because if any schedule meets all deadlines, then the EDF (Earliest-Deadline-First) schedule also meets all deadlines — this is provable by an *exchange argument*: at the earliest point where the optimal schedule differs from EDF, swapping the two jobs cannot worsen any deadline since the EDF job has the earliest deadline. *(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for condition $r_i=0$ & independent, 1 for exchange argument or feasibility check.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 3 Important]**
*What is Horn's algorithm? How does it differ from Jackson's rule?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Horn's algorithm (1974) decides feasibility of unit-execution-time tasks (each $p_i = 1$) with **both** release times $r_i$ **and** deadlines $d_i$. At every tick $t$, it schedules the ready task (with $r_i \le t$) having the smallest deadline $d_i$; idle otherwise. It differs from Jackson's rule in two ways: (i) Horn handles **non-zero release times**, whereas Jackson assumes $r_i = 0$, and (ii) Horn is restricted to **unit-length** tasks, while Jackson allows arbitrary $p_i$. Both are greedy and provably optimal for their respective problem classes. *(3 marks: 1 for unit-time + release time definition, 1 for greedy smallest-d rule, 1 for the two contrasts with Jackson.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule (ESE Module 3):** *"Two full 14-mark questions, one of which carries internal choice between (a) and (b)."*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Jackson + Horn + Feasibility

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 3, Pattern Match]**

**(a)** Consider four independent aperiodic tasks all released at $t=0$:

| Task | $J_1$ | $J_2$ | $J_3$ | $J_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $p_i$ | 2 | 1 | 3 | 1 |
| $d_i$ | 4 | 5 | 9 | 7 |

Apply **Jackson's algorithm**. Is the task set feasible? Show the schedule on a time line. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply **Horn's algorithm** to the following unit-execution-time tasks ($p_i = 1$) and determine if the set is feasible. Construct the schedule table. **(7 marks)**

| Task | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $r_i$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 3 | 4 |
| $d_i$ | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |

---

#### **MODEL ANSWER — Question A**

**Part (a) — Jackson's Algorithm (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Sort tasks by ascending deadline (1 mark):**

$$d_1 = 4 < d_2 = 5 < d_4 = 7 < d_3 = 9 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \sigma = (J_1, J_2, J_4, J_3)$$

**Step 2: Compute cumulative completion times (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{J_1} &= p_1 = 2 \\
C_{J_2} &= 2 + p_2 = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
C_{J_4} &= 3 + p_4 = 3 + 1 = 4 \\
C_{J_3} &= 4 + p_3 = 4 + 3 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Verify $C_{\sigma(k)} \le d_{\sigma(k)}$ for each $k$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{J_1} = 2 \le d_1 = 4 \quad &\checkmark \\
C_{J_2} = 3 \le d_2 = 5 \quad &\checkmark \\
C_{J_4} = 4 \le d_4 = 7 \quad &\checkmark \\
C_{J_3} = 7 \le d_3 = 9 \quad &\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

All four deadlines are satisfied ⇒ **Task set is feasible**.

**Step 4: Time-line schedule (1 mark):**

$$[0,2]:J_1,\ \ [2,3]:J_2,\ \ [3,4]:J_4,\ \ [4,7]:J_3$$

---

**Part (b) — Horn's Algorithm (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Tick-by-tick simulation table (5 marks):**

| $t$ | Ready $R(t)$ | Smallest $d_i$ | Chosen | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\{A\}$ | $d_A=2$ | $A$ | Runs at $t=0$, completes at $t=1$ |
| 1 | $\{B, C\}$ | $d_B=3$ | $B$ | $B$ has smaller deadline than $C$ |
| 2 | $\{C\}$ | $d_C=4$ | $C$ | $C$ only remaining ready task |
| 3 | $\{D\}$ | $d_D=5$ | $D$ | Runs at $t=3$ |
| 4 | $\{E\}$ | $d_E=6$ | $E$ | Runs at $t=4$ |

**Step 2: Verify all completions (1 mark):**

$$C_A=1 \le 2,\ C_B=2 \le 3,\ C_C=3 \le 4,\ C_D=4 \le 5,\ C_E=5 \le 6 \;\;\text{all}\;\checkmark$$

**Step 3: Conclusion (1 mark):**
**Task set is feasible.** Schedule: $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E$ at slots $t=0,1,2,3,4$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)** — Precedence Constraints

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Module 3, Pattern Match]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram how precedence constraints among tasks are modelled using a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**. Define the terms *earliest completion time* $EC_i$ and *latest completion time* $LC_i$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following task set with precedence DAG, compute the **latest start time** $LS_i$ and **earliest start time** $ES_i$ for every task. State whether the system is feasible. **(7 marks)**

| Task | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ | $T_4$ | $T_5$ | $T_6$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $p_i$ | 2 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 4 | 2 |
| $d_i$ | 6 | 9 | 11 | 8 | 15 | 13 |
| Pred | — | $T_1$ | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3, T_4$ | $T_5$ |

---

#### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B**

**Part (a) — DAG Model + Definitions (7 marks)**

A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** $G = (V, E)$ represents the partial order among tasks: each node $v_i \in V$ is a task, and a directed edge $(i, j) \in E$ means *task $i$ must complete before task $j$ begins*. The graph is acyclic because circular dependencies are physically impossible to schedule. **(2 marks for DAG explanation + 1 mark for diagram description)**

Definitions:
- **Earliest Completion Time $EC_i$:** The earliest instant at which task $T_i$ can finish, given its predecessors' earliest completions: $EC_i = ES_i + p_i = p_i + \max_{k \in \text{pred}(i)} EC_k$ (with the convention $\max\emptyset = 0$). **(2 marks)**
- **Latest Completion Time $LC_i$:** The latest instant at which task $T_i$ may finish without violating either its own deadline or any successor's latest start: $LC_i = \min\bigl(d_i, \min_{j \in \text{succ}(i)} LS_j\bigr)$, where $LS_j = LC_j - p_j$. **(2 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify sinks:** $T_6$ is the unique sink. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 2: Backward pass for $LC_i, LS_i$ (3.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_6:&\quad LC_6 = d_6 = 13,\quad LS_6 = 13 - 2 = 11 \\
T_5:&\quad LC_5 = \min(d_5, LS_6) = \min(15, 11) = 11,\quad LS_5 = 11 - 4 = 7 \\
T_4:&\quad LC_4 = d_4 = 8,\quad LS_4 = 8 - 1 = 7 \\
T_3:&\quad LC_3 = \min(d_3, LS_5) = \min(11, 7) = 7,\quad LS_3 = 7 - 2 = 5 \\
T_2:&\quad LC_2 = \min(d_2, LS_4) = \min(9, 7) = 7,\quad LS_2 = 7 - 3 = 4 \\
T_1:&\quad LC_1 = \min(d_1, \min(LS_2, LS_3)) = \min(6, \min(4, 5)) = 4,\quad LS_1 = 4 - 2 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Forward pass for $EC_i, ES_i$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1:&\quad ES_1 = 0,\ EC_1 = 2 \\
T_2:&\quad ES_2 = EC_1 = 2,\ EC_2 = 5 \\
T_3:&\quad ES_3 = EC_1 = 2,\ EC_3 = 4 \\
T_4:&\quad ES_4 = EC_2 = 5,\ EC_4 = 6 \\
T_5:&\quad ES_5 = \max(EC_3, EC_4) = \max(4, 6) = 6,\ EC_5 = 10 \\
T_6:&\quad ES_6 = EC_5 = 10,\ EC_6 = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Feasibility check $LC_i \ge EC_i$ (1 mark):**

| Task | $LC_i$ | $EC_i$ | OK? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | 4 | 2 | ✓ |
| $T_2$ | 7 | 5 | ✓ |
| $T_3$ | 7 | 4 | ✓ |
| $T_4$ | 8 | 6 | ✓ |
| $T_5$ | 11 | 10 | ✓ |
| $T_6$ | 13 | 12 | ✓ |

All six tasks satisfy $LC_i \ge EC_i$. **The system is FEASIBLE.** The critical path length is $EC_6 = 12$, and the latest deadline is $d_6 = 13$, leaving **1 unit of system-level slack**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sorting error in Jackson's rule:** Reversing ascending/descending — Jackson requires **non-decreasing** (smallest first). Wrong order ⇒ wrong conclusion about feasibility. **−1 mark**
> 2. **Skipping the cumulative check:** Only checking the *last* job's completion vs. its deadline is incomplete — you MUST check $C_{\sigma(k)} \le d_{\sigma(k)}$ for **every** $k$. **−2 marks**
> 3. **Confusing Horn with Jackson:** Forgetting that Horn requires $p_i = 1$ and uses real-time tick-by-tick simulation. Students often try to apply Horn to a multi-unit task — that is a different problem (Chetto & Chetto's algorithm). **−2 marks**
> 4. **Forward vs. backward pass confusion:** In the precedence problem, mixing up $EC_i$ (forward from sources) with $LC_i$ (backward from sinks) yields reversed inequalities. **−3 marks**
> 5. **Ignoring the sink's deadline in LC:** Setting $LC_i = \min_{j \in \text{succ}(i)} LS_j$ **without** also taking $\min$ with $d_i$ violates the task's own deadline. **−2 marks**
> 6. **Not stating the theorem name:** KTU expects "by Jackson's rule, EDF is optimal …" — bare calculation without citing the theorem loses a half-mark.
> 7. **Idle-slot blindness:** In Horn's algorithm, if $R(t) = \emptyset$ the processor idles — students sometimes forget to advance $t$ to the next release and declare infeasibility wrongly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Jackson's Rule (1955):** Independent aperiodic tasks with $r_i = 0$ ⇒ schedule by non-decreasing deadline. **EDF is optimal on a uniprocessor.**
- **Horn's Algorithm (1974):** Unit-time tasks ($p_i = 1$) with $r_i, d_i$ given ⇒ at every tick $t$, schedule the **smallest-$d_i$** task among ready ones. Idle if none ready.
- **Both algorithms are greedy** and proven optimal by **exchange arguments**.
- **Precedence constraints** are encoded as a **DAG**; cycle ⇒ impossible.
- **Lawler's method** = (i) backward pass for $LC_i, LS_i$; (ii) forward pass for $EC_i, ES_i$; (iii) check $LC_i \ge EC_i$.
- **Critical Path** $CP = \max_i EC_i$ is the *minimum* possible schedule length on any number of processors; on a uniprocessor, feasibility also needs $LC_i \ge EC_i$.
- **EDF alone is NOT optimal under precedence** — must use modified deadlines $LC_i$.
- **Slack** $s_i = LS_i - ES_i$ — non-negative slack $\Rightarrow$ task $i$ can be delayed without harming feasibility.
- **Necessary (but not sufficient) load condition:** $\sum_i p_i / (d_i - r_i) \le 1$. Always check this first as a sanity gate.
- **Three golden formulas to memorise:**
  $$C_{\sigma(k)} = \sum_{m=1}^{k} p_{\sigma(m)},\quad LC_i = \min\!\Bigl(d_i,\ \min_{j\in\text{succ}(i)} LS_j\Bigr),\quad LS_i = LC_i - p_i$$
- **Mnemonic:** "J**A**ckson schedules by **A**scending **D**eadline"; "**H**orn's tasks are **H**airline-thin (one unit)"; "**P**recedence needs **P**ast and **P**resent (backward + forward)."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LDF and EDF; Periodic Task Scheduling (Basics only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** LDF and EDF; Periodic Task Scheduling (Basics only) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Deadline-Driven Scheduling? In Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS), the **correctness of the system depends not only on the logical result of computation but also on the time at which the results are produced**. Tasks must meet their **deadlines**. To ensure this, the scheduler uses *deadline-driven* algorithms. > [!NOTE] > **Deadline-Driven Scheduling:** A class of scheduling algorithms in which the priority (or the order of execution) of a task is determined primarily by its **deadline**, rather than by its arrival time, period, or computational requirement. Two of the most fundamental deadline-driven algorithms are: 1. **LDF – Latest Deadline First** 2. **EDF – Earliest Deadline First** ### 1.2 LDF – Latest Deadline First (Formal Definition) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Latest Deadline First (LDF):** A *static* priority scheduling algorithm in which the task that has the **latest (furthest) deadline** among all ready tasks is assigned the **highest priority** and is executed first. Priorities are computed and fixed *at design time* (offline). - **Type:** Static / Offline priority assignment - **Optimality:** Optimal under certain preconditions (e.g., when all tasks have zero phasing and identical release times) - **Key Property:** A task with a later absolute deadline is preferred over a task with an earlier absolute deadline. ### 1.3 EDF – Earliest Deadline First (Formal Definition) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Earliest Deadline First (EDF):** A *dynamic* priority scheduling algorithm in which, **at every scheduling decision instant**, the scheduler picks the ready task whose **absolute deadline is the earliest (nearest)**. Priorities are recomputed *at runtime* (online). - **Type:** Dynamic / Online priority assignment - **Optimality:** Proven optimal for **preemptive, uniprocessor, independent** periodic task systems (Liu & Layland, 1973). - **Key Property:** The closer the deadline, the higher the priority. ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Runway Imagine three aircraft are stacked in the holding pattern, all waiting to land on a single runway: - **LDF Analogy:** The air-traffic controller says, *"The aircraft that has the **most fuel left (latest time-pressure) lands first**."* This is the opposite of what is intuitively safe — we always want the plane with the least fuel to land first! - **EDF Analogy:** The controller says, *"The aircraft whose **fuel is about to run out (earliest deadline) lands first**."* This is the natural, safe, optimal behaviour. The intuitive, safety-first choice (EDF) is the one proven to be **mathematically optimal** for uniprocessor real-time systems. ### 1.5 Periodic Task Scheduling – The Basic Model > [!NOTE] > **Periodic Task:** A task $\tau_i$ that is released (becomes ready) repeatedly at fixed regular intervals. It is described by the triple $(C_i, T_i, D_i)$, where: > - $C_i$ = Worst-case **Computation time** (WCET), in time units > - $T_i$ = **Period** between successive releases > - $D_i$ = **Relative deadline** (must be ≤ $T_i$ in implicit-deadline systems) > - **Phase** $\phi_i$ = first release time (often 0 for simplicity) The **utilization** of a periodic task set is: $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$ This single scalar captures the *CPU load* imposed by the workload. ### 1.6 Why These Algorithms Matter | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Aerospace** | Flight control loops, sensor fusion | | **Automotive** | Engine control units (ECUs), anti-lock braking | | **Industrial** | Robotic arm control, PLC systems | | **Medical** | Pacemakers, infusion pumps | | **Telecom** | LTE baseband processing, packet scheduling | In all these domains, **missing a deadline may cost lives or money**, making EDF/LDF analysis critical. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 LDF — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. **Offline Phase:** The system designer enumerates all tasks. Each task is assigned an **absolute deadline** $d_i = r_i + D_i$, where $r_i$ is the release time. 2. **Priority Ordering:** Tasks are sorted in **descending order of $d_i$** (largest deadline → highest priority). 3. **Runtime:** No priority recomputation is needed; the OS simply executes tasks in the precomputed order, applying preemption where a higher-priority (later-deadline) task is released. 4. **Behavior:** LDF is the *dual* of EDF — it privileges tasks with the **furthest** deadlines. > [!IMPORTANT] > LDF is **not optimal** in the general case for periodic real-time tasks. It is a textbook introduction to deadline-based scheduling and is useful when all tasks have **the same release time** (synchronous periodic tasks with phase = 0) and we want to maximize the *latest finishing time* (maximize the schedule horizon). ### 2.2 EDF — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. **At time $t = 0$:** All released tasks are inserted into the *ready queue* with their absolute deadlines $d_i$. 2. **Selection Rule:** The scheduler picks the task with the **minimum** $d_i$. 3. **Preemption:** If a new task arrives with a deadline **earlier** than the currently running task's deadline, the running task is **preempted** immediately. 4. **Completion:** The chosen task executes until it either finishes or is preempted by an even earlier-deadline task. 5. **Recurrence:** The decision is repeated at every job arrival and every job completion. > [!NOTE] > **Optimality Theorem (Liu & Layland, 1973):** *For a set of n independent, preemptable periodic tasks with deadlines equal to their periods, EDF will produce a feasible schedule on a uniprocessor if and only if the total utilization $U \leq 1$.* No other algorithm can do better. ### 2.3 Periodic Task Scheduling — The Two Pillars There are two canonical algorithms for periodic task scheduling: **(a) Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — Static Priority** - Task with the **shorter period** gets the **higher priority**. - Priorities are fixed. - **Sufficient schedulability test:** $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$ - The bound $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ approaches **ln(2) ≈ 0.693** as $n \to \infty$. **(b) Earliest Deadline First (EDF) — Dynamic Priority** - Task with the **earlier deadline** gets the **higher priority**. - **Necessary and sufficient schedulability test:** $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq 1$$ - EDF can schedule **any** feasible workload, while RMS may fail even when $U \leq 1$ if deadlines are arbitrary. ### 2.4 The Three Critical Timing Quantities For a periodic task $\tau_i$ released at time $r_{i,k}$ (the $k$-th release): - **Arrival time (release):** $r_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) \cdot T_i$ - **Absolute deadline:** $d_{i,k} = r_{i,k} + D_i$ - **Worst-case response time:** $R_i$ — the time from release to actual completion The task is said to **meet its deadline** iff $R_i \leq D_i$. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Task utilization (single) | $U_i = C_i / T_i$ | Dimensionless (fraction of CPU) | | 2 | System utilization (set) | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i$ | Sum of all $U_i$ | | 3 | Hyperperiod (LCM period) | $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ | Time units | | 4 | RMS schedulability bound | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Sufficient (not necessary) | | 5 | EDF schedulability bound | $U \leq 1$ | Necessary **and** sufficient | | 6 | Implicit deadline | $D_i = T_i$ | Most common assumption | | 7 | Constrained deadline | $D_i \leq T_i$ | More general | | 8 | Arbitrary deadline | $D_i$ unrelated to $T_i$ | Most general | | 9 | Absolute deadline | $d_{i,k} = r_{i,k} + D_i$ | Time units | | 10 | LDF priority order | $\text{priority} \propto -d_i$ (descending $d_i$) | Static | | 11 | EDF priority order | $\text{priority} \propto d_i^{-1}$ (ascending $d_i$) | Dynamic | | 12 | Maximum CPU load (any algo) | $U \leq 1$ | Hard physical limit | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall #1:** Confusing the direction of LDF vs EDF. **LDF** = *Latest* deadline first (largest $d_i$ goes first). **EDF** = *Earliest* deadline first (smallest $d_i$ goes first). The two are **duals**, not the same algorithm. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Satellite Onboard Computers (OBC):** Use static, table-driven LDF-like schedulers because the task set is fully known at design time and predictability outweighs flexibility. - **Industrial PLCs & Robotics:** Use cyclic executives (a form of static, time-triggered scheduling related to LDF) for deterministic motion control. - **Modern Avionics (ARINC 653, INTEGRITY-178B):** Use a mix — partitions scheduled statically (LDF-style) and tasks within partitions scheduled by EDF or RMS. - **Linux PREEMPT_RT / SCHED_DEADLINE:** Linux kernel's real-time extension implements **EDF + CBS (Constant Bandwidth Server)** for soft real-time workloads. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: RMS Utilization Bound $U_{\text{RMS}} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ **Statement (Liu & Layland, 1973):** For $n$ independent, preemptable periodic tasks scheduled by Rate Monotonic, a sufficient condition for feasibility is: $$U \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$ **Step 1 — Critical Instant Definition.** A *critical instant* for task $\tau_i$ is a time at which the **release of $\tau_i$ coincides with the releases of all higher-priority tasks**. Under RMS, the response time of $\tau_i$ is maximised at the critical instant. **Step 2 — Worst-Case Response Time of $\tau_i$.** At the critical instant, all higher-priority tasks $\tau_j$ (with $T_j < T_i$) are released simultaneously with $\tau_i$ at $t=0$. Within the interval $[0, T_i]$, each $\tau_j$ is released $\lceil T_i / T_j \rceil$ times. The total interference $I_i$ on $\tau_i$ is: $$I_i = \sum_{j : T_j < T_i} C_j \cdot \left\lceil \frac{T_i}{T_j} \right\rceil$$ **Step 3 — Feasibility Condition.** Task $\tau_i$ meets its deadline if the time available for it in $[0, T_i]$ is at least $C_i$: $$C_i + \sum_{j : T_j < T_i} C_j \cdot \left\lceil \frac{T_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \;\leq\; T_i$$ **Step 4 — Worst-Case Ratio (Sketch).** Liu & Layland showed that the worst-case schedulable utilisation occurs when the tasks form a "harmonic-like" sequence and the ratio $T_i/T_j$ approaches the limit where $\lceil T_i / T_j \rceil = T_i / T_j + \epsilon$ with $\epsilon \to 0$. After taking the limit, the bound simplifies to: $$\begin{aligned} U_{\text{RMS}}(n) &= \lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \quad \text{subject to the critical instant constraints} \\ &= n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Asymptotic Limit.** $$\begin{aligned} \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) &= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} - 1}{1/n} \quad \text{(0/0 form)} \\ &= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} \cdot \ln(2) \cdot (-1/n^2)}{-1/n^2} \quad \text{(L'Hôpital)} \\ &= \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \end{aligned}$$ So RMS asymptotically guarantees feasibility only up to **≈ 69.3 %** of the CPU, even though the OS *could* in theory schedule workloads up to 100 %. ### 3.2 Worked Example — Schedulability Test (EDF vs RMS) **Problem.** Consider the periodic task set: | Task | $C_i$ | $T_i$ | $D_i$ | |---|---|---|---| | $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 | | $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 | 6 | | $\tau_3$ | 3 | 8 | 8 | **Step 1 — Compute utilizations.** $$\begin{aligned} U_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250 \\ U_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{6} \approx 0.333 \\ U_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375 \\ U_{\text{total}} &= 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.375 = 0.958 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Apply EDF test.** $$U = 0.958 \;\leq\; 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{EDFSCHEDULABLE} \; ✓$$ **Step 3 — Apply RMS test.** $$\begin{aligned} U_{\text{bound}}(n=3) &= 3 \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) \\ &= 3 \left(1.2599 - 1\right) \\ &= 3 \times 0.2599 \\ &\approx 0.7798 \end{aligned}$$ Since $U = 0.958 > 0.7798$, the **RMS sufficient test fails**. This does *not* mean RMS cannot schedule it — the sufficient test is conservative. We would need a **response-time analysis** to confirm. **Step 4 — Hyperperiod.** $$H = \text{lcm}(4, 6, 8) = 24 \text{ time units}$$ The schedule must be checked only over $[0, 24]$. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — LDF vs EDF vs RMS | Attribute | **LDF** | **EDF** | **RMS** | |---|---|---|---| | Priority basis | Latest deadline | Earliest deadline | Shortest period | | Priority nature | Static (offline) | Dynamic (online) | Static (offline) | | Optimal (uniprocessor)? | No (sub-optimal) | **Yes** | No (sub-optimal) | | Schedulability test | None simple | $U \leq 1$ (N&S) | $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$ (sufficient) | | Runtime overhead | **Low** (no recompute) | High (re-sort ready queue) | **Low** | | Predictability | High (table-driven) | Lower (runtime decisions) | High | | Implementation cost | Simple | Complex scheduler | Simple | | Asymptotic CPU bound | 100 % (only if truly optimal timing) | 100 % | **≈ 69.3 %** | | Best suited for | Synchronous offline schedules | Mixed-period, online systems | Fixed-period hard real-time | | Used in | Table-driven avionics, cyclic executives | Linux SCHED_DEADLINE, VxWorks | VxWorks, FreeRTOS, OSEK | ### 3.4 Trace of an EDF Schedule (Step-by-Step Construction) Using the task set above ($C_1{=}1, T_1{=}4; \; C_2{=}2, T_2{=}6; \; C_3{=}3, T_3{=}8$): | Time | Event | Ready queue (with deadlines) | EDF picks | Runs for | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | $\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3$ released | (1,4), (2,6), (3,8) | $\tau_1$ ($d{=}4$) | [0,1] | | 1 | $\tau_1$ completes | (2,6), (3,8) | $\tau_2$ ($d{=}6$) | [1,2] | | 2 | | (2,6) finishing, (3,8) | $\tau_2$ continues | [2,3] | | 3 | $\tau_2$ completes | (3,8) | $\tau_3$ ($d{=}8$) | [3,4] | | 4 | $\tau_1$ re-released ($d{=}8$) | (3,8), (1,8) | tie — break by tie-break rule | continues $\tau_3$ or $\tau_1$ | | 6 | $\tau_2$ re-released ($d{=}12$) | … | … | … | The detailed trace is what produces the Gantt chart shown in Section 4. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Comparison — LDF vs EDF Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A([New scheduling decision instant t]) --> B{Any task released
or completed?} B -- No --> Z([Idle / Wait]) B -- Yes --> C[Insert / remove from
Ready Queue R] C --> D{Scheduling
policy?} D -- LDF --> E[Sort R by d_i
in DESCENDING order
largest deadline first] D -- EDF --> F[Sort R by d_i
in ASCENDING order
smallest deadline first] E --> G[Dispatch HEAD of R] F --> G[Dispatch HEAD of R] G --> H([CPU executes
chosen task]) H --> A ``` ### 4.2 Periodic Task Lifecycle (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TASK_LIFE["PERIODIC TASK LIFECYCLE (Period T_i, WCET C_i, Deadline D_i)"] R1([Release r_i,k]) --> EX1[Execute for up to C_i
on the CPU] EX1 --> C1([Complete or Preempted]) C1 --> M1{Deadline
d_i,k met?} M1 -- Yes --> SUCC([SUCCESS - Task instance k valid]) M1 -- No --> FAIL([DEADLINE MISS - System failure in hard RT]) SUCC --> WAIT[Wait for next period] WAIT --> R1 end ``` ### 4.3 EDF Gantt Chart — Sample Task Set $C_1{=}1, T_1{=}4;\; C_2{=}2, T_2{=}6;\; C_3{=}3, T_3{=}8$ ```mermaid gantt title EDF Schedule over Hyperperiod H = lcm(4,6,8) = 24 dateFormat X axisFormat %S section Tau1 T1 inst1 (d=4) :a1, 0, 1 T1 inst2 (d=8) :a2, 4, 1 T1 inst3 (d=12) :a3, 8, 1 T1 inst4 (d=16) :a4, 12, 1 T1 inst5 (d=20) :a5, 16, 1 T1 inst6 (d=24) :a6, 20, 1 section Tau2 T2 inst1 (d=6) :b1, 1, 2 T2 inst2 (d=12) :b2, 9, 2 T2 inst3 (d=18) :b3, 17, 2 T2 inst4 (d=24) :b4, 22, 2 section Tau3 T3 inst1 (d=8) :c1, 3, 1 T3 inst1 cont :c1b, 6, 1 T3 inst1 cont2 :c1c, 7, 1 T3 inst2 (d=16) :c2, 11, 1 T3 inst2 cont :c2b, 14, 1 T3 inst2 cont2 :c2c, 15, 1 T3 inst3 (d=24) :c3, 19, 1 T3 inst3 cont :c3b, 23, 1 T3 inst3 cont2 :c3c, 24, 0 ``` > [!NOTE] > The Gantt chart above is one valid EDF schedule for the worked example. Notice how $\tau_3$ is fragmented across multiple intervals — this is the cost of dynamic preemption. ### 4.4 Family Tree of Periodic Scheduling Algorithms ```mermaid flowchart TD RT(["Real-Time Scheduling Algorithms"]) --> STATIC["Static-Priority
(offline priorities)"] RT --> DYNAMIC["Dynamic-Priority
(online priorities)"] STATIC --> RMS["RMS
(Rate Monotonic)"] STATIC --> DMS["DMS
(Deadline Monotonic)"] STATIC --> LDF["LDF
(Latest Deadline First)"] DYNAMIC --> EDF["EDF
(Earliest Deadline First)"] DYNAMIC --> LLF["LLF
(Least Laxity First)"] DYNAMIC --> MUF["MUF
(Maximum Urgency First)"] RMS -. "shorter period -> higher prio" .- NOTE1["Lower bound: ln 2 ~ 0.693"] EDF -. "earlier deadline -> higher prio" .- NOTE2["Tight bound: 1.0"] LDF -. "later deadline -> higher prio" .- NOTE3["Duel of EDF; static"] ``` ### 4.5 EDF Scheduler Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph INTP["Hardware Interrupt Layer"] TICK([Periodic tick / timer interrupt]) ARR([Task arrival / release]) end TICK --> SCED[Scheduler tick handler] ARR --> SCED SCED --> INQ[Update Ready Queue
compute d_i,k = r_i,k + D_i] INQ --> SORT[Re-sort queue by ascending d_i] SORT --> SEL[Select HEAD task with smallest d_i] SEL --> CTX[Perform Context Switch
save old PCB, load new PCB] CTX --> CPU([CPU executes chosen task]) CPU --> TICK ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** Differentiate between **LDF** and **EDF** scheduling algorithms. Mention the type of priority (static / dynamic) for each. **Model Answer:** | Feature | LDF | EDF | |---|---|---| | Full form | Latest Deadline First | Earliest Deadline First | | Priority order | Task with **largest** absolute deadline gets **highest** priority | Task with **smallest** absolute deadline gets **highest** priority | | Priority type | **Static** (assigned at design time) | **Dynamic** (recomputed at every scheduling event) | | Optimality | Sub-optimal in general | **Optimal** for uniprocessor independent tasks | | Runtime cost | Low (table lookup) | Higher (queue resort) | | Use case | Offline table-driven schedules, cyclic executives | Online systems, Linux SCHED_DEADLINE | **(3 marks)** — Award: 1 mark for full form, 1 mark for priority order, 1 mark for static/dynamic + a valid distinguishing point. --- **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** Define **periodic task**. State the parameters used to characterize a periodic task and write the utilization formula. **Model Answer:** > A **periodic task** is a real-time task that is released (becomes ready for execution) repeatedly at fixed, regular intervals called its *period*. A periodic task $\tau_i$ is characterized by the triple $(C_i, T_i, D_i)$ where: - $C_i$ = Worst-case computation time (WCET) - $T_i$ = Period between successive releases - $D_i$ = Relative deadline (often $D_i = T_i$) The **task utilization** and **system utilization** are: $$U_i = \frac{C_i}{T_i}, \qquad U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$ **(3 marks)** — Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark for parameters, 1 mark for utilization formula. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A** (14 Marks) — EDF Analysis **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]** **(a)** [7 marks] Consider three periodic tasks with the following parameters: | Task | $C_i$ | $T_i$ | $D_i$ | |---|---|---|---| | $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 | | $\tau_2$ | 2 | 5 | 5 | | $\tau_3$ | 1 | 10 | 10 | (i) Compute the total utilization $U$ and determine if the task set is schedulable under **EDF**. (ii) Draw the EDF schedule for one hyperperiod and verify all deadlines are met. **(b)** [7 marks] State and prove (or justify) the **optimality theorem of EDF** for a uniprocessor system. State the necessary and sufficient schedulability condition. --- **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a)(i) — Utilization test:** $$\begin{aligned} U_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \\ U_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40 \\ U_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.10 \\ U &= 0.25 + 0.40 + 0.10 = 0.75 \end{aligned}$$ EDF feasibility test: $U \leq 1 \Rightarrow 0.75 \leq 1$ → **SCHEDULABLE ✓** **Part (a)(ii) — Hyperperiod and Gantt trace:** $$H = \text{lcm}(4, 5, 10) = 20 \text{ time units}$$ | Time interval | Running task | Reason (deadlines at that instant) | |---|---|---| | [0, 1] | $\tau_1$ | $d_1=4 < d_2=5 < d_3=10$ | | [1, 3] | $\tau_2$ | $d_2=5 < d_3=10$ | | [3, 4] | $\tau_3$ | $d_3=10$ — runs for the leftover 1 unit | | [4, 5] | $\tau_1$ (new instance, $d=8$) | $d_1{=}8 < d_3{=}10$ | | [5, 6] | $\tau_2$ (new instance, $d=10$) | ties with $d_3{=}10$, choose $\tau_2$ | | [6, 7] | $\tau_3$ (new instance, $d=20$) or continue — say $\tau_3$ | | | [8, 9] | $\tau_1$ (3rd instance, $d=12$) | $d=12$ is earliest among ready tasks | | … | … | (continue similarly up to $t=20$) | All jobs of $\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3$ complete before their deadlines ✓ **Part (b) — EDF Optimality Theorem:** > **Theorem:** *For a set of $n$ independent, preemptable tasks on a single processor, if any algorithm can feasibly schedule the task set, then EDF can also feasibly schedule it.* **Justification (Proof Sketch by Exchange Argument):** 1. Suppose a feasible schedule $S$ exists for the task set. 2. At any **preemption / context-switch** point, $S$ may schedule a task $\tau_a$ whose deadline is *not* the earliest. 3. If we **swap** $\tau_a$ with the task $\tau_b$ that has the *earliest* deadline, both tasks still meet their deadlines (since $\tau_b$ has the tighter deadline and runs first, while $\tau_a$ with a looser deadline can absorb the extra delay). 4. By repeatedly applying this exchange, $S$ can be transformed into the EDF schedule **without losing feasibility**. 5. Therefore, **if $S$ is feasible, EDF is feasible**. By contrapositive: if EDF is *not* feasible, no algorithm is. **Schedulability condition (necessary & sufficient):** $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq 1$$ (Valid for implicit-deadline, independent, preemptable periodic tasks on a uniprocessor.) --- #### **Question B** (14 Marks) — LDF + Periodic Task Basics **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]** **(a)** [7 marks] Define a **periodic task**. Explain the parameters $(C_i, T_i, D_i, \phi_i)$ with suitable units. Compute the system utilization for the following task set and check **Rate Monotonic** schedulability: | Task | $C_i$ | $T_i$ | |---|---|---| | $\tau_1$ | 2 | 5 | | $\tau_2$ | 1 | 10 | | $\tau_3$ | 2 | 15 | **(b)** [7 marks] Explain the **Latest Deadline First (LDF)** algorithm with its priority assignment rule. Give **one real-time example** where LDF-style scheduling is used. --- **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Periodic task definition and parameters:** A **periodic task** $\tau_i$ is a stream of jobs released at regular intervals $T_i$ apart, each needing at most $C_i$ units of CPU and required to complete within $D_i$ time units of its release. | Parameter | Meaning | Typical unit | |---|---|---| | $C_i$ | Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) | ms, µs | | $T_i$ | Period between releases | ms, µs | | $D_i$ | Relative deadline | ms, µs | | $\phi_i$ | Phase (first release offset) | ms, µs | **Utilization computation:** $$\begin{aligned} U_1 &= \frac{2}{5} = 0.400 \\ U_2 &= \frac{1}{10} = 0.100 \\ U_3 &= \frac{2}{15} \approx 0.133 \\ U &= 0.400 + 0.100 + 0.133 = 0.633 \end{aligned}$$ **RMS bound for $n=3$:** $$U_{\text{RMS}}(3) = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) \approx 0.7798$$ Since $0.633 \leq 0.7798$, the task set is **RMS-schedulable** (by the sufficient test) ✓ **Part (b) — LDF algorithm:** > **LDF Rule:** At scheduling time, pick the task with the **largest (latest) absolute deadline** $d_i = \phi_i + k \cdot T_i + D_i$ for its current job $k$. - **Priority type:** Static — assigned offline, stored in a table. - **Scheduler action:** The OS simply dispatches the highest-numbered-priority ready task; no runtime re-sorting is needed. - **Contrast with EDF:** EDF picks the *smallest* $d_i$; LDF picks the *largest* $d_i$. **Real-Time Example — Avionics Mission Computer (Cyclic Executive Style):** In older flight-control computers (e.g., pre-ARINC-653 architectures), the task set is fully known at design time. A *major cycle* (analogous to the hyperperiod) is divided into *minor cycles*. Tasks are statically allocated to minor-cycle slots based on the order of their deadlines. This is essentially an LDF-style table-driven scheduler — predictable, no jitter, easy to certify under DO-178C. Other examples: industrial PLC scan cycles, automotive engine-control unit (ECU) time-triggered schedules, IEC 61131-3 cyclic tasks. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings / Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LDF / EDF / Periodic Scheduling questions:** > 1. **Mixing up LDF and EDF directions.** LDF = *Latest* deadline first (high $d_i$ priority). EDF = *Earliest* deadline first (low $d_i$ priority). A single swapped word can cost full marks on a 7-mark sub-question. > 2. **Using RMS test for EDF or vice versa.** $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$ is for RMS; $U \leq 1$ is the exact test for EDF. Do not cross-apply. > 3. **Forgetting the unit of $U$.** $U$ is dimensionless (a fraction). Do not write "$U = 0.75$ ms" — write "$U = 0.75$ (or 75 %)". > 4. **Ignoring the hyperperiod.** A schedulability trace must be drawn only up to $H = \text{lcm}(T_i)$. Drawing beyond $H$ shows redundancy; drawing less may miss deadline misses. > 5. **Not stating the assumption $D_i = T_i$.** Many textbook problems assume *implicit deadlines*. If not stated, mark may be deducted. > 6. **Confusing "phase" $\phi_i$ with "release jitter".** Phase is the **first** release time; jitter is the **variation** in subsequent releases. They are distinct. > 7. **Claiming RMS is optimal.** RMS is **not** optimal — EDF is. The two are complementary. > 8. **Skipping the "why" in optimality proof.** Examiners award marks for the *exchange argument* reasoning, not just the theorem statement. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist — LDF, EDF & Periodic Task Scheduling Basics** - [ ] A **periodic task** is fully specified by $(C_i, T_i, D_i)$, with phase $\phi_i$ and instance index $k$. - [ ] **Absolute deadline** for the $k$-th instance of $\tau_i$: $d_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) T_i + D_i$. - [ ] **Hyperperiod** $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ — the minimum interval over which the schedule repeats. - [ ] **System utilization** $U = \sum C_i / T_i$ — must be $\leq 1$ for *any* feasible schedule. - [ ] **LDF** = *Latest* Deadline *First* → largest $d_i$ runs first → **static** priority. - [ ] **EDF** = *Earliest* Deadline *First* → smallest $d_i$ runs first → **dynamic** priority. - [ ] **EDF is optimal** for uniprocessor, preemptive, independent, implicit-deadline tasks (Liu & Layland, 1973). - [ ] EDF feasibility: $U \leq 1$ is **necessary and sufficient**. - [ ] RMS feasibility: $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$ is **sufficient only** (asymptotic limit = ln 2 ≈ 0.693). - [ ] RMS priority: shorter $T_i$ → higher priority (static). - [ ] LDF is the **dual** of EDF; not optimal in general periodic settings. - [ ] Real-world uses: LDF → cyclic executives, table-driven avionics; EDF → Linux SCHED_DEADLINE, VxWorks dynamic scheduling, ARINC-653 partitions. - [ ] Exam tip: Always state the *type* of deadline assumption (implicit / constrained / arbitrary) before applying formulas. - [ ] Exam tip: For 7-markers, write the *exchange argument* in your own words — examiners reward understanding, not memorization. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** > **"LDF = Late Departure Flight"** (the flight with the *latest* scheduled departure leaves first — counter-intuitive but rule-based) > **"EDF = Emergency Dispatch First"** (the ambulance with the *earliest* deadline for life-saving is dispatched first — natural and optimal)

topics.title: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), Deadline Monotonic Scheduling (DMS); Real-Time Kernel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), Deadline Monotonic Scheduling (DMS); Real-Time Kernel ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Term):** > Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) is a **static-priority, preemptive scheduling algorithm** designed for periodic real-time tasks, where the priority of a task is assigned *inversely proportional to its period*. The task with the shortest period is assigned the highest priority, and the task with the longest period is assigned the lowest priority. Introduced by Liu and Layland (1973), RMS is mathematically proven to be **optimal** among all fixed-priority algorithms for a particular task model. - **Static-priority** → Priorities are fixed at design time (compile time). - **Preemptive** → A higher-priority task can immediately interrupt a running lower-priority task. - **Optimal** → No other fixed-priority algorithm can schedule a task set that RMS cannot (under its own assumptions). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** RMS is built on four strict assumptions — (1) All tasks are **periodic**, (2) Tasks are **independent** (no shared resources/protocols), (3) Relative deadline $D_i$ equals the period $T_i$, and (4) Worst-case execution time $C_i$ is **constant and known**. ### 1.2 Deadline Monotonic Scheduling (DMS) **Formal Definition:** > Deadline Monotonic Scheduling (DMS) is a **static-priority, preemptive scheduling algorithm** where the priority of a task is assigned *inversely proportional to its relative deadline*. The task with the shortest relative deadline is assigned the highest priority. DMS is the optimal fixed-priority algorithm when **deadlines are less than or equal to periods ($D_i \leq T_i$)** and is the natural generalization of RMS. > [!NOTE] > **RMS is a *special case* of DMS** when $D_i = T_i$ for all tasks. In that case, priority by deadline = priority by period, and the two algorithms are identical. ### 1.3 Real-Time Kernel **Formal Definition:** > A **Real-Time Kernel** is the minimal, deterministic core of a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) that provides essential services — task management, scheduling, inter-task communication, timing, and interrupt handling — under strict, bounded timing constraints. The kernel is responsible for guaranteeing that critical operations complete within their deadlines. ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition | Algorithm | Real-World Analogy | |---|---| | **RMS** | Think of a **hospital emergency triage system** where the *frequency of arrival* determines priority. A patient arriving every 5 minutes (short period) gets a doctor faster than one arriving every hour (long period), because the 5-minute patient cannot afford to wait. | | **DMS** | Imagine a **fire station dispatcher**: each fire has a *deadline* (must be extinguished within X minutes). A fire with a 2-minute deadline gets the closest truck first, regardless of how often fires occur in that district. | | **Real-Time Kernel** | A **conductor of an orchestra**: the conductor doesn't play instruments, but coordinates timing so each musician (task) plays exactly when they should, ensuring the symphony (system) finishes on time. | ### 1.5 Critical Metrics (Bolded) - **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET, $C_i$):** Maximum time the task $i$ needs the CPU under worst-case conditions. - **Period ($T_i$):** The fixed time interval between two successive activations of task $i$. - **Relative Deadline ($D_i$):** The time, measured from activation, by which task $i$ must complete. - **Absolute Deadline:** Release time + Relative Deadline. - **Utilization ($U_i$):** The fraction of CPU time used by task $i$ = $C_i / T_i$. - **CPU Utilization ($U$):** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i$. - **Response Time ($R_i$):** The time from a job's release until it completes execution. - **Hyperperiod (H):** LCM of all task periods — the schedule repeats after this interval. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Aid:** In KTU exam, always remember — for RMS, **S**horter **P**eriod = **H**igher priority (**SPH**), and for DMS, **S**horter **D**eadline = **H**igher priority (**SDH**). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Rate Monotonic Scheduling — Theory of Operation **Core Operating Principle:** RMS works under the *clock-driven* and *priority-driven* hybrid paradigm. At each scheduling decision point (task release or completion), the kernel checks the **highest-priority ready task** and dispatches it. Since priority is a function of period alone, the priority order is computed **once** and never changes. #### Step-by-Step Logic of RMS 1. **Task Set Definition:** Identify all periodic tasks $\{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$ with attributes $(C_i, T_i, D_i)$. 2. **Priority Assignment:** Rank tasks in **ascending order of $T_i$** (shortest period = highest priority). 3. **Preemptive Dispatch:** The kernel always runs the highest-priority *ready* task. 4. **Bound Check:** Compute total utilization $U = \sum C_i / T_i$ and compare against the Liu–Layland bound. 5. **Final Verification:** Use the exact response-time analysis for a definitive answer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why RMS is Optimal (Liu & Layland, 1973):** For a given task set, if *any* fixed-priority schedule exists, then the RMS schedule also exists. No fixed-priority algorithm can outperform RMS under the standard task model. This is a foundational result in real-time systems theory. ### 2.2 Liu & Layland Utilization Bound The **sufficient (but not necessary)** schedulability test for RMS: $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq U_{bound}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ | Number of Tasks $n$ | $U_{bound}(n)$ | Decimal Approximation | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $1 \cdot (2^1 - 1)$ | **1.000** | | 2 | $2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1)$ | **0.828** | | 3 | $3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1)$ | **0.779** | | 4 | $4 \cdot (2^{1/4} - 1)$ | **0.756** | | 5 | $5 \cdot (2^{1/5} - 1)$ | **0.743** | | $\infty$ | $\ln(2)$ | **0.693** | > [!NOTE] > The bound **decreases** with more tasks and asymptotically approaches $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$. For large task sets, RMS guarantees feasibility only up to ~69.3% utilization. ### 2.3 Exact Schedulability Test — Response Time Analysis (Joseph & Pandya, 1986) This is the **necessary and sufficient** test for RMS: $$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$ Where $hp(i)$ is the set of tasks with **higher priority** than task $i$. The task is schedulable **iff** the smallest fixed point satisfies $R_i \leq D_i$. The fixed point is found **iteratively**: $$R_i^{(0)} = C_i, \quad R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$ The iteration **stops** when $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (converged) OR $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ (failed). ### 2.4 Deadline Monotonic Scheduling — Theory of Operation **Operating Principle:** Priorities are assigned in ascending order of **relative deadline** $D_i$, not period. This is critical when: - $D_i < T_i$ (tight deadlines), or - $D_i \leq T_i$ for any task. #### Step-by-Step Logic of DMS 1. **Identify deadlines:** Collect $\{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\}$. 2. **Priority Assignment:** Rank tasks in ascending order of $D_i$ (shortest deadline = highest priority). 3. **Preemptive Dispatch:** Run highest-priority ready task. 4. **Schedulability Test:** Use the same response-time analysis as RMS, with $D_i$ replacing $T_i$ in priority comparisons. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Audsley's Algorithm (1991):** DMS is provably optimal for fixed-priority scheduling when $D_i \leq T_i$. The optimality is proven using *Audsley's algorithm*, which assigns priorities iteratively: at each step, the algorithm tries each remaining task at the current lowest priority until one is found that is schedulable at that level. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value | |:---|:---|:---| | $C_i$ | WCET of task $i$ | Given parameter | | $T_i$ | Period of task $i$ | Given parameter | | $D_i$ | Relative deadline of task $i$ | $\leq T_i$ (general) or $= T_i$ (RMS) | | $U_i$ | Utilization of task $i$ | $C_i \div T_i$ | | $U$ | Total CPU utilization | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \div T_i$ | | $U_{bound}(n)$ | Liu–Layland bound | $n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | | $U_{lim}$ | Asymptotic limit | $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$ | | $H$ | Hyperperiod | $\text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ | | $R_i$ | Worst-case response time | $C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i \div T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$ | | Schedulability (RMS bound) | Sufficient test | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | | Schedulability (exact) | Necessary & sufficient | $R_i \leq D_i$ for all $i$ | | Optimality | RMS | Optimal for $D_i = T_i$ | | Optimality | DMS | Optimal for $D_i \leq T_i$ | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe-Symbol Trap:** When writing $\vert R_i \vert$ or $\vert T_j \vert$ in markdown tables, the `|` breaks the table parser. Always use `\vert R_i \vert` or `\mid$ in LaTeX-rendered math. This is a frequent mark-deduction reason in KTU digital submissions. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace (e.g., NASA CFS, SpaceX Falcon 9):** Flight control loops run at 50–1000 Hz. RMS guarantees that critical flight tasks always preempt navigation displays. - **Automotive (AUTOSAR Classic):** Engine control, ABS, airbag deployment — each is a periodic task with strict deadlines, scheduled by RMS or DMS extensions. - **Industrial PLCs:** Sensor polling and actuator updates are scheduled using static priorities. - **Medical Devices:** Pacemakers, infusion pumps, and ventilators use fixed-priority kernels to meet life-critical deadlines. - **Robotics (ROS 2 with PREEMPT_RT):** Real-time extensions on Linux apply deadline-monotonic policies. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Liu & Layland Utilization Bound (Conceptual Outline) The bound $U_{bound}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ is derived using **critical instant analysis** — identifying the worst-case arrival pattern of higher-priority tasks for a given task $T_i$. The critical instant for task $T_i$ occurs when: - All higher-priority tasks $T_j$ ($j \in hp(i)$) release **simultaneously** with $T_i$, **and** - Each higher-priority task $T_j$ arrives at its maximum rate, contributing $\lceil t / T_j \rceil$ jobs in any interval of length $t$. **Step-by-step mathematical reasoning:** 1. The CPU demand on task $T_i$ in interval $[0, t]$ is: $$W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$ 2. Task $T_i$ is schedulable if $\exists t \in [0, T_i]$ such that $W_i(t) \leq t$. 3. The **worst case** (critical instant) is at $t = T_i$, giving the fixed-point equation for $R_i$. 4. For RMS specifically, Liu & Layland proved that if: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ then $W_i(t) \leq t$ for all $t$ — a *sufficient* condition. > The exact closed form arises from an inductive argument that scales the worst-case response time by a factor of $2^{1/n}$ per priority level. ### 3.2 Full Exhaustive Numerical Problem: Response Time Analysis **Problem Statement:** A system has three periodic tasks scheduled under RMS. Determine whether the task set is schedulable using the **exact response-time analysis**. | Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | $T_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 | | $T_2$ | 2 | 6 | 6 | | $T_3$ | 3 | 10 | 10 | #### Step 1 — Priority Assignment (RMS) Sort by period in ascending order: $$T_1 (T=4) > T_2 (T=6) > T_3 (T=10) \quad \text{(priority rank)}$$ So priority: $T_1$ > $T_2$ > $T_3$. #### Step 2 — Response Time of $T_1$ (highest priority) No higher-priority tasks exist. Therefore: $$R_1 = C_1 = 1 \text{ ms}$$ **Check:** $R_1 = 1 \leq D_1 = 4$ ✓ **Schedulable**. #### Step 3 — Response Time of $T_2$ Higher-priority set $hp(2) = \{T_1\}$. **Iteration 0:** $$R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2 \text{ ms}$$ **Iteration 1:** $$R_2^{(1)} = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1$$ $$R_2^{(1)} = 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3 \text{ ms}$$ **Check convergence:** $R_2^{(1)} = 3 \neq R_2^{(0)} = 2$, continue. **Iteration 2:** $$R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3 \text{ ms}$$ **Converged:** $R_2 = 3$ ms. **Check:** $R_2 = 3 \leq D_2 = 6$ ✓ **Schedulable**. #### Step 4 — Response Time of $T_3$ Higher-priority set $hp(3) = \{T_1, T_2\}$. **Iteration 0:** $$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3 \text{ ms}$$ **Iteration 1:** $$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 6 \text{ ms}$$ **Iteration 2:** $$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$ **Iteration 3:** $$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$ **Wait, recalculation:** $\lceil 7/4 \rceil = 2$, $\lceil 7/6 \rceil = 2$. So $R_3^{(3)} = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9$ ms. Let me redo the iterations carefully: **Iteration 1:** $R_3^{(0)} = 3$ $$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \lceil 3/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 3/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 \text{ ms}$$ **Iteration 2:** $R_3^{(1)} = 6$ $$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \lceil 6/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 6/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$ **Iteration 3:** $R_3^{(2)} = 7$ $$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \lceil 7/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 7/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9 \text{ ms}$$ **Iteration 4:** $R_3^{(3)} = 9$ $$R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \lceil 9/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 9/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 \text{ ms}$$ **Iteration 5:** $R_3^{(4)} = 10$ $$R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \lceil 10/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 10/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 \text{ ms}$$ **Converged:** $R_3 = 10$ ms. **Check:** $R_3 = 10 \leq D_3 = 10$ ✓ **Schedulable**. > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The response time *equals* the deadline exactly for $T_3$, meaning the task is schedulable *only just* — any increase in $C_3$ would cause a deadline miss. #### Step 5 — Final Conclusion All three tasks satisfy $R_i \leq D_i$. The task set is **schedulable under RMS**. ### 3.3 Verification with Liu–Layland Bound (Sufficient Test) $$U = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 = 0.883$$ $$U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.779$$ **Comparison:** $U = 0.883 > U_{bound}(3) = 0.779$ ✗ — the *sufficient* test **fails**! But the *exact* test confirms schedulability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall Lesson:** The Liu–Layland bound is *sufficient* but *not necessary*. Many task sets with $U > U_{bound}$ are still schedulable. The exact test (response-time analysis) is the definitive arbiter. Examiners reward students who use BOTH tests and explain the discrepancy. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: RMS vs DMS | Property | Rate Monotonic (RMS) | Deadline Monotonic (DMS) | |:---|:---|:---| | **Priority basis** | Period $T_i$ | Relative deadline $D_i$ | | **Assumption on $D_i$** | $D_i = T_i$ (implicit) | $D_i \leq T_i$ (general) | | **Optimality scope** | Optimal when $D_i = T_i$ | Optimal for $D_i \leq T_i$ | | **Special case** | Special case of DMS | Generalization of RMS | | **Schedulability test** | Utilization bound + RTA | Response Time Analysis (DMS variant) | | **Algorithm type** | Static-priority | Static-priority | | **Preemption** | Required | Required | | **Practical use** | Simple periodic control | Mixed-deadline systems | | **Computational cost** | $O(n^2)$ per analysis | $O(n^2)$ per analysis | | **Year introduced** | 1973 (Liu & Layland) | 1982 (Leung & Whitehead) | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 RMS Priority Assignment Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Start: Task Set Input] --> readA[Read all tasks: C_i, T_i, D_i] readA --> checkA{All tasks periodic and D_i = T_i?} checkA -->|No| rejectA[Reject: Use DMS or other algorithm] checkA -->|Yes| sortA[Sort tasks ascending by T_i] sortA --> assignA[Assign priorities: rank 1 = shortest T_i = highest priority] assignA --> dispatchA[Kernel: dispatch highest-priority ready task] dispatchA --> preA{Higher-priority task released?} preA -->|Yes| preemptA[Preempt current task immediately] preA -->|No| continueA[Continue current task] preemptA --> dispatchA continueA --> checkDoneA{All jobs complete?} checkDoneA -->|No| dispatchA checkDoneA -->|Yes| endA[Schedule Complete] ``` ### 4.2 DMS Priority Assignment Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startB[Start: Task Set Input] --> readB[Read all tasks: C_i, T_i, D_i] readB --> sortB[Sort tasks ascending by D_i] sortB --> assignB[Assign priorities: rank 1 = shortest D_i = highest priority] assignB --> runB[Kernel: dispatch highest-priority ready task] runB --> rtaB[Apply RTA: R_i less than or equal D_i for all i] rtaB --> testB{All tasks pass RTA?} testB -->|Yes| feasibleB[Task set is FEASIBLE under DMS] testB -->|No| infeasB[Task set is INFEASIBLE — try priority reassignment] infeasB --> sortB ``` ### 4.3 Real-Time Kernel Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph appLayer[Application Layer] app1[Task A: High Priority] app2[Task B: Mid Priority] app3[Task C: Low Priority] end subgraph kernelLayer[Real-Time Kernel Core] scheduler[Scheduler Module: RMS or DMS] dispatcher[Dispatcher Module] timer[Timer and Clock Services] ipc[IPC: Semaphores, Queues, Mailboxes] mem[Memory Manager] interrupt[Interrupt Handler] end subgraph hwLayer[Hardware Layer] cpu[CPU Core] hwTimer[Hardware Timer] ioDev[I/O Devices and Sensors] end app1 --> scheduler app2 --> scheduler app3 --> scheduler scheduler --> dispatcher dispatcher --> cpu timer --> hwTimer timer --> scheduler ipc --> app1 ipc --> app2 ipc --> app3 interrupt --> scheduler mem --> cpu cpu --> ioDev ioDev --> interrupt ``` ### 4.4 Comparison: RMS vs DMS Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD q1{What is the relationship
between D_i and T_i?} q1 -->|D_i equals T_i for all tasks| choiceA[Use RATE MONOTONIC Scheduling] q1 -->|D_i less than T_i for some or all tasks| choiceB[Use DEADLINE MONOTONIC Scheduling] q1 -->|D_i greater than T_i| choiceC[Not standard model — redesign task set] choiceA --> rtaA[Apply Liu-Layland bound or Response Time Analysis] choiceB --> rtaB[Apply Deadline Monotonic RTA] rtaA --> resultA{Schedulable?} rtaB --> resultB{Schedulable?} resultA -->|Yes| goA[Deploy system] resultA -->|No| goB[Reduce WCET or increase deadlines] resultB -->|Yes| goA resultB -->|No| goB ``` ### 4.5 Critical Instant and Worst-Case Response Time Visualization ```mermaid gantt title Critical Instant for Task T3 with Higher-Priority T1 and T2 dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Task T1 (P=4, C=1) T1 Job 1 :a1, 0, 1 T1 Job 2 :a2, 4, 1 T1 Job 3 :a3, 8, 1 section Task T2 (P=6, C=2) T2 Job 1 :b1, 0, 2 T2 Job 2 :b2, 6, 2 section Task T3 (P=10, C=3) T3 Job 1 :c1, 0, 3 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **State and explain the assumptions of the Rate Monotonic Scheduling algorithm. Why is RMS considered optimal among fixed-priority algorithms?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) algorithm assumes: 1. **Periodic Tasks:** All tasks are periodic with known, fixed period $T_i$. 2. **Independence:** Tasks are independent; no shared resources, no precedence constraints, no blocking. 3. **Deadline = Period:** The relative deadline $D_i$ equals the period $T_i$ for every task. 4. **Known WCET:** Worst-case execution time $C_i$ is constant and known a priori. 5. **Static Priorities:** Priorities are fixed at design time and assigned solely by period. 6. **Preemption:** A higher-priority task can immediately preempt a running lower-priority task at any time instant. 7. **Single Processor:** The system runs on a single CPU; no multiprocessor effects. **Optimality:** RMS is optimal among all *fixed-priority* algorithms because, for any task set that is schedulable by some fixed-priority algorithm, RMS will also schedule it successfully. The Liu–Layland theorem (1973) formally proves this result: no fixed-priority assignment can produce a feasible schedule if RMS cannot. > **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for listing 4–5 assumptions; 1 mark for optimality statement with Liu–Layland reference. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) and Deadline Monotonic Scheduling (DMS). When would you prefer DMS over RMS?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | RMS | DMS | |:---|:---|:---| | Priority basis | Period $T_i$ | Relative deadline $D_i$ | | Optimality range | Optimal when $D_i = T_i$ | Optimal when $D_i \leq T_i$ | | Scope | Special case of DMS | Generalization of RMS | **When to prefer DMS over RMS:** DMS is preferred when **at least one task has a relative deadline strictly less than its period** ($D_i < T_i$). In such cases, RMS assigns priority based on period, which may be sub-optimal because the task with the tightest deadline is not guaranteed the highest priority. DMS, by ranking tasks on $D_i$, ensures the most time-constrained task preempts all others. For example, a task with $T = 10$ and $D = 4$ must be assigned higher priority than a task with $T = 8$ and $D = 8$ under DMS, but RMS would assign it lower priority — potentially causing a deadline miss. > **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for tabular difference; 1 mark for the "when to prefer" justification with a real scenario. --- ### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **(a) Derive the Liu–Layland utilization bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling. State the bound for $n = 2$ and $n = 3$ and explain its significance. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define the Problem:** Consider $n$ independent periodic tasks $\{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$ on a single processor under RMS, where task $T_i$ has period $T_i$, WCET $C_i$, and $D_i = T_i$. Let $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i$ be the total CPU utilization. **Step 2 — Critical Instant:** The critical instant of $T_i$ occurs at time $t = 0$ when all higher-priority tasks $T_j$ ($j < i$) are released simultaneously with $T_i$, and continue arriving at their maximum rate. The worst-case response time $R_i$ satisfies: $$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$ **Step 3 — Sufficient Condition via Inductive Argument:** Liu and Layland showed that if: $$U \leq n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$$ then $R_i \leq T_i$ for all tasks. The proof proceeds by induction on $i$: - For $i = 1$: $R_1 = C_1 \leq T_1$ trivially, since $C_1 \leq T_1$. - Inductive step: Assuming $R_k \leq T_k$ for all $k < i$, the worst-case computation demand on $T_i$ in $[0, T_i]$ is bounded, and the inequality reduces to a geometric series whose sum yields the bound $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. **Step 4 — Numerical Values:** $$U_{bound}(2) = 2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) = 2 \cdot (\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 2 \cdot 0.4142 \approx 0.828$$ $$U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 \approx 0.779$$ **Step 5 — Significance:** - The bound is **sufficient** but not necessary: task sets with $U > U_{bound}$ may still be schedulable. - As $n \to \infty$, $U_{bound} \to \ln(2) \approx 0.693$, meaning RMS guarantees feasibility only up to ~69.3% utilization in the worst case. - For practical systems, the bound gives a **quick design-time check**: if $U \leq 0.693$, the system is guaranteed schedulable; otherwise, response-time analysis is needed. > **Mark Allocation:** Step 1 (1 mark), Step 2 (2 marks), Step 3 (2 marks), Step 4 (1 mark), Step 5 (1 mark). --- **(b) A real-time system has four periodic tasks with the following parameters. Check the schedulability using (i) Liu–Layland bound, and (ii) Response Time Analysis. (7 marks)** | Task | $C_i$ | $T_i$ | $D_i$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | $T_1$ | 1 | 5 | 5 | | $T_2$ | 2 | 10 | 10 | | $T_3$ | 2 | 15 | 15 | | $T_4$ | 3 | 20 | 20 | **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Priority Order (RMS):** Sort by period: $T_1 (5) > T_2 (10) > T_3 (15) > T_4 (20)$ (highest to lowest priority). **(i) Liu–Layland Bound Test:** $$U = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{2}{15} + \frac{3}{20} = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.133 + 0.150 = 0.683$$ $$U_{bound}(4) = 4 \cdot (2^{1/4} - 1) = 4 \cdot (1.1892 - 1) = 4 \cdot 0.1892 \approx 0.757$$ **Comparison:** $U = 0.683 \leq 0.757$ ✓ → **Sufficient test passes — task set is schedulable.** **(ii) Response Time Analysis:** **For $T_1$:** $R_1 = C_1 = 1 \leq D_1 = 5$ ✓ **For $T_2$:** $hp(2) = \{T_1\}$ - $R_2^{(0)} = 2$ - $R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \lceil 2/5 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$ - $R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \lceil 3/5 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$ → **Converged: $R_2 = 3 \leq 10$** ✓ **For $T_3$:** $hp(3) = \{T_1, T_2\}$ - $R_3^{(0)} = 2$ - $R_3^{(1)} = 2 + \lceil 2/5 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 2/10 \rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5$ - $R_3^{(2)} = 2 + \lceil 5/5 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 5/10 \rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5$ → **Converged: $R_3 = 5 \leq 15$** ✓ **For $T_4$:** $hp(4) = \{T_1, T_2, T_3\}$ - $R_4^{(0)} = 3$ - $R_4^{(1)} = 3 + \lceil 3/5 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 3/10 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 3/15 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 + 2 = 8$ - $R_4^{(2)} = 3 + \lceil 8/5 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 8/10 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 8/15 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 9$ - $R_4^{(3)} = 3 + \lceil 9/5 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 9/10 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 9/15 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 9$ → **Converged: $R_4 = 9 \leq 20$** ✓ **Final Conclusion:** All tasks satisfy $R_i \leq D_i$. The task set is **schedulable under RMS**, confirmed by both the sufficient bound and the exact test. > **Mark Allocation:** (i) Liu–Layland bound computation (3 marks: 1 mark for $U$, 1 mark for $U_{bound}(4)$, 1 mark for comparison). (ii) RTA iterations (4 marks: 1 mark each for $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$). --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a) Explain the Deadline Monotonic Scheduling algorithm. Under what conditions is DMS preferred over RMS? Prove that DMS is optimal for fixed-priority scheduling using Audsley's algorithm concept. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition of DMS:** Deadline Monotonic Scheduling (DMS) is a static-priority, preemptive scheduling algorithm in which the priority of each periodic task is assigned **inversely proportional to its relative deadline** $D_i$. The task with the shortest relative deadline receives the highest priority. **Conditions for DMS to be preferred over RMS:** 1. **$D_i < T_i$ for some or all tasks:** RMS implicitly assumes $D_i = T_i$. When deadlines are tighter than periods, RMS no longer guarantees optimality. 2. **Mixed deadline systems:** When some tasks have short deadlines and others have long periods, DMS aligns priorities with the actual urgency. 3. **End-to-end deadline tasks:** In pipelined or decomposed tasks, intermediate subtasks may have $D_i < T_i$, requiring DMS. **Optimality Proof Sketch (Audsley's Algorithm):** Audsley (1991) proved DMS optimality for $D_i \leq T_i$ using an iterative priority-assignment algorithm: 1. Sort tasks by *any* order initially. 2. At the current lowest priority slot $k$, try assigning *each unassigned task* to that slot. 3. For each candidate assignment, check schedulability using the response-time equation with all higher-priority tasks fixed. 4. If any task is schedulable at slot $k$, fix it there and move to slot $k-1$. 5. If no task is schedulable at slot $k$, the task set is infeasible. **Proof of optimality:** The algorithm shows that if *any* fixed-priority assignment makes the system schedulable, then a *deadline-ordered* assignment also does. This is because RTA depends on which tasks have *higher* priority, not the *exact* ranking among them. So we can always swap priorities to be in deadline order without losing feasibility. **Conclusion:** DMS dominates RMS in generality. For any task set with $D_i \leq T_i$, if DMS cannot schedule it, no fixed-priority algorithm can. > **Mark Allocation:** Definition (2 marks), Conditions (2 marks), Audsley proof (3 marks). --- **(b) Consider the following task set with $D_i \leq T_i$. Schedule it using DMS and verify schedulability using Response Time Analysis. (7 marks)** | Task | $C_i$ | $T_i$ | $D_i$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | $T_1$ | 1 | 6 | 4 | | $T_2$ | 2 | 8 | 6 | | $T_3$ | 2 | 12 | 8 | | $T_4$ | 3 | 20 | 10 | **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Priority Order (DMS):** Sort by relative deadline in ascending order: - $T_1$ ($D=4$) → Highest priority - $T_2$ ($D=6$) → 2nd priority - $T_3$ ($D=8$) → 3rd priority - $T_4$ ($D=10$) → Lowest priority Note: RMS would have ordered by period, giving $T_1 (6) > T_2 (8) > T_3 (12) > T_4 (20)$ — same order here, but the *test* uses $T_i$ (period) for interference counting and $D_i$ for the deadline check. **Step 2 — Response Time Analysis:** **For $T_1$:** $R_1 = C_1 = 1 \leq D_1 = 4$ ✓ **For $T_2$:** $hp(2) = \{T_1\}$ - $R_2^{(0)} = 2$ - $R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \lceil 2/6 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$ - $R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \lceil 3/6 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$ → **Converged: $R_2 = 3 \leq 6$** ✓ **For $T_3$:** $hp(3) = \{T_1, T_2\}$ - $R_3^{(0)} = 2$ - $R_3^{(1)} = 2 + \lceil 2/6 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 2/8 \rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5$ - $R_3^{(2)} = 2 + \lceil 5/6 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 5/8 \rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5$ → **Converged: $R_3 = 5 \leq 8$** ✓ **For $T_4$:** $hp(4) = \{T_1, T_2, T_3\}$ - $R_4^{(0)} = 3$ - $R_4^{(1)} = 3 + \lceil 3/6 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 3/8 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 3/12 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 + 2 = 8$ - $R_4^{(2)} = 3 + \lceil 8/6 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 8/8 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 8/12 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 9$ - $R_4^{(3)} = 3 + \lceil 9/6 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 9/8 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 9/12 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 + 2 = 11$ - $R_4^{(4)} = 3 + \lceil 11/6 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 11/8 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 11/12 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 + 2 = 11$ **Converged: $R_4 = 11 > D_4 = 10$** ✗ **Final Conclusion:** Task $T_4$ **misses its deadline** under DMS! The task set is **NOT schedulable**. **Remediation Options:** - Reduce $C_4$ (e.g., optimize the code to bring it down to $C_4 = 2$). - Increase $D_4$ (e.g., relax the system requirement). - Apply priority reassignment via Audsley's algorithm to see if a different priority order works. > **Mark Allocation:** Priority order (1 mark), RTA iterations for $T_1, T_2, T_3$ (3 marks), RTA for $T_4$ showing failure (2 marks), Conclusion (1 mark). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Ceiling function errors:** Forgetting to apply $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in RTA. The ceiling is non-negotiable; truncation causes underestimation and is a **frequent 1–2 mark deduction**. > 2. **Confusing RMS and DMS:** Students often assign DMS priorities using period instead of deadline. Always sort by $D_i$ for DMS. > 3. **Bound vs. Exact test confusion:** Treating Liu–Layland bound as *necessary*. It is only sufficient. Always perform RTA for the definitive answer. > 4. **Missing convergence check:** Not verifying that the iteration has stabilized. A single-step check is insufficient; you must iterate until $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$. > 5. **Forgetting $D_i = T_i$ assumption:** RMS requires $D_i = T_i$. Applying RMS to a system with $D_i < T_i$ is technically incorrect — use DMS instead. > 6. **Off-by-one in iteration count:** Showing only one iteration and declaring convergence. This is a mark-losing shortcut in KTU 14-mark problems. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - **RMS Priority Rule:** Sort tasks in **ascending order of period**; shortest period = highest priority. - **DMS Priority Rule:** Sort tasks in **ascending order of relative deadline**; shortest deadline = highest priority. - **RMS is a special case of DMS** when $D_i = T_i$ for all tasks. - **Liu–Layland Bound:** $U_{bound}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; this is **sufficient** but **not necessary**. - **Asymptotic Limit:** $U_{bound} \to \ln(2) \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$. - **Exact Schedulability Test (RTA):** $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$; task is schedulable iff $R_i \leq D_i$. - **RTA Iteration:** Start with $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ and iterate until convergence. - **Optimality:** RMS is optimal for $D_i = T_i$; DMS is optimal for $D_i \leq T_i$ (Audsley's algorithm). - **Real-Time Kernel Services:** Scheduler, Dispatcher, Timer, IPC, Memory Manager, Interrupt Handler. - **Hyperperiod:** $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ — the schedule repeats after this interval. - **Critical Instant:** Worst-case scenario when a task and all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously. - **Examiner's Mantra:** "Always state the assumptions, apply both tests (bound and exact), and show all iterations — partial work = partial marks." > **Final 30-Second Revision Mantra for KTU:** *"RMS = Rate gives priority (short period wins). DMS = Deadline gives priority (short deadline wins). Bound is sufficient. RTA is exact. Kernel = scheduler + dispatcher + services. Deadline met = marks earned."*

topics.title: Structure, State Transition Diagram and Kernel Primitives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structure, State Transition Diagram and Kernel Primitives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 RTOS Architecture & Structure

A **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** is structured as a layered architecture that manages hardware resources while providing deterministic, time-bounded services to real-time applications. The RTOS structure is the **foundational blueprint** defining how hardware, kernel, system services, and application tasks interact with each other.

The standard KTU-defined RTOS architecture consists of the following hierarchical layers (from bottom to top):

| Layer # | Layer Name | Function |
|---------|------------|----------|
| 1 | **Hardware Layer** | CPU, memory, I/O devices, timers, interrupts |
| 2 | **Kernel Layer** | Core scheduling, dispatching, IPC primitives |
| 3 | **System Call Interface** | POSIX/RTOS API for user-level access |
| 4 | **Middleware Layer** | Protocol stacks, file systems, device drivers |
| 5 | **Application Layer** | Real-time tasks and control logic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The *kernel* is the **heart** of the RTOS. It is the only part that runs in **privileged/supervisor mode** and directly interfaces with hardware. Everything above it runs in user mode.

### 1.2 Types of Kernel Structure

| Kernel Type | Description | Real-world Example |
|-------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Monolithic Kernel** | All OS services (scheduler, file system, drivers, network) run in kernel space | Traditional Linux, older VxWorks |
| **Microkernel** | Only essential services (scheduling, IPC) in kernel; rest in user space | QNX, Minix 3 |
| **Hybrid Kernel** | Combination of monolithic and microkernel; performance + modularity | Windows NT |
| **Exokernel** | Minimal abstraction; applications directly manage hardware | MIT Exokernel |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In an RTOS used for embedded control (e.g., **FreeRTOS**, **VxWorks**), a **microkernel** or **nanokernel** design is preferred because it offers **deterministic interrupt latency** and a **small memory footprint** (typically **< 10 KB**).

### 1.3 State Transition Diagram — Definition

A **State Transition Diagram (STD)** is a **finite state machine (FSM)** representation that defines all the legal **states** a real-time task can occupy and the **events/transitions** that move it from one state to another. The KTU 2024 scheme mandates that students must be able to **draw, label, and explain** every transition arrow in this diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Syllabus Mandate):** "The State Transition Diagram of a real-time task specifies the dynamic life cycle of a task as it competes for the processor under the control of the RTOS kernel."

### 1.4 Kernel Primitives — Definition

**Kernel primitives** are the **atomic, indivisible, kernel-level operations** (also called *system calls* or *kernel services*) that an RTOS provides for task management, inter-task communication, synchronization, and resource allocation. They are called "primitives" because they cannot be **interrupted** by other tasks of equal or lower priority once execution begins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room (ER):**
> Imagine a hospital ER as the **RTOS Kernel**.
> - **Patients** arriving continuously = **Tasks/Processes** entering the system.
> - The **triage nurse** who decides who gets a bed = the **Scheduler**.
> - A patient in the **Waiting Room** = task in **Ready State**.
> - A patient currently **on the operating table** = task in **Running State**.
> - A patient moved to **Recovery** (waiting for test results) = task in **Blocked State**.
> - A patient **discharged** = task in **Terminated State**.
> - **Medical instruments, IV drips, ventilators** that the patient must wait for = **Shared Resources / Kernel Primitives (semaphores, mutexes, message queues)**.
>
> Just as the ER has strict, atomic rules (no one cuts in line once a doctor is with a patient), kernel primitives execute **atomically** without preemption.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics in RTOS (KTU Convention)

- **Interrupt Latency (τ_il):** Time from interrupt assertion to first instruction of ISR. Typical value: **1–10 μs**.
- **Context Switch Time (τ_cs):** Time to save old task context and load new task context. Typical value: **< 5 μs**.
- **Deadline:** The absolute time by which a task must complete execution.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse **Interrupt Latency** with **Context Switch Time**. They are **distinct** metrics. Interrupt latency is purely hardware + ISR entry; context switch involves saving/restoring task state.
