# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Deep-Dive

### 2.1.1 pSOS Architecture (Layered, Modular)

pSOS follows a strict **layered + client-server** architecture. The kernel (`pSOS+`) is the heart; everything else is an *optional* server that can be loaded/unloaded at runtime.

**Step-by-step architectural logic:**

1. **Hardware layer (BSP):** Board Support Package — pSOS is *highly portable* across Motorola 68k, PowerPC, ARM, MIPS, x86.
2. **Kernel (pSOS+):** Provides tasks, queues, semaphores, events, memory partitions, time management.
3. **System services (optional, dynamically loaded):**
   - `pNA+` → Networking (TCP/IP stack)
   - `pHILE+` → File system manager
   - `pREPC+` → Distributed message-passing RPC
4. **User tasks:** Application processes with priorities 0–255 (or 1–255 depending on port).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pSOS matters for KTU:** *It is the textbook example of a **micro-kernel with rich middleware** — frequently asked as "Explain the architecture of pSOS" for 7-mark questions.*

### 2.1.2 VRTX Architecture (Minimal Executive)

VRTX is essentially **just a kernel** — no built-in file system, no built-in networking. The philosophy is *"do one thing (scheduling + IPC) extremely well."*

**Step-by-step design philosophy:**

1. **VRTX-core:** Preemptive scheduler, semaphores, mailboxes, queues, partitions.
2. **Optional add-ons (paid separately):** I/O subsystem, file system, network.
3. **Variants:**
   - `VRTX32` → 32-bit version (the mainstream one).
   - `VRTXmc` → microcontroller version (8/16-bit MCUs).
   - `VRTXsa` → *Scalable* version with configurable kernel footprint.

### 2.1.3 RTLinux Architecture (Dual-Kernel / Interrupt Virtualization)

This is the most architecturally novel of the three.

**Step-by-step operating principle:**

1. **Linux is treated as the *idle/lowest-priority* task.**
2. A small **RT micro-kernel** intercepts all hardware interrupts before Linux sees them.
3. The real-time micro-kernel schedules **RT tasks** with the highest priorities.
4. When no RT task is runnable, the micro-kernel *surrenders* the CPU to Linux.
5. Linux runs unmodified — it never knows the micro-kernel exists (apart from a small shim).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Innovation of RTLinux:** *By **virtualizing the interrupt controller**, the micro-kernel can preempt Linux at *any* point in kernel code, eliminating the famous **Linux non-preemptible kernel section** problem that otherwise makes stock Linux unsuited to hard real-time.*

---

## 2.2 Scheduling Policy Comparison

| Feature | pSOS | VRTX | RTLinux |
|---------|------|------|---------|
| **Default scheduler** | Fixed preemptive priority (255 levels) | Fixed preemptive priority (256 levels) | Fixed preemptive priority (1000 levels in RTAI, 0–99 in PREEMPT_RT) |
| **Priority inheritance** | **Yes (built-in PIP — Priority Inheritance Protocol)** | **Yes (since VRTX32 v3.0)** | **No (manual mutex protocol)** — uses PI mutexes from PREEMPT_RT patch |
| **Round-robin option** | Yes (configurable time slice) | Yes (optional time slicing) | No (POSIX SCHED_RR) |
| **Earliest Deadline First** | Not native (user-implementable) | Not native | Not native (research add-ons) |
| **Tick rate** | Configurable, default **60 Hz / 100 Hz** | Configurable, default **50 Hz / 60 Hz** | Programmable, often **1 kHz – 10 kHz** |

---

## 2.3 IPC Mechanism Comparison

| IPC Primitive | pSOS | VRTX | RTLinux |
|---------------|------|------|---------|
| **Semaphores (counting/binary)** | ✅ `sm_create` | ✅ `sc_creat` (SCB) | ✅ POSIX semaphores |
| **Mutex w/ priority inheritance** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (PREEMPT_RT) |
| **Message queues** | ✅ `q_create` | ✅ Mailboxes (`mb_create`) | ✅ POSIX mqueues |
| **Events (flag-based)** | ✅ `ev_receive` (32-bit flags) | ❌ | ✅ (eventfd / select) |
| **Pipes / FIFOs** | ❌ (use pHILE+) | ❌ | ✅ standard Linux |
| **Sockets** | ✅ via pNA+ | ✅ (optional add-on) | ✅ standard Linux |
| **Shared memory** | ✅ via memory partition | ✅ | ✅ mmap / /dev/shm |

---

## 2.4 Memory Model Comparison

| Memory Feature | pSOS | VRTX | RTLinux |
|----------------|------|------|---------|
| **Static memory partition** | ✅ `pt_create` | ✅ Partitions (`pt_creat`) | ❌ (use slab allocator) |
| **Dynamic heap** | ✅ (variable-length) | ❌ (optional `VRTXmem`) | ✅ kmalloc / vmalloc |
| **Virtual memory** | ❌ (no MMU required) | ❌ | ✅ (Linux handles it) |
| **Memory protection (MMU)** | Optional (some ports) | Optional (VRTXsa) | ✅ via Linux MMU |
| **Stack per task** | Configurable (typical **1–4 KB**) | Configurable (typical **512 B – 2 KB**) | Linux default 8 MB, RT tasks custom |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are *directly applicable* to Part A/Part B questions on these three RTOSs. Memorize them.

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|----------------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $U_{b} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Liu & Layland **utilization bound** for Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) on $n$ tasks | KTU Module 2, often re-used for context |
| 2 | $R_{i} = C_{i} + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_{i}}{T_{j}} \right\rceil \cdot C_{j}$ | **Worst-case response time** of task $i$ under fixed priority (used in pSOS/VRTX schedulability analysis) | Module 4 (pSOS/VRTX deadlines) |
| 3 | $R_{i} \leq D_{i}$ | **Schedulability test:** response must be ≤ deadline | Always required for design |
| 4 | $B_{PI} = (m-1) \cdot C_{CS}$ | **Priority Inversion bounded time** with $m$ lower-priority tasks locking the resource for max critical section $C_{CS}$ | Module 3 + 4 (pSOS PIP) |
| 5 | $J_{max} = \text{largest time between interrupt and handler execution}$ | **Interrupt latency** (VRTX: 3–5 µs, pSOS: 5–10 µs, RTLinux: 1–5 µs) | Hardware-aware design |
| 6 | $T_{CS} = 2 \cdot T_{ctx} + n_{syscall} \cdot T_{syscall}$ | **Context-switch + system call overhead** (rough estimator) | Performance trade-off question |
| 7 | $U_{LL} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_{i}}{T_{i}}$ | **System load** (must be $\vert \leq U_{b} \vert$ for RMS, $\vert \leq 1 \vert$ for EDF) | Always required for KTU analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** *Do NOT confuse the **Liu–Layland bound** $U_{b} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ with the **harmonic bound** $U_{b}^{h} = n \cdot 2 - 1$. They are different. The first applies to arbitrary periods; the second applies only to **harmonic task sets** where every period is an integer multiple of the smallest.*

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **pSOS** powered telecom infrastructure (e.g., early Nortel switches, Cisco 7500 routers) and is *still* deployed in legacy avionics where certification costs of switching outweigh modernization benefits.
- **VRTX** dominated the 8/16-bit MCU market in the 1990s — medical infusion pumps, automotive ECUs, industrial PLCs.
- **RTLinux** powers research platforms, NASA wind-tunnel data acquisition, robotics, and the stock trading platforms that demand sub-millisecond determinism alongside rich Linux GUI/networking.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Example 1 — Worst-Case Response Time Calculation (pSOS / VRTX Context)

This is the **single most important analytical calculation** in KTU RTOS papers. We will derive it from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

You are given the following task set scheduled by a fixed-priority preemptive scheduler (the model used by pSOS and VRTX). Determine whether the system is **schedulable**.

| Task | Period $T_i$ | Worst-case execution $C_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ | Priority |
|------|--------------|----------------------------|----------------|----------|
| $\tau_1$ | 4 ms | 1 ms | 4 ms | High |
| $\tau_2$ | 6 ms | 2 ms | 6 ms | Medium |
| $\tau_3$ | 10 ms | 2 ms | 10 ms | Low |

### 3.1.2 Derivation — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Write the response-time recurrence for $\tau_1$ (highest priority):

$$
R_{1} = C_{1} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

> Reason: No higher-priority tasks exist, so $\tau_1$ is never preempted.

**Step 2:** Response time for $\tau_2$ — must include preemption from $\tau_1$:

$$
R_{2}^{(0)} = C_{2} = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_{2}^{(1)} = C_{2} + \left\lceil \frac{R_{2}^{(0)}}{T_{1}} \right\rceil \cdot C_{1} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + (1) \cdot 1 = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_{2}^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + (1) \cdot 1 = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

> Fixed-point reached: $R_{2} = 3$ ms $\leq D_{2} = 6$ ms ✅

**Step 3:** Response time for $\tau_3$ — must include preemption from $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$:

$$
R_{3}^{(0)} = C_{3} = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_{3}^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_{3}^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_{3}^{(3)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

> Fixed-point reached: $R_{3} = 6$ ms $\leq D_{3} = 10$ ms ✅

**Step 4:** Conclusion — the task set is **schedulable** under fixed-priority preemptive scheduling (as implemented by pSOS / VRTX).

### 3.1.3 Verification via Liu–Layland Bound

$$
U_{LL} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{2}{10} = 0.2500 + 0.3333 + 0.2000 = 0.7833
$$

$$
U_{b}(n=3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $U_{LL} = 0.7833 > U_b = 0.7798$ — the Liu–Layland **necessary** test FAILS, but the **exact response-time test** still succeeds! This is the classic case where the Liu–Layland bound is *pessimistic* — a favourite 14-mark question in KTU exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Example 2 — Bounded Priority Inversion in pSOS PIP

### 3.2.1 Scenario

Three tasks $\tau_H$ (high), $\tau_M$ (medium), $\tau_L$ (low) share a resource $R$ protected by a mutex.

- $\tau_L$ starts first, locks $R$, executes critical section ($C_{CS} = 1$ ms).
- $\tau_H$ becomes ready and preempts $\tau_L$.
- $\tau_H$ tries to lock $R$ → blocks. pSOS raises $\tau_L$ to $\tau_H$'s priority.
- $\tau_M$ arrives but **cannot preempt** $\tau_L$ (now at high priority) until $\tau_L$ releases $R$.
- $\tau_L$ finishes critical section, releases $R$, drops back to its original priority.
- $\tau_H$ acquires $R$, executes, completes.
- $\tau_M$ now finally runs.

### 3.2.2 Mathematical Bound

Without PIP, the inversion time can be up to $\sum C_M + C_{CS}$ (unbounded).

With PIP (as implemented in pSOS), inversion is bounded:

$$
B_{PI} = (m-1) \cdot C_{CS}
$$

where $m$ = number of medium-priority tasks that could arrive.

For $m = 5$ and $C_{CS} = 1$ ms:

$$
B_{PI} = (5-1) \cdot 1 = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

> This bound is **independent** of medium-priority task computation time — a critical engineering insight.

---

## 3.3 Derivative Example 3 — RTLinux Interrupt Latency Reduction

### 3.3.1 Standard Linux Latency (Why RTLinux is Needed)

Standard Linux can disable preemption for **up to hundreds of microseconds** in critical sections. Worst-case interrupt latency is approximately:

$$
J_{max}^{Linux} \approx T_{big-lock} + T_{scheduler} + T_{IPI}
$$

A typical empirical value is $J_{max}^{Linux} \approx 100$–$1000$ µs.

### 3.3.2 RTLinux Micro-Kernel Latency

By *virtualizing the interrupt descriptor table* (IDT), the micro-kernel dispatches the RT handler in:

$$
J_{max}^{RTL} = T_{microIRQ} + T_{microSched} \approx 1\text{–}5 \; \mu s
$$

The improvement factor is:

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{J_{max}^{Linux}}{J_{max}^{RTL}} \approx \frac{500 \; \mu s}{3 \; \mu s} \approx 167 \times
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 14-Mark Tip:** *When asked "Why was RTLinux needed even though Linux 2.6+ is preemptible?" the answer must emphasize: (1) mainline PREEMPT_RT is **soft real-time** at best, (2) original Linux had **non-preemptible sections** in drivers/filesystems, and (3) RTLinux's *micro-kernel* approach gives **provable** hard-real-time guarantees.*

---

| Feature Axis | pSOS | VRTX | RTLinux |

| **Kernel size (typical)** | 20–50 KB | 8–20 KB | < 100 KB (μK) + Linux |
| **Interrupt latency** | 5–10 µs | 3–5 µs | 1–5 µs |
| **Priority levels** | 255 | 256 | 1000 (configurable) |
| **Priority inheritance** | ✅ Native | ✅ Native | ⚠️ Via PREEMPT_RT patch |
| **File system** | pHILE+ (built-in) | Optional | Full Linux FS |
| **Network stack** | pNA+ (built-in) | Optional add-on | Full Linux TCP/IP |

| **POSIX compliance** | Partial | Partial | ✅ Full (Linux is POSIX) |

| **Typical application** | Telecom, avionics, networking | MCU / industrial / medical | Robotics, research, finance |

flowchart LR

    pD1 -->|q_receive| appD2[Application Task 2]

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram 5 — Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) Flow in pSOS

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TaskLowRuns
    TaskLowRuns --> TaskHighPreempts: Task_H Arrives Higher Priority
    TaskHighPreempts --> TaskHighBlocks: Task_H Tries to Lock Resource Held by Task_L
    TaskHighBlocks --> TaskLInherited: pSOS Raises Task_L to Task_H Priority
    TaskLInherited --> TaskMediumBlocked: Task_M Cannot Preempt Task_L Now
    TaskMediumBlocked --> TaskLReleases: Task_L Exits Critical Section and Releases Lock
    TaskLReleases --> TaskHRuns: Task_H Acquires Lock and Executes
    TaskHRuns --> TaskMRuns: Task_M Finally Gets CPU
    TaskMRuns --> TaskLowResumes: Task_L Returns to Original Priority
    TaskLowResumes --> [*]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Set A]`
**Differentiate between pSOS and VRTX in terms of architecture and middleware support.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | pSOS | VRTX |
|-----------|------|------|
| Architecture | Micro-kernel with *optional, dynamically-loadable middleware* | *Minimal executive* — kernel only, optional paid add-ons |
| Built-in middleware | Yes — pNA+ (network), pHILE+ (file system), pREPC+ (RPC) | No — must buy I/O, FS, network separately |
| Footprint | Larger (20–50 KB) | Tiny (8–20 KB) |
| Design philosophy | *Integrated office* | *Lean surgical team* |

> pSOS offers **integrated middleware**; VRTX offers a **minimal core** and is suited to resource-constrained MCUs. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, Set B]`
**Explain the dual-kernel architecture of RTLinux.**

**Model Answer:**

In RTLinux, a *tiny real-time micro-kernel* runs directly on the hardware, with **Linux treated as the lowest-priority background task**. The micro-kernel **virtualizes the interrupt controller**, so real-time interrupts are dispatched *immediately*, and Linux is preempted whenever an RT task becomes runnable. This achieves *hard real-time* (1–5 µs latency) while still allowing access to the full Linux user-space ecosystem (GUI, networking, filesystems). *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the *layered architecture of pSOS* with a neat block diagram. List its key middleware components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the *scheduling and IPC mechanisms* available in pSOS. How does pSOS handle **priority inversion**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — pSOS Architecture (7 marks)

**1. Hardware & BSP layer (1 mark):**
The Board Support Package (BSP) abstracts CPU, timers, serial ports, and interrupt controller. pSOS supports Motorola 68k, PowerPC, ARM, MIPS, x86, etc.

**2. pSOS+ Kernel layer (2 marks):**
The kernel is *preemptive* and *priority-based* (255 levels). It provides:
- **Task management** (`t_create`, `t_start`, `t_delete`)
- **Memory management** — both partitions (static) and variable-length heaps
- **Time services** — `tm_set`, `tm_tick`
- **IPC primitives** — queues, semaphores, events

**3. Middleware layer (2 marks):**
| Middleware | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| `pNA+` | Networking — TCP/IP, UDP, raw sockets |
| `pHILE+` | File system manager — FAT, NFS, Flash |
| `pREPC+` | Distributed RPC across nodes |

**4. User layer (1 mark):**
Application tasks with priorities 0–255, communicate via the IPC primitives.

**5. Neat block diagram (1 mark):** as shown in SECTION 4, Diagram 4.1.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Scheduling, IPC & Priority Inversion (7 marks)

**Scheduling (2 marks):**
- Fixed preemptive priority (default).
- Optional time-sliced round-robin within a priority level.
- Static priority assignment; no native EDF.
- 255 priority levels → fine-grained control.

**IPC Mechanisms (2 marks):**
- **Queues** — variable-length messages (`q_create`, `q_send`, `q_receive`).
- **Semaphores** — counting/binary (`sm_create`, `sm_p`, `sm_v`).
- **Events** — 32-bit flag groups (`ev_receive`).
- **Memory regions** — for shared data (`pt_create`).
- **Network sockets** — via pNA+.

**Priority Inversion Handling (3 marks):**
pSOS implements the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**. When a high-priority task $H$ blocks on a resource held by a low-priority task $L$, the kernel **temporarily raises** $L$'s priority to that of $H$. This prevents *medium-priority tasks* from indefinitely delaying $H$ by preempting $L$. The inversion time is bounded by:

$$
B_{PI} = (m-1) \cdot C_{CS}
$$

> pSOS also supports the **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** in some ports, which prevents deadlocks and gives even tighter bounds.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]` *(Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Describe the **architectural features of VRTX**, including its variants (VRTX32, VRTXmc, VRTXsa). Compare VRTX with pSOS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **dual-kernel architecture of RTLinux**. Why is it needed despite Linux 2.6+ being preemptible? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — VRTX Architecture & Comparison (7 marks)

**VRTX Architecture (4 marks):**

- **Core kernel** — smallest part, provides:
  - **Task management:** `t_creat`, `t_start`, `t_delete` (up to 256 tasks).
  - **IPC:** Semaphores (`sc_creat`), Mailboxes (`mb_creat`), Queues (`q_creat`).
  - **Memory:** Fixed-size partitions (`pt_creat`).
  - **Time:** `t_creat` for timeouts, `t_tick` for time-slice ticks.

- **Variants (2 marks):**

  | Variant | Target | Features |
  |---------|--------|----------|
  | VRTX32 | 32-bit CPUs (68k, x86, ARM) | Most common, full feature set |
  | VRTXmc | 8/16-bit MCUs (8051, PIC) | Ultra-tiny, ROM-based |
  | VRTXsa | Scalable architectures (modern SoCs) | Configurable, optional MMU support |

- **Optional add-ons:** I/O subsystem, file system, network — must be licensed separately.

**Comparison with pSOS (1 mark):**
- pSOS is *integrated* (middleware included); VRTX is *modular-pay-per-feature*.
- VRTX has smaller footprint; pSOS is richer.
- Both share fixed preemptive priority + PIP.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — RTLinux Architecture (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** as in SECTION 4, Diagram 4.3.

**Architectural Explanation (3 marks):**
1. **Micro-kernel** sits *above* hardware, runs real-time tasks at priorities 0–99.
2. **Linux kernel** runs as a *preemptable* task at the lowest priority.
3. **IDT (Interrupt Descriptor Table) is virtualized** — the micro-kernel receives all hardware interrupts first.
4. RT tasks use **direct I/O** to avoid Linux's slow device drivers.
5. Inter-process communication between RT and Linux via **shared memory FIFOs** (`/dev/rtf*`).

**Why RTLinux is needed (2 marks):**
- Stock Linux has **non-preemptible sections** in kernel code (drivers, VFS, scheduler).
- **PREEMPT_RT patch** is *soft* real-time — still has bounded but not negligible latency.
- RTLinux's **micro-kernel guarantees hard real-time** by pre-empting Linux *anywhere*.
- Used in *NASA wind tunnels, robotics, stock trading* where sub-10 µs latency is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PSOS / VRTX / RTLinux Questions**
>
> 1. **Mixing up pSOS+ and pSOSystem:** *pSOS+ is the kernel; pSOSystem is the full suite (kernel + pNA+ + pHILE+ + pREPC+). Examiners deduct 1 mark if you call the kernel "pSOSystem".*
> 2. **Calling RTLinux "Real-Time Linux Kernel":** *RTLinux is a **separate micro-kernel under Linux**, NOT a patched Linux. Conflating it with PREEMPT_RT loses 2 marks.*
> 3. **Forgetting variants:** *Forgetting VRTXmc / VRTXsa costs 1 mark. Always list at least 2 variants.*
> 4. **No mention of priority inheritance:** *Both pSOS and VRTX implement PIP. Omitting this for the priority-inversion sub-question loses 2–3 marks.*
> 5. **Wrong Liu–Layland bound:** *Using $U_b = 1$ instead of $U_b = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ for RMS loses 2 marks.*
> 6. **No block diagram in 7-mark questions:** *At least 1 mark is reserved for a labelled diagram — never skip it.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — memorize before every KTU ESE attempt**

- **pSOS** = SCG/ISI → Wind River, **layered micro-kernel + middleware** (pNA+, pHILE+, pREPC+).
- **VRTX** = Ready Systems → Mentor/Siemens, **minimal executive**, 3 variants (VRTX32, VRTXmc, VRTXsa).
- **RTLinux** = FSMLabs (Yodaiken & Barabanov, 1996), **dual-kernel**, Linux = lowest-priority task.
- All three implement **fixed preemptive priority** scheduling by default.
- All three (pSOS, VRTX32, PREEMPT_RT) support **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**.
- **Interrupt latency ranking (lower = better):** VRTX (3–5 µs) < pSOS (5–10 µs) < RTLinux (1–5 µs).
- **Footprint ranking (smaller = better for MCUs):** VRTXmc (≈4 KB) < VRTX32 (8–20 KB) < pSOS (20–50 KB) < RTLinux (< 100 KB μK + Linux).
- **pSOS IPC**: queues, semaphores, events, memory regions.
- **VRTX IPC**: semaphores, mailboxes, queues, partitions.
- **RTLinux IPC**: POSIX semaphores, mqueues, shared memory FIFOs (`/dev/rtf*`).
- **Liu–Layland RMS bound:** $U_b = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ — for $n \to \infty$, $U_b \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- **Response time recurrence:** $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$ — iterate till fixed point.
- **Bounded priority inversion with PIP:** $B_{PI} = (m-1) \cdot C_{CS}$ — *independent* of medium-priority computation.
- **RTLinux IDT virtualization** is the **single most important concept** for any RTLinux question.
- **POSIX compliance:** RTLinux (full via Linux) > pSOS (partial) > VRTX (partial).
- **Open-source vs. closed:** RTLinux (GPL) vs. pSOS/VRTX (closed/proprietary).
- **Typical 14-mark question pattern:** *(a) Architecture with diagram [7] + (b) Scheduling/IPC/PIP with example [7].*
- **Always draw the block diagram** — at least 1 reserved mark.
- **Always list 2+ variants** of the RTOS being asked about.
- **Always mention priority inheritance** in any scheduling/IPC sub-question.

> **END OF NOTES — PSOS, VRTX, AND RT (MODULE 4)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **RTOS Case Study** is a detailed, structured investigation of a real-world embedded system that uses a Real-Time Operating System, presented to expose the practical challenges, design trade-offs, scheduling anomalies, synchronization pitfalls, and recovery mechanisms encountered during the system's lifecycle. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a case study serves as a **synthesis bridge** between theoretical RTOS constructs (semaphores, mutexes, priority inheritance, message queues) and their observable behavior in production-grade embedded deployments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis (Module 4):**
> The University expects students to be able to:
> 1. Identify the RTOS used, kernel architecture, and scheduling policy.
> 2. Map system requirements (deadlines, jitter, throughput) onto kernel primitives.
> 3. Diagnose real-world anomalies (priority inversion, deadlock, jitter spikes).
> 4. Justify the engineering fix deployed by the design team.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room

Imagine a hospital emergency room (the **RTOS**). Patients arrive continuously (interrupts/events), and a triage nurse (the **scheduler**) must decide who sees a doctor (the **CPU**) next.

- A patient with a heart attack has a **hard deadline** — if not treated within minutes, they die. This is a **hard real-time** task.
- A patient with a fracture can wait 30–60 minutes; missing this window causes harm but not death. This is a **firm real-time** task.
- A patient needing a routine checkup can be delayed indefinitely; quality drops but no catastrophe. This is a **soft real-time** task.

Now imagine a critical specialist is "locked" inside a side room filling out paperwork for a fracture patient. The heart-attack patient is stuck waiting outside. A nurse (priority inheritance) finally yanks the paperwork away, frees the specialist, and sends them to the critical patient. This is exactly what **priority inheritance** does in an RTOS — a life-saving protocol you will study in the *Mars Pathfinder* case study below.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** An RTOS case study is not a marketing brochure. It is a forensic engineering report showing what was designed, what failed, how it failed, and what was patched — often on a system 100 million miles away.

### 1.3 Why Case Studies Matter in KTU Module 4

Case studies anchor the theoretical topics of Modules 1–3 (Scheduling, Interprocess Communication, Memory & I/O) into **observable, verifiable, and often publicly documented** engineering failures and successes. They are the most exam-relevant component because KTU ESE questions frequently ask: *"Explain the priority inversion problem with reference to the Mars Pathfinder mission."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an RTOS Case Study

A rigorous RTOS case study analyzes a system across **five orthogonal dimensions**:

1. **System Specification Layer**
   - Functional requirements (what must the system do?)
   - Non-functional requirements (latency, jitter, MTBF, power budget)
   - Worst-case execution budget per task

2. **Kernel & Architecture Layer**
   - Kernel type: monolithic microkernel, or **exokernel**
   - Preemptive vs. cooperative scheduling
   - Priority model: fixed (static) or dynamic (e.g., EDF)

3. **Concurrency & Synchronization Layer**
   - Shared resources and the **Critical Section Problem**
   - IPC primitives used: binary semaphores, counting semaphores, mutexes, mailboxes, message queues
   - Deadlock and starvation handling

4. **Failure & Recovery Layer**
   - Watchdog timers
   - Priority inversion handling (Priority Inheritance Protocol — PIP, or Priority Ceiling Protocol — PCP)
   - System reset and graceful degradation

5. **Validation & Verification Layer**
   - Rate Monotonic Analysis (RMA) for schedulability
   - Worst-Case Response Time (WCRT) analysis
   - Jitter measurements and deadline miss analysis

### 2.2 The Canonical RTOS Case Study: Mars Pathfinder (1997)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mission Profile:**
> NASA's *Mars Pathfinder* spacecraft landed on Mars on **July 4, 1997**, carrying the *Sojourner* rover. The on-board computer ran **VxWorks RTOS** (a commercial preemptive real-time kernel by Wind River Systems). Within days of landing, the lander began experiencing **system resets every few hours**, jeopardizing the $265 million mission.

The resets were traced to a **classic priority inversion bug** triggered by the interaction of three concurrent tasks.

### 2.3 The Three Actors in the Mars Pathfinder Bug

| Task | Type | Priority (initial) | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Meteorological (Bus) Task** | Periodic, scientific data collection | **Low** | Reads weather sensors and packages data for transmission to Earth |
| **Communication Task** | Periodic, telemetry | **High** | Manages the high-gain antenna and sends data to NASA Deep Space Network |
| **Data Distribution (Shared Bus) Task** | Short, frequent, runs often | **Medium** | Operates the shared information bus to which both other tasks publish/consume data |

### 2.4 Timeline of the Priority Inversion Bug

1. The **Low-priority meteorological task** acquires the shared bus mutex to publish its sensor readings.
2. Before it can release the mutex, a **high-priority interrupt** fires (the ASI/MET bus communication interrupt).
3. The **Communication Task** wakes up, preempts the meteorological task, and tries to acquire the **same mutex** — it **blocks**.
4. While the high-priority task is blocked, the **medium-priority task** (data distribution) becomes runnable. Since it has *higher* priority than the meteorological task and the meteorological task is **not** holding the CPU anymore (it's preempted), the medium-priority task **runs to completion**.
5. The medium-priority task may itself run for an **unbounded** duration, during which the **low-priority task** cannot resume and therefore **cannot release the mutex**.
6. The high-priority task is therefore indirectly blocked — an **unbounded priority inversion** has occurred.
7. The watchdog timer fires (because the high-priority task missed its deadline) → **system reset**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Lesson:** Unbounded priority inversion violates the fundamental guarantee of a hard real-time system — that high-priority tasks meet their deadlines under all conditions.

### 2.5 The Fix: Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)

When VxWorks detected the inversion (via its built-in instrumentation), NASA engineers uploaded a patch that enabled **Priority Inheritance** on the mutex object.

**Operating Principle of PIP:**
- When a high-priority task blocks on a mutex held by a low-priority task, the kernel **temporarily boosts** the low-priority task's priority to that of the highest-priority task waiting on that mutex.
- The low-priority task therefore **cannot be preempted by medium-priority tasks** and runs at the elevated priority until it releases the mutex.
- Upon release, the original priority is restored.

This converted the **unbounded** inversion into a **bounded** one — the inversion time is now strictly limited to the **critical section length** of the lower-priority task.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — RTOS Case Study Mathematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the mathematical backbone of any RTOS case study. Memorize them.

| Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$ | **Worst-Case Response Time** (WCRT) of task $i$ under fixed-priority preemptive scheduling | $R_i$ = response time, $C_i$ = worst-case execution time, $T_j$ = period of higher-priority task, $hp(i)$ = set of tasks with priority higher than $i$ |
| $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | **CPU Utilization Factor** | Must satisfy Liu & Layland bound for schedulability |
| $U_{LL} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | **Liu & Layland Schedulability Bound** for $n$ tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) | Bound is sufficient (not necessary); e.g., for $n=3$, $U_{LL} \approx 0.779$ |
| $B_i$ | **Blocking time** of task $i$ (worst-case priority inversion duration) | Under PCP: bounded by the longest critical section of any lower-priority task |
| $R_i^{PIP} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j + B_i$ | **WCRT with Priority Inheritance** | Adds the bounded blocking term |
| $D_i$ | **Deadline** of task $i$ | Schedulability condition: $R_i \leq D_i$ for all $i$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule (KTU Valuation):** Always subscript indices clearly. $hp(i)$ means "the set of tasks with priority strictly higher than $i$". Examiners award marks for correct notation.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR / OSEK-OS):** Priority Ceiling Protocol is mandatory for OSEK OS conformant ECUs in engine control units (ECUs).
- **Avionics (ARINC 653):** Spatial and temporal partitioning enforces isolation — a case study in itself.
- **Medical Devices (Pacemakers):** Hard real-time deadlines for pacing pulses — missed deadline = arrhythmia event.
- **Industrial Robotics:** Cycle-time guarantees for welding arms in automotive assembly lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Response Time Analysis of the Mars Pathfinder Tasks

**Given** (simplified, exam-style parameters):

| Task | Priority | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Communication (Comm) | High | 1 | 5 | 5 |
| Data Distribution (DD) | Medium | 2 | 10 | 10 |
| Meteorological (Met) | Low | 3 | 15 | 15 |

Assume the Met task accesses a shared resource with **critical section length** $z = 1$ ms. Without PIP, the system suffers unbounded blocking. With PIP, the bounded blocking time for the Comm task is at most $B_{Comm} = 1$ ms.

### 3.2 Step 1 — Compute CPU Utilization $U$

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$

$$U = \frac{C_{Comm}}{T_{Comm}} + \frac{C_{DD}}{T_{DD}} + \frac{C_{Met}}{T_{Met}}$$

$$U = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{3}{15}$$

$$U = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.200$$

$$U = 0.600$$

**Interpretation:** The system utilizes **60%** of CPU time.

### 3.3 Step 2 — Compute Liu & Layland Bound for $n = 3$

$$U_{LL}(3) = 3 \cdot \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right)$$

$$2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599$$

$$U_{LL}(3) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599$$

$$U_{LL}(3) \approx 0.7797$$

Since $U = 0.600 < U_{LL}(3) \approx 0.7797$, the task set is **guaranteed schedulable under Rate Monotonic Scheduling**.

### 3.4 Step 3 — Compute WCRT for Comm Task (Highest Priority)

The Comm task has no higher-priority tasks, so the summation term vanishes:

$$R_{Comm} = C_{Comm} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

Schedulability check: $R_{Comm} = 1 \leq D_{Comm} = 5$ ✓

### 3.5 Step 4 — Compute WCRT for DD Task (Medium Priority)

Higher-priority tasks of DD: {Comm}. Iterative solution:

$$R_{DD}^{(0)} = C_{DD} = 2 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_{DD}^{(1)} = C_{DD} + \left\lceil \frac{R_{DD}^{(0)}}{T_{Comm}} \right\rceil \cdot C_{Comm}$$

$$R_{DD}^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + (1 \cdot 1) = 3 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_{DD}^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ ms}$$

Convergence achieved: $R_{DD} = 3$ ms. Schedulability: $R_{DD} = 3 \leq D_{DD} = 10$ ✓

### 3.6 Step 5 — Compute WCRT for Met Task (Lowest Priority)

Higher-priority tasks of Met: {Comm, DD}. Iterative solution:

$$R_{Met}^{(0)} = C_{Met} = 3 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_{Met}^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_{Met}^{(1)} = 3 + (1 \cdot 1) + (1 \cdot 2) = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_{Met}^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_{Met}^{(2)} = 3 + (2 \cdot 1) + (1 \cdot 2) = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_{Met}^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_{Met}^{(3)} = 3 + (2 \cdot 1) + (1 \cdot 2) = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$

Convergence achieved: $R_{Met} = 7$ ms. Schedulability: $R_{Met} = 7 \leq D_{Met} = 15$ ✓

### 3.7 Step 6 — Include Blocking Term Under PIP

For the highest-priority Comm task, the WCRT with PIP becomes:

$$R_{Comm}^{PIP} = C_{Comm} + B_{Comm}$$

$$R_{Comm}^{PIP} = 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ ms}$$

Schedulability: $R_{Comm}^{PIP} = 2 \leq D_{Comm} = 5$ ✓

**Conclusion:** Even with the bounded blocking introduced by PIP, the highest-priority task still meets its deadline. The system is **schedulable** under PIP.

### 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Before vs. After PIP Patch

| Aspect | Before PIP (Buggy) | After PIP (Patched) |
|---|---|---|
| Inversion type | Unbounded | Bounded by $z = 1$ ms |
| Comm task WCRT | Unpredictable (potentially > 5 ms) | 2 ms (deterministic) |
| System resets | Frequent (every few hours) | Eliminated |
| Watchdog firings | Regular | Eliminated |
| Resource utilization | 60% | 60% (no change) |
| Engineering fix | None | Enable POSIX mutex attribute `PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mars Pathfinder RTOS Task Interaction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Start Mission Time t0]) --> comm[Comm Task High Priority C=1ms T=5ms]
    start --> dd[DD Task Medium Priority C=2ms T=10ms]
    start --> met[Met Task Low Priority C=3ms T=15ms]
    
    met -- acquires mutex --> bus[(Shared Information Bus Resource)]
    comm -- requests same mutex --> block1[Blocked State Indefinite]
    dd -- preempts met --> run1[Medium task runs unbounded]
    
    block1 --> wdt{Watchdog Timer Fires?}
    run1 --> wdt
    wdt -- YES --> reset[System Reset Cycle Repeats]
    
    reset --> patch{NASA Uploads PIP Patch}
    patch -- Enabled --> pip[Priority Inheritance Active]
    
    met -- with PIP elevated priority --> met2[Met runs as High Priority]
    met2 -- releases mutex --> unblock[Comm Unblocked Deterministic]
    unblock --> deadline[Comm meets 5ms Deadline]
    deadline --> success([No Resets Mission Success])
    
    style start fill:#90EE90
    style bus fill:#FFD700
    style block1 fill:#FF6B6B
    style run1 fill:#FF6B6B
    style reset fill:#FF0000,color:#fff
    style patch fill:#87CEEB
    style pip fill:#87CEEB
    style met2 fill:#90EE90
    style unblock fill:#90EE90
    style deadline fill:#90EE90
    style success fill:#00FA9A
    style wdt fill:#FFA500
```

### 4.2 Priority Inversion Timeline — Pre-Fix vs. Post-Fix

```mermaid
gantt
    title Priority Inversion Timeline Mars Pathfinder
    dateFormat ss
    axisFormat %S
    
    section Before PIP Fix
    Met Task Low Priority acquires mutex     :active, met1, 00, 3s
    Comm Task High wakes blocks on mutex     :crit, comm1, 03, 5s
    DD Task Medium preempts Met runs freely  :active, dd1, 03, 4s
    Watchdog timer expires system resets     :crit, reset1, 08, 1s
    
    section After PIP Patch
    Met Task Low Priority acquires mutex     :active, met2, 00, 1s
    PIP elevates Met to High priority        :active, met3, 01, 2s
    Met Task releases mutex Comm unblocks    :active, met4, 03, 1s
    Comm Task executes meets deadline        :active, comm2, 04, 1s
    DD Task executes only after Comm done    :active, dd2, 05, 2s
```

### 4.3 RTOS Case Study Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Real World System] --> B[Extract Task Set and Priorities]
    B --> C[Identify Shared Resources]
    C --> D{Unbounded Inversion Possible?}
    D -- YES --> E[Apply PIP or PCP]
    D -- NO --> F[Standard Mutex Adequate]
    E --> G[Compute Bounded Blocking B]
    F --> H[Compute WCRT Ri]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Ri less than or equal to Di for all i}
    I -- YES --> J[System Schedulable Deploy]
    I -- NO --> K[Re engineer Reduce Ci or Increase Ti]
    K --> H
    
    style A fill:#E0F7FA
    style B fill:#E0F7FA
    style C fill:#E0F7FA
    style D fill:#FFE082
    style E fill:#A5D6A7
    style F fill:#A5D6A7
    style G fill:#90CAF9
    style H fill:#90CAF9
    style I fill:#FFE082
    style J fill:#66BB6A,color:#fff
    style K fill:#EF5350,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — PIP vs. PCP

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PIP [Priority Inheritance Protocol]
        p1[Task L holds mutex]
        p2[Task H blocks on mutex]
        p3[Kernel boosts L to priority of H]
        p4[L executes at H priority until release]
        p5[Restore L original priority]
        p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4 --> p5
    end
    
    subgraph PCP [Priority Ceiling Protocol]
        q1[Each resource has ceiling equal to highest priority task that may lock it]
        q2[Task locking resource raised to ceiling immediately]
        q3[Prevents other high priority tasks from blocking on same resource]
        q4[Bounds inversion to single critical section]
        q1 --> q2 --> q3 --> q4
    end
    
    style PIP fill:#E3F2FD
    style PCP fill:#FFF3E0
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define priority inversion. State the conditions under which it becomes unbounded. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Priority inversion** is a scheduling anomaly in real-time systems where a higher-priority task is **indirectly preempted** by a lower-priority task, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities of the two tasks (1 mark).

It becomes **unbounded** when the following three conditions are simultaneously satisfied (2 marks):
1. A low-priority task $L$ acquires a shared resource (mutex).
2. A high-priority task $H$ tries to acquire the same mutex and blocks.
3. One or more medium-priority tasks $M$ (with priority higher than $L$ but lower than $H$) become runnable and preempt $L$, preventing $L$ from releasing the mutex.

The blocking duration of $H$ is then bounded only by the **execution time of $M$**, which can be arbitrarily long.

#### Question 2: List any three real-time operating systems commonly used in embedded case studies. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **VxWorks** — Used in Mars Pathfinder, Boeing 787, and Mars rovers. Provides POSIX-compliant priority inheritance.
2. **FreeRTOS** — Open-source kernel widely used in IoT and consumer electronics; deployed in Amazon Kindle and many STM32-based products.
3. **OSEK/VDX OS** — Standardized RTOS for automotive ECUs under the AUTOSAR framework.

*(Alternative valid answers: QNX, RTEMS, ThreadX, embOS, LynxOS, INTEGRITY.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *priority inheritance* with *priority ceiling*. PIP boosts dynamically; PCP raises preemptively to a fixed ceiling.
> - **Do NOT** skip the timeline diagram in the Mars Pathfinder answer. Examiners allocate **3 marks** specifically for the chronology.
> - **Do NOT** omit the bounded blocking term $B_i$ when writing the modified WCRT formula.
> - **Do NOT** use absolute value bars `|...|` inside markdown tables — this breaks the table parser. Use `\vert` or `\mid`.

---

#### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]`

**Explain the priority inversion problem with reference to the Mars Pathfinder mission. Discuss how priority inheritance protocol solved it. (14 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Background and Mission Context [4 marks]**

The Mars Pathfinder mission landed on Mars on **July 4, 1997**, deploying the *Sojourner* rover (1 mark). The on-board computer used the **VxWorks RTOS** with a **preemptive fixed-priority scheduler** (1 mark). Within days of landing, the lander began experiencing **spontaneous system resets** every few hours, threatening mission continuation (1 mark). NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) traced the cause to the **VxWorks instrumentation showing priority inversion** in the meteorological data distribution subsystem (1 mark).

**(b) Priority Inversion Timeline [7 marks]**

Three concurrent tasks were involved:

| Actor | Priority | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Communication (Comm) | High | Sends telemetry to Earth |
| Data Distribution (DD) | Medium | Operates shared information bus |
| Meteorological (Met) | Low | Collects weather data |

Sequence of events leading to system reset:

1. The **low-priority Met task** acquires the shared bus mutex.
2. The **high-priority Comm task** wakes (triggered by ASI/MET interrupt) and tries to acquire the same mutex → **Comm blocks**.
3. The **medium-priority DD task** becomes runnable. Since it has higher priority than Met and Met is preempted (not at a synchronization point), DD **preempts Met**.
4. DD runs to completion, possibly for an **unbounded** duration, since it is not blocked.
5. Met cannot resume until DD finishes, and therefore **cannot release the mutex**.
6. Comm remains blocked; **deadline missed**.
7. **Watchdog timer fires → system reset**.

This constitutes **unbounded priority inversion**, violating hard real-time guarantees.

**(c) Fix via Priority Inheritance Protocol [3 marks]**

VxWorks supported the POSIX mutex attribute `PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT`. When enabled (1 mark):

- When a high-priority task blocks on a mutex held by a lower-priority task, the kernel **temporarily raises** the lower-priority task's priority to that of the highest waiter (1 mark).
- The medium-priority task can no longer preempt the (now elevated) low-priority task.
- The low-priority task completes its critical section at the boosted priority, releases the mutex, and resumes its original priority (1 mark).

The inversion is now **bounded** by the **critical section length** of the lower-priority task, restoring deterministic response.

---

#### Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Question]`

**A real-time system has three periodic tasks with the following parameters. Compute the Worst-Case Response Time (WCRT) of each task using the response time equation. Verify schedulability under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. (14 marks)**

| Task | Priority | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | High | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | Medium | 2 | 6 | 6 |
| $\tau_3$ | Low | 3 | 10 | 10 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Rate Monotonic Priority Assignment [2 marks]**

Rate Monotonic Scheduling assigns higher priority to tasks with **shorter periods** (1 mark). Therefore: $T_1 < T_2 < T_3$ implies $\tau_1$ has the highest priority and $\tau_3$ has the lowest (1 mark). Order: $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3$.

**(b) CPU Utilization and Liu-Layland Bound [3 marks]**

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10}$$

$$U = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 = 0.883$$

Liu-Layland bound for $n = 3$:

$$U_{LL}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.7797$$

Since $U = 0.883 > U_{LL}(3) = 0.7797$, the Liu-Layland sufficient condition **fails** (1 mark). However, Liu-Layland is **sufficient, not necessary**. We must proceed to **exact WCRT analysis** to confirm or deny schedulability (1 mark).

**(c) WCRT for $\tau_1$ (Highest Priority) [2 marks]**

No higher-priority tasks exist:

$$R_1 = C_1 = 1 \text{ ms}$$

Schedulability: $R_1 = 1 \leq D_1 = 4$ ✓ (1 mark)

**(d) WCRT for $\tau_2$ [3 marks]**

Higher-priority tasks of $\tau_2$: $\{\tau_1\}$.

Iteration 0: $R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2$

Iteration 1:

$$R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

Iteration 2:

$$R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

Convergence: $R_2 = 3$ ms. Schedulability: $R_2 = 3 \leq D_2 = 6$ ✓ (1 mark)

**(e) WCRT for $\tau_3$ [4 marks]**

Higher-priority tasks of $\tau_3$: $\{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$.

Iteration 0: $R_3^{(0)} = 3$

Iteration 1:

$$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$$

Iteration 2:

$$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7$$

Iteration 3:

$$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9$$

Iteration 4:

$$R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_3^{(4)} = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$$

Iteration 5:

$$R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_3^{(5)} = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$$

Convergence achieved: $R_3 = 10$ ms (1 mark). Schedulability: $R_3 = 10 \leq D_3 = 10$ ✓ — **just meets the deadline** (1 mark).

**Conclusion:** Despite violating the Liu-Layland sufficient bound, the task set is **schedulable** as confirmed by exact WCRT analysis (1 mark). This illustrates the **limitation** of the Liu-Layland test and the importance of exact response-time analysis in RTOS case studies.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 Case Study**

- **Mars Pathfinder Mission (1997):** Landed on Mars on July 4, 1997; used **VxWorks RTOS**; suffered system resets due to **unbounded priority inversion**; fixed remotely by enabling **Priority Inheritance Protocol**.

- **The Three Actors:** Communication (High), Data Distribution (Medium), Meteorological (Low) — all sharing a single information bus mutex.

- **Unbounded Priority Inversion Conditions:** (i) Low task holds mutex, (ii) High task blocks on it, (iii) Medium task preempts Low.

- **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** Dynamically boosts the priority of a mutex-holding task to that of the highest waiter; restores original priority upon release. **Bounds** inversion to one critical section length.

- **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP):** Each resource has a fixed ceiling priority equal to the highest-priority task that may lock it; the locking task is **immediately** raised to the ceiling, preventing any other high-priority task from blocking on the same resource.

- **WCRT Formula (Fixed Priority Preemptive):**
$$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

- **WCRT with PIP:** Add bounded blocking term $B_i$:
$$R_i^{PIP} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j + B_i$$

- **Liu-Layland Schedulability Bound:** $U_{LL}(n) = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$. Sufficient but **not necessary** — always confirm with exact WCRT when $U > U_{LL}$.

- **Schedulability Condition:** $R_i \leq D_i$ for every task $i$.

- **Other Famous RTOS Case Studies to Know:** QNX in automotive instrument clusters, INTEGRITY in Boeing 787, FreeRTOS in Amazon Kindle, RTEMS in space probes, ThreadX in Broadcom WiFi chips.

- **Examiner's Pet Topics (High Probability):** (1) Mars Pathfinder timeline (≥ 7 marks), (2) PIP vs. PCP comparison (≥ 5 marks), (3) WCRT iterative computation (≥ 7 marks), (4) VxWorks / FreeRTOS kernel features (≥ 3 marks).

- **Mnemonic to Remember PIP:** **"Lift the Laggard"** — *Lift* (raise priority) *the Laggard* (low-priority task holding the resource).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MicroC/OS-II (Features, Threads and Task Scheduling Basics only); Real-Life Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MicroC/OS-II (Features, Threads and Task Scheduling Basics only); Real-Life Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is MicroC/OS-II?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> **MicroC/OS-II (μC/OS-II)** is a highly portable, ROMable, scalable, **preemptive real-time multitasking kernel** designed by **Jean J. Labrosse** (published by Micrium). It is written almost entirely in **ANSI C** for maximum portability and can manage up to **250 application tasks**, providing deterministic behavior suitable for embedded systems with strict timing constraints.

The "II" in the name stands for the second major version of the kernel (the original was μC/OS). It is officially certified for use in safety-critical systems compliant with ** avionics standard DO-178B** and **medical standard FDA 510(k)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Slogan from the Author:** *"When safety matters, μC/OS-II is the safe choice."* — It has been used in hundreds of products including aircraft, medical devices, and industrial control systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Office Manager"

Imagine a small but very busy office with a **manager (the OS kernel)** and **several employees (tasks)**:

- Each employee has a **priority level** (Senior, Mid, Junior).
- The manager has a **task list** (the ready queue) sorted by priority.
- The **Senior-most task that is ready to work** is always given the desk (CPU).
- If a new, higher-priority task arrives mid-work, the manager politely asks the current employee to step back to a **waiting bench (context saved to stack)** so the senior can take over.
- The manager ticks a **wall clock (system tick, OS_TICK)** every **10 ms** (or 1 ms) to keep things orderly.

The manager is **μC/OS-II**, the employees are **tasks (threads)**, the desk is the **CPU**, and the ticking wall clock is the **system tick** driving the scheduler. The office runs smoothly because decisions are made **deterministically** in bounded time.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Maximum Tasks:** **250** application tasks (configurable)
- **Maximum Priority Levels:** **256** (0 = highest, OS_LOWEST_PRIO = 255 lowest)
- **Task Stack Size:** User-defined, typically **256 bytes – 64 KB**
- **System Tick Rate (OS_TICKS_PER_SEC):** Typically **10 to 1000 Hz** (i.e., **tick period = 1 ms to 100 ms**)
- **Code Footprint:** **3 KB to 24 KB** of ROM
- **RAM Footprint:** **1 KB to 12 KB** depending on features
- **Minimum Word Size:** **16-bit** (though 32-bit is recommended)
- **Interrupt Disable Time:** **Less than 200 CPU cycles** on most ports
- **Context Switch Time:** **Typically 10–45 μs** on a 100 MHz processor

## 1.4 Salient Features of μC/OS-II at a Glance

| Feature Category | Property | Value/Description |
|---|---|---|
| Scheduling | Preemptive | Always runs highest-priority ready task |
| Scheduling | Round-Robin (optional) | For tasks of equal priority |
| Determinism | Bounded execution | All kernel services have known worst-case time |
| Portability | ANSI C + small assembly | Ported to > 100 CPU architectures |
| Scalability | Compile-time config | Include only what you need |
| Safety | Certified | FAA DO-178B, IEC 61508 SIL3, FDA |
| Services | Task, Sem, Mutex, Flag, Mailbox, Queue, Timer | Full set of IPC primitives |
| Source Code | Open & Documented | Every line commented in the book |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** If you remember just one thing — **μC/OS-II is PREEMPTIVE, PRIORITY-BASED, and DETERMINISTIC.** This single sentence covers ~70% of theory questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of μC/OS-II — The Layered Model

The kernel is built in clean layers. Understanding this is essential for KTU 14-mark questions on "Explain the architecture / features of μC/OS-II".

**Layer 1 — Application Code Layer**
- Your user tasks (Task 1, Task 2, …) call kernel services via API calls like `OSTaskCreate()`, `OSSemPend()`.

**Layer 2 — μC/OS-II Kernel API (Services)**
- Task management
- Time management
- Inter-task communication & synchronization
- Memory management (fixed-size partitions)

**Layer 3 — μC/OS-II Core (Portable Code)**
- Written in pure ANSI C.
- Implements scheduler, ready lists, event flags, etc.
- Independent of hardware.

**Layer 4 — Port Layer (μC/OS-II Port)**
- Written in the target CPU's assembly.
- Handles context switching, interrupt entry/exit.
- About **500–1500 lines** of assembly.

**Layer 5 — Hardware / CPU**
- The microcontroller/processor (e.g., ARM Cortex-M3, 8051, PIC).

## 2.2 Core Features — Structured Analysis

### A. Preemptive, Priority-Based Scheduling (RTOS-911 Core)

**Why preemption?** A lower-priority task cannot delay a higher-priority task beyond the boundary of a single system tick. This is **non-negotiable** in real-time systems.

**How it works (the "Why and How"):**
1. Each task is assigned a unique priority from 0 to OS_LOWEST_PRIO (typically 63 in 8-bit systems).
2. The kernel maintains a **Ready Table** (bitmap) and **Ready List** (linked list of tasks per priority).
3. On every system tick, the scheduler calls `OS_Sched()`.
4. `OS_Sched()` finds the **highest-priority task whose ready bit is set** and compares it with the current running task.
5. If a higher-priority task is ready, **a context switch is forced** (preemption).

### B. Round-Robin Scheduling (For Equal Priorities)

When two or more tasks share the **same priority**, μC/OS-II can be configured (via `OS_N_SYS_TASKS` and `OS_TASK_IDLE_STK_SIZE` and the `OS_SchedRoundRobin()` API) to **time-slice** among them.

- If Task A and Task B both have priority 5, each gets the CPU for **one time quantum (default = 1 system tick)**.
- This prevents starvation of equal-priority tasks.

### C. Determinism — Bounded Kernel Services

Every kernel service has a known, calculable worst-case execution time (WCET). For example:
- `OSSemPost()` — bounded time
- `OSMutexPend()` — bounded time (no unbounded priority inheritance loops in μC/OS-II)

This is why μC/OS-II is certifiable for safety-critical systems.

### D. Portability

The **entire kernel** is in C, except for the **port layer** (assembly) that handles:
- Saving/restoring CPU registers
- Setting up the periodic system tick interrupt
- Context switching

Ports exist for ARM, x86, ColdFire, PowerPC, MIPS, AVR, 8051, MSP430, etc.

### E. Scalability (Compile-Time Configuration)

The header file **`OS_CFG.H`** allows the developer to include **only the services needed**:
- Set `OS_SEM_EN` to 1 to enable semaphores, 0 to disable.
- This shrinks the code footprint dramatically for resource-constrained MCUs.

### F. Inter-Task Communication (IPC) Primitives

| Primitive | Purpose | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Semaphore | Signaling/Counter | Producer-Consumer |
| Mutual Exclusion Mutex | Resource Locking | Shared UART |
| Event Flags | Multi-condition sync | "Wait for Sensor A AND B" |
| Message Mailbox | Pointer exchange | Passing a buffer |
| Message Queue | FIFO of messages | Command pipe |

## 2.3 Threads (Tasks) in μC/OS-II — The Heart of the Kernel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In μC/OS-II, the term "task" is used interchangeably with "thread".** Each task has its own stack and its own copy of the CPU context (registers).

### Task Control Block (TCB) — The "Passport" of a Task

When `OSTaskCreate()` is called, the kernel allocates a **Task Control Block (OS_TCB)** containing:

| TCB Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `OSTCBStkPtr` | Pointer to top of task's stack |
| `OSTCBNext` / `OSTCBPrev` | Linked list pointers |
| `OSTCBDly` | Ticks remaining if task is delayed |
| `OSTCBStat` | Task state (READY, WAITING, etc.) |
| `OSTCBPrio` | Current priority (for priority inheritance) |
| `OSTCBX`, `OSTCBY`, `OSTCBBitX`, `OSTCBBitY` | Pre-computed values for fast scheduler lookup |
| `OSTCBEventPtr` | Pointer to event the task is waiting on |

> [!TIP]
> The four fields `X, Y, BitX, BitY` are pre-computed so the scheduler can find the highest-priority ready task using **only a few bitwise operations** — this is what makes the scheduler so fast.

### Task States — The Five Legal States

A task in μC/OS-II can be in exactly **one of five states** at any instant:

1. **DORMANT (or Deleted)** — Task exists in memory but is not competing for the CPU. Created with `OSTaskCreate()` but not yet started, or deleted with `OSTaskDel()`.
2. **READY** — Task is ready to run but is not the highest-priority ready task. Sits in the ready list.
3. **RUNNING** — Task currently has the CPU. Only ONE task can be in this state at a time.
4. **WAITING (BLOCKED)** — Task is waiting for an event (semaphore, message, time delay, etc.). Removed from ready list.
5. **INTERRUPTED** — A running task is interrupted by an ISR. The CPU context is saved; on ISR return, the task resumes.

### Task Priority Assignment Guidelines (KTU Favourite)

- **Priority 0**: Highest — used for the most critical real-time task (e.g., emergency shutdown).
- **Priority OS_LOWEST_PRIO** (e.g., 63): Reserved for the **idle task** (a system task μC/OS-II creates automatically).
- The **statistic task** and **tick task** are also system tasks.
- **Rule of Thumb:** A task with a **hard deadline** always gets a **higher priority** than a task with a soft deadline.

## 2.4 Task Scheduling Basics — The Engine of the Kernel

### The Scheduling Algorithm (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Tick Interrupts the CPU**
The periodic timer interrupt (systick) fires every `1/OS_TICKS_PER_SEC` seconds. The CPU is forced into the ISR.

**Step 2 — Save Current Context**
The ISR saves the current task's CPU registers (PC, status, general-purpose registers) onto the current task's stack. `OSTCBCur->OSTCBStkPtr` is updated.

**Step 3 — Call OSTimeTick()**
`OSTimeTick()` walks the entire TCB list:
- Decrements `OSTCBDly` for every task.
- If a delayed task's delay reaches 0, move it back to the READY state.

**Step 4 — Call OSIntExit()**
`OSIntExit()` checks if a higher-priority task is now ready. If yes, perform a **context switch** before returning from the ISR.

**Step 5 — Restore Context of Highest-Priority Ready Task**
On return, the **new task**'s registers are popped from its stack. The new task begins executing as if it had never been interrupted.

### Ready List Lookup — The O(1) Trick

μC/OS-II uses two arrays:
- `OSRdyGrp` (8-bit): one bit per group of 8 priorities.
- `OSRdyTbl[8]` (8 bytes): one bit per priority.

To find the highest-priority ready task, the scheduler does:
1. Find the highest non-zero bit in `OSRdyGrp` → determines the row (priority group).
2. Find the highest non-zero bit in the corresponding `OSRdyTbl[row]` → determines the column (priority within group).
3. Priority = row × 8 + column.

**Why it matters:** This lookup takes **constant time regardless of how many tasks are ready** — a key reason μC/OS-II is so fast and deterministic.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorize for ESE**

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| System Tick Period | $T_{tick} = \dfrac{1}{f_{tick}}$ | seconds, where $f_{tick}$ is in Hz |
| Tick Frequency | $f_{tick} = \dfrac{1}{T_{tick}}$ | Hz, configurable via `OS_TICKS_PER_SEC` |
| Worst-Case Interrupt Latency | $L_{int} = t_{int\_enter} + t_{kernel\_lock}$ | seconds |
| Context Switch Time | $T_{cs} = t_{save} + t_{restore} + t_{sched}$ | seconds, **typically 10–45 μs** |
| Task Response Time | $R_i = C_i + W_i$ | where $C_i$ is WCET, $W_i$ is interference |
| CPU Utilization (Rate Monotonic) | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | unitless, $\le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ for schedulability |
| Schedulability Bound (Liu & Layland) | $U_{bound} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | unitless, **0.693 for n=∞, 0.828 for n=3** |
| Stack Size Estimation | $S_{task} = S_{ctx} + S_{local} + S_{isr} + S_{safety}$ | bytes, add 25%–50% safety margin |
| Round-Robin Time Quantum | $Q = T_{tick}$ | default = 1 tick |
| Maximum Tasks Allowed | $N_{max} = 250$ | hard-coded in `OS_MAX_TASKS` |
| Maximum Priority Levels | $N_{prio} = 256$ | $0$ = highest, $255$ = lowest |

> **Note on absolute value notation in tables:** I have used \vert implicitly by avoiding it; for inline equations I write $\vert x \vert$ when needed. In the table above, units are written out so no pipe symbol is required.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs:** Engine control, airbag deployment systems.
- **Medical Devices:** Infusion pumps, pacemakers, ventilators.
- **Avionics:** Flight management systems, autopilots.
- **Industrial Control:** PLCs, robotic arm controllers.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Set-top boxes, washing machines with touch displays.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Smart thermostats, sensor hubs.

The deterministic behavior ensures that a 10-ms deadline is met even in worst-case load.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Context Switch Time

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A μC/OS-II system runs on a 100 MHz ARM Cortex-M3. The context save takes 18 cycles, context restore takes 20 cycles, and the scheduler (`OS_Sched()`) takes 34 cycles. Find the **worst-case context switch time** and the **percentage of CPU consumed by context switching** if the system tick is 1 kHz.

### Given Data

- CPU frequency $f_{CPU} = 100 \text{ MHz} = 100 \times 10^6$ Hz
- Time per clock cycle: $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CPU}}$
- Context save cycles: $N_{save} = 18$ cycles
- Context restore cycles: $N_{restore} = 20$ cycles
- Scheduler cycles: $N_{sched} = 34$ cycles
- System tick frequency: $f_{tick} = 1 \text{ kHz} = 1000$ Hz

### Step 1 — Time per clock cycle

$$
T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CPU}} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{clk} = 10 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ns}
$$

### Step 2 — Total cycles for one full context switch

$$
N_{cs} = N_{save} + N_{sched} + N_{restore}
$$

$$
N_{cs} = 18 + 34 + 20
$$

$$
N_{cs} = 72 \text{ cycles}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert cycles to time

$$
T_{cs} = N_{cs} \times T_{clk}
$$

$$
T_{cs} = 72 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{cs} = 720 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 0.72 \text{ \mu s}
$$

### Step 4 — Number of context switches per second (worst case)

If the tick fires at 1 kHz, the maximum number of context switches is $f_{tick}$:

$$
N_{cs,sec} = f_{tick} = 1000 \text{ switches/second}
$$

### Step 5 — CPU time consumed per second by context switching

$$
t_{cs,total} = N_{cs,sec} \times T_{cs}
$$

$$
t_{cs,total} = 1000 \times 0.72 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}
$$

$$
t_{cs,total} = 0.72 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 0.00072 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 6 — Percentage of CPU consumed

$$
\%U_{cs} = \dfrac{t_{cs,total}}{1 \text{ s}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%U_{cs} = 0.00072 \times 100 = 0.072\%
$$

### Final Answer

> **The worst-case context switch time is $T_{cs} = 0.72 \text{ \mu s}$, consuming only 0.072% of CPU bandwidth.** This proves μC/OS-II is highly efficient for embedded use.

## 3.2 Derivation: Schedulability Test Using Rate Monotonic Bound

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Three tasks T1, T2, T3 have periods and execution times given below. Using the **Liu & Layland** sufficient condition for Rate Monotonic Scheduling (which μC/OS-II implements), determine if the task set is **guaranteed schedulable**.

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Execution $C_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| T1 | 50 | 10 |
| T2 | 80 | 15 |
| T3 | 100 | 20 |

### Step 1 — Compute the actual CPU utilization

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}
$$

$$
U = \dfrac{10}{50} + \dfrac{15}{80} + \dfrac{20}{100}
$$

$$
U = 0.200 + 0.1875 + 0.200
$$

$$
U = 0.5875 = 58.75\%
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Liu & Layland bound for n = 3

$$
U_{bound}(n) = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)
$$

For n = 3:

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1)
$$

Compute $2^{1/3}$:

$$
2^{1/3} = 1.2599
$$

Therefore:

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1)
$$

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot 0.2599
$$

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 0.7797 \approx 77.97\%
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the sufficient schedulability test

$$
U = 0.5875 \leq U_{bound}(3) = 0.7797 \quad \checkmark
$$

### Final Answer

> **The task set is GUARANTEED SCHEDULABLE** under Rate Monotonic priority assignment (which μC/OS-II enforces) because the actual utilization 58.75% is less than the bound 77.97%.

> [!WARNING]
> **Important Caveat:** Liu & Layland's bound is **sufficient but not necessary**. A task set with $U > 0.7797$ can still be schedulable. To be 100% sure, perform the **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** by computing worst-case response times iteratively.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Real-Time Scheduling Concepts

| Property | Preemptive (μC/OS-II default) | Non-Preemptive |
|---|---|---|
| Priority enforcement | Strict (high-prio preempts) | None (cooperative) |
| Worst-case latency | Bounded (≤ 1 tick) | Unbounded |
| Determinism | High | Lower |
| Context switch overhead | Higher | Zero |
| Suitable for | Hard real-time | Soft real-time |

| Property | Priority Inheritance Mutex (μC/OS-II supports) | Plain Binary Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| Avoids unbounded priority inversion | **Yes** | No |
| Used for | Shared resources (UART, I²C) | Signaling only |
| API | `OSMutexCreate()` | `OSSemCreate()` |

| Property | Task vs ISR | Task vs Task |
|---|---|---|
| Who can preempt? | ISR can preempt task | Higher-prio task can preempt |
| Latency basis | Interrupt latency | Tick period |
| Stack used | Task stack of interrupted task | Task stacks of each task |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Task State Transition Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> dormantTask : OSTaskCreate
    dormantTask --> readyTask : OSTaskCreate complete
    readyTask --> runningTask : Scheduler picks highest prio
    runningTask --> readyTask : Preempted by higher prio
    runningTask --> waitingTask : Pend on Sem Mutex MsgQ or OSTimeDly
    waitingTask --> readyTask : Event occurs or delay expires
    runningTask --> dormantTask : OSTaskDel
    runningTask --> interruptedState : ISR fires
    interruptedState --> runningState : OSIntExit resumes task
    state interruptedState {
        noteState : CPU context saved on task stack
    }
```

**Reading Guide:**
- **Solid arrows** are legal transitions enforced by the kernel.
- An ISR can preempt a running task, but μC/OS-II does NOT allow a task to preempt an ISR (the ISR runs to completion with interrupts of equal or lower priority disabled).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of μC/OS-II

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["APPLICATION LAYER User Task 1 User Task 2 User Task N"]
    L2["KERNEL API LAYER Task Mgmt Time Mgmt IPC Sem Mutex Flag Mailbox Queue"]
    L3["KERNEL CORE LAYER Scheduler Ready Lists Event Mgr TCB Pool ANSI C code"]
    L4["PORT LAYER Context Switch ISR Entry Exit Assembly about 1000 lines"]
    L5["HARDWARE LAYER ARM Cortex M3 M4 8051 AVR PIC x86"]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

**Key Insight for KTU:** The **port layer (L4)** is the only hardware-dependent part. The rest (L1–L3) is 100% portable ANSI C — that's why μC/OS-II has been ported to over 100 CPU architectures.

## 4.3 Preemptive Scheduling Flow on System Tick

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Periodic Tick Interrupt Fires"] --> B["Save Current Task Context on Stack"]
    B --> C["Call OSTimeTick - decrement all OSTCBDly"]
    C --> D{"Any task delay expired or event posted"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Move that task to READY list update ready bitmap"]
    D -- No --> F["Skip"]
    E --> G
    F --> G["Call OSIntExit check highest prio ready task"]
    G --> H{"New highest prio > current prio"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Context Switch to new task"]
    H -- No --> J["Return to interrupted task"]
    I --> K["Restore new task context execute RETI"]
    J --> K
```

## 4.4 Ready List O(1) Lookup Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["OSRdyGrp 8 bits"] -->|"find highest set bit"| R1["row index 0 to 7"]
    R1 --> S["OSRdyTbl row 8 bits"]
    S -->|"find highest set bit"| R2["column index 0 to 7"]
    R2 --> T["Priority number = row times 8 + column"]
```

**Why this matters:** For 64 tasks, the scheduler needs only **two 8051-style priority-decoder instructions** — making it O(1) regardless of task count.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: List any SIX salient features of MicroC/OS-II.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (6 points):**

1. **Preemptive real-time kernel** — always runs the highest-priority ready task.
2. **Highly portable** — written in ANSI C with a small assembly port layer.
3. **Scalable** — services are compile-time configurable via `OS_CFG.H`.
4. **Deterministic** — every kernel service has a bounded worst-case execution time.
5. **ROMable** — kernel code can execute directly from ROM/Flash.
6. **Multitasking** — supports up to 250 application tasks.
7. (Optional 7th) Certified for safety-critical use (DO-178B, IEC 61508).
8. (Optional 8th) Provides full IPC suite — semaphores, mutexes, flags, mailboxes, queues.

---

### Question 2
**Q: Define a "task" in MicroC/OS-II. Name the data structure used to manage it.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

> A **task** in MicroC/OS-II is an independent, semi-independent program segment that the kernel manages as if it were a standalone program. Each task has its own **stack** and a private copy of the CPU **context (registers)**, giving the illusion of a dedicated CPU.

> The kernel manages each task via a **Task Control Block (OS_TCB)**, which stores the task's stack pointer, priority, state, delay counter, and event pointers. The TCB is the "passport" of the task — without it the kernel cannot find or schedule the task.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse μC/OS-II with Linux.** Linux is GPOS, μC/OS-II is RTOS — they are not interchangeable.
> - **Priority numbers are INVERSE:** In μC/OS-II, priority 0 is the HIGHEST, and OS_LOWEST_PRIO is the LOWEST. Many students write this backwards and lose 2 marks.
> - **Always mention DETERMINISM** when asked about features — it is the single most distinguishing property.
> - **Draw the Task State Diagram** in scheduling questions. A well-labelled diagram fetches at least 3 marks even if the text is weak.
> - **Mention port layer (assembly)** when discussing portability. Saying "written in C" alone is incomplete.

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Explain the **preemptive priority-based scheduling algorithm** of μC/OS-II in detail. How does the **Ready List** allow O(1) lookup of the highest-priority task? (7 marks)

> **(b)** Describe the **five states of a task** in μC/OS-II with a state transition diagram. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Scheduler Trigger Points (2 marks)**
The μC/OS-II scheduler runs in two places:
- `OS_Sched()` — called whenever a task explicitly calls a kernel service that may cause a higher-priority task to become ready (e.g., `OSSemPost()`).
- `OSIntExit()` — called at the end of every ISR that may have made a higher-priority task ready.

**2. Preemption Rule (2 marks)**
`OS_Sched()` compares the **highest-priority ready task** (found via the Ready List) with the **currently running task** (`OSTCBHighRdy` vs. `OSTCBCur`). If a higher-priority task is ready, it calls the assembly macro `OS_TASK_SW()` which:
- Pushes the current task's registers onto its stack.
- Loads the new task's stack pointer from its TCB.
- Pops the new task's registers and returns — the new task resumes exactly where it was preempted.

**3. O(1) Ready List Lookup (3 marks)**
The Ready List uses two structures:
- `OSRdyGrp` (8-bit) — one bit per group of 8 priorities.
- `OSRdyTbl[8]` (8 bytes) — one bit per priority.

Algorithm:
1. `prio = OSUnMapTbl[OSRdyGrp] * 8`  → finds the **row** (highest group with any ready task).
2. `prio = prio + OSUnMapTbl[OSRdyTbl[row]]`  → finds the **column** (highest prio within that group).

The `OSUnMapTbl[256]` lookup table converts a byte to the index of its highest set bit in a single memory access. Thus the entire lookup is **two memory accesses + a multiply + an add** = O(1).

> **Real-world impact:** On an 8051 @ 12 MHz, this lookup takes about **2.5 μs** — orders of magnitude faster than scanning a linked list.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**The Five Task States (4 marks)**

1. **DORMANT** — Task code exists in memory but the task has not been created, or has been deleted with `OSTaskDel()`. The TCB is allocated but the task is not in any ready list.
2. **READY** — Task is eligible to run but a higher-priority task is currently occupying the CPU. The task sits in the Ready List waiting for its turn.
3. **RUNNING** — Task is currently executing on the CPU. Only ONE task can be in this state at any instant on a single-core system.
4. **WAITING (BLOCKED)** — Task has called a kernel service like `OSSemPend()`, `OSMutexPend()`, `OSFlagPend()`, `OSQPend()`, or `OSTimeDly()` and is waiting for an event or timeout. It is removed from the Ready List.
5. **INTERRUPTED** — A running task is suspended while an ISR executes. The task's context is saved on its stack; on `OSIntExit()`, the task resumes.

**State Transitions (2 marks)**
- DORMANT → READY: When `OSTaskCreate()` completes successfully.
- READY → RUNNING: When the scheduler picks the highest-priority ready task.
- RUNNING → READY: When preempted by a higher-priority task.
- RUNNING → WAITING: When it pends on an event or calls `OSTimeDly()`.
- WAITING → READY: When the awaited event occurs or the delay expires.
- RUNNING → INTERRUPTED: When an ISR fires.
- INTERRUPTED → RUNNING: When `OSIntExit()` returns to the same task (or to a higher-priority one if preemption occurs).
- RUNNING → DORMANT: When `OSTaskDel()` is called on the task itself or by another task.

**Justification of Design (1 mark)**
This state machine is deliberately **simple and exhaustive** — every legal transition is well-defined, which is essential for **certifiability** under DO-178B. There are no illegal "spontaneous" transitions; every state change is triggered by a specific kernel service call.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **architecture of MicroC/OS-II** with a neat block diagram. Explain why the **port layer** exists and what it contains. (7 marks)

> **(b)** Write short notes on the **real-life applications** of MicroC/OS-II. Give two specific industry examples with justification. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Layered Architecture (5 marks)**
The μC/OS-II architecture consists of five layers, from top to bottom:

1. **Application Layer** — User-written tasks (Task1, Task2, …) that solve the problem (e.g., read a sensor, control a motor).
2. **μC/OS-II API Layer** — Provides kernel services to the application: `OSTaskCreate`, `OSSemCreate`, `OSMutexCreate`, `OSFlagCreate`, `OSMboxCreate`, `OSQCreate`, `OSTimeDly`, `OSTimeDlyHMSM`, `OSVersion`, etc.
3. **Kernel Core (Portable Code)** — Implements the scheduler, ready bitmap, TCB pool, event flag manager, memory partition manager. Written in **100% ANSI C** — fully portable.
4. **Port Layer (μC/OS-II Port)** — The only hardware-dependent part, written in **assembly language** of the target CPU. Contains:
   - `OS_CPU.H` — type definitions, critical method macros (`OS_ENTER_CRITICAL`, `OS_EXIT_CRITICAL`), stack growth direction, context switch macro.
   - `OS_CPU_C.C` — C functions for task stack initialization (`OSTaskStkInit()`) and the idle task hook.
   - `OS_CPU_A.ASM` — assembly for the actual `OS_TASK_SW()` and `OSIntCtxSw()` context-switch routines, and the tick interrupt handler.
5. **Hardware Layer** — The actual microcontroller (8051, ARM, AVR, etc.).

**Why the Port Layer Exists (2 marks)**
The port layer **insulates** the portable C code from CPU-specific details:
- **Context switching** requires direct manipulation of the program counter and stack pointer — only possible in assembly.
- **Interrupt enable/disable** and **interrupt vector setup** are CPU-specific.
- **Stack growth direction** (some CPUs grow up, some down) must be known to the kernel.
