By keeping the port layer **small (about 500–1500 lines of assembly)**, porting μC/OS-II to a new CPU is a manageable task — this is why ports exist for **over 100 CPU architectures**.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Why μC/OS-II Is Chosen for Real-Life Applications (3 marks)**
- **Safety certification** is often a regulatory requirement.
- **Determinism** is needed for hard real-time deadlines.
- **Small footprint** fits on resource-constrained MCUs.
- **Royalty-free** licensing (for the book + source code) makes it economical for commercial products.

**Industry Example 1 — Medical Infusion Pump (2 marks)**
- **Application:** Patient-controlled analgesia pump that delivers morphine at a precise rate.
- **Why μC/OS-II?** The pump must deliver the dose **at exactly the right time**, even if a button is pressed simultaneously. Failure to do so could result in overdose.
- **Why specifically μC/OS-II?** It is **certifiable to FDA 510(k)** standards — no other free/open RTOS at the time had this pedigree. The kernel has a **bounded interrupt disable time (< 200 cycles)** which keeps the worst-case response time short and predictable.

**Industry Example 2 — Avionics Flight Management System (2 marks)**
- **Application:** A general aviation FMS that integrates GPS, INS, and radio navigation.
- **Why μC/OS-II?** The FMS must update displays, compute position, and warn of terrain within **50 ms** deadlines. μC/OS-II is **certifiable to DO-178B Level A** — the highest criticality level for avionics software.
- **Why specifically μC/OS-II?** The **priority-based preemptive scheduler** guarantees that the terrain-warning task always preempts the route-display task if a conflict occurs. The **priority inheritance mutex** prevents unbounded priority inversion when multiple subsystems share a common bus (e.g., ARINC 429).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-YIELD BULLET POINTS — Read this the night before the exam**

- μC/OS-II is a **preemptive, priority-based, deterministic, portable RTOS** kernel written by **Jean Labrosse**.
- It is written in **ANSI C** with a small **assembly port layer** — making it portable to 100+ CPUs.
- **Maximum tasks:** **250**; **Maximum priority levels:** **256** (0 = highest).
- Each task has its own **TCB (Task Control Block)** and its own **private stack**.
- **Five task states:** Dormant, Ready, Running, Waiting, Interrupted.
- The **scheduler** uses a **two-level bitmap (OSRdyGrp + OSRdyTbl[])** for **O(1)** lookup of the highest-priority ready task.
- **System tick** is a periodic interrupt (typically 1 ms to 100 ms) that drives time delays and round-robin time-slicing.
- **Preemption** means a higher-priority ready task always preempts the running task — no delay, no negotiation.
- **Round-robin** applies only to tasks of **equal priority** — each gets one time quantum (default = 1 tick).
- **Context switch** time is the sum of save + restore + scheduler time, typically **10–45 μs** on 100 MHz CPUs.
- **Determinism** = every kernel service has a **bounded worst-case execution time**. This is why μC/OS-II is **certifiable**.
- **Priority inheritance mutex** prevents unbounded priority inversion — available in μC/OS-II via `OSMutexCreate()`.
- **IPC primitives:** Semaphore, Mutex, Event Flag, Mailbox, Message Queue.
- **Scalability** is achieved by **compile-time configuration** in `OS_CFG.H` — disable what you do not need.
- **Real-life applications** include medical devices, avionics, automotive ECUs, industrial control, IoT edge devices.
- The kernel is **ROMable** — code can execute in-place from flash/ROM.
- **Licensing:** μC/OS-II source is provided with the book; μC/OS-III is commercial; both are royalty-free once licensed.
- **Critical scheduling formulas:**
  - $T_{tick} = 1 / f_{tick}$
  - $T_{cs} = (N_{save} + N_{sched} + N_{restore}) \times T_{clk}$
  - $U_{bound}(n) = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ — Liu & Layland sufficient schedulability test.
  - $U = \sum C_i / T_i \le U_{bound}(n) \implies$ schedulable.
- **Always draw the task state diagram** in scheduling questions — it always fetches 2–3 marks.
- **Remember:** Priority 0 is the HIGHEST priority in μC/OS-II (not the lowest, as in some other systems).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adaptive Cruise Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adaptive Cruise Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** is an advanced driver-assistance system (ADAS) that extends the functionality of conventional cruise control by automatically adjusting the host vehicle's longitudinal speed to maintain a driver-specified *time headway* or *safe inter-vehicle distance* from a preceding target vehicle. In the context of Real Time Operating Systems (RTOS), ACC is a canonical **safety-critical, hard real-time embedded control application** that must execute periodic sensing, computation, and actuation tasks within *deterministic deadlines* — typically in the order of **10 ms to 100 ms** control loop periods.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Mapping)**
> Adaptive Cruise Control is treated under Module 4 as a representative *automotive embedded case study* for demonstrating RTOS concepts such as periodic task scheduling, inter-task communication, deadline-driven execution, sensor fusion, and fault tolerance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking on a busy footpath at a steady pace. Suddenly, the person in front of you slows down. Without thinking, you automatically:

1. **Sense** the change (you see them slow down).
2. **Decide** how much to slow down (you judge the gap and your walking speed).
3. **Act** by reducing your own pace — all within a fraction of a second.

If the person speeds up again, you speed up to maintain the comfortable gap. Now imagine this same "judge-and-react" cycle being done **20 to 100 times per second** by a tiny onboard computer using radar, cameras, and brake/throttle actuators — that is precisely what ACC does inside a modern car, only it must do it *far more reliably* than a human, because a delayed reaction could mean a collision.

## 1.3 System-Level Components at a Glance

| Component | Role | Typical Real-Time Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| Radar / Lidar / Camera Sensor | Measures relative distance and velocity to lead vehicle | Sampling period $\leq 50$ ms |
| Wheel Speed Sensor (WSS) | Measures host vehicle velocity | Sampling period $\leq 10$ ms |
| Electronic Control Unit (ECU) | Runs the RTOS and ACC control law | Worst-case execution time $\leq 5$ ms |
| Throttle Actuator | Applies longitudinal acceleration | Actuation latency $\leq 20$ ms |
| Brake Actuator (ESP/ABS interface) | Applies deceleration | Actuation latency $\leq 20$ ms |
| HMI / Display | Driver interface and set-speed | Refresh $\leq 100$ ms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The hard real-time deadlines in ACC are derived from the **worst-case time-to-collision (TTC)**. At 120 km/h, a vehicle covers **33.3 m every second** — so missing a 100 ms deadline means being off by **3.3 m** in braking distance estimation. This is why deterministic RTOS scheduling is non-negotiable.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Set Speed ($v_{set}$)** — driver-desired cruising speed in m/s.
- **Time Headway ($t_h$)** — desired time gap to the lead vehicle, typically **1.0 s to 2.5 s**.
- **Following Distance ($d_{safe}$)** — the safety gap to be maintained.
- **Maximum Deceleration ($a_{max}$)** — limited to approximately **$-3 \text{ m/s}^2$** for comfort and tire-road friction limits.
- **Control Loop Period ($T_s$)** — typically **$\mathbf{20 \text{ ms}}$ to $\mathbf{50 \text{ ms}}$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The ACC system operates as a closed-loop, multi-rate, multi-task real-time control system. The standard execution flow is:

1. **Sensor Acquisition Task** — Triggered periodically (e.g., every 20 ms) by an RTOS timer interrupt. Reads radar, lidar, and wheel-speed data through CAN/Ethernet buses.
2. **Target Identification & Tracking Task** — Filters raw sensor data (using Kalman filters) to estimate the lead vehicle's relative position and velocity. Typically runs every 40 ms.
3. **Mode Decision Task** — Decides whether the system is in *speed-control mode* (no lead vehicle) or *distance-control mode* (lead vehicle detected). Runs every 20 ms.
4. **Control Law Computation Task** — Computes the desired acceleration $a_{des}$ using the safety-distance error. Hard deadline of **5–10 ms**.
5. **Actuator Command Task** — Sends throttle/brake commands to the vehicle's powertrain and chassis ECUs. Hard deadline of **5 ms**.
6. **HMI / Driver Feedback Task** — Soft real-time, updates the dashboard display. Deadline **100 ms** is acceptable.

> [!NOTE]
> All tasks above are scheduled by the RTOS using either **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** for fixed priorities or **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** for dynamic priority assignment. ACC is a textbook example used in KTU lectures to demonstrate the *Liu & Layland* task model.

## 2.2 The Two Operating Modes

### Mode A — Cruise (Speed) Control Mode
When no lead vehicle is detected within radar range (typically **$R_{max} = 200$ m**), ACC behaves like a classical cruise controller. The objective is to drive the host vehicle speed error to zero:

$$e_v(t) = v_{set} - v_{host}(t)$$

A standard PI (Proportional-Integral) controller is typically used:

$$a_{des}(t) = K_p \cdot e_v(t) + K_i \int_0^t e_v(\tau) d\tau$$

### Mode B — Following (Distance) Control Mode
When a lead vehicle is detected, ACC switches to distance regulation. The controlled variable becomes the *spacing error*:

$$e_d(t) = d_{measured}(t) - d_{safe}(t)$$

where the safe following distance follows a **constant time headway (CTH) policy**:

$$d_{safe}(t) = d_0 + t_h \cdot v_{host}(t)$$

with $d_0$ being the standstill gap (typically **2 m to 5 m**).

## 2.3 Why a Constant Time Headway (CTH) Policy?

The CTH policy is preferred over a *constant spacing* policy because it scales the safety distance proportionally to speed. At higher speeds, a larger absolute gap is needed; at lower speeds, a smaller gap suffices. This is a direct consequence of basic kinematics:

$$\text{Stopping distance} \approx \frac{v^2}{2 \cdot \vert a_{brake} \vert}$$

The required gap must grow quadratically with speed for safety, but CTH gives a linear growth — a *practical compromise* between safety and traffic throughput.

## 2.4 Real-Time Scheduling Constraint — Utilization Bound

For a set of $n$ periodic real-time tasks scheduled under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**, Liu & Layland's sufficient (not necessary) utilization bound is:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \cdot \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

For large $n$, this bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$. For ACC's typical 5–6 tasks, the bound is approximately **0.73**. The system designer must verify:

$$U_{ACC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq 0.73$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $U_{ACC} > 0.73$, RMS *may still schedule feasibly*, but the guarantee is lost. For ACC's safety-criticality, KTU expects students to use the **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** equation for hard guarantees:
> $$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j, \quad \text{with feasibility} \ R_i \leq D_i$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Safe Following Distance (CTH) | $d_{safe} = d_0 + t_h \cdot v_{host}$ | meters; $d_0 \in [2, 5]$ m, $t_h \in [1.0, 2.5]$ s |
| 2 | Spacing Error | $e_d = d_{measured} - d_{safe}$ | meters |
| 3 | Speed Error | $e_v = v_{set} - v_{host}$ | m/s |
| 4 | Relative Velocity | $\Delta v = v_{lead} - v_{host}$ | m/s |
| 5 | Time-to-Collision (TTC) | $TTC = \dfrac{d_{measured}}{\vert \Delta v \vert}$ | seconds; alarm if $TTC < 2$ s |
| 6 | Desired Acceleration (PD form) | $a_{des} = K_p e_d + K_d \Delta v$ | m/s²; clamped to $[\,a_{min},\, a_{max}\,]$ |
| 7 | PI Cruise Law | $a_{des} = K_p e_v + K_i \int e_v \, dt$ | m/s² |
| 8 | RMS Utilization Bound | $U = \sum C_i / T_i \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Response Time Equation | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$ | must be $\leq D_i$ |
| 10 | Stopping Distance (kinematic) | $d_{stop} = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \vert a_{brake} \vert}$ | meters |
| 11 | Maximum Sampling Period | $T_s^{max} \approx \dfrac{0.1 \cdot v_{host}}{a_{max}}$ | s; rule-of-thumb |
| 12 | Jitter Bound (actuator) | $J_{act} \leq \dfrac{\Delta a_{max}}{a_{max}} \cdot T_s$ | affects comfort |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

ACC is the *direct technological precursor* to **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)** and **Level 3 Autonomous Driving**. The same RTOS scheduling principles used in ACC are now applied in:

- **Avionics flight-control systems** (DO-178C compliance).
- **Industrial robotic arms** with deterministic motion control.
- **Medical infusion pumps** requiring hard real-time guarantees.
- **Spacecraft attitude determination** in CubeSat missions.

In every case, the *Liu & Layland periodic task model* used for ACC remains the analytical backbone taught in KTU's PEAET754 curriculum.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Safe Following Distance (CTH Policy)

### Given
- Host vehicle velocity $v_{host}(t)$ in m/s.
- Driver-selected time headway $t_h$ in seconds.
- Standstill gap $d_0$ in meters.

### Goal
Derive the expression for $d_{safe}(t)$ such that if the lead vehicle performs an emergency brake at deceleration $a_{brake}$, the host vehicle can also brake from $v_{host}$ to rest without collision.

### Step 1 — Kinematic Stopping Distance
From constant-deceleration kinematics, the distance required to bring a vehicle from speed $v$ to rest is:

$$d_{stop} = \frac{v^2}{2 \, \vert a_{brake} \vert}$$

### Step 2 — Apply to Both Vehicles
Assume the lead vehicle brakes with deceleration $a_L$ and the host with $a_H$ (both magnitudes). The lead vehicle travels a distance $d_L$ before stopping; the host travels $d_H$:

$$d_L = \frac{v_{lead}^2}{2 \, a_L}, \qquad d_H = \frac{v_{host}^2}{2 \, a_H}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Required Gap
To avoid collision, the gap $d_{safe}$ must satisfy:

$$d_{measured} + d_L - d_H \geq 0 \quad \text{at the moment of rest}$$

Rearranging for the *minimum initial gap*:

$$d_{safe}^{\min} = d_H - d_L = \frac{v_{host}^2}{2 a_H} - \frac{v_{lead}^2}{2 a_L}$$

### Step 4 — Linearization for Engineering Use
The above quadratic form is overly conservative and uncomfortable. For practical ACC, the industry uses a *linear approximation* — the **Constant Time Headway (CTH)** policy:

$$\boxed{\, d_{safe}(t) = d_0 + t_h \cdot v_{host}(t) \,}$$

where the recommended $t_h$ is calibrated so that the linear envelope stays above the quadratic safety curve across the operating speed envelope (typically verified via Monte-Carlo simulation).

> [!NOTE]
> The choice $t_h = 1.5$ s is a KTU-favoured exam value because it represents the median human driver reaction time. Commercial systems like Bosch and Continental use $t_h \in [1.0, 2.2]$ s, configurable by the driver.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Time-to-Collision (TTC)

### Given
- Measured distance to lead vehicle $d_{measured}(t)$ in meters.
- Closing (relative) velocity $\vert \Delta v(t) \vert$ in m/s, where $\Delta v = v_{lead} - v_{host}$.

### Step 1 — Definition
TTC is the time remaining before the two vehicles would collide if both maintain their current velocities and headings.

### Step 2 — Kinematic Closing Equation
The closing rate of the gap is $-\Delta v$ (negative means approaching). The gap closes to zero when:

$$d_{measured}(t) + \int_t^{t+TTC} \Delta v(\tau) \, d\tau = 0$$

### Step 3 — Constant Velocity Assumption
For short horizons (under ~3 s), assume $\Delta v$ is approximately constant. The integral simplifies to $\Delta v \cdot TTC$:

$$d_{measured} + \Delta v \cdot TTC = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve for TTC
Rearranging gives:

$$\boxed{\, TTC = \frac{d_{measured}}{\vert \Delta v \vert} \,}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Threshold
A typical KTU and OEM-accepted warning threshold is:

$$TTC \leq 2.0 \text{ s} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Activate warning / pre-charge brakes}$$
$$TTC \leq 1.4 \text{ s} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Trigger Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The denominator $\vert \Delta v \vert$ uses absolute value to ensure TTC is always positive. In KTU answer scripts, always write the absolute value explicitly as `\vert \Delta v \vert` — the bare pipe symbol `|...|` breaks LaTeX tables.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Desired Acceleration (PD Control Law)

### Given
- Spacing error $e_d = d_{measured} - d_{safe}$.
- Closing rate $\Delta v = v_{lead} - v_{host}$ (negative when closing).
- Controller gains $K_p > 0$ and $K_d > 0$.

### Step 1 — Proportional Term
A pure P-controller would give $a = K_p e_d$. This is stable but produces a *steady-state offset* and slow response.

### Step 2 — Derivative (Damping) Term
Adding a term proportional to the rate of change of error gives damping and faster convergence. Since $\dfrac{de_d}{dt} \approx \Delta v$ in the relative-motion frame, we set:

$$a_{des}(t) = K_p \cdot e_d(t) + K_d \cdot \Delta v(t)$$

### Step 3 — Saturation (Anti-Windup & Comfort)
Real actuators cannot deliver unlimited acceleration. The command is clamped:

$$a_{cmd}(t) = \text{clamp}\bigl( a_{des}(t),\ a_{min},\ a_{max} \bigr)$$

with typical values $a_{min} \approx -3 \text{ m/s}^2$ (decel) and $a_{max} \approx +2 \text{ m/s}^2$ (accel).

### Step 4 — Closed-Loop Stability Check
Substituting the linearized error dynamics and applying the Routh-Hurwitz criterion (a typical KTU Part B sub-question) yields the stability condition:

$$\boxed{\, K_d > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad K_p > 0 \,}$$

and for second-order underdamped response, a *comfortable* damping ratio $\zeta \in [0.7, 1.0]$ is achieved by tuning:

$$\zeta = \frac{K_d + t_h}{2 \sqrt{K_p}}$$

### Step 5 — Final Closed-Form

$$\boxed{\, a_{cmd}(t) = \text{clamp}\!\Bigl( K_p \bigl[ d_{measured} - d_0 - t_h v_{host} \bigr] + K_d \bigl[ v_{lead} - v_{host} \bigr],\ a_{min},\ a_{max} \Bigr) \,}$$

This single equation is the **heart of every KTU Part B 14-mark question on ACC** and students are expected to derive it step-by-step.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A host vehicle is travelling at $v_{host} = 25$ m/s (90 km/h). A lead vehicle is detected at $d_{measured} = 40$ m, moving at $v_{lead} = 20$ m/s. Given $d_0 = 3$ m, $t_h = 1.5$ s, $K_p = 0.4$, $K_d = 0.7$. Compute $d_{safe}$, $e_d$, $TTC$, and $a_{cmd}$.

### Step 1 — Safe Distance
$$d_{safe} = d_0 + t_h \cdot v_{host} = 3 + 1.5 \times 25 = 3 + 37.5 = 40.5 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2 — Spacing Error
$$e_d = d_{measured} - d_{safe} = 40 - 40.5 = -0.5 \text{ m}$$

(Negative ⇒ host is closer than safe; must decelerate.)

### Step 3 — Time-to-Collision
$$\Delta v = v_{lead} - v_{host} = 20 - 25 = -5 \text{ m/s (closing)}$$

$$TTC = \frac{d_{measured}}{\vert \Delta v \vert} = \frac{40}{5} = 8 \text{ s}$$

(Safe; well above 2 s alarm threshold.)

### Step 4 — Desired Acceleration
$$a_{des} = K_p \cdot e_d + K_d \cdot \Delta v$$

$$a_{des} = 0.4 \times (-0.5) + 0.7 \times (-5)$$

$$a_{des} = -0.2 + (-3.5) = -3.7 \text{ m/s}^2$$

### Step 5 — Saturation
With $a_{min} = -3 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$a_{cmd} = \text{clamp}(-3.7,\ -3,\ +2) = -3.0 \text{ m/s}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Students often forget the saturation step. In KTU valuation, missing the clamp costs **2 marks** on a 7-mark sub-part. Always end acceleration answers with the clamped value.

---

## 3.5 Response Time Analysis (RTA) for ACC Task Set

### Task Set
| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) | Deadline $D_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor Read | 20 | 2 | 20 |
| Target Track | 40 | 4 | 40 |
| Control Law | 20 | 3 | 20 |
| Actuate | 20 | 2 | 20 |
| HMI | 100 | 5 | 100 |

### Step 1 — Utilization
$$U = \frac{2}{20} + \frac{4}{40} + \frac{3}{20} + \frac{2}{20} + \frac{5}{100}$$

$$U = 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.50$$

Since $U = 0.50 \leq 0.693$, RMS feasibility is guaranteed by the Liu & Layland bound.

### Step 2 — Worst-Case Response Time (HMI task, lowest priority)
$$R_{HMI} = 5 + \left\lceil \frac{R_{HMI}}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_{HMI}}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_{HMI}}{40} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{R_{HMI}}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

Iteratively, $R_{HMI}$ converges to **15 ms**, which is well below $D_{HMI} = 100$ ms.

The system is **schedulable under RMS**, confirming the ACC design is hard real-time feasible.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the ACC System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Driver Input and HMI Panel] --> nodeB[Set Speed and Time Headway]
    nodeB --> nodeC[ACC Mode Manager]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Sensor Acquisition Task]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Target Tracking and Fusion]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Control Law Computation]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Actuator Command Dispatcher]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Diagnostics and HMI Update]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeI[Throttle Actuator]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Brake Actuator ESP ABS]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Host Vehicle Dynamics]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Wheel Speed Sensor]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Radar Lidar Camera]
    nodeL --> nodeD
    nodeM --> nodeD
    nodeH --> nodeA
```

## 4.2 RTOS Task Scheduling Timeline (Rate Monotonic)

```mermaid
gantt
    title ACC Task Schedule on a Single-Core ECU under RMS
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S
    section High Priority
    Sensor Read (T=20ms)      :a1, 0, 20
    Control Law (T=20ms)      :a2, 0, 20
    Actuate (T=20ms)          :a3, 0, 20
    section Mid Priority
    Target Track (T=40ms)     :a4, 0, 40
    section Low Priority
    HMI Update (T=100ms)      :a5, 0, 100
```

## 4.3 ACC State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ACC_Off
    ACC_Off --> ACC_Standby : Driver presses ON
    ACC_Standby --> Speed_Mode : No lead vehicle detected
    ACC_Standby --> Follow_Mode : Lead vehicle detected
    Speed_Mode --> Follow_Mode : Lead vehicle enters radar range
    Follow_Mode --> Speed_Mode : Lead vehicle leaves radar range
    Speed_Mode --> ACC_Standby : Driver brake or cancel
    Follow_Mode --> ACC_Standby : Driver brake or cancel
    Speed_Mode --> EmergencyBrake : TTC below 1.4 s
    Follow_Mode --> EmergencyBrake : TTC below 1.4 s
    EmergencyBrake --> ACC_Standby : Hazard cleared
    ACC_Standby --> ACC_Off : Driver presses OFF
    ACC_Off --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Sensor Fusion and Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS1[Radar Front Sensor 77 GHz] --> nodeF1[Pre-Processor]
    nodeS2[Lidar Unit Optional] --> nodeF1
    nodeS3[Front Camera Mobileye] --> nodeF1
    nodeS4[Wheel Speed Sensor via CAN] --> nodeF1
    nodeF1 --> nodeF2[Kalman Filter Tracker]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3[Object List Lead Vehicle]
    nodeF3 --> nodeF4[Safety Distance Calculator]
    nodeF4 --> nodeF5[Control Law PI or PD]
    nodeF5 --> nodeF6[Actuator Arbitration]
    nodeF6 --> nodeF7[Throttle via CAN]
    nodeF6 --> nodeF8[Brake via CAN to ESP]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All four diagrams above represent **block-level functional architecture** rather than physical wiring diagrams, in line with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguard for complex physical schematics. Each block corresponds to a real RTOS task and is schedulable using the formulas in Section 2.5.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between conventional Cruise Control and Adaptive Cruise Control. List the major sensors used in an ACC system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Conventional CC | Adaptive CC |
|---|---|---|
| Speed Adjustment | Manual only; driver must brake/cancel if vehicle ahead slows | Automatic longitudinal adjustment using sensor feedback |
| Sensors | Throttle actuator, wheel speed | Radar, lidar, camera, wheel speed, ESP interface |
| Operating Mode | Single (speed-hold) | Dual (speed mode + following mode) |
| Safety | Driver's responsibility | System actively maintains safe headway |

Sensors: **Radar (77 GHz), Lidar, Front camera, Wheel speed sensor, Yaw-rate sensor**.

*(1 mark differentiation table, 1 mark sensors list, 1 mark diagram mention)*

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define Time Headway and Time-to-Collision (TTC). Why is TTC a critical real-time metric in ACC?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Time Headway ($t_h$):** The desired time gap, in seconds, between the host vehicle and the lead vehicle, used in the CTH policy $d_{safe} = d_0 + t_h \cdot v_{host}$. **(1 mark)**
- **TTC:** The estimated time before a collision occurs if both vehicles maintain current speed and heading, given by $TTC = d_{measured} / \vert \Delta v \vert$. **(1 mark)**
- **Why critical:** TTC directly drives the RTOS task deadlines and the AEB activation threshold. At 120 km/h, every 100 ms of sensing delay changes TTC by ~1 s, which can be the difference between a safe brake and a collision. Hence, RTOS tasks must be hard real-time. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an Adaptive Cruise Control system implemented on an RTOS-based ECU. List all real-time tasks, their typical periods, and deadlines. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the constant time headway (CTH) safe-distance formula. A host vehicle is moving at $v_{host} = 30$ m/s. The lead vehicle is at $d = 45$ m and moving at $v_{lead} = 22$ m/s. Given $d_0 = 4$ m, $t_h = 1.8$ s, compute the spacing error, the time-to-collision, and the desired acceleration using a PD controller with $K_p = 0.5$ and $K_d = 0.8$. Apply saturation at $a_{min} = -3$ m/s² and $a_{max} = +2$ m/s². **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the functional architecture showing *Driver HMI → ACC Mode Manager → Sensor Task → Tracker Task → Control Task → Actuator Task → Vehicle Dynamics → Sensors (feedback loop)*. Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1.

2. **RTOS Task Table (3 marks):**

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Deadline $D_i$ (ms) | Priority (RMS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor Read | 20 | 20 | Highest |
| Target Track | 40 | 40 | Mid |
| Control Law | 20 | 20 | Highest |
| Actuate | 20 | 20 | Highest |
| HMI | 100 | 100 | Lowest |

3. **Scheduling Discussion (1 mark):** Mention Liu & Layland bound, verify $U \leq 0.693$ for feasibility, and note that the system uses **preemptive fixed-priority scheduling** typical of OSEK/VDX or AUTOSAR OS.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Safe distance (1 mark):**
$$d_{safe} = d_0 + t_h \cdot v_{host} = 4 + 1.8 \times 30 = 4 + 54 = 58 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Spacing error (1 mark):**
$$e_d = d_{measured} - d_{safe} = 45 - 58 = -13 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Relative velocity and TTC (1.5 marks):**
$$\Delta v = v_{lead} - v_{host} = 22 - 30 = -8 \text{ m/s}$$

$$TTC = \frac{45}{\vert -8 \vert} = 5.625 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Desired acceleration (2 marks):**
$$a_{des} = K_p \cdot e_d + K_d \cdot \Delta v = 0.5 \times (-13) + 0.8 \times (-8)$$

$$a_{des} = -6.5 + (-6.4) = -12.9 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Saturation (1.5 marks):**
$$a_{cmd} = \text{clamp}(-12.9,\ -3,\ +2) = -3.0 \text{ m/s}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):**
> - Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s costs **2 marks**.
> - Skipping the saturation step costs **1.5 marks**.
> - Writing $\vert$ instead of `\vert` or `\mid` in the TTC formula will not be penalized in math, but consistency is expected.
> - Failing to state units (m, m/s, m/s²) at every line costs **1 mark** cumulatively.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2025, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) algorithm. For the ACC task set below, verify schedulability using both the Liu & Layland utilization bound and the Response Time Analysis (RTA) method. **(7 Marks)**

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ Sensor | 2 | 20 | 20 |
| $\tau_2$ Control | 3 | 20 | 20 |
| $\tau_3$ Track | 4 | 40 | 40 |
| $\tau_4$ HMI | 5 | 100 | 100 |

**(b)** Discuss the safety implications of ACC as a hard real-time system. What is the worst-case deadline miss impact at 120 km/h, and how does the RTOS mitigate it? Mention any two fault-tolerance techniques used in production ACC ECUs. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Liu & Layland Bound (2 marks):**
$$U = \frac{2}{20} + \frac{3}{20} + \frac{4}{40} + \frac{5}{100} = 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.40$$

$$U_{bound} = 4 \cdot (2^{1/4} - 1) = 4 \cdot (1.1892 - 1) = 4 \cdot 0.1892 = 0.757$$

Since $U = 0.40 \leq 0.757$, the system is **guaranteed schedulable under RMS**.

**Step 2 — RTA for $\tau_4$ (HMI, lowest priority) (3 marks):**

Initial guess $R_4^{(0)} = C_4 = 5$ ms.

Iteration 1:
$$R_4^{(1)} = 5 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{40} \right\rceil \cdot 4$$
$$R_4^{(1)} = 5 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot 4 = 14 \text{ ms}$$

Iteration 2:
$$R_4^{(2)} = 5 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{40} \right\rceil \cdot 4$$
$$R_4^{(2)} = 5 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot 4 = 14 \text{ ms}$$

Converged: $R_4 = 14 \text{ ms} \leq D_4 = 100$ ms. ✔

**Step 3 — RTA for $\tau_3$ (1 mark):**
$$R_3 = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 2 + 3 = 9 \text{ ms} \leq 40 \text{ ms}$$ ✔

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):** All tasks meet their deadlines; the ACC task set is **hard real-time schedulable** under RMS.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Hard real-time classification (1 mark):** ACC is a **safety-critical hard real-time system** because missing a deadline can directly cause loss of life.

**Step 2 — Worst-case impact at 120 km/h (2 marks):**
$$v = 120 \text{ km/h} = 33.33 \text{ m/s}$$

A 100 ms deadline miss results in:
$$\Delta d = v \cdot \Delta t = 33.33 \times 0.1 = 3.33 \text{ m}$$

This is **larger than the standstill gap $d_0$**, meaning a single missed sensor reading can cause a collision in dense traffic. Hence deadlines must be **hard and verified at design time**.

**Step 3 — RTOS mitigations (2 marks):**
- **Preemptive fixed-priority scheduling** (RMS) ensures the control law task always preempts lower-priority work.
- **Watchdog timers** reset the ECU if any task overruns its budget.
- **Time-triggered architecture (TTA)** like AUTOSAR OS provides deterministic jitter bounds.

**Step 4 — Fault-tolerance techniques (2 marks):**
1. **Dual-channel redundant sensors** (two independent radar units with voting logic).
2. **Plausibility checks and sensor fusion** with Kalman filter consistency tests to reject outliers.
3. **Graceful degradation** — fall back to conventional cruise or warn driver if ACC self-test fails.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Choice B):**
> - In RTA, students often forget to **iterate** until convergence. The first computation alone is acceptable only if it already exceeds $D_i$ (proving infeasibility). For feasible systems, **iterate at least twice** to demonstrate convergence — KTU awards **1 mark** for showing iteration explicitly.
> - Mixing up $\Delta v$ sign convention costs 1 mark. Define it once and stick to it: $\Delta v = v_{lead} - v_{host}$, *negative means closing*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ACC = Cruise Control + Radar/Lidar + RTOS-based longitudinal control.** It is the canonical *automotive case study* for RTOS in KTU Module 4.
- **Two modes:** *Speed mode* (PI controller on speed error) and *Following mode* (PD controller on spacing error with CTH policy).
- **CTH policy:** $d_{safe} = d_0 + t_h \cdot v_{host}$, with $d_0 \in [2,5]$ m and $t_h \in [1.0, 2.5]$ s.
- **TTC formula:** $TTC = d_{measured} / \vert \Delta v \vert$ — alarm at 2 s, AEB at 1.4 s.
- **PD control law:** $a_{cmd} = \text{clamp}(K_p e_d + K_d \Delta v,\ a_{min},\ a_{max})$.
- **Hard real-time deadlines:** Control loop 20–50 ms; sensor read ≤ 50 ms; actuator command ≤ 20 ms.
- **Liu & Layland bound for $n$ tasks:** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. For ACC, $n=5$ gives $\approx 0.74$.
- **RTA equation:** $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$, feasibility iff $R_i \leq D_i$.
- **Saturation is mandatory** in every ACC acceleration answer — forgetting it costs 1–2 marks.
- **At 120 km/h, 100 ms = 3.33 m** — this single fact is used in nearly every KTU Part B question on ACC.
- **Fault tolerance:** sensor redundancy, plausibility checks, graceful degradation, watchdog timers.
- **Industry context:** AUTOSAR OS, OSEK/VDX, and ISO 26262 (ASIL-B or ASIL-D) compliance govern all production ACC implementations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practical Implementation Challenges and Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET754 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Practical Implementation Challenges and Solutions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Practical Implementation Challenges and Solutions** in the context of Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) refers to the systematic study of the engineering difficulties encountered when deploying a real-time kernel onto constrained embedded hardware, along with the verified algorithmic, architectural, and software-engineering techniques used to mitigate them. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEAET754**, these challenges span five canonical domains: 1. **Temporal Correctness Issues** — Priority Inversion, Deadlock, Jitter, Deadline Misses. 2. **Resource Contention** — Shared peripherals, mutually exclusive I/O buses (I²C, SPI, CAN), DMA channels. 3. **Memory Subsystem Issues** — Fragmentation, stack overflow, deterministic allocation. 4. **Interrupt and ISR Latency** — Non-determinism in interrupt handling, nested interrupts, interrupt storms. 5. **Power, Thermal, and Hardware Integration** — Tick suppression, low-power mode entry/exit, watchdog supervision. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > The Board Examiner expects students to map **each** challenge to a *named* protocol or pattern. Vague statements like "use semaphores" fetch zero marks. The expected phrasing is "Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) is used to bound unbounded priority inversion." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Emergency Room" Imagine a hospital Emergency Room (ER) with the following patients: - **Patient H** (High priority) — Heart attack, must be treated *now*. - **Patient M** (Medium priority) — Severe fracture, can wait 10 minutes. - **Patient L** (Low priority) — Mild fever, can wait an hour. The doctor (CPU) must respect triage. Now imagine **Patient L** is currently being treated and is holding the only **defibrillator** (shared resource). Suddenly **Patient H** arrives needing the defibrillator, but **Patient L** has not finished using it. Worse, **Patient M** keeps interrupting the doctor, pushing **Patient H's** treatment indefinitely. This is **Priority Inversion**. The hospital solution: the *Charge Nurse* enforces the **Priority Inheritance Protocol** — if a low-priority patient is blocking a high-priority one, the low-priority patient is *temporarily promoted* to high priority so they finish and release the resource quickly. This is exactly how an RTOS kernel resolves the same problem in an automotive ECU, a flight controller, or a Mars rover. ## 1.3 The Five Pillars of RTOS Implementation Challenges | Pillar | Core Question | KTU-Mandated Term | |---|---|---| | **Temporal** | Will the deadline be missed? | **Schedulability** | | **Synchronization** | Can a task block another indefinitely? | **Priority Inversion** | | **Memory** | Is `malloc()` deterministic? | **Fragmentation** | | **Interrupt** | What is the worst-case latency? | **Worst-Case Interrupt Latency (WCIL)** | | **Power** | Can we sleep and still meet deadlines? | **Tickless Idle** | > [!NOTE] > The KTU examiner will frequently ask: *"Differentiate between Priority Inversion and Deadlock."* The crisp one-liner is: **Deadlock is a permanent block (circular wait) — Priority Inversion is a temporary block whose duration is unbounded without protocol-based mitigation.** --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Priority Inversion — Deep Structural Analysis Priority inversion occurs when a **higher-priority task is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority task**, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities of the two tasks. There are three recognized forms: ### (a) Bounded Priority Inversion The blocking time is **bounded** by the duration of the lower-priority task's critical section. Acceptable in soft real-time systems. ### (b) Unbounded Priority Inversion An intermediate-priority task preempts the lower-priority task *while it holds the resource*, extending the high-priority task's wait *indefinitely*. This was the **root cause of the 1997 Mars Pathfinder reset bug**. ### (c) Priority Change Protocol Violations Improper use of `OSTaskChangePrio()` or `vTaskPrioritySet()` causing runtime priority corruption. ### Why does this happen? - Tasks share mutually exclusive resources protected by **mutexes** or **binary semaphores**. - A high-priority task **T_H** requests a resource held by **T_L**. - T_H is forced to block on the semaphore — by definition, T_H cannot run. - An intermediate-priority task **T_M** preempts T_L (perfectly legal scheduling). - T_H waits for T_M to finish, *then* T_L to release the resource. **Result:** T_H's blocking time = T_M's worst-case execution time + T_L's critical section time. The blocking time is **unbounded by any RTOS scheduling theory** unless a protocol is applied. ## 2.2 Deadlock — The Four Coffman Conditions A deadlock requires *all four* conditions (Coffman, 1971) to hold simultaneously: 1. **Mutual Exclusion** — Resource is non-sharable. 2. **Hold and Wait** — Task holds one resource while requesting another. 3. **No Preemption** — Resources cannot be forcibly taken. 4. **Circular Wait** — A cycle exists in the wait-for graph. Breaking **any one** of the four prevents deadlock. RTOS kernels typically break condition (3) using **priority ceiling**, or condition (4) using **lock ordering**. ## 2.3 Jitter and Latency — The Temporal Pillar - **Jitter (J):** Variation in the inter-arrival time of periodic events. $J = T_{max} - T_{min}$ - **Interrupt Latency (L_i):** Time from interrupt assertion to first user instruction of the ISR. $L_i = T_{recog} + T_{vec} + T_{kernel} + T_{dispatch}$ - **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET, C_i):** Upper bound on task execution time under all execution paths. - **Response Time (R_i):** Time from task release to completion. ## 2.4 Memory Fragmentation - **External Fragmentation:** Free memory exists in small, non-contiguous blocks. `malloc()`/`free()` over time leads to this. - **Internal Fragmentation:** Allocated block is larger than requested (due to alignment). - RTOS solution: **Memory Pools** (block allocators), **Static Allocation**, or **TLSF (Two-Level Segregate Fit)** algorithm. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **SAVE THIS TABLE — these are the equations that earn marks.** | # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX | Engineering Meaning | Unit / Domain | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Response Time (exact, for **fixed-priority pre-emptive** scheduling) | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Time from release of task $i$ to its completion under interference from higher-priority tasks. | Time (s, ms, µs) | | 2 | Schedulability Condition (Hard RT) | $R_i \leq D_i$ | A task is schedulable if its worst-case response time does not exceed its deadline. | Boolean | | 3 | Maximum Blocking Time (PIP/PCP bounded inversion) | $B_i = \max_{k \in lp(i)} (\text{cs}_k)$ | Worst-case blocking a high-priority task $i$ can suffer from any lower-priority task's critical section. | Time | | 4 | Unbounded Inversion (no protocol) | $B_i^{unbounded} = \sum_{k \in lp(i)} C_k$ | Without PIP/PCP, blocking can equal sum of all lower-priority WCETs. | Time | | 5 | Interrupt Latency | $L_i = T_{rec} + T_{vec} + T_{k} + T_{dsp}$ | Total time from IRQ line assertion to first ISR instruction. | Time | | 6 | Tick Jitter | $J_{tick} = \Delta t_{max} - \Delta t_{min}$ | Variation between successive system tick intervals. | Time | | 7 | CPU Utilization (Liu & Layland bound, Rate Monotonic) | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Necessary (not sufficient) utilization bound. | Dimensionless | | 8 | Priority Ceiling Value (PCP) | $P_{ceiling}(R) = \max_{i: R \in \text{used}(i)} P_i$ | Resource R inherits the maximum priority of any task that may lock it. | Priority | | 9 | Memory Pool Block Count | $N_{blocks} = \lfloor \dfrac{M_{pool}}{S_{block} + H_{overhead}} \rfloor$ | Number of allocatable blocks in a fixed-size pool. | Integer | | 10 | Stack Overflow Margin | $S_{safe} = S_{task} - S_{max\ usage}$ | Safety margin; should be $\geq 25\%$ for KTU-recommended design. | Bytes | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Conditions to memorize:** > - For $n = 1$ task, Liu & Layland bound = $1(2^{1} - 1) = 1$ (100% utilization possible). > - For $n \to \infty$, bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ (69.3%). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Response Time Analysis (RTA) Iteration **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style):** Consider three periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS). Determine if the task set is schedulable using exact Response Time Analysis. | Task | Period $T_i$ | WCET $C_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ | |---|---|---|---| | $\tau_1$ (highest) | 5 ms | 1 ms | 5 ms | | $\tau_2$ | 10 ms | 2 ms | 10 ms | | $\tau_3$ | 20 ms | 4 ms | 20 ms | ### Step 1 — Set up the iterative equation For task $\tau_3$ (lowest priority), the response time is the sum of its own execution time plus all interference from higher-priority tasks $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$. $$ R_3^{(n+1)} = C_3 + \sum_{j \in \{1,2\}} \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(n)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j $$ ### Step 2 — Initialize the iteration We start with the lower bound: $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 4$ ms. ### Step 3 — First iteration ($n = 0 \to 1$) $$ R_3^{(1)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 $$ Evaluate the ceilings one-by-one: $$ \left\lceil \frac{4}{5} \right\rceil = 1, \quad \left\lceil \frac{4}{10} \right\rceil = 1 $$ Therefore: $$ R_3^{(1)} = 4 + (1 \cdot 1) + (1 \cdot 2) = 7 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 4 — Second iteration ($n = 1 \to 2$) $$ R_3^{(2)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 $$ $$ \left\lceil \frac{7}{5} \right\rceil = 2, \quad \left\lceil \frac{7}{10} \right\rceil = 1 $$ $$ R_3^{(2)} = 4 + (2 \cdot 1) + (1 \cdot 2) = 8 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 5 — Third iteration ($n = 2 \to 3$) $$ R_3^{(3)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 $$ $$ \left\lceil \frac{8}{5} \right\rceil = 2, \quad \left\lceil \frac{8}{10} \right\rceil = 1 $$ $$ R_3^{(3)} = 4 + (2 \cdot 1) + (1 \cdot 2) = 8 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 6 — Fixed point reached & decision Since $R_3^{(3)} = R_3^{(2)} = 8$ ms, the iteration has **converged** at the fixed point $R_3 = 8$ ms. Now check the deadline condition: $$ R_3 = 8 \text{ ms} \quad \text{vs.} \quad D_3 = 20 \text{ ms} $$ $$ R_3 \leq D_3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 8 \leq 20 \quad \text{✓ SCHEDULABLE} $$ ### Step 7 — Check the other tasks (KTU examiner always tests this) For $\tau_1$: $R_1 = C_1 = 1$ ms $\leq 5$ ms ✓ For $\tau_2$: $$ R_2 = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 $$ Iteration: $R_2^{(0)} = 2$. $R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \lceil 2/5 \rceil \cdot 1 = 3$. Converged. $R_2 = 3 \leq 10$ ✓. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Verdict:** Task set is **schedulable** under RMS using exact RTA. The Liu & Layland utilization bound for $n=3$ is $3(2^{1/3}-1) = 0.7798$ or **77.98%**. Our actual utilization is $\frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{4}{20} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.60$ (60%) — well within the bound. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Bounding Unbounded Priority Inversion **Problem Statement:** Three tasks share a mutex $M$ protecting a shared I²C bus. Critical section durations: $\tau_L$ holds $M$ for 6 ms, $\tau_M$ for 4 ms, $\tau_H$ for 2 ms. Calculate (a) Bounded inversion with **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**, (b) Worst-case unbounded inversion without any protocol. ### Part (a) — With Priority Inheritance Protocol Under PIP, when $\tau_H$ blocks on mutex $M$, the kernel **temporarily raises** $\tau_L$'s priority to $P_H$ for the duration of the critical section. This prevents $\tau_M$ from preempting $\tau_L$. $$ B_H^{PIP} = \max_{k \in lp(H)} \text{cs}_k = \text{cs}_L = 6 \text{ ms} $$ ### Part (b) — Without Protocol (Unbounded) In the worst case, $\tau_L$ enters its critical section, $\tau_M$ preempts $\tau_L$ and runs to completion ($C_M = 4$ ms), then $\tau_L$ resumes and finishes its critical section (6 ms). $$ B_H^{unbounded} = C_M + \text{cs}_L = 4 + 6 = 10 \text{ ms} $$ If there were $k$ intermediate tasks, this bound would grow to: $$ B_H^{unbounded} = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} C_k + \text{cs}_L $$ This is **why PIP/PCP is non-negotiable in safety-critical systems** (DO-178C, ISO 26262 ASIL-D). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Protocol Should I Use?" | Challenge | Recommended Solution | Algorithm / API | KTU Tag | |---|---|---|---| | Unbounded Priority Inversion | **Priority Inheritance Protocol** | `pthread_mutexattr_setprotocol(PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT)` | Mandatory in POSIX RT | | System-wide deadlocks | **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** | Immediate Priority Ceiling (IPC) or Original PCP | Used in AUTOSAR OS | | Memory fragmentation | **Static / Pool Allocation** | `OSMemCreate()`, `pvPortMalloc()` from `heap_4.c` | FreeRTOS recommended | | Interrupt latency too high | **Deferred Procedure Call (DPC)** | Top-half / Bottom-half split | Linux RT, VxWorks | | Tick jitter in low power | **Tickless Idle** | `portSUPPRESS_TICKS_AND_SLEEP()` | FreeRTOS `configUSE_TICKLESS_IDLE` | | Stack overflow | **Stack Overflow Detection** | Pattern `0xA5A5A5A5` + MPU guard | FreeRTOS `configCHECK_FOR_STACK_OVERFLOW = 2` | | Task deadlock | **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** | Hardware WDT, Windowed WDT | All safety MCUs | | Shared peripheral bus | **Lock ordering + ceiling** | AUTOSAR Resource Manager | Automotive standard | | Multi-core contention | **Spinlock with preemption disable** | `OS_SPINLOCK`, `irqsave`/`irqrestore` | SMP RTOS | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Unbounded Priority Inversion — Timeline (Classic Mars Pathfinder Scenario) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant TL as Task L (Low Prio) participant TM as Task M (Mid Prio) participant TH as Task H (High Prio) participant K as RTOS Kernel Note over TL,TH: Time progresses left to right TL->>K: Acquire Mutex M K-->>TL: Granted Note over TL: TL inside critical section TH->>K: Request Mutex M (blocks) K-->>TH: Blocked on M Note over K: WITHOUT PIP, this scenario occurs: TM->>K: Preempts TL (legal - higher priority) K-->>TM: TM runs to completion Note over TH: TH forced to wait for TM TM->>K: TM completes Note over TL: TL resumes critical section TL->>K: TL releases Mutex M K-->>TH: TH finally unblocks and runs Note over TH: Worst-case blocking = C_M + cs_L = UNBOUNDED ``` ## 4.2 Solution — Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) Timeline ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant TL as Task L (boosted to P_H) participant TM as Task M participant TH as Task H participant K as RTOS Kernel TL->>K: Acquire Mutex M K-->>TL: Granted at priority P_L TH->>K: Request Mutex M K-->>TH: Blocked K->>TL: Priority RAISED to P_H (Inheritance) Note over TM: TM CANNOT preempt TL
because TL is now at P_H TL->>K: TL finishes critical section TL->>K: Releases Mutex M K->>TL: Priority RESTORED to P_L K-->>TH: TH immediately unblocks and runs Note over K,TH: Blocking time bounded by cs_L only ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robust RTOS Implementation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Application Tasks] --> B[Task Scheduler
RMS or EDF] B --> C{Resource Request} C -->|Mutex Acquire| D[Priority Ceiling
Manager] C -->|Memory Alloc| E[Memory Pool
Block Allocator] C -->|ISR Entry| F[Interrupt Service
Routine Handler] D --> G[Lock Order
Validator] E --> H[Fragmentation
Monitor] F --> I[Deferred Procedure
Call Queue] G --> J[Deadline Miss
Detector] H --> J I --> J J -->|Violation| K[Watchdog Timer
and Error Hook] J -->|OK| L[Normal Execution] K --> M[System Reset or
Safe State] ``` ## 4.4 Tickless Idle Power Management Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Idle Hook Entered] --> B{Any Task
Ready?} B -->|Yes| C[Normal Idle
No Sleep] B -->|No| D[Compute Next
Wakeup Time] D --> E[Program HW Timer
for Wakeup] E --> F[Suppress SysTick
Updates] F --> G[Enter Low Power
Mode WFI or WFE] G --> H{HW Event?} H -->|Timer Tick| I[Restore Context
Re-enable SysTick] H -->|External IRQ| I I --> J[Resume Scheduler] C --> J ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Define priority inversion. Differentiate between bounded and unbounded priority inversion.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Priority inversion is a scheduling anomaly in which a **higher-priority task is forced to wait for the execution of a lower-priority task**, thereby inverting their relative priorities due to a shared mutually-exclusive resource. - **Bounded Priority Inversion:** The blocking duration is confined to the critical section of the lower-priority task holding the resource. It is acceptable in soft real-time systems and equals $\text{cs}_L$. - **Unbounded Priority Inversion:** One or more intermediate-priority tasks preempt the lower-priority task *while it holds the resource*, causing the high-priority task's wait time to grow to $C_M + \text{cs}_L$, which is **unbounded** with respect to the system's task set cardinality. > **[1 mark]** Definition of priority inversion. **[1 mark]** Bounded definition. **[1 mark]** Unbounded definition with the formula or Mars Pathfinder reference. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024] **Q: List any three practical challenges faced when implementing an RTOS on resource-constrained embedded hardware.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Deterministic Memory Allocation** — Standard `malloc()` is non-deterministic; RTOS requires memory pools or static allocation to bound allocation time. 2. **Interrupt Latency Minimization** — Long ISRs delay high-priority tasks; top-half/bottom-half (DPC) splitting is used. 3. **Priority Inversion and Deadlock** — Shared resources introduce blocking; protocols like PIP and PCP are mandatory. 4. (Any other valid: power management, stack overflow, watchdog supervision, jitter control.) > **[1 mark]** each correctly named challenge with one-line justification. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### 📘 QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **Q: (a)** Explain the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** in detail. How does it solve unbounded priority inversion? Describe its limitations. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Consider a system with three tasks executing under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. Task $\tau_1$ has period $T_1 = 6$ ms, WCET $C_1 = 1$ ms; $\tau_2$: $T_2 = 12$ ms, $C_2 = 3$ ms; $\tau_3$: $T_3 = 24$ ms, $C_3 = 4$ ms. Deadlines equal periods. Using **exact Response Time Analysis**, determine if the task set is schedulable. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) PIP **Definition [1 mark]:** The Priority Inheritance Protocol, formalized by **Sha, Rajkumar and Lehoczky (1990)**, is a synchronization protocol for mutexes in real-time systems. When a high-priority task $H$ blocks on a mutex held by a lower-priority task $L$, the kernel **transitively raises** $L$'s effective priority to that of $H$ (or the highest priority of any task blocked on that mutex) for the duration of $L$'s critical section. **Operational Steps [3 marks]:** 1. Task $L$ acquires mutex $M$ at its original priority $P_L$. 2. Task $H$ requests $M$ and is blocked. 3. The kernel raises $L$'s priority to $P_H$ (or $\max(P_H, P_{H2}, \ldots)$ if multiple tasks are blocked). 4. $L$ inherits the new priority and completes its critical section *without preemption* by any intermediate-priority task. 5. $L$ releases $M$; priority is restored to $P_L$. 6. $H$ unblocks and proceeds. **How PIP solves unbounded inversion [2 marks]:** By boosting $L$ to $P_H$, intermediate-priority tasks $M_1, M_2, \ldots$ can no longer preempt $L$. The blocking time of $H$ is bounded to **at most the duration of $L$'s critical section**, not the sum of all intermediate tasks' WCETs. This converts unbounded blocking into bounded blocking. **Limitations [1 mark]:** - PIP does **not prevent deadlocks** in all cases (chain blocking can still occur in systems with multiple resources). - It introduces **runtime overhead** for priority manipulation. - It does not prevent **priority change-related** anomalies. - It is **insufficient** for systems requiring the stronger guarantee of PCP. #### Model Solution — Part (b) RTA **Given:** $\tau_1: (T_1=6, C_1=1)$, $\tau_2: (T_2=12, C_2=3)$, $\tau_3: (T_3=24, C_3=4)$, $D_i = T_i$. **Step 1: Check $\tau_1$ [1 mark]** $$ R_1 = C_1 = 1 \text{ ms} \leq D_1 = 6 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 2: Check $\tau_2$ [2 marks]** $$ R_2 = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 $$ Iterate: $R_2^{(0)} = 3$. $R_2^{(1)} = 3 + \lceil 3/6 \rceil \cdot 1 = 4$. Converged. $R_2 = 4 \leq D_2 = 12$ ✓ **Step 3: Check $\tau_3$ [3 marks]** $$ R_3 = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2 $$ Iterate: - $R_3^{(0)} = 4$ - $R_3^{(1)} = 4 + \lceil 4/6 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 4/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 1 + 3 = 8$ - $R_3^{(2)} = 4 + \lceil 8/6 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 8/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 2 + 3 = 9$ - $R_3^{(3)} = 4 + \lceil 9/6 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 9/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 2 + 3 = 9$ ✓ Converged **Step 4: Final decision [1 mark]** $R_3 = 9$ ms $\leq D_3 = 24$ ms ✓ **Conclusion:** The task set is **schedulable** under RMS using exact RTA. --- ### 📗 QUESTION B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **Q: (a)** Discuss **practical memory management challenges** in RTOS. Compare static allocation, memory pools, and TLSF. **[7 marks]** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**. How does it prevent both deadlock and unbounded priority inversion in a single mechanism? **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) Memory Management **Challenges [2 marks]:** 1. **Non-determinism of `malloc()`** — Standard glibc `malloc()` may take microseconds to milliseconds, violating hard real-time guarantees. 2. **External Fragmentation** — Repeated `malloc`/`free` leaves unusable gaps. 3. **Internal Fragmentation** — Block alignment padding wastes memory. 4. **Stack Overflow** — Recursion or deep call chains in tasks can corrupt adjacent memory. 5. **Memory Leaks** — In long-running embedded systems (10+ year uptime), even tiny leaks are catastrophic. **Comparison Table [4 marks]:** | Feature | Static Allocation | Memory Pool | TLSF | |---|---|---|---| | Determinism | **O(1)** (compile-time) | **O(1)** (constant) | **O(1)** (worst-case) | | Flexibility | None (fixed at compile) | Fixed block size | Variable size | | Fragmentation | None | Internal only | Minimal external | | Use Case | Safety-critical, small MCUs | Uniform-sized messages | Mixed-size alloc in RT | | Example API | `static uint8_t buf[256]` | `OSMemCreate()` (uC/OS) | `tlsf_malloc()` | **Conclusion [1 mark]:** Hard real-time systems should prefer **static allocation** or **pools**; TLSF is the best choice for variable-sized allocations with O(1) bounds. #### Model Solution — Part (b) PCP **Definition [1 mark]:** The Priority Ceiling Protocol, defined by **Sha, Rajkumar and Lehoczky (1990)**, assigns a **priority ceiling** to every shared resource. The ceiling equals the **highest priority of any task that may lock that resource**. A task may lock a resource only if its priority is **strictly higher** than the ceilings of all resources currently locked by *other* tasks. **Diagram Description [3 marks]:** ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Task Requests Mutex M] --> B{Is Own Priority
strictly greater than
all current ceilings?} B -->|Yes| C[Grant Lock
Raise task to ceil of M] B -->|No| D[Block Task
Prevent Deadlock] C --> E[Execute Critical Section] E --> F[Release Mutex M] F --> G[Restore Original Priority] D --> H[Wait until owner
releases M] H --> B ``` **Working Steps [2 marks]:** 1. Compute $P_{ceiling}(M) = \max(P_i)$ over all tasks $i$ that may lock $M$. 2. When $\tau_i$ requests $M$, the kernel checks: $P_i > \max(\text{all currently held ceilings})$. 3. If yes → grant lock and raise $\tau_i$'s effective priority to $P_{ceiling}(M)$. 4. If no → block $\tau_i$ (this *prevents* the circular-wait condition). 5. On release, restore priority and re-evaluate pending requests. **Why PCP prevents both deadlock and unbounded inversion [1 mark]:** - **No Deadlock:** A task can never wait for a resource held by a *lower* or *equal* priority task whose ceiling blocks it; circular wait is structurally impossible. - **No Unbounded Inversion:** The ceiling mechanism ensures a task is blocked by **at most one critical section** of a lower-priority task, bounding the inversion to $\max(\text{cs}_k)$ for $k \in lp(i)$. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting the iteration convergence check:** RTA must be iterated until $R_i^{(n+1)} = R_i^{(n)}$. Students who stop after 1–2 iterations without convergence lose 2–3 marks. > 2. **Confusing PIP with PCP:** PIP only bounds blocking; **PCP also prevents deadlock**. The two are not interchangeable. > 3. **Omitting units in formulas:** Always write "ms" or "µs" after numerical results in RTA problems — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units. > 4. **Using vague verbs:** Never write "use a mutex to solve priority inversion." Write "**apply the Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) to bound blocking to the duration of the lower-priority task's critical section**." Specificity = marks. > 5. **Skipping the response time check for high-priority tasks:** Students often only compute $R_3$ and forget $R_1$ and $R_2$. **All tasks must be checked.** > 6. **In RTA iteration, the ceiling function is `⌈ ⌉`, NOT rounding to nearest.** KTU examiners test this specifically. > 7. **Do not write "deadlock = priority inversion"** — this is the most common conceptual error. They are fundamentally different (permanent vs. temporary blocking). --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST Use this section as your **last-night revision sheet** before the KTU exam. ### ✅ Must-Know Definitions - [ ] **Priority Inversion** (bounded vs. unbounded) - [ ] **Deadlock** (Coffman's 4 conditions) - [ ] **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** — Sha et al., 1990 - [ ] **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** — Sha et al., 1990 - [ ] **Tickless Idle** — for low-power RTOS - [ ] **Deferred Procedure Call (DPC)** — top-half/bottom-half ISR split - [ ] **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** — including windowed WDT - [ ] **Memory Pool** — block allocator with O(1) determinism - [ ] **TLSF** — Two-Level Segregate Fit, O(1) variable-size allocator - [ ] **Stack overflow detection** via pattern `0xA5A5A5A5` ### ✅ Must-Memorize Formulas - [ ] $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$ - [ ] $R_i \leq D_i$ (schedulability test) - [ ] $B_i^{PIP} = \max_{k \in lp(i)} cs_k$ - [ ] $B_i^{unbounded} = \sum_{k \in lp(i)} C_k$ - [ ] $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ (Liu & Layland RMS bound) - [ ] $J = T_{max} - T_{min}$ (jitter definition) - [ ] $P_{ceiling}(R) = \max_{i: R \in \text{used}(i)} P_i$ ### ✅ Must-Recognize Patterns - [ ] Three-task timing diagram of Mars Pathfinder-style priority inversion - [ ] PIP timeline showing priority boost + restoration - [ ] PCP lock-acquisition gate logic - [ ] Tickless idle state machine ### ✅ Must-Name Standards - [ ] **POSIX 1003.1b/c** — real-time extensions - [ ] **AUTOSAR OS** — automotive RTOS spec (uses PCP) - [ ] **DO-178C** — avionics software standard - [ ] **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** — automotive functional safety - [ ] **OSEK/VDX** — legacy automotive RTOS standard - [ ] **FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS** — example kernels ### ✅ Common 14-Mark Question Templates 1. "Explain PIP/PCP with diagram and one worked example" → 7 marks theory + 7 marks example. 2. "Apply RTA to a 3-task system and conclude schedulability" → full iteration required. 3. "Compare memory allocation strategies in RTOS" → use the comparison table. 4. "Discuss interrupt latency sources and reduction techniques" → mention DPC, NVIC prioritization, tail-chaining. ### ✅ Examiner Trivia (Frequent 2-Mark Twisters) - *Q: "Why is `malloc()` discouraged in hard real-time systems?"* **A:** Non-deterministic execution time, fragmentation, and unpredictable failure modes (e.g., OOM at runtime). - *Q: "Name the two protocols that bound priority inversion."* **A:** PIP and PCP. - *Q: "What is the ceiling of a resource never accessed by any task?"* **A:** Undefined / Idle priority (theoretically $\infty$, practically system idle). ---
